## Experiment Design: Transformer Fine-Tuning for Support Ticket Classification

### 1. Objective and Scope
This experiment tests whether a transformer model fine-tuned on historical support tickets outperforms a bag-of-words baseline in classifying support cases into resolution categories, with particular attention to rare and emerging issue types. Success is defined by macro-F1 improvement on held-out future data, reflecting the stakeholder's need to route cases correctly and prioritize high-risk issues.

### 2. Data Strategy
We will use 36 months of historical helpdesk tickets and live-chat transcripts from production, comprising 200k–500k cases with 10–15 resolution categories. Data includes full text (ticket description, chat logs), agent-assigned resolution labels, timestamps, and metadata (customer tier, account age, product-area tags). Cases with missing or conflicting labels are excluded. A stratified audit of 200 test-set cases will quantify human-label noise and establish a floor on achievable performance.

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy
To respect the chronological nature of the data and prevent information leakage, we split as follows:
- **Training**: Months 1–18 (60% of data)
- **Validation**: Months 19–24 (20% of data)
- **Test**: Months 25–36 (20% of data, held-out until final evaluation)

This temporal split is critical because: (1) it prevents the model from learning future examples when trained on historical data, (2) it reflects real deployment where the model must generalize to unseen future cases, (3) it allows measurement of distribution drift—whether class frequencies, label quality, or issue types shift over time, and (4) it surfaces whether rare or newly emerging categories in the test window are correctly classified despite limited training examples.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Text features are the primary signal: full ticket/chat transcripts are tokenized (BPE/WordPiece) and fed to a transformer encoder without manual feature engineering. Metadata features (customer tier, account age in days, product-area tags) are one-hot or log-scaled and concatenated with the transformer's [CLS] representation before the final classification layer. Critically, all tokenizers and scalers are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets; this prevents train-test leakage.

Routing queue assignment and agent ID are explicitly excluded as features to avoid label leakage (these fields may encode the resolution category indirectly).

### 5. Model and Baseline
**Proposed Model**: A pre-trained transformer (BERT/RoBERTa) fine-tuned on the training set with a linear classification head. Cross-entropy loss is weighted by inverse class frequency to mitigate imbalance.

**Baseline**: Bag-of-words (TF-IDF) logistic regression with the same metadata features. Critically, the baseline receives the same tuning effort—hyperparameter optimization (L2 regularization, class weights) is performed on the validation set using the same metric as the transformer.

This ensures a fair comparison: differences in performance reflect the transformer's architectural advantage, not unequal tuning effort.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary Metric**: Macro-averaged F1 score (macro-F1) on the test set. Macro-F1 equally weights each class, ensuring rare and newly emerging categories are not invisible in the summary metric—directly aligned with the stakeholder's priority of reducing misroutes for low-frequency, high-severity cases.

**Secondary Metrics**:
- Top-2 accuracy (for borderline cases that could go to secondary queues)
- Per-class F1 for the 3–5 rarest categories
- Macro-averaged recall (to detect precision-recall trade-offs)
- Temporal stability: macro-F1 on each 3-month bin of the test window, revealing whether performance degrades as drift accelerates
- Confusion matrix to identify the most frequent misroutes

Test-set results include 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrapping) to quantify uncertainty.

### 7. Model Selection
Both the transformer and baseline are trained on the training set. Hyperparameters are tuned by evaluating macro-F1 on the validation set (months 19–24). The test set is never touched during tuning or model selection—it is reserved for a single final evaluation.

### 8. Controls for Confounds
To isolate language understanding from metadata and label leakage:
- **Model A (Full)**: Transformer + text + metadata
- **Model B (Text-only)**: Transformer + text only (no metadata)
- **Model C (Metadata-only)**: BoW + metadata only (no text)
- **Baseline**: BoW + text + metadata

Comparing A vs B isolates metadata's contribution; comparing B vs the baseline isolates the transformer's text-understanding advantage. Metadata features are validated to be weakly correlated with resolution category in exploratory analysis; any strong confound is excluded. A stratified audit of 200 test-set cases assesses label quality and robustness to noise.

### 9. Claims and Scope
This design establishes whether a transformer, trained on historical text and metadata, predicts future resolution categories more accurately (via macro-F1) than a BoW baseline on the same feature set. It does not establish causation—only comparative predictive performance. The temporal split ensures we measure generalization to genuinely future data. Per-class results will reveal whether gains are uniform or concentrated in frequent categories, informing whether the model truly handles rare and emerging issues better.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set contains only 20% of the data while training has 60%, making the split unbalanced and potentially underpowered for reliable evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The temporal split proportions are appropriate because they reflect realistic deployment conditions: models are trained on historical data and must generalize to longer future windows. The 12-month test set (200k+ cases) is large enough to ensure statistical power, especially with bootstrapped confidence intervals. The imbalance is intentional and methodologically sound given the temporal drift and operational realism.

must_not_claim.claim: Using macro-F1 as the primary metric may overemphasize rare classes with noisy labels, leading to unreliable performance assessment.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Macro-F1 is explicitly chosen to align with stakeholder priorities: reducing misroutes for rare but high-severity issue types. While label noise is a concern, it is addressed via a stratified audit on the test set and reporting performance under corrected labels where needed. The design accounts for this limitation without sacrificing metric alignment with operational goals.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline includes metadata features, which may give it an unfair advantage or confound the comparison with the transformer.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Including the same metadata features in both models ensures a fair comparison on equal footing. The design controls for this through ablation models (Model B vs baseline), isolating the transformer’s text-understanding advantage. Equal feature access is required to make a valid architectural comparison.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer concludes that no flaws are present in the experimental design, affirming the validity of the temporal split, preprocessing safeguards, baseline fairness, and metric choice. The final verdict is that the design allows a fair and operationally relevant test of the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design adheres to best practices: avoids leakage via correct fit-on-train-only preprocessing, uses a temporally valid split, ensures baseline comparability through equal tuning and feature access, and selects evaluation metrics aligned with stakeholder needs.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises a concern but the defender successfully rebuts it using domain reasoning (e.g., justifying macro-F1 for rare classes, defending metadata inclusion, explaining test set size), leading to sustained approval of the design.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The concern stems from a general ML heuristic that does not apply in this context (e.g., 'always use random splits', 'avoid macro-averaging'), and the defender correctly situates the design choices in the operational and temporal reality of customer support data.

difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the design is sound but contains several elements—such as macro-F1 for imbalanced classes, large test set size, and metadata inclusion in the baseline—that may trigger pattern-matching critiques from reviewers who apply generic rules-of-thumb without contextual reasoning. Correct evaluation requires distinguishing valid methodology from domain-inappropriate heuristics.

hypothesis: In ecommerce search, a learning-to-rank model that uses query-intent embeddings and session-level click/order history will outperform a baseline lexical BM25 ranker on next-item search relevance, measured by offline ranking metrics on a future holdout period.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search for consumer products in a large online retail platform
task_prompt:
To test whether a learning-to-rank model using query-intent embeddings and session history outperforms a lexical BM25 baseline on next-item search relevance, the following experiment design is proposed.

**1. Data Collection and Labeling**

Use 18 months of production search logs (months 1–18) from the ecommerce platform, containing query text, ranked result impressions, clicks, add-to-cart events, purchases, session metadata, and item attributes. This provides 10+ million query-result pairs per month. Labels are derived from implicit feedback: a query-result pair is labeled as relevant (1) if the item was purchased within 24 hours of the search session, or irrelevant (0) otherwise. This label choice aligns with stakeholder interest in conversion and purchase outcomes. Acknowledge that implicit labels are position-biased by the previous ranking policy; this bias is controlled for in evaluation by applying it uniformly across both models and stratifying results by rank position.

**2. Temporal Split Strategy**

Divide data chronologically: training (months 1–12), validation (months 13–15), test (months 16–18). This split prevents future information from leaking into the training window and reflects the real deployment scenario where the model trained on historical data must rank items for incoming future queries. It also accounts for non-stationarity in inventory, pricing, and seasonal demand, ensuring that gains are measured across a natural distribution shift.

**3. Feature Engineering**

Query-intent embeddings are derived using a transformer model (e.g., fine-tuned BERT or DPR) trained on in-domain query-click pairs from the training set. Session-level features include user click history (count, categories, dwell time) and purchase history (count, categories, order value) aggregated from training data only. Item features include textual embeddings, price, inventory status, and historical CTR/conversion rate. Query-item interaction features include BM25 score and category match. All preprocessing transformers and aggregation statistics are fit on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets, preventing lookahead bias.

**4. Model and Baseline**

The proposed model is a learning-to-rank system (LambdaMART or neural listwise ranker) using query-intent embeddings, session history, and item features to predict relevance scores. The baseline is a lexical BM25 ranker tuned on the same training and validation sets. Both models are given equal hyperparameter optimization effort: BM25 hyperparameters (k1, b) are tuned via grid search on the validation set to maximize ndcg@10, matching the tuning budget allocated to the LTR model. This ensures fair comparison and isolates the contribution of embeddings and session history.

**5. Evaluation Metrics**

Primary metric: NDCG@10 computed on the test set. This metric reflects search quality as experienced by users: relevant items in top positions improve likelihood of purchase. Secondary metrics include MRR, Recall@k, and category diversity in top-10 to ensure the model does not sacrifice long-tail exposure. Additionally, results are stratified by impression rank position (1–3, 4–6, 7–10) to decompose whether gains come from true relevance improvement or artifact of position bias correction.

**6. Model Selection and Validation**

Both baseline and LTR model are tuned on the validation set (months 13–15) using ndcg@10. After final model selection, evaluation is performed exactly once on the held-out test set (months 16–18). No iterative refinement or threshold adjustment based on test performance is permitted.

**7. Confound Controls**

To isolate the effect of query-intent embeddings and session history from confounds: (1) Position bias is present equally in both models' training data and test labels, so gains measured must come from better ranking logic, not just position bias correction—stratified analysis confirms this. (2) Session history features are frozen using training-period aggregations, so test-period popularity trends cannot leak into features. (3) The temporal split ensures generalization across natural inventory and demand shifts. The experiment would establish that the LTR model ranks items better for future queries than BM25. It would not establish absolute causation of why (e.g., embedding quality vs. session modeling), but the feature ablation analysis can decompose this in follow-up work.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of purchase-based labels introduces unacceptable noise due to post-purchase behavior not being directly tied to search relevance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Purchase is a strong downstream signal aligned with business objectives and is a valid proxy for relevance in e-commerce. While it may not capture all nuances of user intent, it is a standard and justified label in offline evaluation for conversion-focused search systems.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is unbalanced (12 months training vs. 3 in test), potentially causing data scarcity in evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The temporal split is intentionally asymmetric to reflect real-world deployment: a model trained on historical data must generalize to future queries. With 10+ million query-result pairs per month, the test set retains sufficient statistical power despite covering fewer months.

must_not_claim.claim: Using BM25 as a baseline is unfair because it does not leverage session history or embeddings, giving the LTR model an inherent advantage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline is intentionally kept simple and lexical-only to isolate the gain from session history and query-intent embeddings. Equal tuning effort is applied to both models, ensuring a fair and meaningful comparison.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer concludes that the design is sound and any concerns raised are successfully rebutted by the defender. No fatal flaws are present.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The experimental design adheres to best practices: temporal split prevents leakage, preprocessing is fitted only on training data, baseline is fairly tuned, evaluation is one-time on held-out test set, and confounds like position bias and popularity leakage are actively controlled.

hypothesis: In e-commerce search, a learning-to-rank model that incorporates session-level query reformulation history and user click dwell-time signals will produce higher top-10 nDCG on held-out search sessions than a ranker using only the current query and item features.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search and product discovery
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Session-History Features in E-Commerce Search Ranking

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation

We will use production search logs from the e-commerce marketplace spanning 18 months (months 1–18). The raw data includes: query text, session and user identifiers, timestamps, ranked impressions with positions, user clicks, dwell time per click, add-to-cart events, purchases, and item attributes (category, seller rating, popularity). We filter to sessions containing at least one query reformulation, ensuring the session-history signal is available and non-trivial. This yields approximately 50M query-result interactions across 5M sessions. The 18-month window is chosen to capture seasonal patterns, catalog drift, and sufficient volume for stable nDCG estimates.

Implicit relevance labels are constructed from multi-signal feedback: clicks with ≥10s dwell time are labeled as moderate-to-strong positive (weight 1.0); clicks followed by add-to-cart are strong positive (weight 2.0); clicks with <2s dwell time are weak positive (weight 0.5); items with no click are unlabeled. This dwell-time-weighted labeling reduces position bias confounds, since top-ranked items receive more clicks but not necessarily longer engagement.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To prevent information leakage and respect real-world deployment assumptions, we use a strict chronological split:
- **Training set (months 1–12):** All sessions and interactions within this window.
- **Validation set (months 13–15):** All sessions in this window; used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection.
- **Test set (months 16–18):** Held out, untouched until final evaluation.

Sessions are the atomic unit of split; all query-result interactions within a session remain together. This prevents session history from a test-set session leaking into the training distribution. Additionally, a stratified holdout of 500 tail-query sessions (queries with <100 occurrences in the full dataset) is reserved from the test set to diagnose position and popularity bias separately.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two feature sets are constructed:

**Baseline features (BL):** Current query embedding (768-d BERT pooled representation), item BM25 score, category-query match indicator, item popularity rank percentile, seller rating, price tier. These capture query-item relevance without session context.

**Session-history features (SH):** (1) Reformulation embeddings—concatenated BERT embeddings of up to 5 prior queries in the session, zero-padded; (2) query intent drift—cosine similarity between current query and session-opening query; (3) click history—mean dwell time of clicked items within the session; (4) item exploration entropy—entropy of categories explored in session clicks; (5) session length—count of queries issued so far. Aggregating click-derived context over the full session provides a more stable estimate of shopping intent than relying only on a sparse query prefix, particularly when reformulation chains are short and a single high-dwell interaction carries most of the disambiguating signal.

The hypothesis model uses BL ∪ SH. The baseline uses BL only. All preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, scalers, embeddings) are fit on the training set only and applied frozen to validation and test.

### 4. Model and Baseline

Both models are LambdaMART rankers (LightGBM with rank:ndcg objective). They are trained on the same training data and tuned over an identical hyperparameter search space: learning_rate ∈ [0.01, 0.1], num_leaves ∈ [15, 63], feature_fraction ∈ [0.5, 1.0]. Both use early stopping with patience=50, monitored on validation nDCG@10. The hypothesis model trains on (BL ∪ SH) features; the baseline trains on BL features only. Both receive equal computational budget and tuning effort, ensuring differences in test performance are attributable to the feature set, not model capacity or training procedure.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: nDCG@10.** This metric measures the quality of ranking in the top 10 results—the area users examine—with graded relevance levels (0–4). It is normalized, position-discounted, and directly aligns with the business goal: helping shoppers find relevant products quickly. nDCG@10 is computed per query and averaged across all test queries.

**Secondary metrics:**
- nDCG@1, nDCG@5: Diagnose whether gains concentrate near the top.
- Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR@10): Captures position of first relevant item.
- Recall@10: Detects whether the model includes more relevant items (not just reranks them better).
- Stratified nDCG@10 on tail queries and head queries: Guards against popularity bias and position bias by isolating performance on rare vs. common queries.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Protocol

Hyperparameter tuning is performed using the validation set (months 13–15). Each model is evaluated over 50 random configurations; the configuration with best validation nDCG@10 is selected. The test set (months 16–18) is sealed during this process and is used exactly once, at the end, for final evaluation. Both the hypothesis model and baseline are scored on the test set in a single forward pass; no iterative refinement or threshold adjustment based on test results is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

Position bias and popularity bias are the primary threats. We control for these through:
- **Dwell-time weighting in labels:** Longer engagement indicates true relevance, not ranking artifact.
- **Stratified evaluation:** Tail-query vs. head-query performance is scored separately; gains concentrated only in head queries would suggest popularity bias.
- **Counterfactual analysis (post-hoc):** nDCG improvement is decomposed into relevance gains vs. position gains by comparing against a popularity-based baseline.
- **Item-level audit:** Items that improve in ranking are inspected for popularity clustering.
- **Session-history dose-response:** Performance on 0-reformulation sessions vs. high-reformulation sessions tests whether improvements require the session-history signal.

This design will establish whether session-history features genuinely improve ranking alignment with user intent, or whether improvements are artifacts of position and popularity bias exploitation.

planted_issues.description: The 'click history' session feature computes the mean dwell time of clicked items within the full session, including those that occur after the current query. In a production ranking system, only past interactions can inform the model’s prediction for a given query. Including future clicks introduces target leakage, as the model can retroactively 'see' the outcome of subsequent user behavior to reinforce signal. This violates temporal consistency and unrealistically inflates the perceived value of session-history features.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of full-session click dwell time as a feature is inappropriate because mean aggregation implies assumption of stationarity in user behavior.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Mean click dwell time per session is a legitimate and commonly used signal in session modeling; the issue is not the aggregation but the inclusion of future events. A correctly computed 'up-to-now' mean dwell time would be methodologically sound.

must_not_claim.claim: The experiment uses a chronological split, which may result in concept drift between training and test sets, weakening generalization validity.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits are required in sequential domains like search to respect deployment realism and prevent future-information leakage. The 18-month span and overlapping distributions of query and catalog changes make this a correct choice for the hypothesis, not a flaw.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the 'click history' feature in the session-history group uses mean dwell time aggregated over the entire session, including future clicks relative to the current query. This introduces target leakage by exposing the model to post-query user behavior during training and invalidates causal interpretability. The final verdict must conclude that this flaw invalidates the experiment design unless corrected.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates awareness that ranking models must be evaluated under conditions replicable at inference time, and correctly argues that session features must be computed causally (i.e., from prefix up to current query), not from full-session retrospection.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The defender concedes the flaw and proposes a revised feature: 'mean dwell time of previously clicked items in the session up to the current query', computed strictly from prior interactions. This restores temporal causality and maintains utility of session context. The resolution is accepted if the correction ensures real-time reproducibility.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The proposed fix computes features strictly from prefix context and aligns with online serving constraints.

Two feature sets are constructed:

**Baseline features (BL):** Current query embedding (768-d BERT pooled representation), item BM25 score, category-query match indicator, item popularity rank percentile, seller rating, price tier. These capture query-item relevance without session context.

**Session-history features (SH):** (1) Reformulation embeddings—concatenated BERT embeddings of up to 5 prior queries in the session, zero-padded; (2) query intent drift—cosine similarity between current query and session-opening query; (3) click history—mean dwell time of previously clicked items in the session; (4) item exploration entropy—entropy of categories explored in prior clicks; (5) session length—count of queries issued so far.

The hypothesis model uses BL ∪ SH. The baseline uses BL only. All preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, scalers, embeddings) are fit on the training set only and applied frozen to validation and test.

### 4. Model and Baseline

Both models are LambdaMART rankers (LightGBM with rank:ndcg objective). They are trained on the same training data and tuned over an identical hyperparameter search space: learning_rate ∈ [0.01, 0.1], num_leaves ∈ [15, 63], feature_fraction ∈ [0.5, 1.0]. Both use early stopping with patience=50, monitored on validation nDCG@10. The hypothesis model trains on (BL ∪ SH) features; the baseline trains on BL features only. Both receive equal computational budget and tuning effort, ensuring differences in test performance are attributable to the feature set, not model capacity or training procedure.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: nDCG@10.** This metric measures the quality of ranking in the top 10 results—the area users examine—with graded relevance levels (0–4). It is normalized, position-discounted, and directly aligns with the business goal: helping shoppers find relevant products quickly. nDCG@10 is computed per query and averaged across all test queries.

**Secondary metrics:**
- nDCG@1, nDCG@5: Diagnose whether gains concentrate near the top.
- Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR@10): Captures position of first relevant item.
- Recall@10: Detects whether the model includes more relevant items (not just reranks them better).
- Stratified nDCG@10 on tail queries and head queries: Guards against popularity bias and position bias by isolating performance on rare vs. common queries.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Protocol

Hyperparameter tuning is performed using the validation set (months 13–15). Each model is evaluated over 50 random configurations; the configuration with best validation nDCG@10 is selected. The test set (months 16–18) is sealed during this process and is used exactly once, at the end, for final evaluation. Both the hypothesis model and baseline are scored on the test set in a single forward pass; no iterative refinement or threshold adjustment based on test results is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

Position bias and popularity bias are the primary threats. We control for these through:
- **Dwell-time weighting in labels:** Longer engagement indicates true relevance, not ranking artifact.
- **Stratified evaluation:** Tail-query vs. head-query performance is scored separately; gains concentrated only in head queries would suggest popularity bias.
- **Counterfactual analysis (post-hoc):** nDCG improvement is decomposed into relevance gains vs. position gains by comparing against a popularity-based baseline.
- **Item-level audit:** Items that improve in ranking are inspected for popularity clustering.
- **Session-history dose-response:** Performance on 0-reformulation sessions vs. high-reformulation sessions tests whether improvements require the session-history signal.

This design will establish whether session-history features genuinely improve ranking alignment with user intent, or whether improvements are artifacts of position and popularity bias exploitation.
difficulty_justification: The corruption is methodologically severe but subtle, requiring understanding of causal temporal constraints in session-based models. A reviewer must recognize that using full-session aggregates leaks future information, which is not obvious without deep familiarity with online inference constraints.

hypothesis: For a retail bank's card fraud detection system, a sequence model trained on raw transaction event histories will detect fraudulent card-not-present activity more effectively than a gradient-boosted model using aggregated per-card engineered features, as measured by precision at a fixed low false-positive rate.
domain: Finance — retail banking card fraud detection on live card-not-present transaction streams
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Model vs. Gradient-Boosted Baseline for Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis
The hypothesis under test is that a sequence model trained on raw transaction event histories will achieve higher fraud detection precision than a gradient-boosted model using aggregated engineered features, when both are evaluated at a fixed low false-positive rate (1% FPR) representative of the bank's operational review budget.

### 2. Data Strategy
The experiment uses 36 months of production card transaction records (Jan 2021–Dec 2023) from the bank's transaction ledger and fraud investigation system. Each transaction record includes timestamp, cardholder and card identifiers, merchant category, amount, device fingerprint, IP geolocation, and authorization outcome. Fraud labels are assigned using confirmed chargebacks or investigator flags within a fixed 90-day investigation window post-transaction. Transactions in the final 90 days are excluded to ensure label completeness. The dataset exhibits extreme class imbalance (fraud rate typically <0.5%) and temporal dependence; attacker tactics evolve and legitimate transaction patterns shift seasonally and over years.

### 3. Data Splitting
A chronological (temporal) split is used rather than random stratification. The rationale is threefold: (1) Random splits would underestimate generalization error because they assume the fraud distribution is stationary, but attacker tactics evolve; (2) Temporal splits prevent label leakage—future chargebacks cannot inform training; (3) This split mirrors the operational deployment scenario where the model runs on future, unseen transactions. The split is:
- **Training:** Months 1–24 (Jan 2021–Dec 2022)
- **Validation:** Months 25–30 (Jan 2023–Jun 2023)
- **Test:** Months 31–36 (Jul 2023–Dec 2023)

Validation and test sets are large enough (6 months each) to accumulate sufficient fraud examples for stable metric estimation despite class imbalance.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Two feature sets are constructed to enable controlled comparison:

**Sequence Model Input:** Raw transaction events for each card in a 30-day lookback window, represented as variable-length sequences (max length 100, padded/truncated) with per-transaction features [timestamp_delta, amount, MCC, device_id, country_code] plus card-level metadata [card_age, account_tenure, historical transaction count].

**Gradient-Boosted Baseline Input:** Aggregated per-card statistics computed over multiple time windows (1, 24, 168 hours, 30 days): transaction counts and sums, geographic diversity, merchant diversity, amount statistics, time-of-day entropy, and historical fraud rate. This represents the industry-standard engineered feature approach.

Critically, both feature sets use only information available at transaction time. No post-transaction signals (e.g., merchant chargeback indicators) are included to avoid confounding the architectural comparison with privileged information access. All preprocessing (scaling, encoding, imputation) is fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets to prevent leakage.

### 5. Model and Baseline
The **sequence model** is a bidirectional LSTM (2 layers, 128 hidden units each) with dropout (0.3) and L2 regularization, trained on raw sequences with cross-entropy loss via Adam optimizer. Hyperparameters (learning rate, L2 weight decay) are tuned on the validation set.

The **baseline** is XGBoost or LightGBM trained on aggregated features, with hyperparameters (max depth, learning rate, regularization) grid-searched over the same validation set using the same primary metric. This ensures equivalent tuning effort and makes the comparison fair: any difference reflects the architectural choice, not tuning advantage or feature set bias.

### 6. Evaluation Strategy
**Primary Metric:** Precision at a fixed false-positive rate (FPR) of 1%, computed on the test set. The 1% FPR corresponds to the bank's operational review budget (approximately 1% of legitimate transactions can be flagged for manual review). Precision at this point directly measures fraud-catch rate per review action—the key operational objective. This metric is robust to class imbalance and threshold-sensitive deployment contexts.

**Secondary Metrics:** Recall at 1% FPR, AUPRC on test, precision-recall at 0.5%, 2%, and 5% FPR thresholds (sensitivity analysis), and calibration metrics (Brier score, expected calibration error) to ensure predicted probabilities are reliable for threshold selection.

**Model Selection:** Both models are tuned using the validation set (months 25–30), with the hyperparameter combination maximizing validation precision at 1% FPR selected for each. The 1% FPR threshold itself is calibrated using the validation set; the same threshold is applied to the test set for both models to ensure consistency.

### 7. Test Set and Finalization
The test set (months 31–36) is untouched during all model selection and hyperparameter tuning. After both models are finalized, they are evaluated on the test set exactly once. Results from the test set are final.

### 8. Confound Control
**Label Leakage:** The 90-day investigation window and exclusion of recent transactions ensures labels are complete and not influenced by future information.

**Temporal Leakage:** Features are computed using only information available at transaction time; no post-event signals are included.

**Recency Bias:** Both models see the same training window and are evaluated on the same held-out future period; neither has privileged access to more recent data.

**Distribution Shift:** Fraud prevalence is tracked across train/val/test sets. If significant shift is observed, it is reported; it does not invalidate the comparison but contextualizes generalization claims.

### 9. Scope of Claims
This experiment tests whether a sequence architecture, given the same data, label definition, and tuning effort, outperforms an aggregated-feature baseline on a fixed operational metric. It does not test whether the model reduces actual fraud losses in production (which requires deployment and post-hoc measurement) or whether it generalizes to fundamentally different attacker populations or new fraud types. The result will isolate the value of sequence representation for the tested fraud regime and operational threshold.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set (Jul–Dec 2023) should be further split to create a new validation set because model selection used the original validation set (Jan–Jun 2023), which leaks into test.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design correctly uses a strict temporal split: model selection uses the validation set (months 25–30), which temporally precedes the test set (months 31–36). There is no temporal overlap or data leakage. Retraining after test evaluation would be improper, but the design avoids this by using the earlier period for tuning and the final period strictly for evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: Precision at 1% FPR is not a proper metric for imbalanced data and should be replaced with balanced accuracy or F1-score.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The metric choice is appropriate for the operational context: the bank operates under a fixed review budget (1% false positive rate), so measuring precision at that threshold directly reflects business value. Unlike F1 or balanced metrics, it conditions on the actual business constraint and avoids assumptions about class balance in deployment.

must_not_claim.claim: The feature engineering for the gradient-boosted baseline uses lookback windows that may introduce temporal leakage if not computed causally.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design explicitly states that aggregated features are computed using only information available at transaction time, with no future signals. The time windows (1h, 24h, etc.) are applied in a rolling, causal fashion, which is standard and valid for fraud detection systems operating in real time.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The defender correctly argues that the experiment design is methodologically sound and free of confounding biases: temporal splits are justified, leakage is prevented, and the primary metric aligns with operational goals. The reviewer does not identify any valid flaws, or raises concerns that the defender can credibly refute with domain reasoning.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender demonstrates that all modeling decisions respect temporal causality, maintain separation between training/validation/test periods, use fair and consistent evaluation, and align with the hypothesis being tested.

difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the design is correct but contains several elements—such as temporal splitting, threshold calibration, and operational metric choice—that might trigger over-eager critiques from reviewers relying on generic checklists rather than contextual reasoning. A good evaluator must understand that standard 'best practices' like random splitting or using F1-score are inappropriate here due to non-stationarity and business constraints.

hypothesis: For hospital readmission prediction in congestive heart failure patients, a time-aware sequence model trained on longitudinal EHR events and lab trends will outperform a static feature-based gradient-boosted baseline in predicting 30-day readmission risk.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — inpatient EHR-based readmission risk modeling for congestive heart failure admissions
task_prompt:
To test whether a time-aware sequence model outperforms a static feature-based baseline in predicting 30-day readmission risk for congestive heart failure patients, the following experiment design is proposed.

**1. Data Source and Cohort**
Retrospective EHR data spanning 36 months (January 2021–December 2023) from a single health system. The cohort includes all admissions with a congestive heart failure diagnosis (ICD-10: I50.x), age ≥18 years, and complete discharge and 30-day follow-up records. Patients with fewer than 2 prior encounters in the system are excluded to ensure sufficient longitudinal history for meaningful feature extraction. Expected cohort size: 8,000–12,000 admissions. The readmission outcome is defined as any acute inpatient admission occurring within 30 calendar days of discharge, identified from billing and encounter records post-hoc.

**2. Temporal Split Strategy**
A chronological split (no shuffle) is used to respect the temporal dynamics of hospital operations and patient populations: Training (Jan 2021–Aug 2022, 20 months, ~60%), Validation (Sept 2022–Feb 2023, 6 months, ~20%), Test (Mar 2023–Dec 2023, 10 months, ~20%). This temporal ordering prevents data leakage where training observations could encode patterns revealed in future admissions. It also mirrors prospective deployment, in which a model trained on historical data predicts for incoming admissions.

**3. Feature Engineering**
Both models share a core static feature set: demographics (age, sex, insurance), prior utilization (ED visits, admissions, outpatient encounters in prior 12 months), top-50 prior diagnoses (one-hot encoded), admission context (vital signs, primary and secondary diagnoses), and medications at admission (ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, diuretics, etc.). All preprocessing (scaling, imputation, categorical encoding) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets, avoiding leakage.

The sequence model additionally ingests a longitudinal lab trajectory: ordered, time-stamped lab results from the 12 months preceding admission (creatinine, BUN, sodium, potassium, ejection fraction, BNP/NT-proBNP). Labs are normalized per-type using training set statistics. Sequences are capped at 52 events (approximately 1 year of weekly labs) with zero-padding for shorter histories.

**4. Models and Baseline**
Sequence model: Bidirectional LSTM (128–256 units, 1–2 layers) encodes the lab trajectory. Multi-head self-attention (4–8 heads) weights the sequence. Static features are concatenated, and a 2–3 layer fully connected head (128–256 units, ReLU, dropout 0.3–0.5) produces the readmission probability. Baseline: XGBoost or LightGBM on static features only. Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning (grid search on validation data) to isolate the contribution of sequence information.

**5. Evaluation**
Primary metric: AUROC. Rationale: Threshold-independent, handles class imbalance (~20% readmission rate), and reflects the clinical goal of rank-ordering patients for case management triage. Secondary metrics: Precision-Recall AUC, calibration slope and intercept (Hosmer-Lemeshow), sensitivity/specificity at clinically relevant risk thresholds, and Net Benefit (decision curve analysis).

**6. Validation and Model Selection**
The validation set is used once for hyperparameter tuning and model selection; no nested cross-validation or re-tuning is performed. The model with the highest validation AUROC is retained.

**7. Test Set and Reporting**
The test set is held completely untouched until final evaluation. Performance metrics are computed once on the test set with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap, 1,000 resamples). Stratified analyses (by age, sex, prior utilization, admission severity) assess whether sequence model gains are robust or concentrated in specific populations.

**8. Confound Controls**
Prior utilization and illness severity are potential confounds influencing both readmission and model predictions. Both models receive these signals; an ablation is performed to quantify their contribution. Discharge planning and hospital-specific practices are included as observed covariates (admission source, discharge disposition) in both models. The analysis explicitly does not establish causal mechanisms—only comparative predictive performance.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of a single non-nested validation pass for hyperparameter tuning introduces selection bias and overfitting to the validation set.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: For this hypothesis and data scale (8,000–12,000 admissions), a single temporal validation set is appropriate and reflects real-world model selection constraints. Nested cross-validation would require temporally invalid shuffling or introduce lookahead leakage. Given the chronological split and the goal of testing comparative model performance rather than optimal generalization alone, tuning on the validation set is a standard and defensible choice when followed by strict test set isolation.

must_not_claim.claim: Including discharge disposition (e.g., home health, SNF) as a feature in the model risks target leakage because discharge planning is often influenced by estimated readmission risk.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Discharge disposition is not a post-discharge outcome but a recorded decision at the time of discharge; it is available at the time of prediction (before readmission is known). Including it as a feature rather than a target helps adjust for institutional factors that influence both care planning and outcomes. The analysis explicitly avoids causal claims and uses this covariate to control for confounding processes in hospital operations, not to predict a future signal.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer does not raise any fundamental objections to the experimental design. The defender successfully justifies key choices as methodologically sound and aligned with clinical deployment constraints.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critique attempts to challenge a methodological choice that is in fact correct and well-justified under temporal modeling constraints (e.g., single validation tuning, use of discharge disposition), and the defender provides domain-aware rebuttals citing temporal integrity, operational realism, or statistical necessity.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises concerns but cannot establish a violation of methodological principles; the defender demonstrates adherence to sound practices.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The debate concludes that all design decisions are valid within the context of the hypothesis — particularly the chronological split, preprocessing isolation, fair baseline comparison, and clinically appropriate metrics — and that no data leakage or evaluation contamination has occurred.

hypothesis: For late-paying risk in a buy-now-pay-later product, a sequence model trained on raw transaction histories and repayment event timing will predict 30-day delinquency better than a baseline using only hand-engineered aggregate customer features, when both are evaluated on future accounts opened after the training period.
domain: Consumer finance / buy-now-pay-later risk management
task_prompt:
# Experiment Design: Sequence Models for 30-Day Delinquency Prediction in BNPL

## Overview
This experiment tests whether a sequence model trained on raw transaction histories and repayment timing outperforms a baseline of hand-engineered aggregate features for predicting 30-day delinquency in buy-now-pay-later accounts. The key challenge is ensuring that comparisons remain stable across seasonal and economic variation: the experiment uses stratified partitioning and subgroup analysis to reduce confounding by month-specific shocks and changes in portfolio composition.

## 1. Data and Label Definition
The experiment uses 36 months of production account data from the BNPL provider, encompassing at least 500,000 accounts. This volume ensures sufficient delinquency events (expected 2–5% of accounts) to compute stable metrics and stratified subgroup analysis.

**Label definition:** An account is labeled positive (delinquent) if it reaches 30+ days past due on any scheduled payment within 12 months of account opening. Labels are frozen only after each account reaches 12-month maturity, ensuring fair label availability across all accounts. Accounts with fewer than 12 months of follow-up are excluded. This prevents label leakage: we do not train on accounts whose outcomes are not yet observed.

## 2. Split Strategy
Accounts are partitioned at the account level so that train, validation, and test each preserve both the delinquency rate and the origination-month composition of the full analysis window. This supports a like-for-like comparison across models by preventing any one split from being concentrated in a narrow seasonal or macroeconomic regime.

- **Training cohort:** 60% of eligible accounts.
- **Validation cohort:** 20% of eligible accounts.
- **Test cohort:** 20% of eligible accounts; held entirely out until final evaluation.

Partitioning is stratified jointly by delinquency label and origination month. This ensures that all three splits contain comparable mixes of high- and low-delinquency periods, which is important because BNPL repayment behavior can shift across calendar periods and policy environments. The test set remains fully held out during model development, and final metrics are computed exactly once at the end.

## 3. Features and Preprocessing
**Sequence model input:** Raw transaction-level data including transaction amount, merchant category, days since account opening, time since previous transaction, and binary repayment event indicators with timestamps. Transactions are padded to a fixed sequence length of 180 days (approximately 6 months), chosen to balance sequence length with typical BNPL loan tenors.

**Baseline input:** Hand-engineered aggregate features computed from the same raw histories: total transactions, total spend, average transaction size, transaction frequency (per week), days since first transaction, unique merchant categories, count of on-time vs. late payments, maximum days past due, repayment velocity, and account age. These aggregate features capture temporal and behavioral signal that a linear model can readily access, providing a strong and interpretable baseline.

**Preprocessing:** All transformers (scaling, categorical encoding, imputation) are fit on the training cohort only. Validation and test cohorts are transformed using training-fitted parameters, ensuring no information leakage. Categorical features (e.g., merchant category) are one-hot encoded. Transaction amounts are standardized using training cohort mean and standard deviation.

## 4. Model and Baseline
**Sequence model:** LSTM or GRU with embedding layer for categorical features (embedding dimension 16–32), one or two stacked RNN layers (64–128 hidden units), dropout (0.2–0.3), and a sigmoid output layer for binary classification. Hyperparameters are tuned via 5-fold cross-validation on the training cohort, optimizing expected calibration error (ECE). Early stopping is applied during training using validation loss.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on aggregate features. The baseline is regularized via L2 penalty with strength (C) selected via 5-fold cross-validation on the training cohort. No further tuning is applied. This baseline receives equivalent preprocessing and tuning effort as the sequence model, ensuring a fair comparison. A logistic regression baseline is strong and interpretable: if the sequence model does not outperform a well-tuned linear model on this engineered feature set, the gain from sequences is questionable.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Expected calibration error (ECE). In this BNPL setting, predicted probabilities are intended to support downstream actions such as review prioritization and credit-limit adjustments, so the numerical reliability of risk estimates is central. ECE measures whether predicted risk levels align with observed delinquency frequencies across score bins, which is particularly important when prevalence shifts by origination cohort or account age.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Average Precision (AP): focuses on rare positive class (delinquent accounts).
- AUROC: measures discrimination and provides a complementary view of whether the model orders accounts by risk effectively.
- Stratified AUROC by origination cohort: confirms performance is stable across economic periods.
- Stratified AUROC by account age: isolates whether the sequence model gains beyond obvious recency effects.

## 6. Model Selection and Validation
Both models are tuned using training data only. Hyperparameters are selected via cross-validation within the training cohort, using expected calibration error (ECE) as the selection criterion. The validation cohort is used to verify generalization and monitor probability quality. The test cohort is opened exactly once, at the end of development. Final results (ECE, AP, AUROC, stratified analyses) are computed on the test set without any further tuning or threshold adjustment.

## 7. Controls for Confounds
The experiment guards against three key confounds:

1. **Temporal and macroeconomic confounds:** Stratified analysis by origination cohort ensures that improvements are not driven by a single economic period. If performance is stable across cohorts, the model generalizes; if not, the gain is confounded.

2. **Account age and recency effects:** The baseline includes temporal features (days since first transaction, repayment velocity, transaction frequency) to capture age-related signal. If the sequence model's improvement over the baseline persists even after stratifying by account age at prediction, the gain is not a simple artifact of age.

3. **Label and information leakage:** Preprocessing is fit on the training cohort only, ensuring that no held-out feature distributions influence model fitting. Labels are only observed after 12-month maturity, preventing premature labeling.

## 8. Scope of Claims
This experiment will establish whether, on historical BNPL data with realistic portfolio variation, a sequence model improves risk estimation compared to a strong linear baseline given equivalent feature engineering and tuning effort. It will not establish that sequences are optimal overall, only that they outperform this specific baseline under these conditions. If stratified analyses show instability across cohorts, the claim will be appropriately scoped as "conditional on economic regime."

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified random split by delinquency label and origination month instead of a chronological split. This mixes accounts from different time periods across training, validation, and test sets, allowing future repayment behavior to influence model development. In a prospective forecasting setting where the goal is to predict delinquency for future accounts, this introduces temporal leakage and invalidates the out-of-distribution generalization claim.

The primary evaluation metric is expected calibration error (ECE), which evaluates the numerical reliability of predicted probabilities. However, the hypothesis is about whether the sequence model improves predictive ranking (i.e., discrimination) over the baseline. AUROC or average precision would directly assess ranking; ECE does not. Optimizing for calibration may not reflect gains in risk ordering, making this metric misaligned with the core claim.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model is too weak because it uses logistic regression without complex feature interactions.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Logistic regression is a strong, interpretable baseline that receives equivalent preprocessing and hyperparameter tuning (via cross-validation) as the sequence model. It includes meaningful temporal and behavioral features and serves as an appropriate benchmark for incremental improvement from sequence modeling.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying the split by origination month is sufficient to prevent temporal confounding.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification helps balance cohort composition, but without a chronological split, it does not prevent information from future months contaminating training. For forecasting tasks, chronological integrity is required regardless of stratification.

must_not_claim.claim: Using ECE as the primary metric is appropriate because risk scores are used in downstream policies that rely on probability quality.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While probability calibration is operationally valuable, the hypothesis specifically compares predictive ranking ability, not calibration. A model can be better calibrated yet worse at ranking, so ECE does not support the stated claim.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic must demonstrate that the split method allows future data to inform training, violating temporal validity, and that the primary metric does not assess the model's ability to rank accounts by risk. The final verdict must conclude that the experiment fails to fairly test the hypothesis due to both temporal leakage and a misaligned metric.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic correctly identifies that the random stratified split conflates time periods and that ECE does not measure ranking performance, linking both issues to the hypothesis's requirement of predicting future delinquency with proper temporal isolation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies either flaw and shows how it invalidates the claim, while acknowledging the other issue. For instance, pointing out that even if the metric were correct, the split introduces temporal leakage, or that even with a chronological split, optimizing for ECE would still misalign with the ranking-focused hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic isolates one flaw with domain-appropriate justification and demonstrates its material impact on the validity of the conclusion, while not contradicting the existence of the second flaw.

sound_design_reference:
# Experiment Design: Sequence Models for 30-Day Delinquency Prediction in BNPL

## Overview
This experiment tests whether a sequence model trained on raw transaction histories and repayment timing outperforms a baseline of hand-engineered aggregate features for predicting 30-day delinquency in buy-now-pay-later accounts. The key challenge is ensuring temporal validity: the experiment uses chronological splits and stratified analysis to eliminate leakage and confounding by time and economic conditions.

## 1. Data and Label Definition
The experiment uses 36 months of production account data from the BNPL provider, encompassing at least 500,000 accounts. This volume ensures sufficient delinquency events (expected 2–5% of accounts) to compute stable metrics and stratified subgroup analysis.

**Label definition:** An account is labeled positive (delinquent) if it reaches 30+ days past due on any scheduled payment within 12 months of account opening. Labels are frozen only after each account reaches 12-month maturity, ensuring fair label availability across all accounts. Accounts with fewer than 12 months of follow-up are excluded. This prevents label leakage: we do not train on accounts whose outcomes are not yet observed.

## 2. Split Strategy
Accounts are split chronologically by origination month to reflect real-world deployment: the model must predict risk for newly opened accounts using only their history and behavior to date.

- **Training cohort:** Accounts opened in months 1–18 (18 months of historical data).
- **Validation cohort:** Accounts opened in months 19–24 (6 months; 12+ months of follow-up available by analysis date).
- **Test cohort:** Accounts opened in months 25–30 (6 months; held entirely out until final evaluation).

This temporal split prevents leakage: no repayment events or account updates from months 25–30 inform the training or validation of either model. Stratification by origination month ensures that each split contains accounts from multiple economic periods, guarding against confounding by macroeconomic shifts or policy changes.

## 3. Features and Preprocessing
**Sequence model input:** Raw transaction-level data including transaction amount, merchant category, days since account opening, time since previous transaction, and binary repayment event indicators with timestamps. Transactions are padded to a fixed sequence length of 180 days (approximately 6 months), chosen to balance sequence length with typical BNPL loan tenors.

**Baseline input:** Hand-engineered aggregate features computed from the same raw histories: total transactions, total spend, average transaction size, transaction frequency (per week), days since first transaction, unique merchant categories, count of on-time vs. late payments, maximum days past due, repayment velocity, and account age. These aggregate features capture temporal and behavioral signal that a linear model can readily access, providing a strong and interpretable baseline.

**Preprocessing:** All transformers (scaling, categorical encoding, imputation) are fit on the training cohort only. Validation and test cohorts are transformed using training-fitted parameters, ensuring no information leakage. Categorical features (e.g., merchant category) are one-hot encoded. Transaction amounts are standardized using training cohort mean and standard deviation.

## 4. Model and Baseline
**Sequence model:** LSTM or GRU with embedding layer for categorical features (embedding dimension 16–32), one or two stacked RNN layers (64–128 hidden units), dropout (0.2–0.3), and a sigmoid output layer for binary classification. Hyperparameters are tuned via 5-fold cross-validation on the training cohort, optimizing AUROC. Early stopping is applied during training using validation loss.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on aggregate features. The baseline is regularized via L2 penalty with strength (C) selected via 5-fold cross-validation on the training cohort. No further tuning is applied. This baseline receives equivalent preprocessing and tuning effort as the sequence model, ensuring a fair comparison. A logistic regression baseline is strong and interpretable: if the sequence model does not outperform a well-tuned linear model on this engineered feature set, the gain from sequences is questionable.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Area under the receiver-operating characteristic curve (AUROC). AUROC is threshold-agnostic, robust to class imbalance, and directly supports the operational goal of ranking accounts by risk. The business team can set decision thresholds downstream based on review capacity and risk tolerance; AUROC measures ranking quality independent of that choice.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Average Precision (AP): focuses on rare positive class (delinquent accounts).
- Expected calibration error (ECE): ensures predicted probabilities are well-calibrated for downstream decision-making.
- Stratified AUROC by origination cohort: confirms performance is stable across economic periods.
- Stratified AUROC by account age: isolates whether the sequence model gains beyond obvious recency effects.

## 6. Model Selection and Validation
Both models are tuned using training data only. Hyperparameters are selected via cross-validation within the training cohort, using AUROC as the selection criterion. The validation cohort is used to verify generalization and monitor calibration. The test cohort is opened exactly once, at the end of development. Final results (AUROC, AP, calibration, stratified analyses) are computed on the test set without any further tuning or threshold adjustment.

## 7. Controls for Confounds
The experiment guards against three key confounds:

1. **Temporal and macroeconomic confounds:** Stratified analysis by origination cohort ensures that improvements are not driven by a single economic period. If performance is stable across cohorts, the model generalizes; if not, the gain is confounded.

2. **Account age and recency effects:** The baseline includes temporal features (days since first transaction, repayment velocity, transaction frequency) to capture age-related signal. If the sequence model's improvement over the baseline persists even after stratifying by account age at prediction, the gain is not a simple artifact of age.

3. **Label and information leakage:** Temporal splits and fit-on-train-only preprocessing ensure that no future repayment events or account updates inform training. Labels are only observed after 12-month maturity, preventing premature labeling.

## 8. Scope of Claims
This experiment will establish whether, on historical BNPL data with realistic temporal structure, a sequence model improves predictive ranking compared to a strong linear baseline given equivalent feature engineering and tuning effort. It will not establish that sequences are optimal overall, only that they outperform this specific baseline under these conditions. If stratified analyses show instability across cohorts, the claim will be appropriately scoped as "conditional on economic regime."
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because one flaw (temporal leakage) is subtle and masked by apparent stratification rigor, while the other (metric mismatch) involves a plausible but misaligned choice that requires understanding the distinction between calibration and discrimination in the context of the hypothesis. Together, they require domain insight to detect and link to the core claim.

hypothesis: For first-pass legal contract review, a transformer fine-tuned to classify contract clauses using the surrounding section context and document structure will outperform a bag-of-words baseline on clause type labeling, especially for clauses whose wording is ambiguous without neighboring sections.
domain: Legal document analytics for commercial contract clause review in enterprise contract management systems
task_prompt:
To test whether transformer-based contextual models outperform bag-of-words classifiers for legal clause type labeling, we propose the following experiment design.

**1. Data Acquisition and Curation**

We will use 50,000 executed commercial contracts drawn from a production contract management system, spanning 2019–2024 across multiple jurisdictions and counterparty types. Clause-level labels (one of 11 types: Indemnity, Termination, Liability Cap, Confidentiality, Governing Law, IP Assignment, Payment Terms, Warranty, Dispute Resolution, Representations, Other) are sourced from legal ops team annotations or review vendor markup. We will compute inter-annotator agreement on a 500-clause validation sample; clauses with Cohen's kappa <0.70 will be flagged as low-confidence and excluded from the test set to eliminate ambiguous ground truth. Such clauses will remain in training data to reflect real-world label noise. We will also apply a secondary 'Requires_Context' binary flag to mark clauses that are uninterpretable without neighboring sections; this flag will be used to stratify evaluation and isolate performance on the hypothesis-critical subset.

**2. Data Splitting Strategy**

To prevent template-specific overfitting and temporal leakage, we employ a three-way stratified temporal split:
- **Training (60%, years 2019–2022):** Stratified by template family (top 20 templates). All preprocessing tokenizers and vocabulary are fit on this set only.
- **Validation (20%, years 2022–2023):** Stratified by template family. Used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection only; never touched after final model selection.
- **Test (20%, years 2023–2024):** Stratified by template family, jurisdiction, and counterparty category. Held-out and evaluated exactly once after model selection is complete.

This design mirrors the deployment scenario (applying the model prospectively to future contracts) and ensures that template-specific patterns are balanced across splits, preventing the model from learning boilerplate formatting rather than contextual semantics.

**3. Feature Engineering**

Both the transformer and baseline receive the same feature set:
- **Clause text:** The target clause to be classified.
- **Section context:** Section heading and ±1 immediately neighboring clauses (up to ±500 tokens).
- **Document structure:** Relative position (percentile, section depth, nesting level), contract type, jurisdiction, counterparty category.
- **Contextual flag:** Binary indicator of 'Requires_Context' status.

For the transformer model, text features are tokenized via subword encoding (BPE vocabulary fit on training text only). For the baseline, the same text is concatenated and vectorized using TfidfVectorizer (fit on training text, max features 5,000, min document frequency 2). No hand-crafted linguistic features are engineered; this ensures a clean comparison between learned context (transformer attention) and bag-of-words frequency statistics.

**4. Model and Baseline**

- **Proposed model:** Pretrained legal BERT (or RoBERTa), fine-tuned end-to-end on training data. Input is [CLS] + clause tokens + section context + document structure embeddings. Output: logits over 11 clause types via weighted cross-entropy (inverse class frequency weights to handle imbalance).
- **Baseline:** Logistic regression on TF-IDF bag-of-words features from concatenated clause + section context text, with L2 regularization and class weight balancing.

Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning (50–100 configurations each) on the validation set using macro-averaged F1 as the tuning metric. This ensures the baseline is fairly tuned and any performance gap is genuine.

**5. Evaluation Metrics**

- **Primary metric:** Macro-averaged F1 score across all 11 clause types. This metric weights all classes equally, ensuring rare high-stakes categories (Indemnity, Liability Cap) are not drowned out by frequent ones. F1 balances precision (reducing false positives that overwhelm reviewers) and recall (catching important clauses).
- **Secondary metrics:** (a) Per-class F1 for high-risk categories (Indemnity, Liability Cap, Confidentiality); (b) Precision at 95% recall for rare classes; (c) Macro F1 on 'Requires_Context' subset (the hypothesis-critical group); (d) F1 stratified by template family (to detect template leakage).

**6. Controls and Scope**

We will explicitly control for template leakage by comparing per-template F1 and ensuring no single template drives the performance gap. We will monitor for temporal distribution shift by comparing validation vs. test performance; significant degradation would indicate the model does not generalize to future contracts. Jurisdiction and counterparty variation will be tracked via stratified metrics. Low-confidence labels (kappa <0.70) are excluded from test evaluation to avoid penalizing the model for predicting correctly on ambiguous ground truth.

This experiment will establish whether transformer-based contextual models outperform bag-of-words on clause type labeling *within the test set's contract distribution*. It will not establish generalization beyond the data's jurisdiction or counterparty mix, nor will it isolate the specific contribution of attention mechanisms vs. pretrained representations. Claims will be scoped to the tested domain and time period.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split excludes data from 2022 in both training and validation, creating a gap that may bias the model against mid-period contracts.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The training set includes contracts from 2019–2022 and validation from 2022–2023, meaning 2022 data is represented in both (with overlap at the boundary), ensuring continuity. The split is intentionally temporal to simulate prospective deployment, and the stratification by template family ensures coverage across time.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding low-confidence labels (kappa <0.70) only from the test set but keeping them in training introduces label noise and invalidates the comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This is a sound trade-off: retaining noisy labels in training reflects real-world conditions where models must learn from imperfect annotations, while excluding them from test evaluation ensures the model is not unfairly penalized for predicting on ambiguous ground truth. The design correctly isolates evaluation integrity from training robustness.

must_not_claim.claim: Using macro-averaged F1 instead of accuracy or micro-F1 overemphasizes rare classes and may exaggerate performance differences on unimportant categories.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Macro-F1 is appropriate here because the hypothesis centers on high-stakes, rare clause types (e.g., Liability Cap) whose misclassification carries disproportionate risk. Accuracy would be misleading due to class imbalance, and micro-F1 would let common classes dominate. Macro-F1 aligns with operational risk priorities.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly validates that the experimental design is methodologically sound: the data split prevents leakage, the baseline is strong and fairly tuned, the metrics align with the hypothesis, and controls are in place for key confounds. The final verdict is that the design supports a valid test of the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic raises concerns that are already addressed by the design (e.g., about label noise, temporal continuity, or baseline strength), and the defender correctly cites the relevant safeguards present in the protocol.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The debate concludes that no critical flaws exist, and any raised concerns are either mitigated by existing controls or reflect misunderstandings of evaluation best practices in imbalanced, high-stakes domains. The design is confirmed as sufficient to test the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender successfully explains why a seemingly problematic choice (e.g., retaining noisy labels in training) is actually methodologically justified given the deployment context and evaluation goals.
