## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Tabular Baseline for 30-Day Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Cohort
This experiment uses 36 months of longitudinal EHR data from a health system (or multi-site cohort with explicit stratification). The cohort includes all adult inpatient discharges (age ≥18, discharged alive, LOS ≥1 day) with sufficient prior history for feature construction. All timestamped clinical events—diagnoses (ICD codes), procedures, medications, lab results, vitals, and structured notes—are extracted. The outcome is binary: 30-day unplanned readmission within 30 calendar days of discharge, with planned readmissions and procedures (dialysis, chemotherapy) excluded via admission reason codes.

### 2. Temporal Split to Prevent Leakage
The data is split chronologically by discharge date: months 1–24 (training), months 25–30 (validation), months 31–36 (test). This chronological split is essential because readmission is fundamentally a time-series prediction task; a random split would allow future information to leak into training. The 4:1:1 ratio provides ample training data while preserving realistic evaluation horizons. If data spans multiple hospital sites, the split is stratified by site to ensure proportional representation across train/val/test, reducing site-level confounding.

### 3. Feature Construction: Parity and Fairness
**Transformer model:** The input is a time-ordered sequence of all clinical events from admission to discharge, each encoded as (event_type, code, value, relative_timestamp). Static admission features (age, sex, admission source) are concatenated at the sequence level. All event vocabularies and scaling parameters are fit exclusively on the training set.

**Tabular baseline:** To ensure fair comparison, the baseline receives engineered summary statistics from the same EHR data within the same lookback windows. Features include: count of unique diagnoses in the prior 1 year, presence of high-risk diagnoses, count of prior readmissions (1 year), summary statistics of lab values (mean, min, max from past 30 days), medication count at discharge, Charlson comorbidity index, length of stay, and admission demographics. Imputation and scaling are fit on training data only. Crucially, the baseline is not given raw event sequences; it uses aggregated features, which is the natural comparison to test whether sequence modeling adds value beyond aggregated features.

### 4. Model Architecture and Training
The transformer consists of a learned embedding layer, positional encodings, 2–4 transformer encoder blocks with 8 multi-head attention heads, global average pooling, concatenation with static features, and a 2-layer MLP with dropout (0.2) feeding to a sigmoid output. Hyperparameters (layer count, dropout, learning rate, attention heads) are tuned on the validation set. The baseline is logistic regression with a fixed L2 regularization setting specified in advance to preserve interpretability and keep it as a stable linear reference on the aggregate feature space. Both models are trained on the training set; early stopping monitors validation performance for the transformer.

### 5. Evaluation: Metrics and Strategy
**Primary metric:** Brier score, which evaluates the quality of predicted readmission probabilities rather than only their ordering. This is appropriate for discharge-time deployment because care teams often need risk estimates that are numerically meaningful for triage thresholds, outreach capacity planning, and communication of expected readmission risk.

**Secondary metrics:** Expected calibration error (ECE), AUROC, precision-recall AUC, and sensitivity/specificity at clinically relevant thresholds. Performance is stratified by age, comorbidity burden, admission source, and prior hospitalization history to detect subgroup performance differences and validate generalization.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Integrity
Hyperparameter tuning uses grid/random search on the validation set (months 25–30), optimizing for validation Brier score for the transformer. The configuration with the best validation Brier score is selected. The test set (months 31–36) is held out entirely—no development, tuning, or feature adjustment occurs on test data. A single final evaluation run is performed on the test set and reported.

### 7. Confound Control and Scope
To isolate the effect of sequence modeling: (1) Feature parity—the baseline has access to equivalent EHR information, aggregated rather than sequenced. (2) Leakage prevention—only information available at discharge is used. (3) Site stratification (if applicable) detects distribution shift. (4) Subgroup analysis on patients with irregular event timing validates the hypothesis that transformers specifically help for sparse or erratic utilization patterns. (5) Imputation, scaling, and vocabulary fitting occur on training data only.

This design establishes whether a transformer trained on longitudinal event sequences outperforms tabular aggregates in predicting readmission risk—testing the core hypothesis while controlling for data access, temporal leakage, and feature engineering advantage.

planted_issues.description: The primary metric is Brier score, which prioritizes overall probability accuracy (calibration and sharpness) over ranking performance. However, the hypothesis is focused on whether sequence modeling improves identification and prioritization of high-risk patients—essentially a risk stratification task requiring strong discrimination across patients. In such settings, AUROC or PR-AUC better align with clinical utility than Brier score, as care teams need to order patients by risk to allocate limited outreach resources effectively. Optimizing for Brier score may select a well-calibrated but less discriminative model, obscuring whether the transformer truly improves prioritization.

The logistic regression baseline uses a fixed, pre-specified L2 penalty without validation-based tuning, while the transformer undergoes extensive hyperparameter search. This creates an unfair comparison: any performance gap may reflect greater optimization effort on the transformer rather than intrinsic architectural advantages. For a valid test of the hypothesis—that sequence modeling adds value beyond aggregated features—both models must be tuned with comparable rigor. Otherwise, the experiment conflates architectural differences with tuning disparity.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set window (months 31–36) is too short compared to training (24 months), risking unreliable evaluation due to smaller sample size.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The 6-month test set is proportionally consistent with real-world deployment cycles and provides sufficient statistical power given typical hospital readmission rates and cohort sizes. Duration-based splits are standard in longitudinal EHR studies to prevent temporal leakage.

must_not_claim.claim: Using logistic regression as a baseline is inappropriate because it is too simple to capture complex clinical patterns.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Logistic regression is a well-established, interpretable baseline in clinical ML. The validity of the comparison hinges on fair tuning and feature parity—not model complexity. It serves as a controlled reference point for evaluating whether sequential modeling adds value beyond standard aggregated features.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that Brier score as the primary optimization metric misaligns with the objective of patient risk stratification, and establishes that AUROC or PR-AUC would better reflect the clinical goal of prioritizing high-risk individuals. The defender cannot legitimately rebut this, as the operational use case emphasizes ranking.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic argues that when the goal is targeted intervention under constrained care-management capacity, ranking quality (discrimination) is more important than probability calibration, and thus AUROC should be primary.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic exposes that the tabular baseline is not tuned on the validation set, creating an asymmetry in optimization effort that biases the comparison in favor of the transformer. A fair evaluation requires equivalent tuning rigor for both models to isolate the effect of architecture.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that hyperparameter tuning significantly impacts logistic regression performance and that fixing the regularization strength arbitrarily handicaps the baseline.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Tabular Baseline for 30-Day Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Cohort
This experiment uses 36 months of longitudinal EHR data from a health system (or multi-site cohort with explicit stratification). The cohort includes all adult inpatient discharges (age ≥18, discharged alive, LOS ≥1 day) with sufficient prior history for feature construction. All timestamped clinical events—diagnoses (ICD codes), procedures, medications, lab results, vitals, and structured notes—are extracted. The outcome is binary: 30-day unplanned readmission within 30 calendar days of discharge, with planned readmissions and procedures (dialysis, chemotherapy) excluded via admission reason codes.

### 2. Temporal Split to Prevent Leakage
The data is split chronologically by discharge date: months 1–24 (training), months 25–30 (validation), months 31–36 (test). This chronological split is essential because readmission is fundamentally a time-series prediction task; a random split would allow future information to leak into training. The 4:1:1 ratio provides ample training data while preserving realistic evaluation horizons. If data spans multiple hospital sites, the split is stratified by site to ensure proportional representation across train/val/test, reducing site-level confounding.

### 3. Feature Construction: Parity and Fairness
**Transformer model:** The input is a time-ordered sequence of all clinical events from admission to discharge, each encoded as (event_type, code, value, relative_timestamp). Static admission features (age, sex, admission source) are concatenated at the sequence level. All event vocabularies and scaling parameters are fit exclusively on the training set.

**Tabular baseline:** To ensure fair comparison, the baseline receives engineered summary statistics from the same EHR data within the same lookback windows. Features include: count of unique diagnoses in the prior 1 year, presence of high-risk diagnoses, count of prior readmissions (1 year), summary statistics of lab values (mean, min, max from past 30 days), medication count at discharge, Charlson comorbidity index, length of stay, and admission demographics. Imputation and scaling are fit on training data only. Crucially, the baseline is not given raw event sequences; it uses aggregated features, which is the natural comparison to test whether sequence modeling adds value beyond aggregated features.

### 4. Model Architecture and Training
The transformer consists of a learned embedding layer, positional encodings, 2–4 transformer encoder blocks with 8 multi-head attention heads, global average pooling, concatenation with static features, and a 2-layer MLP with dropout (0.2) feeding to a sigmoid output. Hyperparameters (layer count, dropout, learning rate, attention heads) are tuned on the validation set. The baseline is logistic regression with L2 regularization, tuned with equivalent rigor on validation AUROC. Both models are trained on the training set; early stopping monitors validation performance.

### 5. Evaluation: Metrics and Strategy
**Primary metric:** Area under the ROC curve (AUROC), which measures the model's ability to rank-order patients by readmission risk across all decision thresholds—appropriate for clinical practice where the threshold is unknown and care-management capacity is limited.

**Secondary metrics:** Expected calibration error (ECE) and Brier score (calibration is critical for risk communication), precision-recall AUC (informative under class imbalance), and sensitivity/specificity at clinically relevant thresholds. Performance is stratified by age, comorbidity burden, admission source, and prior hospitalization history to detect subgroup performance differences and validate generalization.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Integrity
Hyperparameter tuning uses grid/random search on the validation set (months 25–30), optimizing for validation AUROC. The configuration with the highest validation AUROC is selected. The test set (months 31–36) is held out entirely—no development, tuning, or feature adjustment occurs on test data. A single final evaluation run is performed on the test set and reported.

### 7. Confound Control and Scope
To isolate the effect of sequence modeling: (1) Feature parity—the baseline has access to equivalent EHR information, aggregated rather than sequenced. (2) Leakage prevention—only information available at discharge is used. (3) Site stratification (if applicable) detects distribution shift. (4) Subgroup analysis on patients with irregular event timing validates the hypothesis that transformers specifically help for sparse or erratic utilization patterns. (5) Imputation, scaling, and vocabulary fitting occur on training data only.

This design establishes whether a transformer trained on longitudinal event sequences outperforms tabular aggregates in predicting readmission risk—testing the core hypothesis while controlling for data access, temporal leakage, and feature engineering advantage.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because the flaws are methodologically subtle and require understanding of clinical deployment priorities (ranking vs. calibration) and fairness in model comparison (equal tuning rigor). A reviewer may overlook the metric misalignment if they overvalue calibration, or accept the fixed baseline as 'stable' without recognizing the unfair advantage conferred to the transformer.

hypothesis: A sequence model trained on raw minute-level smart meter electricity consumption plus weather covariates predicts next-day household peak-demand exceedance better than a baseline using only daily aggregate usage features, as measured on held-out months from the same utility service area.
domain: Residential electricity demand forecasting for a utility serving mixed-usage neighborhoods with smart meter telemetry
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models for Household Peak-Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data Strategy

The analysis uses 36 months of smart meter electricity consumption readings from 5,000–10,000 households served by a single utility. Meter readings are recorded at 60-minute intervals (or 15-minute if available). Covariate data include daily area-level weather (temperature, solar irradiance, precipitation), household tariff tier, and calendar features (day-of-week, holiday flags). The target label is binary: whether a household's total daily consumption exceeds its peak-demand threshold, computed deterministically from the same metering system.

The peak threshold is defined per household using historical percentiles (e.g., 95th percentile of each household's observed daily consumption) or contracted limits. This ensures the label is stable, non-null, and grounded in operational reality.

Known data quality issues (e.g., missing readings, sensor drift) are handled as follows: missing meter readings within a household–day are imputed using the household's mean value for that hour-of-day; weather data gaps are filled by area-level interpolation. Imputation parameters are fit on the training set and applied uniformly to validation and test.

### 2. Split Strategy

A chronological split with household stratification is used:
- **Training:** Months 1–24 (24 months)
- **Validation:** Months 25–30 (6 months)
- **Test:** Months 31–36 (6 months)

This temporal split is essential: it prevents information leakage from future weather patterns, usage trends, and seasonal dynamics into the training window. A sequence model can otherwise exploit subtle date or seasonal correlations that are not causal and would fail in deployment.

Within each set, households are stratified by their baseline peak-exceedance rate (binned into low, medium, high tiers) to ensure each split contains similar proportions of rare-event households. This reduces variance in metric estimation and ensures calibration can be evaluated across cohorts.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, encoding) are fit on the training set only and applied consistently to validation and test, preventing data leakage.

**Sequence Model Features:**
- 24-hour consumption history: 1,440 minute-level values (or 96 × 15-minute intervals), taken from the household’s most recent 24-hour meter history aligned to the prediction day and normalized within each household by dividing by the household's mean daily consumption.
- Area-level weather covariates (temperature, solar irradiance, precipitation) for the preceding day and current day, interpolated to meter frequency.
- Static features: tariff tier, annual consumption quintile.
- Calendar: day-of-week and month-of-year one-hot encodings.

**Baseline Features:**
- Aggregate daily consumption over the household’s most recent 7-day history window.
- Same weather and calendar features as the sequence model.

Both feature sets include identical weather and calendar signals, ensuring the comparison isolates the benefit of fine-grained temporal modeling, not superior seasonality capture. This feature construction also matches the utility’s operational scoring feed, where the latest finalized meter history is used when generating next-day risk estimates.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Model:** An LSTM or temporal CNN with attention, consuming the 24-hour sequence plus weather covariates. Hidden dimensions (64–128 units) are tuned via validation performance. Dropout regularization (0.2–0.3) prevents overfitting. Training uses Adam optimizer with early stopping on validation AUPRC (patience = 10 epochs). Batch size 32, max epochs 100.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on daily aggregate features. Crucially, the baseline receives identical preprocessing, feature scaling, and tuning effort: L2 regularization strength is chosen via grid search on the validation set, and class weights are calibrated on training data to reflect the observed peak-exceedance rate. This ensures a fair comparison—the baseline is not weakened by weaker hyperparameter tuning.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC)**

Peak-exceedance events are rare (imbalanced), making AUC-ROC and accuracy poor proxies for utility-relevant performance. PR-AUC directly encodes the utility's operational concern: identifying peak-risk households (high recall) while avoiding false alarms (high precision). It enables threshold selection for demand-response campaigns.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- **AUC-ROC:** Confirms ranking ability across the full probability space.
- **F1 Score (at validation-optimized threshold):** Interpretable precision–recall trade-off.
- **Expected Calibration Error (ECE):** Ensures predicted probabilities match observed peak rates; critical for utilities.
- **Recall at Fixed Precision:** Supports operational deployment decisions (e.g., recall at 80% precision).

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

The validation set (months 25–30) is used for all hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. No information from the test set is used during model selection. After all tuning is complete, the test set (months 31–36) is evaluated exactly once, and results are reported with 95% confidence intervals computed via stratified bootstrap over households.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Seasonality and weather are controlled by including them in both the sequence model and baseline. Any improvement in the sequence model cannot be attributed to better seasonality capture, only to superior modeling of intra-day consumption dynamics.

A sensitivity analysis trains a second baseline using only lagged consumption (no weather) to isolate the benefit of the sequence model's ability to capture within-day patterns independent of macro weather trends.

Temporal autocorrelation is preserved by the chronological split; household heterogeneity is managed by stratification. The design establishes whether sequence models, given equivalent weather and calendar information, outperform aggregate baselines—not whether either model captures seasonality.

planted_issues.description: The sequence model’s consumption features are described as taken from the 'household’s most recent 24-hour meter history aligned to the prediction day,' which could include meter readings from day t itself. However, the label is defined as whether consumption on day t exceeds a peak threshold. This creates target leakage: the model receives information from the same period used to compute the label, violating strict temporal boundaries and enabling spurious performance that would not generalize to real-world prediction conditions where meter data from day t is not available at forecast time.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model is unfairly weakened by not using weather data.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The sound design explicitly includes identical weather and calendar features in both the sequence model and the baseline. Omitting weather from the baseline would be incorrect, so this claim misrepresents the design. The corruption does not affect this—weather is correctly used in both models—so this is a distractor for shallow reviewers.

must_not_claim.claim: The chronological split is inappropriate because it reduces training data size.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: A chronological split is necessary to avoid temporal leakage from future patterns and ensure generalization to future deployment. Reducing training data is a trade-off accepted for methodological rigor, and the dataset size (36 months) is sufficient. Thus, this concern is invalid.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the 24-hour consumption feature is described in a way that permits inclusion of measurements from the same day as the target label, which introduces target leakage. The defender cannot legitimately rebut this because the feature construction violates causal time ordering: at forecast time (for day t+1), data from day t may not yet be finalized and should not be used. Therefore, the flaw undermines the validity of the comparison.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that the 'most recent 24-hour meter history aligned to the prediction day' could include consumption data from day t, which is used to compute the label for day t, thereby contaminating training signals with information that would be unavailable operationally.

difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because the language mimics real-world deployment ('latest finalized meter history') and seems plausible. However, a correct evaluation requires recognizing that using data from the prediction day (day t) to predict an event *on* day t violates temporal causality. This requires domain-aware scrutiny beyond pattern matching.

hypothesis: Training a transformer-based model on full customer support conversation histories and issue-resolution outcomes will predict 30-day churn more accurately than a tabular model using only static account attributes and recent usage aggregates for a subscription-based SaaS product.
domain: B2B subscription SaaS customer success and retention analytics
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Churn Prediction vs. Tabular Baseline

### 1. Data and Label Definition

We will use 18–24 months of production data spanning support tickets, chat transcripts, account metadata, product usage logs, and renewal/cancellation records. For each account, we define an observation point as the timestamp of the final support message in a conversation sequence. The label is binary: churn equals 1 if the account cancelled or did not renew within 30 calendar days following the observation point; 0 if the account remained active through day 30 or beyond. We exclude accounts with fewer than 30 days of post-observation follow-up to avoid censoring bias. This operationalization ensures the label is cleanly defined and fully observed at evaluation time.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy

We will partition observation points using stratified sampling across the full 18–24 month window, assigning approximately 70% to training, 15% to validation, and 15% to the held-out test set. Stratification will be performed jointly on churn label, account tenure cohort (new, established, mature), and contract value quartile so that each split preserves the same mix of customer segments and event rates. This is important because churn prevalence and support intensity vary meaningfully across seasons and renewal cycles; preserving those distributions in all splits yields a more stable estimate of comparative model performance and reduces the chance that apparent gains are driven by an atypical validation or test period.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer model inputs:** Raw support conversation text (all messages in the thread up to and including the observation point), tokenized and truncated to 2048 tokens. The tokenizer vocabulary will be established once during dataset construction and then frozen so that all candidate models and all evaluation splits share the same text representation.

**Tabular baseline features:** (a) Static attributes: company size, contract value, plan tier, account tenure at observation point; (b) Recent aggregates (30-day window before observation): login frequency, feature adoption rate, API call volume; (c) Support signals: ticket count and total message count in the past 90 days (excluding the current conversation). All features are computed strictly before the observation point; no post-observation events are included. Tabular scaling statistics and categorical encoding/imputation maps will likewise be fixed once at dataset construction time and applied identically during training, validation, and test so that every model is trained and assessed on an identical feature space.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The transformer model is a fine-tuned pre-trained language model (DistilBERT or RoBERTa) with a 2-layer MLP classifier on top, trained with AdamW (learning rate 2e-5, batch size 32) for up to 20 epochs with early stopping on validation PR-AUC.

The baseline is logistic regression on the same tabular feature set (static attributes, recent usage, support volume). We will keep the baseline specification intentionally simple and avoid extensive hyperparameter search so that it serves as a stable reference for the incremental value of text modeling rather than as a separately optimized system. The operating threshold for the baseline will be chosen using the same validation procedure as the transformer, and both models will be evaluated on the same metrics. Critically, the baseline is *not* given access to raw conversation text. Any improvement of the transformer must therefore come from semantic understanding of text, not simply from access to more signal.

### 5. Primary and Secondary Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area under the precision-recall curve (PR-AUC). Churn is heavily imbalanced (5–15% base rate); the stakeholder's goal is to identify at-risk customers with high precision so that customer success teams can prioritize interventions. PR-AUC directly aligns with this objective: improvements in PR-AUC translate to higher recall at fixed precision, enabling the team to save more customers without wasting intervention capacity.

**Secondary metrics:** Precision at 80% recall (a fixed operating point for capacity planning), F1 score at the validation-optimized threshold, and stratified PR-AUC and precision@80% recall broken down by account tenure, company size, and contract value quartile. We also compute expected calibration error (ECE) to ensure predicted probabilities are well-calibrated for decision-making.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping are performed on the validation set. For the transformer, we monitor validation PR-AUC and apply early stopping if no improvement occurs for 3 epochs. The test set remains separate from routine tuning, but before finalizing the training recipe we will run a single lockbox check on the held-out test partition to verify that the selected validation winner maintains its advantage under an independent sample. If the leading configuration exhibits a clear reversal relative to validation, we will treat that as evidence of validation instability and select the more robust configuration for the final model fit. We will then retrain the chosen model on the combined training and validation data and report final test performance with 95% confidence intervals computed via bootstrap.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To address the key confound—that transformer improvements might reflect conversation length or support volume rather than semantic content—we implement multiple controls: (a) The baseline explicitly includes conversation length and support volume as features, so transformer gains must exceed surface statistics. (b) We train a length-matched transformer baseline that uses only conversation length (binned) and support volume to isolate semantic uplift. (c) We stratify evaluation by conversation length and support volume quintiles to verify consistent improvements across conversation complexities. (d) We train a shuffled-text control (random word order) to confirm the model uses word order and semantic structure. (e) Stratified evaluation by account cohort and contract value controls for confounds correlated with account characteristics. (f) Explicit feature engineering checks confirm no post-observation leakage.

The experiment establishes whether the transformer extracts predictive semantic signals beyond tabular features; it does not establish causality or explain which conversational patterns drive predictions.

planted_issues.description: The design uses random temporal sampling instead of a chronological train-validation-test split. Since the data involve time-ordered support interactions and churn labels defined relative to observation points, mixing future and past events across splits allows time-based leakage. The model may appear to generalize better than it would in production deployment, where it must predict forward in time.

Preprocessing artifacts (tokenizer vocabulary, scaling statistics, encoding maps) are fit on the full dataset before splitting. This leaks distributional information from validation and test into training, especially problematic when language usage and feature distributions evolve over time. It invalidates the assumption that validation and test represent unseen data.

The tabular baseline is intentionally left untuned while the transformer undergoes full hyperparameter optimization. This creates an unfair comparison: apparent gains may stem from differential optimization effort rather than the value of text semantics. A valid test of the hypothesis requires equally rigorous tuning for both models.

The test set is used for a 'lockbox check' to select the final configuration, violating the principle that test data must remain untouched during development. Even a single use of test data for model selection biases the final performance estimate and undermines the validity of reported metrics.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying the split on churn label is inappropriate and introduces data leakage
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by outcome is common in imbalanced classification to preserve label distributions; in a properly temporally split setup, it does not constitute leakage as long as the temporal order is preserved. The sound design correctly employs temporal splitting with stratification.

must_not_claim.claim: Freezing preprocessing artifacts (e.g., vocabulary, scaling stats) at dataset construction time is bad practice
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Freezing preprocessing is correct *if* those artifacts are derived solely from training data. The sound design does this correctly by fitting only on training. The issue arises only when these are computed on the full dataset, which is the corruption.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline not having access to raw text is a flaw in the experiment
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline is correctly deprived of raw conversation text to isolate the value of semantic understanding. The hypothesis specifically tests whether the transformer adds value *beyond* tabular features; giving the baseline text access would muddle the comparison.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies and the defender cannot rebut: temporal split is replaced by random sampling across time, invalidating forward-looking generalization.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer demonstrates that support interactions and churn risk are time-dependent, and mixing time points breaks i.i.d. assumptions and inflates performance estimates.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies and the defender cannot rebut: preprocessing uses population-level statistics from entire dataset, leaking future distributional information.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer shows that vocabulary and scaler derivation from test observations biases model evaluation, especially when considering evolving language patterns or feature drift.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies and the defender cannot rebut: baseline model is deliberately under-optimized compared to the transformer, creating an unfair advantage.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer establishes that both models should receive equivalent tuning effort to fairly test whether text semantics provide marginal value.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies and the defender cannot rebut: test set is used to select model configuration, contaminating performance estimates.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer argues that any use of test data to influence model development—including one-time checks—bias selection and invalidates confidence intervals.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Churn Prediction vs. Tabular Baseline

### 1. Data and Label Definition

We will use 18–24 months of production data spanning support tickets, chat transcripts, account metadata, product usage logs, and renewal/cancellation records. For each account, we define an observation point as the timestamp of the final support message in a conversation sequence. The label is binary: churn equals 1 if the account cancelled or did not renew within 30 calendar days following the observation point; 0 if the account remained active through day 30 and beyond. We exclude accounts with fewer than 30 days of post-observation follow-up to avoid censoring bias. This operationalization ensures the label is cleanly defined and fully observed at evaluation time.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy

We employ a temporal split with stratification to prevent information leakage and ensure representative evaluation. All observation points from months 1–12 are assigned to training; months 13–15 to validation; months 16–18 to the held-out test set. This chronological ordering ensures that no future information (post-observation support activity, usage patterns, or churn signals) enters the training window. Within each temporal window, we further stratify by account tenure cohort (new, established, mature) and contract value quartile. Stratification ensures balanced representation of customer segments across splits and enables segment-specific analysis of model performance without single large customers dominating test set error.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer model inputs:** Raw support conversation text (all messages in the thread up to and including the observation point), tokenized and truncated to 2048 tokens. Vocabulary and tokenization are fit on training set transcripts only and applied without refitting to validation and test sets.

**Tabular baseline features:** (a) Static attributes: company size, contract value, plan tier, account tenure at observation point; (b) Recent aggregates (30-day window before observation): login frequency, feature adoption rate, API call volume; (c) Support signals: ticket count and total message count in the past 90 days (excluding the current conversation). All features are computed strictly before the observation point; no post-observation events are included. Scaling and categorical encoding are fit on the training set and applied identically to validation and test.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The transformer model is a fine-tuned pre-trained language model (DistilBERT or RoBERTa) with a 2-layer MLP classifier on top, trained with AdamW (learning rate 2e-5, batch size 32) for up to 20 epochs with early stopping on validation PR-AUC.

The baseline is logistic regression on the same tabular feature set (static attributes, recent usage, support volume). This is a fair and disciplined comparison: both models use equivalent hyperparameter tuning (grid search on validation set for L2 strength, threshold optimization for precision-recall trade-off), identical class weighting for imbalance, and are evaluated on the same metrics. Critically, the baseline is *not* given access to raw conversation text. Any improvement of the transformer must therefore come from semantic understanding of text, not simply from access to more signal.

### 5. Primary and Secondary Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area under the precision-recall curve (PR-AUC). Churn is heavily imbalanced (5–15% base rate); the stakeholder's goal is to identify at-risk customers with high precision so that customer success teams can prioritize interventions. PR-AUC directly aligns with this objective: improvements in PR-AUC translate to higher recall at fixed precision, enabling the team to save more customers without wasting intervention capacity.

**Secondary metrics:** Precision at 80% recall (a fixed operating point for capacity planning), F1 score at the validation-optimized threshold, and stratified PR-AUC and precision@80% recall broken down by account tenure, company size, and contract value quartile. We also compute expected calibration error (ECE) to ensure predicted probabilities are well-calibrated for decision-making.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping are performed exclusively on the validation set (months 13–15). For the transformer, we monitor validation PR-AUC and apply early stopping if no improvement occurs for 3 epochs. For logistic regression, we conduct grid search over L2 regularization (10^−4 to 10^2) on the validation set. The test set (months 16–18) is locked and evaluated exactly once after all tuning is complete, with 95% confidence intervals computed via bootstrap.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To address the key confound—that transformer improvements might reflect conversation length or support volume rather than semantic content—we implement multiple controls: (a) The baseline explicitly includes conversation length and support volume as features, so transformer gains must exceed surface statistics. (b) We train a length-matched transformer baseline that uses only conversation length (binned) and support volume to isolate semantic uplift. (c) We stratify test evaluation by conversation length and support volume quintiles to verify consistent improvements across conversation complexities. (d) We train a shuffled-text control (random word order) to confirm the model uses word order and semantic structure. (e) Stratified evaluation by account cohort and contract value controls for confounds correlated with account characteristics. (f) Explicit feature engineering checks confirm no post-observation leakage.

The experiment establishes whether the transformer extracts predictive semantic signals beyond tabular features; it does not establish causality or explain which conversational patterns drive predictions.
difficulty_justification: This case requires understanding multiple subtle but high-impact corruptions: temporal leakage (moderate), preprocessing leakage (subtle), and evaluation contamination (moderate), compounded by an obviously unfair baseline. The interaction between temporal and preprocessing flaws increases difficulty, as detecting one may not suffice without seeing the full picture.

hypothesis: For first-pass fraud detection on card transactions, a sequence model trained on each user’s last 30 transactions will detect fraudulent purchases with higher PR-AUC than a gradient-boosted model using only aggregated customer-level features, when both are evaluated on a future-month holdout.
domain: Retail card payments fraud detection at a consumer bank or payment processor
task_prompt:
To test whether a sequence model trained on a user's last 30 transactions detects fraud with higher PR-AUC than a boosted-tree baseline using aggregated features, we design a temporally rigorous experiment that controls for label delay, class imbalance, and temporal drift.

**1. Data Strategy**

We source 24 months of timestamped card transaction logs from a production payment system, encompassing ~10–50 million transactions. Each transaction record includes: user ID, merchant code, transaction amount, device/channel, timestamp, and binary fraud label (assigned post-hoc via chargeback or investigation, typically within 15–90 days). We exclude transactions with labels arriving >90 days post-authorization to ensure label quality and avoid stale information. Known data quality issues—label delay, severe imbalance (~0.1–0.5% fraud), and temporal drift in fraud tactics—are handled by: (a) enforcing strict temporal splits that respect label delay; (b) using PR-AUC, which handles imbalance; and (c) evaluating per-month metrics to detect drift.

**2. Temporal Split Strategy**

We apply a strict chronological split: months 1–18 for training (72%), months 19–21 for validation (12.5%), and months 22–24 for test (12.5%). Temporal splits prevent information leakage from future labels into the training window, which is critical because fraud labels arrive with delay. All preprocessing transformers and statistics are fit exclusively on the training set and applied (without re-fitting) to validation and test, ensuring that no validation or test statistics influence the training procedure.

**3. Feature Engineering**

Sequence Model receives: per-transaction features (amount, merchant category, hour-of-day, days-since-last-transaction, and a rolling 24-hour dispute-risk score from the processor's case-management system) organized into sequences of the last 30 transactions in chronological order. The dispute-risk score is included because it captures short-horizon account friction signals already produced in the operational risk stack and may help the model represent abrupt behavioral instability preceding fraud review. Baseline Model receives: user-level aggregates (transaction count, amount statistics, merchant diversity, time-of-day entropy, account age, and the same rolling 24-hour dispute-risk score), computed from history up to each transaction. Both models access the same raw signals and the same operational risk feature; the difference is the temporal window and level of aggregation. No model receives merchant fraud rates from held-out periods or other leakage-prone statistics tied to the validation/test split.

**4. Model and Baseline**

Sequence Model: A bidirectional LSTM (64–128 hidden units) or Transformer encoder processes the 30-transaction sequence. The final hidden state is passed through 1–2 dense layers (ReLU, dropout=0.2) and a sigmoid output. Baseline: Gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost/LightGBM) trained on user-level features. Both models are tuned using the validation set (months 19–21) and evaluated on the same primary metric (PR-AUC), ensuring equivalent effort. An ablation baseline (sequence features + boosted trees) isolates whether gains come from the architecture or feature design.

**5. Evaluation Metrics**

Primary Metric: Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC). Justification: (a) Fraud is rare, so standard ROC-AUC is insensitive to the fraud class; (b) the operational objective is high precision and recall on fraud, which PR-AUC directly measures; (c) PR-AUC is threshold-sensitive, reflecting deployment reality where different operating points have different business costs.

Secondary Metrics: (1) Recall at 90% Precision (operational target), (2) Average Precision, (3) Per-merchant recall (category-level patterns), (4) Temporal stability (PR-AUC per month in the test set, to assess robustness to drift).

**6. Controls and Confounds**

Recency is controlled because the baseline receives recent aggregates; the sequence model's advantage (if real) is due to order, not recency alone. Merchant leakage is prevented by using learned embeddings and by excluding merchant fraud statistics estimated on held-out data. Label delay is handled by temporal splits. Drift is monitored via per-month test metrics. A retrained monthly baseline is included if drift is severe. User memorization is prevented via hashing.

**7. Scope**

This design establishes whether sequence models outperform aggregated baselines for fraud detection on future transactions, under realistic label delay and temporal drift. It does not establish causality in fraud (why a sequence is fraudulent) or generalization to other payment networks, merchant types, or geographies—those would require separate experiments.

planted_issues.description: The 'rolling 24-hour dispute-risk score' is a post-hoc risk signal generated by the processor's case-management system in response to suspicious transaction patterns, including disputes and investigation triggers. Because the ground truth fraud label is defined as chargeback or investigation within 90 days, this score may encode partial or probabilistic versions of the outcome itself, leading to target leakage. Even if the score is temporally proximate and operationally available, its causal proximity to the label makes it an invalid input for a model intended to predict that label pre-emptively.

must_not_claim.claim: The sequence model accesses 'last 30 transactions including the current transaction' — this leaks future information.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Including the current transaction is appropriate because a real-time fraud model processes transactions as they arrive, with full information about the current transaction. The model does not use future transactions beyond this point. This is not leakage as long as no future transactions or outcomes beyond the current one are used.

must_not_claim.claim: Using PR-AUC instead of ROC-AUC is inappropriate or overly pessimistic.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: PR-AUC is the correct metric for fraud detection because the class is severely imbalanced (~0.1–0.5% fraud). ROC-AUC can be misleadingly high due to easy correct classification of negatives, while PR-AUC focuses on the precision-recall tradeoff in the minority class, aligning with operational goals of minimizing both fraud escapes and false declines.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies the inclusion of the rolling dispute-risk score as a source of target leakage, explaining that it is derived from the same investigative pipeline that generates the ground truth labels, thereby creating an illegitimate informational advantage. The defender cannot credibly argue this feature is clean, as its coupling to the label definition breaks the predictive premise of the experiment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates awareness that a feature derived from downstream risk systems — especially those tied to dispute initiation or investigation triggers — is likely correlated with the outcome label even before formal fraud confirmation.

difficulty_justification: The corruption is subtle because the dispute-risk score appears to be a legitimate, operationally available signal; spotting the flaw requires understanding that proximity to the label-generating process invalidates its use as a predictor. However, it is not hidden syntactically, allowing knowledgeable reviewers to identify the issue.

hypothesis: A two-tower retrieval model that uses session sequence embeddings and product image embeddings will rank products higher for click-through prediction than a baseline using only tabular item metadata and user demographics in a fashion e-commerce marketplace.
domain: Fashion e-commerce marketplace search and recommendation with clickstream, catalog, and image data
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Two-Tower Retrieval Model for Fashion E-Commerce Ranking

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 18 months of production clickstream data from the fashion marketplace, encompassing search and browse logs with impression-level records (item shown, position in list, user context), binary click outcomes, secondary conversion events (add-to-cart, purchase within 7 days), user session histories (recent clicked items), and product catalog metadata (category, price, brand, color, size). Product images are available for all catalog items. This 18-month window captures seasonal variation in fashion trends and user behavior, mitigating the risk of fitting to a single season.

A critical observation: the logged click data is biased by the incumbent ranker. High-ranking positions receive more clicks regardless of true user preference, and only items the incumbent chose to show are observed. This position bias and exposure bias must be acknowledged and managed throughout the design.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The data is partitioned chronologically: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, and months 16–18 for held-out testing. This temporal split prevents future information from leaking into the training window and directly evaluates whether the model generalizes to out-of-sample time periods where user preferences, inventory, and seasonal factors may differ. A random split would be inappropriate here because ranking performance is sensitive to temporal distribution shifts (e.g., summer fashion trends differing from winter); temporal splits expose this drift and are more predictive of future production performance.

Within each temporal window, the data is further stratified by user activity level (power users vs. casual), item freshness (new catalog vs. established), and product category to ensure train/val/test distributions are similar across these dimensions. This stratification improves the stability of the validation and test performance estimates and allows stratified analysis to detect whether the model overfits to specific user segments or item types.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

The two-tower architecture comprises a user tower and an item tower:

**User Tower:** Session sequence embeddings are constructed from the last 50 items clicked by the user in the current session, encoded via an LSTM or Transformer to produce a fixed-size dense vector (128-dim). Additional user features include session length, time since last click, user tenure, and historical CTR. These features are concatenated and fed through an MLP.

**Item Tower:** Product images are encoded using a CNN (e.g., ResNet-50 or Vision Transformer), fine-tuned on the training set, producing a dense image embedding (128-dim). This is concatenated with tabular metadata: category (one-hot), price (log-scaled), brand, days-in-catalog (log-scaled), item popularity CTR, and a short-horizon engagement feature summarizing add-to-cart and purchase activity accrued for the item within 7 days of impression serving. The engagement feature is included because it captures near-term item quality signals that raw clicks alone may miss and is already derivable from the logged event stream used to construct the secondary conversion outcome. These are passed through an MLP.

**Baseline Features:** For fair comparison, the baseline model (XGBoost) receives identical tabular metadata features plus user demographics and aggregate statistics, but no image embeddings or session sequences.

All preprocessing (scaling, categorical encoding, imputation) is fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test. Embedding vocabularies (item IDs, categories) are frozen after training. This prevents leakage of validation and test statistics into the model.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a two-tower neural retrieval model trained end-to-end: the user and item towers produce embeddings, their dot product is computed and passed through sigmoid, and binary cross-entropy loss is applied to click labels. Hyperparameters (embedding dimension, LSTM hidden size, image encoder depth, dropout, learning rate, batch size) are tuned on the validation set using random search or grid search.

The baseline is a LightGBM or XGBoost model trained on tabular features (metadata + demographics) with the same hyperparameter tuning effort on the validation set. This baseline represents the incumbent ranking approach and provides a fair comparison: any improvement of the two-tower model is attributed to image and sequence embeddings, not architectural flexibility.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at k=5 (NDCG@5), computed on held-out test impressions. NDCG is appropriate for ranking tasks because it (a) respects the ordering of predictions, (b) weights top-ranked items more heavily, (c) is normalized to a 0–1 scale for interpretability, and (d) is more robust to position bias than absolute CTR. Relevance is defined as binary click (1) or no-click (0).

**Secondary Metrics:**
- Conversion rate among clicked items to ensure ranking improvements do not surface low-quality items.
- AUROC on the click prediction task.
- Stratified NDCG by user segment (power vs. casual), item freshness (new vs. catalog), category, and time period to detect overfitting or seasonal degradation.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning and model checkpoint selection occur on the validation set (months 13–15). Early stopping is performed within the training loop using validation loss or NDCG on validation data. Once the best model and baseline are selected, they are evaluated once on the held-out test set (months 16–18). The test set is never touched during development.

### 7. Confound Controls

**Position Bias:** The primary metric (NDCG) evaluates relative ranking and is more robust to position bias than CTR. Stratified analysis by item popularity quantile detects whether the model is simply re-learning the incumbent ranker's popularity signal.

**Temporal and Seasonal Drift:** The temporal split explicitly measures out-of-sample performance on future months. Stratified metrics by item recency and calendar month detect reliance on spurious recency or seasonal signals.

**Cold-Start:** Stratified NDCG for new items vs. catalog items ensures the image embeddings and sequence features provide signal for items with sparse interactions, not just re-ranking of warm-start items.

**Scope Limitation:** This offline evaluation does not rule out that the model is simply fitting the biased click data more effectively. Online A/B testing in production is necessary to confirm that ranking improvements translate to genuine user satisfaction and downstream conversions.

planted_issues.description: The item tower includes a 'short-horizon engagement feature' summarizing add-to-cart and purchase activity within 7 days of impression serving. Since the prediction task is to rank items at impression time, this feature encodes user behavior that occurs *after* the ranking decision and is therefore not available at inference time. Including it leaks future information highly correlated with user engagement, artificially inflating model performance and invalidating the offline evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: The inclusion of a 7-day post-impression engagement metric as a feature is acceptable because it is derived from the same data used for secondary outcomes.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While the data source is legitimate, the timing makes this feature unusable at inference time: when ranking items for a user, the system cannot know what will happen in the next 7 days. Features must be constructed only from information available at impression time. The secondary outcome is valid for evaluation, but using it as input to the model creates target leakage.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set is touched during training because the temporal split might allow contamination via aggregated features.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: All preprocessing and aggregation (including item popularity CTR) are explicitly fit on the training set only, and the controversial engagement feature is not aggregated over time but directly uses future outcomes. The temporal split and fitting protocol otherwise prevent contamination — the flaw is not in timing but in feature causality.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies the short-horizon engagement feature as introducing target leakage because it depends on post-impression user actions not available at ranking time. The defender fails to justify its inclusion, and the verdict concludes that the experiment design is invalid due to data leakage.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that the feature uses future outcomes (add-to-cart/purchase within 7 days) that are not known when the model must make a ranking decision, making it causally impossible to use in production.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies the flaw and proposes removing the engagement feature and recomputing all metrics without it. The resolution accepts this as sufficient to restore validity, acknowledging that other design choices (temporal split, metric choice, baseline fairness) are sound.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates understanding that while feature quality signals are desirable, they must be constructed from pre-impression data (e.g., historical conversion rates on similar items or categories), not forward-looking aggregates.
