Hyperparameters are selected based on IPW-adjusted nDCG@5 on the validation set. The test set is held out and evaluated only once at the end, after model selection is complete. No information from the test set informs model development.

### 8. Confound Controls

Richer metadata bias is controlled by ensuring the TF-IDF baseline also uses category and price tier as tie-breakers. Position bias is addressed via IPW in the evaluation metric and confirmed via sensitivity analysis using alternative propensity methods (e.g., causal forests). Catalog freshness is monitored as a secondary metric. Temporal and seasonal confounds are mitigated by chronological splits and query-frequency stratification. The propensity model is fit on training data only, preventing the ranking model from exploiting test-set click patterns.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the design is technically sound but includes domain-specific choices (e.g., coarse labeling, frozen embeddings, chronological split with stratification) that may appear suspicious to reviewers without deep e-commerce ranking expertise. A correct evaluation requires distinguishing methodological rigor from superficial red flags.

hypothesis: For marketplace search on a large e-commerce platform, a learning-to-rank model that incorporates session-level behavioral sequences and product text embeddings will rank relevant items higher than a baseline ranker using only static query-product features, as measured by offline NDCG on held-out search sessions.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search ranking for consumer product discovery
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Session-Aware Learning-to-Rank for E-Commerce Search

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18 months of production search session logs (minimum total: 50M impressions) from the e-commerce platform, spanning diverse product categories, geographies, and seasonal periods. For each session, we collect: session ID, timestamp, user ID, query text, full impression lists with position order, click/purchase/add-to-cart events, dwell time per item, and product metadata (title, description, price, category, historical sales volume).

Relevance labels are derived from user behavior: a query-item pair is labeled 1 (relevant) if the item was clicked or purchased within the session; 0 (not relevant) otherwise. This implicit feedback reflects real user intent and matches deployment-time feedback. Position bias (users click higher-ranked items more often) and feedback loops (the previous production ranker influences which items are clicked) are acknowledged as inherent confounds. They are not removed from the data but controlled via stratified evaluation and the choice of evaluation metric.

### 2. Split Strategy

To maintain comparable label prevalence and representation of seasonal categories across development stages, sessions are assigned to train, validation, and test using stratified sampling over month, product category, and query-frequency tier. The split targets the same overall traffic volume as a 12/3/3 month allocation, but each partition includes examples from across the full observation horizon so that head and tail queries, promotional periods, and category mix remain balanced.

No user ID appears in more than one split, so each user's history is confined to a single partition. Product-level feature-engineering aggregates are derived from the training partition and then applied unchanged to validation and test. Query text may overlap across splits (same query in different months), which is realistic and acceptable—queries recur seasonally. This split design reduces the risk that any one partition is dominated by a narrow seasonal window while preserving consistent evaluation coverage across categories and query regimes.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two feature groups are constructed:

**Static baseline features:** Query term frequency, product category, price, popularity (log historical sales volume in the training partition), text similarity (cosine distance between pre-computed query and product title embeddings), and query-product co-occurrence frequency in the training partition.

**Session-level behavioral features (treatment addition):** For each session, extract the user's prior 20 queries and clicked items (in chronological order); encode them as a sequence of product embeddings. Compute aggregated session statistics: clicks per session, add-to-cart count, dwell time distribution. Concatenate session-level query embedding with the static features.

All preprocessing transformers (text embeddings, categorical encoders, scalers) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. Text embeddings are pre-computed on the product catalog and frozen; they do not leak information from the evaluation partitions. No feature directly encodes the label (clicks/purchases); relevance signals are strictly post-training evaluation only.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The treatment model is a learning-to-rank ranker (LambdaMART or gradient boosted decision tree ranker such as LightGBM Rank) trained on static + session-level behavioral features using pairwise or listwise ranking loss.

The baseline is an identically structured ranker trained on static features only, receiving the same hyperparameter search budget and cross-validation procedure. This isolates the marginal contribution of behavioral sequences.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Both models are tuned via 5-fold cross-validation on the training partition, with folds stratified to preserve product-category and query-frequency composition. Hyperparameters (learning rate, tree depth, L2 regularization, sample rate) are selected to maximize average AUROC across CV folds. The selected hyperparameters are applied to both baseline and treatment models, ensuring fair comparison. The held-out validation partition is used once to confirm that the cross-validated hyperparameters generalize; no model selection occurs on the validation set. The test partition is locked until final evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Per-item AUROC on the test set, computed over impressed query-item pairs and reported overall as well as by product category and query-frequency tier. AUROC reflects how well the model separates clicked/purchased impressions from non-clicked impressions when session context is added, and it supports stable comparison across heterogeneous query classes without tying the analysis to a single cutoff. Significance: treatment is superior if it achieves ≥2% absolute AUROC improvement with p < 0.05 (paired bootstrap over queries).

**Secondary metrics:**
- NDCG@10 (top-page ranking quality)
- NDCG@3 (top-3 visibility)
- Mean Reciprocal Rank (penalizes misranking first relevant item)
- Recall@10 (coverage of clickable items)
- Average position of first click (user effort)
- Category-stratified AUROC (detect if gains are concentrated in specific product types)
- Query frequency stratification (head/torso/tail) to verify gains generalize across query popularity

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Position bias and popularity bias are mitigated through stratified evaluation; gains are reported separately by query frequency and category. Seasonal concentration in any one partition is reduced by the stratified split design, which keeps category mix and query regimes comparable across train, validation, and test. Product catalog shifts are documented by comparing category and price distributions across partitions. User population changes are checked by stratifying test results by user segment (new vs. returning) and region. Query-product pair overlap is measured; if >5%, a secondary evaluation on novel (query, product) pairs is performed.

The experiment establishes whether session-aware behavioral features improve offline relevance discrimination and ranking behavior on held-out search sessions. It does not establish causality (due to observational data) nor does it measure end-to-end user satisfaction or business metrics (e.g., revenue)—those would require online A/B testing or user studies. The claim is scoped to offline evaluation on the test set distribution.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses stratified sampling across time rather than a chronological split, allowing future-period data patterns, seasonal trends, and product popularity shifts to leak into the training set. This invalidates the claim of generalization to future traffic, which is essential for e-commerce search under non-stationary conditions like promotions and catalog churn.

The primary evaluation metric is per-item AUROC, which measures the model's ability to distinguish relevant from non-relevant items globally, but ignores the relative ordering of items within each search session. This fails the core objective of ranking: placing the most relevant items near the top of the list. A model can have high AUROC while still performing poorly on top-k relevance, which is what users and stakeholders actually experience.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of query text overlap across train and test splits introduces data leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Query text naturally repeats over time in e-commerce (e.g., 'winter boots' each December), and the model must be expected to handle recurring queries. As long as the *instances* are temporally separated and no user/product IDs leak, this overlap is operationally realistic and not a methodological flaw.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratified sampling across time improves data balance and is a valid alternative to temporal splitting.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In a non-stationary domain like e-commerce with seasonality and catalog drift, maintaining perfect balance at the cost of temporal contamination invalidates generalization claims. The correct principle is to simulate deployment conditions—evaluating on truly future data—not to optimize for statistical balance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the split strategy introduces temporal leakage, making the evaluation overly optimistic and failing to test generalization to future sessions. The defender cannot legitimately justify a non-temporal split given the hypothesis emphasizes performance on 'held-out search sessions' under real-world drift.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that stratified time sampling allows future patterns (e.g., new product launches, holiday demand) to appear in training, violating temporal integrity and invalidating generalization claims.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic argues that using AUROC as the primary metric misaligns with the ranking task by ignoring within-session item order, thus failing to measure whether relevant items are ranked higher. The correct primary metric must reflect rank position, such as NDCG@10.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that AUROC treats all positive-negative pairs equally regardless of rank, so a model that ranks a relevant item at position 10 vs. position 1 receives the same credit in discrimination but very different user impact—which NDCG captures via rank discounting.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Session-Aware Learning-to-Rank for E-Commerce Search

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18 months of production search session logs (minimum total: 50M impressions) from the e-commerce platform, spanning diverse product categories, geographies, and seasonal periods. For each session, we collect: session ID, timestamp, user ID, query text, full impression lists with position order, click/purchase/add-to-cart events, dwell time per item, and product metadata (title, description, price, category, historical sales volume).

Relevance labels are derived from user behavior: a query-item pair is labeled 1 (relevant) if the item was clicked or purchased within the session; 0 (not relevant) otherwise. This implicit feedback reflects real user intent and matches deployment-time feedback. Position bias (users click higher-ranked items more often) and feedback loops (the previous production ranker influences which items are clicked) are acknowledged as inherent confounds. They are not removed from the data but controlled via stratified evaluation and the choice of ranking metric.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To test generalization to future traffic and account for non-stationarity (seasonality, promotions, catalog churn), the data is split chronologically:
- **Training:** Months 1–12
- **Validation:** Months 13–15
- **Test:** Months 16–18

No user ID appears in more than one split; no product from a test session appears in the training impression lists used for feature engineering (preventing id-level leakage). Query text may overlap across splits (same query in different months), which is realistic and acceptable—queries recur seasonally. The temporal split prevents the model from exploiting future information and ensures the evaluation measures true generalization to unseen future sessions, which is the operational requirement.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two feature groups are constructed:

**Static baseline features:** Query term frequency, product category, price, popularity (log historical sales volume in the training period), text similarity (cosine distance between pre-computed query and product title embeddings), and query-product co-occurrence frequency in the training set.

**Session-level behavioral features (treatment addition):** For each session, extract the user's prior 20 queries and clicked items (in chronological order); encode them as a sequence of product embeddings. Compute aggregated session statistics: clicks per session, add-to-cart count, dwell time distribution. Concatenate session-level query embedding with the static features.

All preprocessing transformers (text embeddings, categorical encoders, scalers) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. Text embeddings are pre-computed on the product catalog and frozen; they do not leak information from the test set. No feature directly encodes the label (clicks/purchases); relevance signals are strictly post-training evaluation only.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The treatment model is a learning-to-rank ranker (LambdaMART or gradient boosted decision tree ranker such as LightGBM Rank) trained on static + session-level behavioral features using pairwise or listwise ranking loss.

The baseline is an identically structured ranker trained on static features only, receiving the same hyperparameter search budget and cross-validation procedure. This isolates the marginal contribution of behavioral sequences.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Both models are tuned via 5-fold temporal cross-validation on the training set (months 1–12 divided into 5 consecutive windows, each validating on the next window). Hyperparameters (learning rate, tree depth, L2 regularization, sample rate) are selected to maximize average NDCG@10 across CV folds. The selected hyperparameters are applied to both baseline and treatment models, ensuring fair comparison. The held-out validation set (months 13–15) is used once to confirm that cross-validated hyperparameters generalize; no model selection occurs on the validation set. The test set (months 16–18) is locked until final evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** NDCG@10 on the test set. NDCG@10 directly reflects ranking quality at the page size stakeholders optimize for, rewards higher placement of relevant items (via rank discounting), and is normalized to 0–1 for interpretability. Significance: treatment is superior if it achieves ≥2% absolute NDCG@10 improvement with p < 0.05 (paired bootstrap over queries).

**Secondary metrics:**
- NDCG@3 (top-3 visibility)
- Mean Reciprocal Rank (penalizes misranking first relevant item)
- Recall@10 (coverage of clickable items)
- Average position of first click (user effort)
- Category-stratified NDCG@10 (detect if gains are concentrated in specific product types)
- Query frequency stratification (head/torso/tail) to verify gains generalize across query popularity

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Position bias and popularity bias are mitigated via the choice of metric (NDCG is rank-aware) and stratified evaluation; gains are reported separately by query frequency and category. Temporal non-stationarity is addressed by the chronological split, which tests the model on future conditions. Product catalog shifts (e.g., major new inventory) are documented by comparing category and price distributions across train and test periods. User population changes are checked by stratifying test results by user segment (new vs. returning) and region. Query-product pair overlap is measured; if >5%, a secondary evaluation on novel (query, product) pairs is performed.

The experiment establishes whether session-aware behavioral features improve ranking quality on held-out future search sessions. It does not establish causality (due to observational data) nor does it measure end-to-end user satisfaction or business metrics (e.g., revenue)—those would require online A/B testing or user studies. The claim is scoped to offline ranking quality (NDCG) on the test set distribution.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the flaws are technically subtle: temporal leakage via stratified time sampling mimics good practice (balance) but violates deployment realism, and AUROC seems like a reasonable discrimination metric unless one deeply understands the difference between classification and ranking objectives in information retrieval.

hypothesis: A transformer-based demand forecaster that uses weather, calendar, and local event features will produce lower 7-day-ahead forecast error than a seasonal ARIMA baseline for last-mile delivery package volume at a regional logistics network.
domain: Transportation / logistics — regional last-mile parcel volume forecasting for delivery planning and staffing
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based 7-Day Demand Forecasting vs. Seasonal ARIMA

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 36–48 months of daily historical parcel volume data from a regional logistics network. Data sources include:
- **Volume labels**: Realized daily parcel counts aggregated by depot or service region, sourced from production systems.
- **Temporal identifiers**: Timestamps indicating the forecast date.
- **Covariates**: Day-of-week, holiday flags, promotional periods, temperature, precipitation, wind speed, major local events, and depot identifiers.

All data are collected at the end-of-day window and treated as available at forecast time. Data quality issues (missing values, anomalous spikes from system errors) are flagged and excluded from both training and evaluation to avoid introducing noise that neither model would face in production. The data distribution matches the deployment scenario because it represents actual historical demand at the same network where the model will operate.

### 2. Split Strategy: Representative Forecast Instances

Forecast instances are partitioned into three sets:
- **Training**: ≈70% of instances.
- **Validation**: ≈15% of instances.
- **Test**: ≈15% of instances.

The partitioning is stratified by depot volume tier and calendar regime so that regular weeks, holiday periods, promotional windows, and adverse-weather episodes are represented in each split. This is intended to make the comparison less sensitive to any single contiguous period and to ensure that hyperparameter selection reflects the full range of operating conditions rather than one particular season. Leakage is controlled at the forecast-instance level: preprocessing (scaling, lagging, encoding) is fit on the training partition alone and then applied without modification to validation and test sets. Lagged features are computed using only historical data relative to each forecast date, never future data.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive an identical feature set to isolate the effect of sequence modeling architecture:
- **Lagged volume**: Prior 1, 7, 14, 30 days.
- **Temporal**: Day-of-week, month, holiday binary, days-until-next-holiday, promotional-period flag.
- **Weather**: Temperature, precipitation, wind speed (standardized using training-set statistics).
- **Historical statistics**: 7-, 14-, and 30-day rolling mean and standard deviation of volume.
- **Depot identifier**: One-hot or embedding representation.

All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder, rolling-statistic calculators) are fit on the training set only and applied consistently to validation and test. No feature directly encodes the label, and all features are available at the point of forecast.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer model**: A sequence-to-sequence architecture with a 30-day lookback window, 2–3 attention layers (8 heads, 128-dim hidden), feedforward sublayers, and residual connections. The decoder produces 7 daily forecasts, summed for the 7-day horizon.

**Baseline**: Seasonal ARIMA (SARIMA). To ensure fair comparison, the baseline includes the same external regressors (weather, calendar, events) as the transformer. Hyperparameter tuning for both models is performed on the validation set using grid search; early stopping and cross-validation are applied uniformly.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: RMSE aggregated over the 7 predicted daily volumes and then averaged across depots. RMSE emphasizes the large misses that matter most operationally when staffing and line-haul allocation are disrupted, and it matches the day-level prediction structure of the sequence decoder more directly than a purely percentage-based summary. Using the same squared-error formulation for model selection and final reporting also keeps training, tuning, and evaluation centered on the same notion of forecast quality.

**Secondary metrics**:
- Peak-week RMSE (to verify gains during high-demand periods when staffing is most critical).
- Bias (mean signed error) and, if applicable, prediction interval coverage (for service-level planning).
- Depot-level error slices by volume tier to detect whether performance gains are concentrated in specific operating segments.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both the transformer and SARIMA are tuned on the validation set using the primary metric (RMSE). Once hyperparameters are fixed, both models are re-trained on the combined training and validation partitions to use all available non-test data. The test set is held out and used exactly once at the end, computing final metrics with 95% confidence intervals (via block bootstrap over forecast instances, preserving short-range dependence by resampling contiguous 4-week blocks within each evaluation fold).

### 7. Confound Controls

- **Feature matching**: Identical feature sets eliminate the confound of the transformer simply having access to more covariates.
- **Tuning parity**: Both models receive equal optimization effort on the validation set.
- **Representative coverage**: Stratified partitioning ensures that each split contains comparable mixes of depot scale and demand regime.
- **Stratification**: Results are reported separately by depot volume tier to detect whether gains are uniform or concentrated.
- **Held-out test partition**: Prevents selection bias and provides a clean test of generalization.

The experiment establishes that the transformer produces lower 7-day forecast error than SARIMA under controlled conditions on a held-out test partition. It does not establish causality of specific architectural components, nor does it extend to other logistics networks without additional validation.

planted_issues.description: The split strategy uses random stratified sampling across all time periods instead of a temporal split, which introduces future-to-past leakage. Even though preprocessing is fit on training and lagged features use only past data, forecast instances from the future are mixed into training while adjacent instances in the past are in test, allowing temporal dependence and regime patterns to bleed across splits. This invalidates the prospective forecasting claim because the model may effectively 'see' information from future demand regimes during training.

The primary evaluation metric is unweighted RMSE averaged across depots, which does not align with operational priorities. The hypothesis emphasizes reducing forecast error in a way that improves staffing and vehicle allocation decisions across the network, where high-volume depots matter more. WMAPE, which weights depots by historical volume, is appropriate for this; RMSE instead allows high-volume depots to dominate numerically without intentional weighting and emphasizes large absolute errors rather than proportional accuracy — potentially masking poor performance in smaller but critical depots.

must_not_claim.claim: The rolling mean and standard deviation features cause target leakage because they incorporate future labels.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Rolling statistics are computed only over past historical volumes relative to each forecast date and do not include future information. The corrupted narrative explicitly states that lagged features are computed using only historical data available at forecast time, so these features are valid.

must_not_claim.claim: Testing on only 15% of the data is insufficient and leads to high variance in results.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The actual test period spans 12 months (months 37–48), which covers multiple seasonal cycles and disruptive regimes. The block bootstrap with 4-week resampling accounts for temporal dependence and quantifies uncertainty appropriately. The duration, not the percentage, matters here, and the test window is operationally meaningful.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the stratified random split invalidates the temporal forecasting premise by allowing future data to inform model training through temporal autocorrelation, even if individual features are properly lagged. The final verdict is critique because this flaw invalidates the model’s ability to generalize prospectively.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that time-series evaluation requires chronological separation to avoid leakage of long-range temporal dependencies, and demonstrates that the stratified split enables models to learn patterns from future regimes.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Null — there is no legitimate defense of a non-temporal split in a prospective forecasting task where trend and seasonality are present.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that RMSE as a primary metric fails to reflect the operational stakeholder objective of proportional accuracy prioritized by depot importance. The correct resolution requires adoption of a weighted or scale-invariant metric like WMAPE.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic references stakeholder needs — staffing impact correlates with volume scale — and shows that unweighted RMSE does not prioritize high-volume depots intentionally or handle heteroskedasticity across depots fairly.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender could argue RMSE is acceptable if the goal was minimizing worst-case disruptions from large-absolute misses, but this conflicts with the stated objective of general 7-day-ahead forecast quality across the network.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based 7-Day Demand Forecasting vs. Seasonal ARIMA

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 36–48 months of daily historical parcel volume data from a regional logistics network. Data sources include:
- **Volume labels**: Realized daily parcel counts aggregated by depot or service region, sourced from production systems.
- **Temporal identifiers**: Timestamps indicating the forecast date.
- **Covariates**: Day-of-week, holiday flags, promotional periods, temperature, precipitation, wind speed, major local events, and depot identifiers.

All data are collected at the end-of-day window and treated as available at forecast time. Data quality issues (missing values, anomalous spikes from system errors) are flagged and excluded from both training and evaluation to avoid introducing noise that neither model would face in production. The data distribution matches the deployment scenario because it represents actual historical demand at the same network where the model will operate.

### 2. Split Strategy: Temporal Ordering

The 36–48 months are divided into three contiguous periods:
- **Training**: Months 1–30 (≈30 months).
- **Validation**: Months 31–36 (≈6 months).
- **Test**: Months 37–48 (≈12 months).

Temporal splitting is mandatory here because (a) the hypothesis concerns prospective forecasting, so no future information should inform past predictions; (b) demand exhibits seasonality and trend, which random shuffling would destroy; (c) a transformer's advantage in capturing long-range dependencies is only meaningful if tested on unseen temporal patterns. Leakage is prevented by ensuring that all preprocessing (scaling, lagging, encoding) is fit on the training set alone and applied without modification to validation and test sets. Lagged features are computed using only historical data relative to each forecast date, never future data.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive an identical feature set to isolate the effect of sequence modeling architecture:
- **Lagged volume**: Prior 1, 7, 14, 30 days.
- **Temporal**: Day-of-week, month, holiday binary, days-until-next-holiday, promotional-period flag.
- **Weather**: Temperature, precipitation, wind speed (standardized using training-set statistics).
- **Historical statistics**: 7-, 14-, and 30-day rolling mean and standard deviation of volume.
- **Depot identifier**: One-hot or embedding representation.

All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder, rolling-statistic calculators) are fit on the training set only and applied consistently to validation and test. No feature directly encodes the label, and all features are available at the point of forecast.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer model**: A sequence-to-sequence architecture with a 30-day lookback window, 2–3 attention layers (8 heads, 128-dim hidden), feedforward sublayers, and residual connections. The decoder produces 7 daily forecasts, summed for the 7-day horizon.

**Baseline**: Seasonal ARIMA (SARIMA). To ensure fair comparison, the baseline includes the same external regressors (weather, calendar, events) as the transformer. Hyperparameter tuning for both models is performed on the validation set using grid search; early stopping and cross-validation are applied uniformly.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Weighted Mean Absolute Percentage Error (WMAPE) aggregated across all depots, weighted by average historical volume. WMAPE = Σ|actual − predicted| / Σ|actual| aligns with operational objectives: it is interpretable (e.g., 15% error), scale-invariant, robust to sparse-demand spikes, and prioritizes high-volume depots where staffing decisions have the most impact.

**Secondary metrics**:
- RMSE per depot (to detect sparse-demand performance gaps).
- Peak-week WMAPE (to verify gains during high-demand periods when staffing is most critical).
- Bias (mean signed error) and, if applicable, prediction interval coverage (for service-level planning).

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both the transformer and SARIMA are tuned on the validation set (months 31–36) using the primary metric (WMAPE). Once hyperparameters are fixed, both models are re-trained on months 1–36 (combined training and validation) to use all available pre-test data. The test set (months 37–48) is held out and used exactly once at the end, computing final metrics with 95% confidence intervals (via block bootstrap, respecting temporal order).

### 7. Confound Controls

- **Feature matching**: Identical feature sets eliminate the confound of the transformer simply having access to more covariates.
- **Tuning parity**: Both models receive equal optimization effort on the validation set.
- **Temporal protection**: The temporal split ensures evaluation on unseen demand regimes.
- **Stratification**: Results are reported separately by depot volume tier to detect whether gains are uniform or concentrated.
- **Held-out test window**: Prevents selection bias and provides a clean test of generalization.

The experiment establishes that the transformer produces lower 7-day forecast error than SARIMA under controlled conditions on a held-out test period. It does not establish causality of specific architectural components, nor does it extend to other logistics networks without additional validation.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the temporal leakage flaw is subtle — the corrupted design maintains proper per-instance feature construction and claims to avoid leakage, making it plausible to reviewers unfamiliar with the necessity of chronological splits in forecasting. The metric mismatch is more apparent but requires domain awareness to correctly critique as a misalignment with stakeholder goals, not just a suboptimal choice.

hypothesis: A transformer model trained on multivariate sensor sequences with self-supervised pretraining will forecast 7-day remaining useful life of industrial pumps more accurately than a gradient-boosted model trained on hand-engineered summary statistics, when evaluated on unseen equipment units from later time periods.
domain: Manufacturing / industrial predictive maintenance for rotating equipment in a process plant
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Models for Pump RUL Prediction

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis
We aim to test whether a transformer model trained on raw multivariate sensor sequences with self-supervised pretraining predicts 7-day remaining useful life (RUL) more accurately than a gradient-boosted baseline trained on hand-engineered summary statistics. Success is measured by better prioritization of imminent failures on an unseen test set of future pump failures, reflecting operational value: when maintenance capacity is constrained, the model should rank the most urgent pumps ahead of less critical ones.

### 2. Data Collection and Labeling
Data source: 4–5 years of SCADA and condition-monitoring records from a single process plant, comprising 200–400 unique pump units of 3–5 distinct models. Raw signals include vibration (10 Hz+), temperature, pressure, flow, and motor current, all sampled at 1-minute intervals or faster.

Label definition: Remaining useful life is defined as the time in days from a sensor snapshot to the next failure event. Failure is operationally defined as: (a) unplanned shutdown due to malfunction, (b) maintenance intervention explicitly noting imminent degradation risk, or (c) out-of-service status. We exclude same-day interventions (RUL < 1 day) as potential data entry errors and right-censor units still operating at the observation window end (RUL > 365 days excluded from test). This ensures labels reflect genuine failure risk within a relevant operational window.

### 3. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy
The dataset is split chronologically to prevent leakage from future maintenance decisions into training:
- **Training set:** first 60% of the timeline (by calendar date)
- **Validation set:** next 20% (chronologically later, non-overlapping)
- **Test set:** final 20% (latest time period)

Within each temporal block, samples are stratified by pump model (5 strata) to ensure balanced representation and test generalization across equipment types. This temporal + stratified approach is critical because RUL prediction is inherently forward-looking; a model trained on mixed or future data is not credible at deployment.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Preprocessing transformers (z-score scaling, spectral feature extraction, imputation) are fit on the training set only and applied without re-fitting to validation and test sets. This prevents information leakage.

**Transformer model input:** Raw multivariate sequences of 6 channels sampled at 1-minute intervals, 7-day windows (10,080 samples per window). No hand-engineered features; the model learns directly via self-supervised pretraining (masked autoencoding, 15% masking, 50 epochs) followed by supervised fine-tuning (100 epochs with early stopping).

**Baseline model input:** Hand-engineered 7-day rolling features: vibration RMS, crest factor, kurtosis, spectral centroid; temperature mean, max, trend slope; pressure and flow mean/variance; motor current THD and peak. Identical feature computation for both models ensures fair comparison.

### 5. Model and Baseline
**Transformer:** 4-layer temporal convolutional transformer, 8 attention heads, 512 hidden dimensions, with a linear regression head for RUL prediction.

**Baseline:** XGBoost gradient boosted regressor on hand-engineered features. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search (100 trials, random search) on the validation set, same computational budget, and same evaluation metric (validation concordance index).

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Concordance index (C-index) on the test set (samples with RUL ∈ [1, 365] days). C-index measures whether the model correctly orders pumps by time-to-failure, which is operationally meaningful when maintenance teams must decide which assets to inspect first under limited capacity. It is also less dominated by a small number of large RUL misses than squared-error-based summaries and therefore provides a stable view of degradation prioritization quality.

**Secondary metrics:** Median absolute error (robustness), 7-day recall (% of imminent failures caught), false positive rate (% of no-failure samples mispredicted as failures), per-model-type C-index (generalization across pump types), and per-site C-index (absence of location artifacts).

### 7. Model Selection and Validation
Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set using concordance index as the criterion. The test set is held entirely out and touched only once at the end for final evaluation. Results include 95% confidence intervals.

### 8. Confound Controls
**Unit identity leakage:** We probe latent representations to detect whether unit identity alone predicts test residuals. **Maintenance history:** Test set is stratified by prior maintenance frequency; per-stratum C-index detects if the model exploits service patterns rather than degradation dynamics. **Sensor drift:** Per-unit detrending (fitted on training data per unit) removes global drift. **Imbalance:** Censored samples are excluded; test set composition is reported to ensure fair RUL distribution across both models. **Site effects:** Multi-site data is stratified across train/val/test and per-site error is reported.

### 9. Scope and Conclusion
This design will establish whether transformers + self-supervised pretraining outperform classical approaches on this specific equipment cohort and time period. It will not establish generalization to other plants, equipment types, or datasets—that requires separate validation. The experiment controls for temporal leakage, unit identity artifacts, and maintenance-history confounds, enabling a credible claim about the predictive model's advantage.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric has been changed from mean absolute error (MAE) to concordance index (C-index), despite the hypothesis focusing on accurate prediction of remaining useful life in days. C-index measures ranking performance but does not validate the calibration or accuracy of predicted RUL values. A model could achieve high C-index by correctly ordering failure times while being systematically miscalibrated in absolute terms (e.g., predicting 100 days when the true RUL is 50), which would not satisfy the operational objective of scheduling accurate early warnings.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set should be randomly split rather than temporally split to ensure balanced pump model representation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal split is correct here because the hypothesis explicitly requires evaluation on 'unseen equipment units from later time periods', and the operational use case is forward-looking prediction. Randomly splitting would risk future-to-past leakage and invalidate the evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: Using secondary metrics like 7-day recall and false positive rate is problematic because the primary focus is regression, not classification.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: These metrics are valid as diagnostics: they help assess operational utility under resource constraints, even in a regression setting. Monitoring how well the model captures imminent failures is appropriate and complements the primary metric.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic must identify that the use of C-index as the primary metric misaligns with the hypothesis, which emphasizes accurate RUL forecasting (a calibrated regression task), not just relative prioritization. The correct resolution concludes that MAE—or another absolute error metric—is necessary to verify the stated advantage.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that C-index evaluates ranking quality but is insufficient for validating accurate time-to-event predictions, and that systematic bias can be masked under high rank correlation. They reference the stakeholder objective (accurate scheduling of maintenance) as dependent on calibrated predictions.

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis

### 3. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy

- **Training set:** first 60% of the timeline (by calendar date)
- **Validation set:** next 20% (chronologically later, non-overlapping)
- **Test set:** final 20% (latest time period)

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Preprocessing transformers (z-score scaling, spectral feature extraction, imputation) are fit on the training set only and applied without re-fitting to validation and test sets. This prevents information leakage.

**Transformer model input:** Raw multivariate sequences of 6 channels sampled at 1-minute intervals, 7-day windows (10,080 samples per window). No hand-engineered features; the model learns directly via self-supervised pretraining (masked autoencoding, 15% masking, 50 epochs) followed by supervised fine-tuning (100 epochs with early stopping).

**Baseline model input:** Hand-engineered 7-day rolling features: vibration RMS, crest factor, kurtosis, spectral centroid; temperature mean, max, trend slope; pressure and flow mean/variance; motor current THD and peak. Identical feature computation for both models ensures fair comparison.

### 5. Model and Baseline

**Baseline:** XGBoost gradient boosted regressor on hand-engineered features. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search (100 trials, random search) on the validation set, same computational budget, and same evaluation metric (validation MAE).

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Mean absolute error (MAE) in days on the test set (samples with RUL ∈ [1, 365] days). MAE is operationally interpretable (e.g., ±3 days error), robust to outliers, and directly aligns with the stakeholder's need for accurate, early warnings.

**Secondary metrics:** Median absolute error (robustness), 7-day recall (% of imminent failures caught), false positive rate (% of no-failure samples mispredicted as failures), per-model-type MAE (generalization across pump types), and per-site MAE (absence of location artifacts).

### 7. Model Selection and Validation

### 8. Confound Controls

### 9. Scope and Conclusion
This design will establish whether transformers + self-supervised pretraining outperform classical approaches on this specific equipment cohort and time period. It will not establish generalization to other plants, equipment types, or datasets—that requires separate validation. The experiment controls for temporal leakage, unit identity artifacts, and maintenance-history confounds, enabling a credible claim about the predictive model's advantage.
difficulty_justification: This case is subtly challenging because the corrupted design uses a plausible and commonly-used metric (C-index) that might appear reasonable in a prioritization context. However, correctly identifying the flaw requires aligning the evaluation metric with the hypothesis's emphasis on accurate RUL forecasting in calibrated time units—not just relative ranking—making deep domain understanding essential.

hypothesis: For semiconductor manufacturing, a sequence model trained on multivariate sensor streams from the last 60 minutes of each wafer run will predict final quality-bin failures more accurately than a tabular model built from aggregated summary statistics, especially for rare defect classes.
domain: Manufacturing / industrial process monitoring for semiconductor wafer fabrication
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models vs. Tabular Aggregates for Wafer Quality Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

Data is sourced from production metrology logs spanning 18 months of continuous fab operation. Each record represents a single wafer run and includes: (1) time-stamped multivariate sensor streams (temperature, pressure, flow rate, voltage, etc.) recorded at native equipment sampling rate (typically 1 Hz), (2) process parameters (recipe ID, thermal setpoints, timing), (3) wafer and lot metadata (tool ID, operator shift, batch identifier), and (4) final quality-bin label assigned by downstream metrology inspection within 72 hours of run completion. Runs with inspection latency exceeding 72 hours are excluded to prevent conflation of model uncertainty with delayed labeling. The target is a multi-class label: 'pass', 'oxide_defect', 'metal_defect', 'particle_contamination', or 'other_fail'. The dataset is expected to contain 10,000–50,000 wafer runs with severe class imbalance (~92% pass, ~2–6% per rare defect, <1% for the rarest).

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The dataset is split chronologically into three disjoint periods:
- **Training**: months 1–12  
- **Validation**: months 13–15  
- **Test**: months 16–18  

**Justification**: Semiconductor fab environments exhibit strong concept drift driven by tool maintenance cycles, recipe updates, and seasonal throughput variation. A random split would conflate model sensitivity to these temporal patterns (which may not generalize) with true predictive signal. A chronological split enforces temporal causality: the model is trained on historical data and evaluated on genuinely future runs, preventing leakage and providing a realistic estimate of deployment performance. Within each split, sequences are *not* randomized; temporal order is preserved to maintain the integrity of multivariate sensor dynamics and tool-state dependencies.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Two aligned feature sets** are constructed to enable fair model comparison:

**Sequence Features (LSTM input):**  
For each run, extract the final 60 minutes of sensor data at 10 Hz resolution, yielding a matrix of shape (600 timesteps, n_sensors). Append static contextual features (recipe ID, setpoint values, one-hot encoded tool ID and shift) as constants across all timesteps. This preserves temporal dynamics at the highest available resolution.

**Tabular Features (XGBoost input):**  
From the same 60-minute sensor window, compute per-channel aggregates: mean, median, standard deviation, min, max, range, skewness, autocorrelation (lag-1), and linear trend slope. Concatenate with one-hot encoded metadata (tool, shift, batch). This represents the industrial baseline: summary statistics instead of sequences.

**Preprocessing Protocol:**  
All scaling, imputation, and transformation are fit *exclusively* on the training set and then applied identically to validation and test. Sensor values are z-score normalized using training-set mean and standard deviation. Missing sensor values within a sequence are linearly interpolated using parameters estimated from training data only. No validation or test information is used during preprocessing.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Primary Model (Sequence):**  
Bidirectional LSTM with multi-head attention. Architecture: static feature embedding → concatenate with sensor sequence → 2 stacked LSTM layers (128 units, 30% dropout) → temporal attention mechanism → global average pooling → dense layers (64, 32 units, ReLU activation) → softmax output. Early stopping is applied using validation-set loss (patience=10 epochs). Hyperparameters (learning rate ∈ [0.0001, 0.01], attention heads ∈ [1, 8], dropout ∈ [0.2, 0.4]) are selected via grid search maximizing validation macro ROC-AUC.

**Baseline Model (Tabular):**  
Gradient boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBoost) trained on the aggregated summary statistics and metadata. Hyperparameter grid spans max_depth ∈ [3, 10], learning_rate ∈ [0.01, 0.1], n_estimators ∈ [100, 500], and subsample ∈ [0.7, 1.0]. Model selection uses the same validation set and optimization metric (macro ROC-AUC) to ensure equivalent tuning effort. Both models apply class weights to handle imbalance.

**Why this comparison is fair:** Both models receive the same metadata features, are tuned on the same validation data to optimize the same metric, and are evaluated under identical conditions. The comparison isolates the effect of temporal sequence information.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric: Macro-averaged one-vs-rest ROC-AUC**  
Justification: The models will output class probabilities that may support different downstream review and intervention policies for different defect categories, so the primary comparison should measure how well each model ranks true defect cases ahead of non-cases without baking in a single operating threshold. A macro one-vs-rest formulation gives each class equal influence on the headline metric despite the strong dominance of the 'pass' category, and it allows defect-specific analysis under a common probabilistic framework.

**Secondary Metrics:**  
- Per-class F1, precision, and recall (separately reported for each defect category)  
- Macro-averaged recall (to confirm the model is not sacrificing recall for precision)  
- Normalized confusion matrix (to identify systematic cross-class errors)  
- Macro-averaged F1 at the default decision rule used for reporting thresholded predictions  

These secondary metrics ensure the sequence model's advantage (if any) is not an artifact of class imbalance and also show whether ranking improvements translate into usable class decisions.

### 6. Model Selection and Test-Set Handling

The validation set (months 13–15) is used for hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, and model selection. All iteration and comparison occurs on validation data only. Once hyperparameters are frozen, each model is evaluated on the test set (months 16–18) exactly once. No adjustment based on test performance is permitted; the test set simulates production data encountered after model deployment.

### 7. Confound Controls

To isolate sequence-model benefits from metadata-driven leakage:

1. **Metadata parity**: Both models receive identical encoded metadata (tool ID, shift, batch), preventing the sequence model from appearing superior solely due to memorizing tool-specific patterns.  
2. **Temporal stratification**: The chronological split ensures tool population and maintenance schedules remain consistent across train–val–test, controlling for concept drift.  
3. **Metadata-free ablation**: A variant of the tabular baseline trained *without* metadata is evaluated; if the sequence model outperforms this metadata-free baseline by a large margin, sequence information is the genuine driver.  
4. **Within-tool analysis**: Post-hoc, compute metrics separately for the top 3 tools by run volume; large divergence across tools signals tool-specific confounding.  
5. **Shift-stratified test metrics**: Report results separately for each shift (day, night) to detect shift-dependent bias.  

**Scope**: This design establishes whether sequence models outperform tabular aggregates on this specific fab's data over an 18-month operational window. It does not establish generalization to other fabs, process nodes, or equipment generations without explicit cross-fab evaluation.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric is set to macro-averaged ROC-AUC, which optimizes for the quality of probability rankings rather than actionable classification performance. However, the operational goal requires early, reliable detection of rare defects, which depends on threshold-dependent precision and recall at usable operating points. A model can score well on ROC-AUC while producing poorly calibrated probabilities or suboptimal decisions under real-world constraints, especially in highly imbalanced multiclass settings. This misalignment risks favoring models that rank well but perform poorly when deployed for early intervention.

must_not_claim.claim: The chronological temporal split introduces unfair train-test leakage because future maintenance patterns are excluded from training.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Chronological splitting is appropriate and necessary in this domain due to strong concept drift. The split preserves temporal causality and realistic deployment conditions. Excluding future tool states or process changes is not leakage—it is a correct simulation of model deployment on genuinely future data, which is essential for valid generalization assessment in semiconductor manufacturing.

must_not_claim.claim: Using macro-averaged metrics is inappropriate because it overweights rare defect classes with few samples, leading to high variance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Macro-averaging is intentionally chosen to ensure fair treatment of rare but critical defect classes, in line with the operational objective of catching minority failures. While it may increase variance, this is justified and mitigated by reporting confidence intervals and complementing with per-class and secondary metrics. It prevents the model from being optimized solely for the majority 'pass' class.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that ROC-AUC is not aligned with the real-world goal of making early, thresholded decisions to prevent scrap. The resolution requires switching the primary optimization and evaluation metric to F1-macro or another threshold-dependent, balanced metric that reflects deployment objectives.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that ROC-AUC can yield misleading performance rankings when class distributions are extremely imbalanced and when downstream action depends on precise recall and false-positive tradeoffs for rare events.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic acknowledges that ROC-AUC has value as a calibration and ranking diagnostic but argues it should be demoted to a secondary metric. The primary metric must reflect operational utility—F1-macro is superior because it directly balances precision and recall across all classes at a standard threshold, which is required for early defect screening.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic provides domain-specific reasoning: in semiconductor yield management, catching rare defects requires actionable alerts with bounded false-positive rates, and ranking-only metrics fail to capture model behavior at decision thresholds.
