hypothesis: For hospital sepsis surveillance, a time-aware transformer trained on irregular multivariate vitals and labs from the first 12 hours of admission will predict onset within the next 6 hours more accurately than a gradient-boosted model built from hand-engineered summary statistics, when both are evaluated on future admissions from the same hospitals.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — early sepsis onset prediction from inpatient EHR telemetry and laboratory time series
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Time-Aware Transformer vs. Gradient Boosting for Early Sepsis Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Labeling

We would retrospectively collect timestamped EHR data from 3–5 large academic hospitals spanning 2019–2024. For each inpatient admission meeting inclusion criteria (≥12 hours length, complete vital and lab records in first 12 hours), we would extract: (1) vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, temperature, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation) at native collection timestamps; (2) laboratory results (lactate, WBC, creatinine, bilirubin, platelets, procalcitonin, CRP) at native timestamps; (3) medication administrations and clinical documentation; (4) static admission features (age, sex, type, comorbidities).

Sepsis onset is operationalized using a composite rule: (1) sepsis diagnosis code (ICD-10 R65.2x) during admission, (2) antibiotic initiation within ±6 hours of diagnosis, and (3) organ dysfunction (lactate ≥4, creatinine ≥1.5× baseline, bilirubin ≥2× baseline, platelets <100k, or vasopressor-requiring hypotension) within ±12 hours of diagnosis. The binary outcome is 1 if sepsis onset occurs 12–18 hours post-admission (the prediction window); 0 if sepsis occurs >18 hours after admission or never. Admissions censored before 18 hours are excluded from test evaluation but retained in training with an explicit censoring indicator.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split

Data are split in a site-stratified manner. Within each hospital, admissions are partitioned into training, validation, and test sets using a 60/20/20 allocation, while enforcing that no patient appears in multiple splits. Stratification is performed on the sepsis label and admission year so that each split preserves both site-specific event prevalence and the year-to-year case-mix variation observed across 2019–2024. This keeps model comparison from being driven by an unusually easy or difficult evaluation window and ensures that train, validation, and test remain comparable within each hospital.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two parallel feature pipelines are constructed, designed to be fair and control for confounds.

**Model A (Transformer):** Raw irregular multivariate time series for the first 12 hours. For each vital and lab, we retain the native timestamps and values. We also construct: (1) missingness indicators (binary flag per measurement type), (2) time-since-last-measurement (in minutes) for each feature. No hand-crafted aggregation. Static admission features are appended.

**Model B (Gradient Boosting):** Hand-engineered summary statistics over sliding windows (0–4h, 4–8h, 8–12h): mean, max, min, range, and linear trend (slope) for each vital and lab. We also include: (1) count of measurements per window, (2) fraction of time with no measurement, (3) rate of change between windows, (4) the same missingness indicators and timing features as the transformer, (5) static admission features.

The inclusion of missingness and timing features in both models controls for the confound that the transformer might win simply by better handling sparse/irregular data.

All preprocessing (imputation, scaling, encoding) is fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test, avoiding leakage.

### 4. Model Specifications and Baseline

**Model A:** A time-aware transformer encoder with sinusoidal positional encoding based on clock time (not sequence position), 4–6 transformer encoder blocks (8-head multi-head attention, 256-dim hidden), mean pooling, and a 2-layer MLP head (128 units, ReLU, 0.2 dropout). Trained end-to-end on the irregular time series.

**Model B (Baseline):** XGBoost or LightGBM trained on the engineered feature set. This baseline represents current clinical practice and is given equivalent tuning effort: grid search over learning rate, tree depth, L2 regularization, with early stopping on validation metric. Both models are tuned to optimize validation AUROC.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area under the ROC curve (AUROC) on the held-out test set. Because prevalence and coding intensity can vary across hospitals and calendar periods, AUROC provides a prevalence-stable view of how well the model orders sepsis versus non-sepsis admissions independent of the absolute event rate in any one slice of the data. This supports the core architectural comparison by focusing on whether the transformer consistently assigns higher risk to admissions that will develop sepsis across the full operating range.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Sensitivity at fixed false positive rates (5%, 10%, 20%).
- Calibration: Brier score and calibration slope (slope close to 1 and intercept close to 0 indicate good calibration).
- Time-dependent C-index, restricted to non-censored admissions.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed via grid search on the validation set, optimizing AUROC. The best configuration for each model is selected and retrained on training + validation data combined. This single trained model is then evaluated once on the held-out test set. Test results are reported with 95% confidence intervals, accounting for class imbalance via stratified bootstrap.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

This design controls for several potential confounds: (1) **Missingness handling:** Both models receive explicit missingness and timing features, so the transformer cannot win by implicit superior sparse-data handling. (2) **Equal tuning:** Hyperparameter search uses equivalent budget and grid size for both. (3) **Site stratification:** Ensures that each split reflects the same hospitals and preserves site-level prevalence structure. (4) **Year-balanced partitioning:** Prevents evaluation from being dominated by a single atypical period within a hospital. (5) **Calibration reporting:** Confirms performance gain is not driven by miscalibration.

**What this establishes:** Whether the transformer architecture, given access to the raw time series and explicit temporal/missingness signals, discriminates better than well-tuned gradient boosting on the hand-engineered feature set within the same hospital system.

**What this does not establish:** Whether the transformer generalizes to unseen hospitals, whether any gain justifies the added complexity in production, or whether the gain is clinically meaningful (that requires stakeholder input on acceptable alert rates and clinical outcomes).

planted_issues.description: The train/validation/test split uses random stratified sampling across time instead of a chronological split. This introduces temporal leakage, as future admissions could influence model training and tuning. Since the hypothesis explicitly evaluates performance on *future admissions*, this invalidates the generalization claim and inflates expected real-world performance by allowing the model to learn patterns from data that would not be available at deployment time.

The primary evaluation metric is AUROC, which is insensitive to class imbalance and does not reflect operational alerting constraints. In a low-prevalence sepsis setting (~5–15%), high AUROC can coexist with poor precision under realistic alert volume limits. The appropriate metric is AUPRC, which directly measures trade-offs between true positive alerts and false alarms, aligning with clinical needs to maintain trust and avoid alert fatigue.

must_not_claim.claim: The feature set for the gradient-boosted model is too rich compared to the transformer — it includes hand-crafted aggregations that give it an unfair advantage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hand-engineered features are part of a fair comparison: the transformer has access to raw temporal structure while the baseline uses traditional summary statistics. The inclusion of the same missingness and timing indicators in both models ensures the comparison isolates architectural capability rather than feature engineering effort.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying the split by sepsis label and admission year introduces bias or invalidates train/test separation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by outcome and time-varying covariates is methodologically sound *only if* done within a temporal framework. The real issue is the absence of chronological ordering — not stratification itself. In a proper temporal split with such stratification, this would be a valid technique to stabilize prevalence estimates.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that the train/validation/test split does not respect the temporal order of patient admissions, which violates the forward-in-time evaluation requirement in the hypothesis. The critic demonstrates that random mixing across years allows future data patterns to influence model development, invalidating the claim about future performance.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument centers on deployment realism: a model used in 2025 cannot use data from 2025 for training. Temporal structure must be preserved.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer argues that AUROC is ill-suited for imbalanced clinical alerting systems where operational utility depends on precision at controlled alert rates. The critic establishes that AUPRC is the correct primary metric because it reflects the clinician's need to maximize true alerts while minimizing alert fatigue.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument links the metric choice to stakeholder goals: AUROC can be high even when precision is too low for clinical trust.

acceptable_resolutions.description: A debate is resolved as valid critique if either flaw is fully justified. Because both corruptions are independent and of high severity, the critic must only establish one to win. However, full credit requires recognizing both, as mitigation of one flaw alone does not make the design sound.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer successfully rebuts a defense that attempts to justify AUROC or the random temporal mixing by appealing to stability or statistical efficiency.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Time-Aware Transformer vs. Gradient Boosting for Early Sepsis Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Labeling

We would retrospectively collect timestamped EHR data from 3–5 large academic hospitals spanning 2019–2024. For each inpatient admission meeting inclusion criteria (≥12 hours length, complete vital and lab records in first 12 hours), we would extract: (1) vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, temperature, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation) at native collection timestamps; (2) laboratory results (lactate, WBC, creatinine, bilirubin, platelets, procalcitonin, CRP) at native timestamps; (3) medication administrations and clinical documentation; (4) static admission features (age, sex, type, comorbidities).

Sepsis onset is operationalized using a composite rule: (1) sepsis diagnosis code (ICD-10 R65.2x) during admission, (2) antibiotic initiation within ±6 hours of diagnosis, and (3) organ dysfunction (lactate ≥4, creatinine ≥1.5× baseline, bilirubin ≥2× baseline, platelets <100k, or vasopressor-requiring hypotension) within ±12 hours of diagnosis. The binary outcome is 1 if sepsis onset occurs 12–18 hours post-admission (the prediction window); 0 if sepsis occurs >18 hours after admission or never. Admissions censored before 18 hours are excluded from test evaluation but retained in training with an explicit censoring indicator.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split

Data are temporally and site-stratified. Within each hospital, admissions are ordered chronologically: first 60% → training, next 20% → validation, final 20% → test. This temporal split prevents information leakage from future admissions and reflects real-world deployment. Site stratification ensures the test set represents future admissions from the same hospitals, matching the hypothesis scope. No patient appears in multiple splits.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two parallel feature pipelines are constructed, designed to be fair and control for confounds.

**Model A (Transformer):** Raw irregular multivariate time series for the first 12 hours. For each vital and lab, we retain the native timestamps and values. We also construct: (1) missingness indicators (binary flag per measurement type), (2) time-since-last-measurement (in minutes) for each feature. No hand-crafted aggregation. Static admission features are appended.

**Model B (Gradient Boosting):** Hand-engineered summary statistics over sliding windows (0–4h, 4–8h, 8–12h): mean, max, min, range, and linear trend (slope) for each vital and lab. We also include: (1) count of measurements per window, (2) fraction of time with no measurement, (3) rate of change between windows, (4) the same missingness indicators and timing features as the transformer, (5) static admission features.

The inclusion of missingness and timing features in both models controls for the confound that the transformer might win simply by better handling sparse/irregular data.

All preprocessing (imputation, scaling, encoding) is fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test, avoiding leakage.

### 4. Model Specifications and Baseline

**Model A:** A time-aware transformer encoder with sinusoidal positional encoding based on clock time (not sequence position), 4–6 transformer encoder blocks (8-head multi-head attention, 256-dim hidden), mean pooling, and a 2-layer MLP head (128 units, ReLU, 0.2 dropout). Trained end-to-end on the irregular time series.

**Model B (Baseline):** XGBoost or LightGBM trained on the engineered feature set. This baseline represents current clinical practice and is given equivalent tuning effort: grid search over learning rate, tree depth, L2 regularization, with early stopping on validation metric. Both models are tuned to optimize validation AUPRC.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) on the held-out test set. In the deployment context, clinicians trigger alerts at a fixed or near-fixed positive prediction rate (to manage alert fatigue). Precision-recall directly reflects this: at a given alert volume (recall), how many are true positives? AUPRC is more appropriate than ROC-AUC for imbalanced sepsis prediction (~5–15% prevalence) and aligns with operational objectives.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Sensitivity at fixed false positive rates (5%, 10%, 20%).
- Calibration: Brier score and calibration slope (slope close to 1 and intercept close to 0 indicate good calibration).
- Time-dependent C-index, restricted to non-censored admissions.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed via grid search on the validation set, optimizing AUPRC. The best configuration for each model is selected and retrained on training + validation data combined. This single trained model is then evaluated once on the held-out test set. Test results are reported with 95% confidence intervals, accounting for class imbalance via stratified bootstrap.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

This design controls for several potential confounds: (1) **Missingness handling:** Both models receive explicit missingness and timing features, so the transformer cannot win by implicit superior sparse-data handling. (2) **Equal tuning:** Hyperparameter search uses equivalent budget and grid size for both. (3) **Temporal split:** Ensures test data are genuinely future admissions, preventing overfitting to hospital-specific patterns. (4) **Site stratification:** Ensures test set matches deployment scope (same hospitals). (5) **Calibration reporting:** Confirms performance gain is not driven by miscalibration.

**What this establishes:** Whether the transformer architecture, given access to the raw time series and explicit temporal/missingness signals, discriminates better than well-tuned gradient boosting on the hand-engineered feature set *from the same hospitals and forward in time*.

**What this does not establish:** Whether the transformer generalizes to unseen hospitals, whether any gain justifies the added complexity in production, or whether the gain is clinically meaningful (that requires stakeholder input on acceptable alert rates and clinical outcomes).
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because both flaws are methodologically sound on the surface — stratified sampling and AUROC are widely used — and require domain-specific understanding of clinical deployment and temporal dependencies in EHR data to detect. A superficial reviewer might miss that these standard practices are inappropriate in this context.

hypothesis: A survival model using longitudinal claims and pharmacy refill histories will predict first-time severe asthma exacerbation risk over the next 12 months more accurately than a binary classification model on the same features, because it can properly handle censoring and variable follow-up time.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — outpatient asthma management using insurance claims, pharmacy fills, and encounter histories
task_prompt:
# Experimental Design: Survival Model vs. Binary Classification for Asthma Exacerbation Prediction

## 1. Hypothesis and Objective

We will test whether a Cox proportional hazards survival model, trained on longitudinal claims and pharmacy refill histories, achieves significantly higher predictive accuracy for first-time severe asthma exacerbation than a logistic regression binary classifier using identical features. The survival model should excel because it explicitly models censoring and variable follow-up time, whereas binary classification ignores these aspects of the data structure.

## 2. Data Source and Cohort Definition

We will use 36 months of claims data (Jan 2021–Dec 2023) from a single large health insurer. The study population comprises all members with an asthma diagnosis code in the 12 months prior to baseline (Jan 2020–Dec 2020). We exclude members with a severe exacerbation event in that prior 12 months to ensure we are predicting first-time exacerbations on a comparable cohort. We expect this to yield tens to hundreds of thousands of eligible members.

## 3. Outcome Definition and Censoring

Severe asthma exacerbation is defined as the first occurrence of: (1) ED visit with primary asthma diagnosis, (2) inpatient hospitalization with primary asthma diagnosis, or (3) outpatient visit + systemic corticosteroid fill within 3 days. Time-to-event is measured in days from cohort entry to the first exacerbation event or censoring date, with a 12-month observation window. Members who do not experience an event are censored at 12 months or at disenrollment, whichever comes first.

## 4. Temporal Split Strategy

We employ a temporal split by cohort entry date—the gold standard for claims data to prevent information leakage. Members are divided into three non-overlapping cohorts: Train (cohort entry Jan–Apr 2021, ~40%), Validation (May–Aug 2021, ~30%), and Test (Sep–Dec 2021, ~30%). All members in each cohort receive identical 12-month follow-up, eliminating confounding from variable observation windows. We stratify each cohort by age (pediatric <18 vs. adult ≥18) to ensure balanced representation and enable stratified performance assessment.

## 5. Feature Engineering

All baseline features are constructed from the 12-month period prior to cohort entry using only pre-baseline claims; no post-baseline information is used. Features include: (1) Utilization metrics: ED visit count, hospitalizations, outpatient encounters by type. (2) Medication patterns: controller fill counts, reliever fill counts, systemic corticosteroid fills. (3) Recency indicators: days since last controller fill, days since last ED visit. (4) Adherence proxy: proportion of baseline months with ≥1 controller fill. (5) Comorbidities: binary flags for allergic rhinitis, GERD, obesity. (6) Demographics: age (continuous), sex. (7) Seasonality: indicator for high-risk entry season (Nov–Mar).

Preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, one-hot encoders, imputation) are fit on the training cohort only and applied without refitting to validation and test cohorts.

## 6. Model and Baseline

**Survival Model:** Cox proportional hazards regression with L1/L2 penalization. Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation cohort, maximizing the integrated Brier score over the 12-month horizon. The fitted model is evaluated on the test set.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression (binary classification: event within 12 months vs. no event). To keep the comparator anchored to a simple deployable status-quo workflow, the logistic model will use a fixed regularization setting rather than a broad hyperparameter search. It receives identical feature engineering and preprocessing, and at test time its predictions are interpreted as 12-month event probabilities.

This baseline is the appropriate comparison because it represents the status quo of practitioners unaware of censoring; the comparison isolates the benefit of the survival framework.

## 7. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary:** Integrated Brier score over the 12-month follow-up on the test set. This metric summarizes prediction error for survival probabilities across the entire risk horizon while accounting for censoring. Lower values indicate better performance. Justification: in this clinical setting, care-management decisions depend not only on rank ordering but on assigning stable absolute risk over time, so a horizon-wide calibration-sensitive metric is the most decision-relevant summary of model quality.

**Secondary:** (1) AUC-ROC at 12 months (enables direct comparison to logistic baseline). (2) Calibration plot: observed vs. predicted survival probability in risk quintiles. (3) Stratified integrated Brier score by age group, verifying that the survival model performs consistently across pediatric and adult subgroups. (4) Concordance index (C-index) on the test set as a supplementary ranking-oriented measure.

## 8. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation cohort. Candidate survival models are trained on the training cohort, evaluated on the validation cohort, and the hyperparameter set minimizing validation integrated Brier score is selected. The test cohort is held completely out during development and tuning and is used only for final evaluation.

## 9. Confound Controls and Sensitivity Checks

We address the known confounds as follows:

- **Observation window length:** Fixed 12-month follow-up for all members eliminates this confounder.
- **Temporal trends:** Non-overlapping cohort entry quarters capture seasonal and secular variation; stability across quarters confirms robustness.
- **Feature leakage:** All features use pre-baseline data only.
- **Censoring bias:** Sensitivity analysis on fully-observed (no-disenrollment) subset checks whether results hold when censoring is minimized.
- **Demographic heterogeneity:** Stratified integrated Brier score by age ensures findings generalize.
- **Baseline severity:** Stratified results by prior exacerbation count (high vs. low baseline utilization) verify the model works across risk strata.

## 10. Success Criterion

The survival model is considered superior if it achieves a statistically significantly lower test integrated Brier score than the baseline logistic regression over the 12-month horizon (e.g., absolute improvement exceeding a prespecified margin with a two-tailed significance test or 95% confidence interval). The secondary metrics must show no degradation in calibration or generalization.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric is the integrated Brier score, which emphasizes calibration and absolute probability accuracy over time. However, the hypothesis is centered on superior risk stratification — correctly ranking patients by time-to-event — which is best assessed via C-index or concordance. By prioritizing a calibration-sensitive metric, the evaluation no longer cleanly isolates the survival model’s advantage in handling censoring and time-to-event structure for ranking purposes. This misalignment threatens the validity of the conclusion.

The survival model undergoes hyperparameter tuning on the validation set to optimize integrated Brier score, while the logistic regression baseline uses a fixed regularization setting without tuning. This creates an unfair comparison: any performance advantage may stem from differential optimization effort rather than the methodological benefit of survival modeling. A valid comparison requires both models to undergo equivalent tuning procedures to isolate the impact of modeling strategy.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split strategy introduces data imbalance because the training cohort enters earlier than the test cohort, potentially missing recent trends.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The non-overlapping temporal split by entry date is the gold standard for claims data and is specifically designed to prevent information leakage. The design explicitly acknowledges and controls for temporal trends by using adjacent cohorts and stratifying by high-risk season — any stability across entry periods strengthens external validity rather than weakening it.

must_not_claim.claim: Using C-index as a secondary metric is insufficient — a model can be well-calibrated but poorly ranked.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The sound design correctly prioritizes C-index as the primary metric because the operational goal is risk stratification. Calibration is assessed secondarily via Brier score and calibration plots, ensuring both ranking and probability fidelity are monitored, but the primary decision-relevant capability is ranking, which C-index directly measures.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that the primary metric (integrated Brier score) does not align with the hypothesis’s focus on risk ranking, and argues that C-index should be the primary metric to assess survival model superiority in time-to-event ordering.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that integrated Brier score rewards calibration more than ranking and that the clinical use case emphasizes triage based on relative risk rather than absolute probability.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic establishes that the baseline model is under-tuned compared to the survival model, making the comparison confounded by optimization effort rather than modeling framework.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that the logistic baseline should undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning (e.g., via AUC-ROC on validation set) to ensure a fair comparison that isolates the effect of survival modeling vs. binary classification.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic notes both flaws and argues that, together, they invalidate the comparison: the evaluation metric misaligns with the hypothesis, and the advantage observed could be due to unequal tuning rather than structural benefits of survival modeling.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic combines the two issues to demonstrate that the experimental design does not cleanly test the stated hypothesis.

sound_design_reference:
# Experimental Design: Survival Model vs. Binary Classification for Asthma Exacerbation Prediction

## 1. Hypothesis and Objective

We will test whether a Cox proportional hazards survival model, trained on longitudinal claims and pharmacy refill histories, achieves significantly higher predictive accuracy for first-time severe asthma exacerbation than a logistic regression binary classifier using identical features. The survival model should excel because it explicitly models censoring and variable follow-up time, whereas binary classification ignores these aspects of the data structure.

## 2. Data Source and Cohort Definition

We will use 36 months of claims data (Jan 2021–Dec 2023) from a single large health insurer. The study population comprises all members with an asthma diagnosis code in the 12 months prior to baseline (Jan 2020–Dec 2020). We exclude members with a severe exacerbation event in that prior 12 months to ensure we are predicting first-time exacerbations on a comparable cohort. We expect this to yield tens to hundreds of thousands of eligible members.

## 3. Outcome Definition and Censoring

Severe asthma exacerbation is defined as the first occurrence of: (1) ED visit with primary asthma diagnosis, (2) inpatient hospitalization with primary asthma diagnosis, or (3) outpatient visit + systemic corticosteroid fill within 3 days. Time-to-event is measured in days from cohort entry to the first exacerbation event or censoring date, with a 12-month observation window. Members who do not experience an event are censored at 12 months or at disenrollment, whichever comes first.

## 4. Temporal Split Strategy

We employ a temporal split by cohort entry date—the gold standard for claims data to prevent information leakage. Members are divided into three non-overlapping cohorts: Train (cohort entry Jan–Apr 2021, ~40%), Validation (May–Aug 2021, ~30%), and Test (Sep–Dec 2021, ~30%). All members in each cohort receive identical 12-month follow-up, eliminating confounding from variable observation windows. We stratify each cohort by age (pediatric <18 vs. adult ≥18) to ensure balanced representation and enable stratified performance assessment.

## 5. Feature Engineering

All baseline features are constructed from the 12-month period prior to cohort entry using only pre-baseline claims; no post-baseline information is used. Features include: (1) Utilization metrics: ED visit count, hospitalizations, outpatient encounters by type. (2) Medication patterns: controller fill counts, reliever fill counts, systemic corticosteroid fills. (3) Recency indicators: days since last controller fill, days since last ED visit. (4) Adherence proxy: proportion of baseline months with ≥1 controller fill. (5) Comorbidities: binary flags for allergic rhinitis, GERD, obesity. (6) Demographics: age (continuous), sex. (7) Seasonality: indicator for high-risk entry season (Nov–Mar).

Preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, one-hot encoders, imputation) are fit on the training cohort only and applied without refitting to validation and test cohorts.

## 6. Model and Baseline

**Survival Model:** Cox proportional hazards regression with L1/L2 penalization. Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation cohort, maximizing the concordance index (C-index). The fitted model is evaluated on the test set.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression (binary classification: event within 12 months vs. no event). The baseline receives identical feature engineering, preprocessing, and validation-based hyperparameter tuning (maximizing AUC-ROC on validation), ensuring a fair comparison. At test time, baseline predictions are treated as 12-month event probabilities.

This baseline is the appropriate comparison because it represents the status quo of practitioners unaware of censoring; the comparison isolates the benefit of the survival framework.

## 7. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary:** Concordance index (C-index) on the test set. C-index measures the fraction of member pairs for which the model correctly ranks the earlier-event member as higher risk, accounting for censoring. Range 0.5–1.0; C-index ≥0.65 is considered clinically meaningful. Justification: C-index is the standard for survival model evaluation in clinical practice and aligns with the operational goal of risk stratification for intervention prioritization.

**Secondary:** (1) Time-dependent Brier score at 12 months (calibration loss under censoring). (2) AUC-ROC at 12 months (enables direct comparison to logistic baseline). (3) Calibration plot: observed vs. predicted survival probability in risk quintiles. (4) Stratified C-index by age group, verifying that the survival model outperforms across pediatric and adult subgroups.

## 8. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation cohort. Candidate models are trained on the training cohort, evaluated on the validation cohort, and the hyperparameter set maximizing validation C-index is selected. The test cohort is held completely out during development and tuning and is used only for final evaluation.

## 9. Confound Controls and Sensitivity Checks

We address the known confounds as follows:

- **Observation window length:** Fixed 12-month follow-up for all members eliminates this confounder.
- **Temporal trends:** Non-overlapping cohort entry quarters capture seasonal and secular variation; stability across quarters confirms robustness.
- **Feature leakage:** All features use pre-baseline data only.
- **Censoring bias:** Sensitivity analysis on fully-observed (no-disenrollment) subset checks whether results hold when censoring is minimized.
- **Demographic heterogeneity:** Stratified C-index by age ensures findings generalize.
- **Baseline severity:** Stratified results by prior exacerbation count (high vs. low baseline utilization) verify the model works across risk strata.

## 10. Success Criterion

The survival model is considered superior if it achieves a statistically significantly higher test C-index than the baseline logistic regression (e.g., Δ C-index ≥0.03, two-tailed significance test or 95% confidence interval). The secondary metrics must show no degradation in calibration or generalization.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because one flaw is subtle (metric misalignment with hypothesis) and requires understanding the clinical decision context, while the other (asymmetric tuning) is methodologically clear but may be overlooked if the reviewer assumes unequal effort is justified by 'realism.' Both require careful attention to experimental fairness and intent.

hypothesis: In a hospital pathology department, a sequence model that combines whole-slide image embeddings with the associated pathology report text will predict tumor subtype more accurately than an image-only model, especially for rare subtypes where morphology alone is ambiguous.
domain: Healthcare / digital pathology tumor subtype classification using whole-slide histopathology images and corresponding pathology reports
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal Pathology Subtype Classification

### Objective
Test whether a multimodal model combining whole-slide image embeddings and pathology report text predicts tumor subtype more accurately than image-only baseline, with particular emphasis on rare subtypes where morphology alone is ambiguous.

### Data Strategy

**Source and Scope:**
The experiment uses a multi-center pathology archive spanning 3–5 years (e.g., 2019–2024) from 2–3 large hospital systems. Each case comprises: (1) a digitized whole-slide histopathology image; (2) the final signed pathology report as text; (3) specimen metadata (type, stain protocol); and (4) the pathologist-assigned tumor subtype label. Target cohort size is 5,000–10,000 cases, with at least 50–100 cases per rare subtype class to enable statistically meaningful evaluation.

**Label Definition:**
Tumor subtype is extracted from the final signed diagnosis in each report, standardized into a controlled vocabulary. Cases with ambiguous, conflicting, or revised diagnoses are flagged; only the final diagnosis is retained, and cases with incomplete reports are excluded. Class frequencies are documented; rare subtypes are operationally defined as occurring in <5% of the cohort.

### Split Strategy

**Rationale for Site-and-Time Stratification:**
Random splitting would risk leakage: cases from the same institution in adjacent time periods likely share staining protocols, scanner parameters, and templated report language, creating artificial similarity that inflates validation performance and does not generalize to new sites or time periods. Instead, the design uses a stratified split that respects both institutional and temporal boundaries.

**Split Procedure:**
1. Designate one entire institution as the held-out test set (institution-level hold-out). This population tests generalization to a site never seen during training.
2. Within the remaining institutions, perform a temporal split: use cases from the first 60% of the time range for training, the next 20% for validation, and the final 20% for test. This mimics deployment: the model trains on historical cases and is evaluated on future cases.
3. Within each split (train/val/test), stratify by tumor subtype to preserve class proportions, ensuring all classes are represented in all splits.

**Leakage Mitigation:**
This approach ensures that: (a) the test set includes both temporal future cases and an unseen institution, preventing site-specific and temporal overfitting; (b) no case and its near-duplicates (from the same site and adjacent time period) appear across splits; (c) performance can be separately evaluated by institution and time period to detect domain drift or site-specific confounds.

### Feature Engineering

**Image Features:**
Pre-extracted embeddings from a pretrained vision model (e.g., ResNet-50 or Vision Transformer, optionally fine-tuned on PathImageNet or TCGA histopathology data) are used to represent the whole-slide image. Alternatively, patch-level embeddings are aggregated (e.g., mean pooling or attention-based aggregation) to obtain a slide-level representation. The choice is fixed before training and applied consistently.

**Text Features:**
The full pathology report (or pre-diagnostic sections only, if post-hoc diagnostic language is a concern) is tokenized and embedded using a pretrained biomedical language model (e.g., SciBERT, BioBERT, or PubMedBERT). The embeddings capture semantic content of the report.

**Metadata:**
Spécimen type, stain protocol, and patient age (if available) are included as additional features.

**No Future Information:**
All features are computed from information available at the time of diagnosis—no treatment outcomes, follow-up diagnoses, or retrospective clinical notes are included.

**Fit-Apply Separation:**
All preprocessing (image normalization, text tokenization, vocabulary building, and any outlier detection) is fit on the training set only. The same fitted transformers are applied to validation and test sets to avoid data leakage.

### Model and Baseline

**Multimodal Model:**
The image and text embeddings are fused—options include concatenation followed by dense layers, early fusion (shared initial layers), mid-fusion (fusion after initial processing), or attention-based fusion mechanisms. Architecture selection is performed on the validation set using a small grid of standard choices. The final layer is a softmax classifier over subtype classes.

**Image-Only Baseline:**
A model with identical architecture, training procedure, and hyperparameter tuning effort but using only the image embedding (text embedding is zeroed or omitted). This baseline isolates the contribution of text: if the multimodal model outperforms the image-only baseline significantly, the improvement is attributed to multimodal fusion.

**Fair Comparison:**
Both models are trained on the same data splits, use the same optimization algorithm, batch size, learning rate schedule, data augmentation, and number of epochs. Hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, dropout, weight decay) uses the same budget (e.g., grid search over a 3×3 space of hyperparameters) for both. This ensures that performance differences reflect the value of text, not tuning effort.

### Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric: Macro-F1**
Macro-F1 is the unweighted F1 score averaged across all subtype classes. This metric directly aligns with the stakeholder objective of reducing diagnostic ambiguity and avoiding misclassification of rare subtypes. Because all classes are weighted equally, macro-F1 penalizes poor performance on rare classes and is not dominated by accuracy on common subtypes. This is essential in pathology, where missing a rare but critical subtype has high clinical cost.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- **Balanced accuracy:** Mean recall per class, providing another perspective on class-balanced performance.
- **Per-class F1 and recall by subtype frequency:** Separately report metrics for rare vs. common subtypes to isolate performance gains on the clinically important rare cases.
- **Per-site and per-time-period macro-F1:** Assess whether performance generalizes across institutions and time periods, detecting domain shift.
- **Calibration (Expected Calibration Error, ECE) per class:** Ensure that predicted probabilities reflect true likelihood, critical for clinical deployment.
- **PPV and NPV per rare subtype:** Operational metrics; report confidence intervals to establish reliability.

### Validation and Model Selection

**Validation Set:** The temporal middle period (20% of cases) from the training institutions is used to tune hyperparameters and select the architecture variant. Validation macro-F1 is monitored during training; early stopping halts training when validation performance plateaus to prevent overfitting.

**Test Set Policy:** The test set (final 20% temporal split + held-out institution) is locked until all model selection is complete. No hyperparameter tuning, threshold adjustment, or ensemble re-weighting occurs on test data. Final predictions are generated once. Statistical significance is assessed using McNemar's test (for paired classification errors) or bootstrap confidence intervals on test predictions, without retraining.

### Handling Confounds

**Temporal and Institutional Confounds:** The site-stratified temporal split mitigates leakage. Sensitivity analysis reports performance per site and time period to detect domain drift or site-specific confounds (e.g., different staining protocols leading to distribution shift).

**Text as Post-Hoc Diagnostic Clue:** Audit report language for post-diagnostic phrasing (e.g., "consistent with subtype X") that would leak the label. If evidence of label leakage is found, rerun the experiment using pre-diagnostic report sections (clinical history and gross description) only. Verify that report text is available at prediction time in the deployment workflow.

**Templated Reporting:** Compute text embedding similarity within and across sites. If sites use highly similar templated language, the model may overfit to site-specific wording. Include site as a stratification variable in sensitivity analysis.

**Class Imbalance:** Use stratified train/val/test splits and class-weighted loss (inverse frequency weighting) during training. Report per-class metrics (F1, recall, PPV) to identify performance disparities. Perform sensitivity analysis on rare subtypes separately to ensure rare-class performance does not degrade.

**Label Noise in Rare Subtypes:** Identify cases with low pathologist confidence or conflicting annotations. Re-run evaluation excluding these cases to assess robustness; stable results indicate the finding is not an artifact of label uncertainty.

### Expected Outcomes and Scope

This design establishes whether text embeddings improve subtype prediction on held-out future data across multiple sites. It does not establish the mechanism (e.g., which report features are informative) or whether the text encodes clinically appropriate information or post-hoc diagnostic clues. Post-hoc analysis of learned feature importance and error case audits are recommended for subsequent work.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split (60%/20%/20%) within the non-held-out institutions should be replaced with a random split to maximize data usage and statistical efficiency.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Random splitting would risk data leakage due to shared staining protocols, scanner calibration, and templated report language within institutions over time. The temporal split within sites prevents overfitting to site-specific patterns and better mimics real-world deployment where models predict future cases. Temporal integrity is more important than statistical efficiency in this domain.

must_not_claim.claim: Macro-F1 is inappropriate because it gives equal weight to rare and common subtypes, potentially overemphasizing rare classes with high uncertainty.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The stakeholder objective is to reduce diagnostic ambiguity and avoid missing rare tumors with high clinical cost. Macro-F1 directly aligns with this goal by penalizing poor performance on rare subtypes, whereas overall accuracy would mask such failures. The metric choice is clinically justified and appropriate for imbalanced medical classification.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer recognizes that the design is methodologically sound and raises no fatal flaws. Any concerns about rare class evaluation, data splitting, or confound handling are adequately addressed by the design's use of stratified temporal-site splits, macro-F1, and sensitivity analyses. The final verdict is that the experiment is valid and the multimodal hypothesis can be fairly tested.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic acknowledges that the split strategy prevents leakage, the metric aligns with clinical goals, and confound checks are built into the design. The defender successfully explains why temporal and site boundaries are preserved and why macro-F1 is preferred over accuracy.

### Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric: Macro-F1**
Macro-F1 is the unweighted F1 score averaged across all subtype classes. This metric directly aligns with the stakeholder objective of reducing diagnostic ambiguity and avoiding misclassification of rare subtypes. Because all classes are weighted equally, macro-F1 penalizes poor performance on rare classes and is not dominated by accuracy on common subtypes. This is essential in pathology, where missing a rare but critical subtype has high clinical cost.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- **Balanced accuracy:** Mean recall per class, providing another perspective on class-balanced performance.
- **Per-class F1 and recall by subtype frequency:** Separately report metrics for rare vs. common subtypes to isolate performance gains on the clinically important rare cases.
- **Per-site and per-time-period macro-F1:** Assess whether performance generalizes across institutions and time periods, detecting domain shift.
- **Calibration (Expected Calibration Error, ECE) per class:** Ensure that predicted probabilities reflect true likelihood, critical for clinical deployment.
- **PPV and NPV per rare subtype:** Operational metrics; report confidence intervals to establish reliability.

### Validation and Model Selection

**Validation Set:** The temporal middle period (20% of cases) from the training institutions is used to tune hyperparameters and select the architecture variant. Validation macro-F1 is monitored during training; early stopping halts training when validation performance plateaus to prevent overfitting.

**Test Set Policy:** The test set (final 20% temporal split + held-out institution) is locked until all model selection is complete. No hyperparameter tuning, threshold adjustment, or ensemble re-weighting occurs on test data. Final predictions are generated once. Statistical significance is assessed using McNemar's test (for paired classification errors) or bootstrap confidence intervals on test predictions, without retraining.

### Handling Confounds

**Temporal and Institutional Confounds:** The site-stratified temporal split mitigates leakage. Sensitivity analysis reports performance per site and time period to detect domain drift or site-specific confounds (e.g., different staining protocols leading to distribution shift).

**Text as Post-Hoc Diagnostic Clue:** Audit report language for post-diagnostic phrasing (e.g., "consistent with subtype X") that would leak the label. If evidence of label leakage is found, rerun the experiment using pre-diagnostic report sections (clinical history and gross description) only. Verify that report text is available at prediction time in the deployment workflow.

**Templated Reporting:** Compute text embedding similarity within and across sites. If sites use highly similar templated language, the model may overfit to site-specific wording. Include site as a stratification variable in sensitivity analysis.

**Class Imbalance:** Use stratified train/val/test splits and class-weighted loss (inverse frequency weighting) during training. Report per-class metrics (F1, recall, PPV) to identify performance disparities. Perform sensitivity analysis on rare subtypes separately to ensure rare-class performance does not degrade.

**Label Noise in Rare Subtypes:** Identify cases with low pathologist confidence or conflicting annotations. Re-run evaluation excluding these cases to assess robustness; stable results indicate the finding is not an artifact of label uncertainty.

### Expected Outcomes and Scope

This design establishes whether text embeddings improve subtype prediction on held-out future data across multiple sites. It does not establish the mechanism (e.g., which report features are informative) or whether the text encodes clinically appropriate information or post-hoc diagnostic clues. Post-hoc analysis of learned feature importance and error case audits are recommended for subsequent work.
difficulty_justification: The case is realistically challenging because a reviewer must distinguish between legitimate concerns (e.g., rare class handling, metric choice) and sound design choices tailored to real-world medical constraints. The correct decision requires domain-specific reasoning about clinical impact and data leakage rather than applying generic ML heuristics.

hypothesis: For hospital laboratory operations, a time-series forecasting model that uses both historical test-order volume and calendar/holiday covariates will predict next-day blood draw demand more accurately than a model trained only on past volume, reducing mean absolute percentage error on held-out future weeks.
domain: Hospital laboratory operations planning for daily phlebotomy and blood specimen processing in a large urban health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Exogenous Calendar Covariates in Hospital Blood Draw Forecasting

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation

The experiment uses 36 months of timestamped laboratory test-order and specimen-collection logs from the hospital LIS, aggregated to daily granularity by test family. Calendar variables (day of week, holiday flags, flu-season indicators, and planned clinic volumes) are sourced from the hospital administrative calendar and CDC public databases. The outcome label is the realized next-day blood draw count, naturally available in operational records. Any days with system downtime or incomplete logging are identified and excluded uniformly from all downstream analysis to prevent data quality issues from introducing bias.

### 2. Split Strategy

Data is partitioned at the day level using a seasonality-aware stratified holdout so that training, validation, and test each contain comparable proportions of weekdays, holidays, flu-season days, and low- vs. high-volume periods observed across the 36-month record:
- **Training set:** 67% of days
- **Validation set:** 16.5% of days (for hyperparameter tuning and model selection via rolling validation)
- **Test set:** 16.5% of days (held out until final evaluation)

This split is chosen because the primary comparison is about whether exogenous calendar covariates improve forecast quality across the full operating regime; preserving the same mix of calendar contexts in each partition reduces the chance that model selection is driven by an idiosyncratic run of holidays, outbreaks, or unusual clinic schedules concentrated in a single contiguous window. The validation set remains large enough to allow multiple re-training cycles during hyperparameter search while remaining distinct from the final evaluation set.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature groups are constructed:
- **Temporal features:** Lagged volumes at lags 1, 7, 14, and 30 days, capturing daily, weekly, and monthly autocorrelation.
- **Exogenous calendar covariates:** Day-of-week (one-hot encoded, 6 binary indicators), holiday binary flag, flu-season binary flag, and planned clinic volume (normalized).

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation rules) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents future information from entering training preprocessing. Calendar features are deterministic and pre-defined; no label information is encoded in any feature.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Baseline:** Seasonal ARIMA (SARIMA) trained only on historical volumes, no calendar covariates. The model is tuned via grid search over (p, d, q, P, D, Q) parameters on the validation set, using AIC or rolling validation error as the selection criterion. This baseline represents the 'volume-only' forecast.

**Proposed model:** LSTM or transformer-based neural network that ingests both lagged volumes and calendar covariates. Hyperparameters (hidden units, layers, dropout, learning rate) are tuned via grid search or Bayesian optimization on the validation set, maximizing directional accuracy. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort and are evaluated on identical test data.

The hypothesis claim is that the proposed model outperforms SARIMA; the gap demonstrates the marginal value of exogenous calendar features in a properly tuned, fairly compared forecasting system.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Directional accuracy on the test set, defined as the percentage of days for which the model correctly predicts whether next-day blood draw volume will increase or decrease relative to the current day. This metric is operationally meaningful because staffing actions are often triggered by anticipated movement rather than exact count magnitude, and it supports a direct comparison of whether calendar covariates improve trend anticipation across all 6 months and within each test family.

**Secondary metrics:** 
- RMSE and MAE to identify high-variance failures during surge periods.
- MAPE as a supplementary magnitude-based error summary for operational interpretability.
- Performance stratified by day-of-week and holiday status to detect whether improvements are driven by calendar features capturing fixed seasonality (a known confound) rather than temporal modeling.
- A calendar-only baseline (mean volumes by day-of-week and holiday) is evaluated separately to confirm that exogenous features improve SARIMA but do not alone drive the proposed model's performance.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

During the validation phase, both baseline and proposed models are tuned using a rolling validation window: predictions are made 1 week or 1 month ahead, with retraining on all data available up to the forecast date. This mimics operational deployment (weekly or monthly retraining) and provides a realistic estimate of hyperparameter performance. Hyperparameters are selected to maximize directional accuracy on the validation set.

### 7. Final Evaluation

Once hyperparameters are fixed, both baseline and proposed models are retrained on the combined training + validation data to maximize information before final testing. They are then evaluated once on the test set to compute final directional accuracy and secondary metrics. The test partition is touched only at this final stage.

### 8. Confound Control

Several known confounds are explicitly managed: (1) Weekly seasonality is captured by the SARIMA baseline; if a calendar-only dummy baseline achieves comparable directional accuracy to the SARIMA + exogenous model, seasonality is the driver, not temporal modeling. (2) Rare disruptions (outbreaks, policy changes) affect both models equally within the held-out evaluation partition; if a second non-overlapping evaluation partition is available, it is assessed separately to check robustness. (3) Missing data is handled uniformly for both models to prevent bias.

The experiment establishes that exogenous calendar covariates, when added to a properly tuned baseline model, improve next-day demand trend prediction on held-out data. It does not establish that a particular architecture (LSTM vs. SARIMA) is superior in isolation; rather, it establishes the marginal value of feature engineering in forecasting.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified random split instead of a chronological split, allowing future data to influence training. In time series forecasting, this introduces temporal leakage: the model can learn patterns from future holidays, outbreaks, or high-volume days that it would not know at inference time. As a result, performance estimates are inflated and do not reflect true out-of-time generalization, invalidating the claim about forecasting future demand.

The primary evaluation metric is directional accuracy, which measures whether the model predicts an increase or decrease in demand. However, the hypothesis specifically claims improved forecast accuracy in terms of reduced MAPE—i.e., better prediction of actual specimen volume magnitudes. A model can be directionally accurate but still have large magnitude errors that undermine staffing and supply planning. Thus, the metric does not align with the operational objective and fails to test the actual hypothesis.

must_not_claim.claim: The preprocessing step fits scaling and imputation rules on the entire dataset, including validation and test sets.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Preprocessing transformers are correctly fit only on the training set and applied consistently to validation and test. This prevents information leakage and is a sound practice; criticizing it would reflect a misunderstanding of preprocessing hygiene.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a rolling validation window during hyperparameter tuning introduces contamination because it reuses data.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Rolling validation is an appropriate technique in time series settings to simulate operational retraining and evaluate hyperparameter robustness over time. It is performed within the validation period only, which is temporally before the test set, so it does not violate chronological integrity when the split is correct.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that the stratified split allows future temporal patterns to leak into training, undermining the forecasting claim. The correct resolution is to require a chronological split (months 1–24, 25–30, 31–36) so that models are evaluated on true future data.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains how a non-chronological split enables the model to learn from future seasonal events (e.g., holidays in the test set appearing in training), thus inflating performance unrealistically.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that directional accuracy does not measure the magnitude of forecast error, so it fails to evaluate the hypothesis that forecast accuracy (as MAPE) improves. The experiment should use MAPE as the primary metric to assess operational impact on staffing and supply planning.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that a model could have high directional accuracy but still incur large MAPE (e.g., predicting a small increase when a large surge occurs), making it inadequate for the stated operational goal.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies both flaws and argues that even if one flaw were fixed (e.g., changing the metric), the other (temporal leakage) would still invalidate the conclusion. The final verdict must be critique because both issues independently undermine the validity of the experimental claim.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that the two flaws are independent and each sufficient to invalidate the hypothesis test: one breaks temporal realism, the other misaligns evaluation with stakeholder objectives.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Exogenous Calendar Covariates in Hospital Blood Draw Forecasting

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation

The experiment uses 36 months of timestamped laboratory test-order and specimen-collection logs from the hospital LIS, aggregated to daily granularity by test family. Calendar variables (day of week, holiday flags, flu-season indicators, and planned clinic volumes) are sourced from the hospital administrative calendar and CDC public databases. The outcome label is the realized next-day blood draw count, naturally available in operational records. Any days with system downtime or incomplete logging are identified and excluded uniformly from all downstream analysis to prevent data quality issues from introducing bias.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Data is divided chronologically to respect the time-series structure and prevent information leakage:
- **Training set:** Months 1–24 (24 months of history for learning seasonal and autocorrelation patterns)
- **Validation set:** Months 25–30 (6 months for hyperparameter tuning and model selection via rolling validation)
- **Test set:** Months 31–36 (6 months held-out future data, untouched until final evaluation)

Chronological split is essential because the hypothesis concerns prediction of future demand; a random split would leak future seasonal patterns and autocorrelative structure into training, inflating apparent accuracy and invalidating the forecast claim. The validation set is large enough (6 months) to allow multiple re-training cycles during hyperparameter search while remaining distinct from test data.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature groups are constructed:
- **Temporal features:** Lagged volumes at lags 1, 7, 14, and 30 days, capturing daily, weekly, and monthly autocorrelation.
- **Exogenous calendar covariates:** Day-of-week (one-hot encoded, 6 binary indicators), holiday binary flag, flu-season binary flag, and planned clinic volume (normalized).

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation rules) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents future information from entering training preprocessing. Calendar features are deterministic and pre-defined; no label information is encoded in any feature.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Baseline:** Seasonal ARIMA (SARIMA) trained only on historical volumes, no calendar covariates. The model is tuned via grid search over (p, d, q, P, D, Q) parameters on the validation set, using AIC or rolling validation error as the selection criterion. This baseline represents the 'volume-only' forecast.

**Proposed model:** LSTM or transformer-based neural network that ingests both lagged volumes and calendar covariates. Hyperparameters (hidden units, layers, dropout, learning rate) are tuned via grid search or Bayesian optimization on the validation set, minimizing MAPE. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort and are evaluated on identical test data.

The hypothesis claim is that the proposed model outperforms SARIMA; the gap demonstrates the marginal value of exogenous calendar features in a properly tuned, fairly compared forecasting system.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) on the test set, computed across all 6 months and separately by test family. MAPE is operationally interpretable (e.g., a 10% error translates to staffing adjustments) and scale-independent across test families with different baseline volumes.

**Secondary metrics:** 
- RMSE and MAE to identify high-variance failures during surge periods.
- Directional accuracy (% days forecast direction correct).
- Performance stratified by day-of-week and holiday status to detect whether improvements are driven by calendar features capturing fixed seasonality (a known confound) rather than temporal modeling.
- A calendar-only baseline (mean volumes by day-of-week and holiday) is evaluated separately to confirm that exogenous features improve SARIMA but do not alone drive the proposed model's performance.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

During the validation phase (months 25–30), both baseline and proposed models are tuned using a rolling validation window: predictions are made 1 week or 1 month ahead, with retraining on all data available up to the forecast date. This mimics operational deployment (weekly or monthly retraining) and provides a realistic estimate of hyperparameter performance. The test set is not accessed during this phase.

### 7. Final Evaluation

Once hyperparameters are fixed, both baseline and proposed models are retrained on the combined training + validation set (months 1–30) to maximize information before the held-out period. They are then evaluated once on the test set (months 31–36) to compute final MAPE and secondary metrics. The test set is touched only at this final stage.

### 8. Confound Control

Several known confounds are explicitly managed: (1) Weekly seasonality is captured by the SARIMA baseline; if a calendar-only dummy baseline achieves comparable MAPE to SARIMA + exogenous model, seasonality is the driver, not temporal modeling. (2) Rare disruptions (outbreaks, policy changes) affect both models equally during the test period; if a second non-overlapping test set is available, it is evaluated separately to check robustness. (3) Missing data is handled uniformly for both models to prevent bias.

The experiment establishes that exogenous calendar covariates, when added to a properly tuned baseline model, reduce next-day demand MAPE on held-out future data. It does not establish that a particular architecture (LSTM vs. SARIMA) is superior in isolation; rather, it establishes the marginal value of feature engineering in forecasting.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because both corruptions are methodologically plausible at first glance: stratified sampling appears to improve balance, and directional accuracy seems operationally intuitive. A correct evaluation requires understanding that time series forecasting demands chronological integrity and that metric choice must align precisely with the hypothesis—not just operational plausibility.

hypothesis: In a hospital emergency department, a time-aware gradient-boosted model trained on the first 6 hours of ED vitals, labs, medications, and triage notes will predict 48-hour ICU transfer more accurately than a baseline using only triage demographics and arrival-acuity features, as measured by AUROC and early-warning recall.
domain: Emergency department triage and escalation prediction using hospital EHR and clinical notes
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Time-Aware ICU Transfer Prediction in the ED

### 1. Data and Label Definition

We will use 24–36 months of continuous ED encounter records from one or more large tertiary hospitals. Each encounter includes timestamped triage demographics (age, sex, acuity level), vital signs (HR, BP, RR, O2 sat, temp) recorded at variable intervals, lab orders and results with result timestamps, medication administration records, and nursing notes. The outcome label is ICU transfer within 48 hours, obtained from hospital admission/transfer logs and linked to ED arrival time.

The prediction window is strictly defined as the first 6 hours of each ED encounter. Any information from outside this window—e.g., final diagnosis, later ICU procedures, or vitals recorded after 6 hours—is excluded. The label itself is not revealed during the first 6 hours; it is only used for training after the window closes. Data quality issues (missing vitals, variable documentation) are not corrected via population-level imputation; instead, missingness is encoded as a feature, preserving information about data availability patterns.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split

We employ a **temporal split with no random shuffling**. Encounters are ordered by ED arrival time:
- **Training set:** Months 1–18 (engagement period for model development)
- **Validation set:** Months 19–21 (model selection and hyperparameter tuning)
- **Test set:** Months 22–24 (final evaluation, touched once)

Temporal splitting is essential here because the data are inherently time-series (encounters happen in sequence). A random split would leak future information into the training set, violating the causal structure of the task—the model must make predictions using only data available at or before the 6-hour mark. Temporal split also ensures the test set represents a realistic deployment scenario: the model is evaluated on recent, unseen encounters that arrive after the training and validation periods. This design avoids confounding from systematic changes in ED processes, patient acuity, or documentation practices over the 24–36 month period.

### 3. Features and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (imputation strategy selection, feature scaling, categorical encoding, NLP tokenization) are **fit on the training set only** and applied to validation and test sets without re-fitting. This prevents leakage of distributional information from held-out data into the training process.

The **baseline model** uses a minimal feature set available at triage: age, sex, ESI acuity level, and chief complaint category (one-hot encoded). These features are available immediately at arrival, before the 6-hour observation window begins.

The **proposed model** adds time-aware features constructed from the first 6 hours:
- Vital sign summaries (min, max, mean, SD, trend [linear slope]) for each vital
- Lab abnormality indicators (critical values, abnormal result counts in 0–2h, 2–4h, 4–6h windows)
- Medication classes administered (vasopressors, antibiotics, sedatives, etc.)
- Note-derived features (note count, note length, keyword indicators of instability from NLP fit only on training)
- Time-aware aggregations (hours since arrival, proportion of window with vital data)

Critically, no features encode future information (e.g., ICU procedures, discharge diagnosis). We verify that all 6-hour windows are complete before inclusion; encounters with ED stays < 6 hours are excluded to ensure fair evaluation.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **proposed model** is a gradient boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on the full feature set. Hyperparameters (boosting rounds, learning rate, max depth, L2/L1 regularization) are tuned on the validation set via grid search or Bayesian optimization, maximizing AUROC. Early stopping uses the validation set.

The **baseline model** is logistic regression on the triage-only feature set. For the baseline, L2 regularization is fixed at a single clinically interpretable operating point rather than re-optimized across the validation window. This keeps the reference model stable across calendar periods and avoids attributing gains to aggressive re-tuning of a low-capacity model rather than to the additional time-aware information and nonlinear interactions in the proposed approach. Both models are evaluated under identical conditions on the test set.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: AUROC.** AUROC is threshold-independent and appropriate for clinical deployment where the alert threshold is configurable. It is robust to class imbalance (ICU transfer is rare, likely < 10% of encounters) and directly quantifies discrimination—whether high-risk patients are ranked higher than low-risk patients.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Recall at fixed FPR thresholds (5%, 10%) — operationally relevant, quantifying the trade-off between catching true ICU transfers and alert burden.
- Precision-Recall AUC — alternative metric sensitive to class imbalance.
- Expected Calibration Error — ensures predicted probabilities are reliable for threshold-setting.
- Net Benefit (decision curve) — clinical utility metric if stakeholder preferences are available.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

The validation set is used for all hyperparameter tuning and model selection for the proposed model. The test set is **locked away** during development and evaluated exactly once after all decisions are finalized. Both models are reported with point estimates and 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap if test set size permits).

### 7. Controls for Confounds

**Documentation intensity confound:** We control by (a) restricting prediction window to 6 hours for all encounters; (b) including documentation frequency features (note count, lab count) in both models; (c) stratifying test performance by documentation intensity quartiles to verify gains hold across high and low documentation groups.

**Temporal trends:** Temporal split inherently controls this; we verify by computing AUROC and recall per month in the test period to check stability.

**Informative missingness:** We encode missingness as features and report results stratified by data completeness.

This design will establish whether the proposed time-aware model improves ICU transfer prediction accuracy compared to a fair triage-time reference, without confounding from proxy leakage or information cascade.

planted_issues.description: The baseline model is not fairly optimized: its L2 regularization strength is fixed at a single predefined value instead of being tuned on the validation set. This handicaps the baseline by not giving it equivalent tuning effort compared to the proposed model, which may lead to an unfair performance advantage attributed to the proposed model's complexity and features. Since the hypothesis aims to test whether time-aware features and advanced models improve prediction, the comparison must start from a competitively tuned baseline to isolate the true contribution of the richer method.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is invalid because it uses consecutive time windows and could fail to capture seasonal trends or rare events.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits are appropriate and even necessary in time-series clinical data where future leakage must be prevented. The design justifiably uses a chronological split to reflect real-world deployment. Seasonal stability is checked post-hoc by monthly performance analysis, mitigating this concern.

must_not_claim.claim: Encoding missingness as a feature instead of imputing values is inappropriate and will bias the model.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Missingness is informative in clinical settings—patients with rapid deterioration often have incomplete documentation. Preserving missingness as a feature is a recognized best practice in health ML and helps prevent unintended proxy leakage. Imputation using population statistics would obscure this signal and introduce bias.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the baseline model is not given fair tuning effort, which invalidates the direct performance comparison. The defender fails to provide a justification for unequal treatment, and the correct resolution is to re-tune the baseline on the validation set using the same protocol. The final verdict is that the experiment design is flawed and requires revision.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that a valid ablation requires equal optimization effort across models to isolate the effect of added features and model class. They note that fixing hyperparameters on the baseline without tuning gives an unfair advantage to the proposed model.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender provides evidence that the fixed regularization point is known to be optimal from prior studies on similar populations, and demonstrates via sensitivity analysis that tuning would not significantly improve the baseline's AUROC. (Note: This is unlikely in practice and not supported by the sound design, so acceptable defense is null in typical use.)

A surface-level reviewer might overlook hyperparameter treatment in the baseline, especially when masked by plausible clinical justification.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology labs, a time-aware survival model using longitudinal CBC and chemistry trends plus recent procedure history will predict 7-day turnaround delay better than a static tabular regression model using only same-day order metadata and patient demographics.
