task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Time-Aware Survival Model for Lab Turnaround Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 36 months of consecutive laboratory orders from a large hospital network, joined with EHR data at the patient-encounter level. The outcome is defined as binary: whether an order exceeded the 7-day turnaround service-level agreement. Orders still pending at the data snapshot are right-censored and contribute survival time but not an observed event.

The data sources are the laboratory information system (order placement, specimen type, analyte panel, accession time, result release time) and EHR (patient demographics, admission status, procedure history). All timestamps are recorded with order placement as the time origin (t=0).

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split

A chronological split is used over the 36-month window: months 1–24 form the training set, months 25–30 the validation set, and months 31–36 the held-out test set. This temporal split prevents future information from leaking into training and mimics real deployment where the model is trained on historical data and evaluated prospectively on subsequent periods.

Within each chronological segment, orders are stratified by specimen type (top 10 high-volume types) and setting (inpatient vs. outpatient). Stratification ensures balanced representation across each split and prevents performance gains from coming disproportionately from a single specimen type or setting, which would indicate the model is exploiting service differences rather than learning generalizable temporal patterns.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two categories of features are engineered at order placement time using only information available before the order:

**Static features:** Patient age, admission status, sex, specimen type, analyte panel complexity (count of analytes).

**Temporal/longitudinal features:** Prior lab result turnaround trajectories over the past 90 days, including 7-day moving average turnaround time per analyte, trend (linear slope) in turnaround times, coefficient of variation, recency of last similar test, order frequency in the past 7 days, recent procedure history (presence and timestamp of invasive procedures in past 30 days), and institutional workload proxy (count of all orders in the same 4-hour window).

All preprocessing (scaling, encoding, imputation) is fit on the training set only and applied consistently to validation and test sets, preventing leakage of validation/test statistics.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a Cox proportional hazards model with elastic net regularization, the standard approach for right-censored time-to-event data. This model naturally incorporates censoring into the likelihood and produces interpretable hazard ratios.

The baseline is a logistic regression model trained on static features only (specimen type, analyte complexity, admission status, demographics, institutional workload). To keep the baseline as a stable operational reference, it is fit with a fixed regularization setting rather than an extensive search procedure, while the Cox model undergoes 5-fold stratified cross-validation on the training set to tune the elastic-net penalty. This keeps the comparator lightweight and makes improvements easier to attribute to temporal features and survival-aware modeling rather than to optimization over a simple linear model.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

The Cox model is tuned on the validation set (months 25–30) after 5-fold stratified cross-validation on the training set to select the elastic-net configuration. The logistic baseline is fit with its fixed specification and assessed on the same validation period for comparison. Once the Cox hyperparameters are selected, each model is retrained on the combined training + validation set (months 1–30), then evaluated once on the test set. The test set is never touched during tuning.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Time-dependent AUC at 7 days (AUC-TD) on the test set.** Because the operational decision point is the 7-day service-level threshold, the primary evaluation focuses on discrimination at that horizon: how well each model separates orders that will exceed the threshold from those that will not by day 7. The time-dependent formulation allows this comparison to remain compatible with censoring while placing the emphasis on the clinically actionable intervention window.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Concordance index (C-index): overall risk-ranking quality across observed event times.
- Calibration slope and intercept: whether predicted survival probabilities match observed outcomes.
- AUC-TD and C-index stratified by specimen type and setting: ensures gains generalize across specimen diversity and do not indicate service-level confounding.

### 7. Confound Control

Right censoring is modeled explicitly; censored orders do not inflate the event rate. Stratification by specimen type and setting prevents confounding by order mix. Temporal features are constructed at order time using only historical data, preventing leakage from post-order process variables. Institutional workload is included as a covariate in both models. Performance is evaluated separately by specimen type and setting to detect whether improvements are genuine or driven by exploiting service differences. The baseline model remains a stable linear reference built from same-day metadata, while the survival model is tuned to capture temporal structure and censoring.

planted_issues.description: The primary metric is AUC-TD at 7 days, which evaluates discrimination only at a single time point, but the operational goal is to rank-orders by overall delay risk over time to prioritize interventions. This mismatch narrows the evaluation and risks undervaluing the survival model's ability to correctly order risk trajectories under censoring. The concordance index (C-index) would be more appropriate as it evaluates overall ranking performance across the entire follow-up period.

The baseline logistic regression uses a fixed regularization parameter without tuning, while the Cox model undergoes extensive hyperparameter tuning via cross-validation. This creates an unfair comparison: any performance gain may stem from differential optimization effort rather than the value of temporal features or survival modeling. A fair baseline must undergo equivalent tuning rigor to isolate the contribution of model architecture and feature engineering.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set is too small compared to training — only 6 months vs. 30 months combined training+validation — which may reduce estimate precision.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: A small test set is acceptable here because the goal is long-term stability assessment; 6 months of prospective evaluation under real operational shifts is sufficient to estimate real-world performance, and extending training+validation improves model stability. The split ratio reflects realistic deployment scenarios and is not statistically unsound.

must_not_claim.claim: Using AUC-TD as a secondary metric is inappropriate because it relies on time-to-event predictions from a logistic model not designed for survival outcomes.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUC-TD can be computed for any model that outputs time-specific risk scores, including logistic regression adapted with censored data methods. It is valid as a secondary metric to assess performance at the 7-day horizon, even if not the primary focus.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic must identify that the primary metric (AUC-TD) misaligns with the operational ranking objective and that the baseline is unequally tuned, undermining the fairness of the comparison. The final verdict must conclude that both flaws invalidate confident attribution of improvement to the proposed methodology.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer argues that prioritizing orders by risk requires overall ranking quality (C-index), not single-horizon discrimination, and that unequal tuning effort introduces confounding in model comparison.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies either flaw and demonstrates that correcting it would change the interpretation of results. For example, showing that a tuned baseline closes the performance gap, or that C-index reveals different relative performance than AUC-TD.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer provides reasoning that the chosen metric favors short-horizon discrimination over full-risk modeling, or that fixing the baseline tuning would make the comparison more rigorous.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Time-Aware Survival Model for Lab Turnaround Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 36 months of consecutive laboratory orders from a large hospital network, joined with EHR data at the patient-encounter level. The outcome is defined as binary: whether an order exceeded the 7-day turnaround service-level agreement. Orders still pending at the data snapshot are right-censored and contribute survival time but not an observed event.

The data sources are the laboratory information system (order placement, specimen type, analyte panel, accession time, result release time) and EHR (patient demographics, admission status, procedure history). All timestamps are recorded with order placement as the time origin (t=0).

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split

A chronological split is used over the 36-month window: months 1–24 form the training set, months 25–30 the validation set, and months 31–36 the held-out test set. This temporal split prevents future information from leaking into training and mimics real deployment where the model is trained on historical data and evaluated prospectively on subsequent periods.

Within each chronological segment, orders are stratified by specimen type (top 10 high-volume types) and setting (inpatient vs. outpatient). Stratification ensures balanced representation across each split and prevents performance gains from coming disproportionately from a single specimen type or setting, which would indicate the model is exploiting service differences rather than learning generalizable temporal patterns.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two categories of features are engineered at order placement time using only information available before the order:

**Static features:** Patient age, admission status, sex, specimen type, analyte panel complexity (count of analytes).

**Temporal/longitudinal features:** Prior lab result turnaround trajectories over the past 90 days, including 7-day moving average turnaround time per analyte, trend (linear slope) in turnaround times, coefficient of variation, recency of last similar test, order frequency in the past 7 days, recent procedure history (presence and timestamp of invasive procedures in past 30 days), and institutional workload proxy (count of all orders in the same 4-hour window).

All preprocessing (scaling, encoding, imputation) is fit on the training set only and applied consistently to validation and test sets, preventing leakage of validation/test statistics.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a Cox proportional hazards model with elastic net regularization, the standard approach for right-censored time-to-event data. This model naturally incorporates censoring into the likelihood and produces interpretable hazard ratios.

The baseline is a logistic regression model trained on static features only (specimen type, analyte complexity, admission status, demographics, institutional workload). Importantly, the baseline receives equal hyperparameter tuning effort: both models undergo 5-fold stratified cross-validation on the training set, with hyperparameters selected to maximize performance on the validation set. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of temporal features and survival-aware modeling, not unequal tuning effort.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Both models are tuned by maximizing the concordance index (C-index) on the validation set (months 25–30). Once hyperparameters are selected, each model is retrained on the combined training + validation set (months 1–30) using those hyperparameters, then evaluated once on the test set. The test set is never touched during tuning.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Concordance index (C-index) on the test set.** This metric evaluates the model's ability to rank orders by risk correctly, which directly aligns with operational needs: lab managers use risk rankings to prioritize interventions, not absolute probability calibration. C-index naturally handles right censoring and avoids arbitrary classification thresholds.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Time-dependent AUC at 7 days: discrimination at the operationally relevant time horizon.
- Calibration slope and intercept: whether predicted survival probabilities match observed outcomes.
- C-index and AUC-TD stratified by specimen type and setting: ensures gains generalize across specimen diversity and do not indicate service-level confounding.

### 7. Confound Control

Right censoring is modeled explicitly; censored orders do not inflate the event rate. Stratification by specimen type and setting prevents confounding by order mix. Temporal features are constructed at order time using only historical data, preventing leakage from post-order process variables. Institutional workload is included as a covariate in both models. Performance is evaluated separately by specimen type and setting to detect whether improvements are genuine or driven by exploiting service differences. The baseline model receives equivalent tuning rigor, ensuring the comparison is fair and differences are attributable to temporal feature engineering and censoring-aware modeling.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because both corruptions are justified with plausible rationale that appeals to operational simplicity or interpretability, requiring the reviewer to distinguish principled methodology from convincing but flawed reasoning.

hypothesis: For hospital radiology workflows, a multimodal model that combines chest X-ray images with the preceding free-text radiology history and indication will predict 30-day severe respiratory deterioration better than an image-only convolutional baseline, especially for cases with subtle or ambiguous findings.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — chest radiography risk prediction in hospital radiology workflows using imaging and report metadata
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal Chest X-ray Risk Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy and Cohort Definition

The experiment uses de-identified chest X-ray studies from a single hospital system collected over 36 consecutive months. Each study record includes the radiograph image(s), free-text clinical indication and radiology report, patient demographics, and complete 30-day EHR follow-up data. The target cohort includes all adult patients (≥18 years) with a complete chest X-ray study and uninterrupted EHR follow-up for 30 days post-exam. Exclusion criteria are applied prospectively: studies with missing images or reports, incomplete outcome follow-up, patients already intubated at the time of exam, and studies ordered after mechanical ventilation initiation. These exclusions prevent the model from making predictions in clinically inappropriate scenarios and eliminate trivial cases.

The binary outcome (label) is defined as any ICU transfer, intubation, or initiation of mechanical ventilation within 30 days following the X-ray exam, confirmed via timestamped EHR records. Critically, the label is derived solely from objective clinical events recorded in the EHR at or after the exam timestamp and never from the radiologist's impression or post-exam clinical notes. This approach avoids contaminating the label with clinician suspicion or triage bias, which is a key confound in this domain.

### 2. Split Strategy and Chronological Integrity

The 36-month cohort is divided temporally and stratified by outcome class: months 1–18 form the training set, months 19–27 the validation set, and months 28–36 the test set. Within each temporal window, the data is stratified to preserve the marginal prevalence of the positive class (30-day deterioration events), ensuring each set is representative of the deployment distribution.

This temporal split is essential for two reasons. First, it prevents leakage of future clinical information: the validation and test models are evaluated on temporally unseen data, mimicking the deployment scenario where the model must make predictions on current patients without knowledge of future outcomes. Second, stratification by outcome ensures neither training nor validation is enriched for high-risk populations, which could inflate performance metrics and produce misleading comparisons. The split respects the temporal nature of clinical decision-making and reflects how the model would actually be used in practice.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

The multimodal model ingests three feature streams:

- **Image stream**: Chest X-ray pixels are resized to 512×512, normalized to zero mean and unit variance using intensity statistics computed from the training set only, and passed to a convolutional backbone.
- **Text stream**: The clinical indication and preceding radiology report history (prior 1 exam, if available) are tokenized using a clinical language model (ClinicalBERT or BioBERT) vocabulary, truncated to 512 tokens, and embedded using a pre-trained or fine-tuned transformer encoder.
- **Metadata stream**: Patient age, sex, admission status, and a contemporaneous acuity indicator derived from structured EHR activity around the exam encounter are standardized using training set statistics.

All preprocessing transformers (normalization statistics, tokenizer, imputation rules) are fit exclusively on the training data and applied identically to validation and test. This ensures no information from the validation or test sets influences preprocessing, preventing data leakage.

Importantly, no engineered features encoding clinician suspicion from the report itself (e.g., radiologist confidence scores or flagged findings) are extracted from the text. Only the raw free-text indication and report are used, passed through the transformer encoder. The added encounter-level acuity context is included to capture immediate care intensity that may not be visible in the image alone while keeping the text pathway free of hand-engineered clinical heuristics.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed multimodal model concatenates learned representations from all three streams (image, text, metadata) and passes the fused representation through fully connected layers ending in a sigmoid output for binary probability. The image stream uses a ResNet-50 or EfficientNet-B3 backbone pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned on training images. The text stream uses a pre-trained ClinicalBERT encoder, optionally fine-tuned on training reports. Hyperparameters (learning rate, dropout, fusion layer widths, fine-tuning depth) are tuned on the validation set to maximize AUROC.

The baseline is an image-only convolutional model using an identical ResNet-50 or EfficientNet-B3 backbone with identical preprocessing, augmentation, and training dynamics. The baseline receives only the chest X-ray; it does not see text or metadata. Critically, the baseline hyperparameters are tuned separately on the validation set with equivalent computational effort to maximize its AUROC. This ensures any performance difference is attributable to the multimodal fusion and not to insufficient tuning of the baseline or an unfair architectural advantage.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Clinical Alignment

The primary metric is the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) computed on the held-out test set. AUROC is chosen because: (1) it is threshold-agnostic and therefore reflects the model's discriminative ability across all decision points, (2) it is robust to severe class imbalance (expected here, as deterioration events are rare), and (3) it directly quantifies the probability that the model ranks a random positive case higher than a random negative case — the core clinical question.

Secondary metrics include:
- **AUPRC** (precision-recall curve): especially sensitive to performance on the minority positive class in imbalanced scenarios.
- **Sensitivity and specificity at a clinically-motivated threshold** (e.g., threshold achieving 95% specificity to minimize alert fatigue): interpretable to clinicians as the true positive and true negative rates at an operationally chosen point.
- **Calibration metrics** (Brier score, expected calibration error): measure whether predicted probabilities reflect observed frequencies, essential for decision-making at low prevalence.
- **Positive predictive value (PPV)**: operationally relevant — when the model predicts high-risk, how often is the patient truly at risk?

Thresholds for sensitivity/specificity and PPV are selected post-hoc based on clinical input or calibration analysis, not used to drive model selection.

### 6. Validation Approach and Test Set Integrity

Model selection is performed exclusively on the validation set. At each training epoch, both multimodal and baseline models are evaluated on validation data, and the model checkpoint with the highest validation AUROC is retained. Early stopping is applied based on validation AUROC plateauing. The test set is locked and held out until final evaluation; no hyperparameter tuning, architecture search, or threshold selection occurs on the test set.

After model selection is finalized using training and validation data, the test set is evaluated exactly once to produce the primary AUROC comparison. This ensures test results are unbiased and reflect genuine out-of-sample generalization.

### 7. Confound Control and Scope

The primary confound is that the radiology report or indication may encode clinician suspicion or triage intent rather than genuine patient-state information. The design addresses this via: (1) temporal split preventing post-exam documentation leakage, (2) outcome labels derived from objective EHR events rather than radiologist assessment, (3) stratified splits ensuring no population shift in training/validation/test, and (4) explicit exclusion of derived features that encode clinician confidence from the report text.

Post-hoc, an ablation study (using test data for transparency only after primary results are reported) can mask or randomize the text features to quantify whether performance degradation is genuine or reproduces spurious clinician bias. Additionally, subgroup analysis on prospectively-defined ambiguous cases can directly test whether multimodal fusion provides the largest gain when imaging is subtle.

### 8. Claims and Scope

This design establishes whether a multimodal model discriminates 30-day respiratory deterioration better than image-only baseline when both are given equivalent tuning effort and evaluated on truly unseen test data. It does not establish whether the performance gain is clinically actionable, what threshold should be used in practice, or whether deployment improves patient outcomes — those are separate evidence requirements. The design rigorously controls temporal leakage and label contamination in the split and evaluation protocol, making the comparison valid and generalizable to deployment.

planted_issues.description: The inclusion of a 'contemporaneous acuity indicator' derived from structured EHR activity during the exam encounter risks incorporating clinical actions such as ICU transfer or initiation of respiratory support that are part of the outcome definition (30-day severe respiratory deterioration). Because these actions occur post-exam and contribute to the label, using them as a feature introduces direct label leakage into the model, artificially inflating performance and invalidating the evaluation. This undermines the causal directionality required for valid prospective risk prediction.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is invalid because it uses chronological months rather than patient-level or study-level temporal order.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Chronological splitting by time windows is appropriate and standard in healthcare ML when data is collected sequentially and deployment will occur in a streaming fashion. The 36-month span ensures sufficient patient-level separation across splits, and the design explicitly preserves chronological integrity, which reflects real-world deployment conditions.

must_not_claim.claim: Standardizing patient age and sex using training set statistics causes data leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Fitting normalization parameters (mean, std) exclusively on the training set and applying them to validation and test is the correct procedure to prevent leakage. This is standard preprocessing practice and ensures deployment realism.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the 'contemporaneous acuity indicator' may encode events (e.g., ICU transfer, initiation of mechanical ventilation) that are part of the 30-day deterioration label. The defender cannot plausibly argue that these structured EHR markers are always recorded prior to or independent of the outcome-defining actions. The final verdict must be that the flaw invalidates the experimental conclusion unless the feature is removed or restricted to pre-exam-only signals.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that the acuity indicator includes clinical interventions (such as level-of-care escalation or respiratory support orders) that occur after the imaging exam and are themselves components of the 'deterioration' outcome. Temporal adjacency and clinical semantics confirm the risk of leakage.

hypothesis: For forecasting day-ahead parcel delivery volumes at a national logistics network, a model that combines route-level historical shipment counts with exogenous signals such as weather, holiday calendars, and local event schedules will predict next-day demand more accurately than a model trained on shipment history alone, as measured by lower weighted MAPE on held-out future weeks.
domain: Transportation / logistics — next-day parcel volume forecasting for a last-mile delivery network operating across depots and service regions
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Exogenous Signals for Day-Ahead Parcel Volume Forecasting

### 1. Data and Label Definition

We would use 3–4 years of daily operational data from the parcel carrier, including total shipment counts by route/depot, captured from scan logs. Each record is timestamped and includes the parcel count for that day. In parallel, we would curate exogenous data: daily weather (temperature, precipitation, wind) from NOAA or equivalent service for each route's service area; national and state holiday calendars; school break schedules; and a log of major local events (sports events, retail promotions, conferences) flagged by the carrier. All exogenous data sources are historical and decided before the forecast date, ensuring no look-ahead bias.

The target label is next-day parcel volume (continuous, in parcel count). The label is naturally observed from operational logs and is unambiguous.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split

We employ a **temporal (chronological) split** to respect the time-series structure and prevent leakage:
- **Training:** months 1–24 (approximately 24 months of history)
- **Validation:** months 25–30 (6 months for hyperparameter tuning and model selection)
- **Test:** months 31–36 or remainder of data (6–12 months, held out until final evaluation)

This split reflects real deployment: the model trains on historical data, is tuned on recent data, and is evaluated on future data unseen during development. Each window spans full seasonal cycles, ensuring representative coverage of weekday/weekend and seasonal patterns.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation) are **fit on the training set only** and applied to validation and test without refitting. This prevents leakage of validation/test statistics into the model.

Features include:
- **Temporal:** day-of-week, month, is_weekend, day-of-year (capturing intra-weekly and annual seasonality)
- **Exogenous calendar:** is_holiday, is_school_break, is_major_event, days_to_holiday, days_since_holiday (all defined using data known on or before the forecast date)
- **Exogenous weather:** daily temperature, precipitation, wind speed (aggregated to route service area; lagged 0 and 1 day only, no future look-ahead)
- **Route-level history:** lagged volumes at lags 1, 7, 14, 365 days; 7-day and 30-day rolling means and standard deviations (fit on training data, looked up for validation/test); day-of-week average volumes (computed from training data, then looked up for val/test)

All lag and rolling-window features use only data strictly prior to the target date, preventing forward leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Primary model:** Gradient boosting regressor (LightGBM or XGBoost) trained to predict next-day volume per route. Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set via grid search, optimizing the primary metric (weighted MAPE, defined below).

**Baselines:**
- **Baseline 1 (Persistence):** Next-day volume = volume from 7 days prior (day-of-week seasonal heuristic)
- **Baseline 2 (Seasonal + Linear Exogenous):** Day-of-week and day-of-year average volumes (fit on training data) + linear regression on exogenous features (weather, holiday, events) to predict residuals

Both baselines are tuned on the validation set and evaluated on the test set under the same conditions as the primary model. These represent sensible operational fallbacks and isolate the contribution of the full gradient-boosting model.

### 5. Primary and Secondary Metrics

**Primary metric: Weighted Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)**

Weighting scheme: routes/days in the top quartile of historical demand receive weight 4; middle two quartiles receive weight 1; bottom quartile receives weight 0.25. This prioritizes accuracy on peak-demand days, where forecast errors are most operationally costly. MAPE is used (not MSE or MAE) because percentage error reflects relative impact on staffing: a 10% error on a 100-parcel day is less damaging than a 10% error on a 1000-parcel day.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Unweighted MAPE (routine-day performance)
- Quantile loss at 90th percentile (additional tail focus)
- Mean Absolute Error in absolute volume (bias detection)
- Coverage: fraction of test days within ±10% and ±20% of actual (operational thresholds)
- Per-route MAPE (to detect whether improvement is uniform or route-specific)

### 6. Model Selection and Test Evaluation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 25–30) using grid search. The best hyperparameters are selected based on weighted MAPE on the validation set. The model is then retrained on train + validation combined and evaluated on the test set exactly once. The test set is never used for tuning or intermediate evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls

**Leakage from future-known calendar variables:** All calendar features are coded using information available on or before the forecast date. No data from the target date or beyond is used.

**Exogenous signals as proxies for route identity or trend:** Per-route MAPE is reported to detect whether improvements are uniform or concentrated in specific routes. Long-term trends are explicitly modeled via rolling-window statistics.

**Rare event days:** An ablation analysis removes weather and event signals; if performance degrades significantly, the signals are contributing. A stratified analysis compares forecast error on flagged high-risk days (major holidays, severe weather) vs. routine days.

**Data quality:** Routes with <80% coverage in training, or missing exogenous data, are excluded; coverage is reported.

### 8. Claim Scope

This design establishes whether a gradient-boosting model augmented with exogenous signals (weather, events, holidays) outperforms simpler seasonal baselines on next-day parcel volume forecasting, specifically on peak-demand days. It does not establish causation (e.g., that weather *caused* a volume change), nor does it guarantee performance on routes or time periods outside the test window. The finding is scoped to the carrier's network and data distribution.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is invalid because the test set starts exactly at month 31 — there's no gap between validation and test, which could allow information leakage via model state.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In standard time series forecasting, contiguous chronological splits without gaps are valid and common. The only requirement is that no future data is used during training or tuning. Since model tuning ends at month 30 and evaluation starts at month 31, there is no overlap and no leakage.

must_not_claim.claim: Using MAPE as the primary metric is inappropriate due to potential division-by-zero or instability when actual volumes are very low.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design excludes routes/days with poor data coverage or low activity (via quality filtering and bottom quartile down-weighting), making near-zero volumes rare. MAPE is operationally meaningful in this context because percentage error directly maps to staffing impact, and the weighting scheme mitigates undue influence from low-volume outliers.

must_not_claim.claim: The model is retrained on train + validation before test evaluation, which invalidates the hyperparameter tuning process because the same data used for tuning is later reused in training.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Retraining on train + validation after validation-based tuning is standard practice in time series forecasting. It uses the best hyperparameters identified on the validation set and then maximizes data efficiency by retraining on all available historical data (train + val) before final deployment and test evaluation. This does not introduce overfitting as long as the test set remains untouched until the final evaluation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer raises a concern, but the defender correctly explains why the design choice is methodologically sound and appropriate for the domain and hypothesis. The exchange ends with the reviewer accepting the defense or acknowledging no flaw exists.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender correctly appeals to time series best practices, operational relevance of the metric, and standard model deployment protocols. The reviewer accepts that the design does not contain methodological flaws.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer remains skeptical but fails to identify any actual violation of methodological principles. The defender rebuts all points with domain- and task-specific reasoning, and the final verdict affirms the soundness of the design.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critique is based on generic ML rules of thumb that don’t apply in temporal forecasting contexts (e.g., 'validation must not be used in final training'), and the defender successfully contextualizes the decision within real-world forecasting deployment norms.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Exogenous Signals for Day-Ahead Parcel Volume Forecasting

### 1. Data and Label Definition

We would use 3–4 years of daily operational data from the parcel carrier, including total shipment counts by route/depot, captured from scan logs. Each record is timestamped and includes the parcel count for that day. In parallel, we would curate exogenous data: daily weather (temperature, precipitation, wind) from NOAA or equivalent service for each route's service area; national and state holiday calendars; school break schedules; and a log of major local events (sports events, retail promotions, conferences) flagged by the carrier. All exogenous data sources are historical and decided before the forecast date, ensuring no look-ahead bias.

The target label is next-day parcel volume (continuous, in parcel count). The label is naturally observed from operational logs and is unambiguous.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split

We employ a **temporal (chronological) split** to respect the time-series structure and prevent leakage:
- **Training:** months 1–24 (approximately 24 months of history)
- **Validation:** months 25–30 (6 months for hyperparameter tuning and model selection)
- **Test:** months 31–36 or remainder of data (6–12 months, held out until final evaluation)

This split reflects real deployment: the model trains on historical data, is tuned on recent data, and is evaluated on future data unseen during development. Each window spans full seasonal cycles, ensuring representative coverage of weekday/weekend and seasonal patterns.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation) are **fit on the training set only** and applied to validation and test without refitting. This prevents leakage of validation/test statistics into the model.

Features include:
- **Temporal:** day-of-week, month, is_weekend, day-of-year (capturing intra-weekly and annual seasonality)
- **Exogenous calendar:** is_holiday, is_school_break, is_major_event, days_to_holiday, days_since_holiday (all defined using data known on or before the forecast date)
- **Exogenous weather:** daily temperature, precipitation, wind speed (aggregated to route service area; lagged 0 and 1 day only, no future look-ahead)
- **Route-level history:** lagged volumes at lags 1, 7, 14, 365 days; 7-day and 30-day rolling means and standard deviations (fit on training data, looked up for validation/test); day-of-week average volumes (computed from training data, then looked up for val/test)

All lag and rolling-window features use only data strictly prior to the target date, preventing forward leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Primary model:** Gradient boosting regressor (LightGBM or XGBoost) trained to predict next-day volume per route. Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set via grid search, optimizing the primary metric (weighted MAPE, defined below).

**Baselines:**
- **Baseline 1 (Persistence):** Next-day volume = volume from 7 days prior (day-of-week seasonal heuristic)
- **Baseline 2 (Seasonal + Linear Exogenous):** Day-of-week and day-of-year average volumes (fit on training data) + linear regression on exogenous features (weather, holiday, events) to predict residuals

Both baselines are tuned on the validation set and evaluated on the test set under the same conditions as the primary model. These represent sensible operational fallbacks and isolate the contribution of the full gradient-boosting model.

### 5. Primary and Secondary Metrics

**Primary metric: Weighted Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)**

Weighting scheme: routes/days in the top quartile of historical demand receive weight 4; middle two quartiles receive weight 1; bottom quartile receives weight 0.25. This prioritizes accuracy on peak-demand days, where forecast errors are most operationally costly. MAPE is used (not MSE or MAE) because percentage error reflects relative impact on staffing: a 10% error on a 100-parcel day is less damaging than a 10% error on a 1000-parcel day.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Unweighted MAPE (routine-day performance)
- Quantile loss at 90th percentile (additional tail focus)
- Mean Absolute Error in absolute volume (bias detection)
- Coverage: fraction of test days within ±10% and ±20% of actual (operational thresholds)
- Per-route MAPE (to detect whether improvement is uniform or route-specific)

### 6. Model Selection and Test Evaluation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 25–30) using grid search. The best hyperparameters are selected based on weighted MAPE on the validation set. The model is then retrained on train + validation combined and evaluated on the test set exactly once. The test set is never used for tuning or intermediate evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls

**Leakage from future-known calendar variables:** All calendar features are coded using information available on or before the forecast date. No data from the target date or beyond is used.

**Exogenous signals as proxies for route identity or trend:** Per-route MAPE is reported to detect whether improvements are uniform or concentrated in specific routes. Long-term trends are explicitly modeled via rolling-window statistics.

**Rare event days:** An ablation analysis removes weather and event signals; if performance degrades significantly, the signals are contributing. A stratified analysis compares forecast error on flagged high-risk days (major holidays, severe weather) vs. routine days.

**Data quality:** Routes with <80% coverage in training, or missing exogenous data, are excluded; coverage is reported.

### 8. Claim Scope

This design establishes whether a gradient-boosting model augmented with exogenous signals (weather, events, holidays) outperforms simpler seasonal baselines on next-day parcel volume forecasting, specifically on peak-demand days. It does not establish causation (e.g., that weather *caused* a volume change), nor does it guarantee performance on routes or time periods outside the test window. The finding is scoped to the carrier's network and data distribution.
difficulty_justification: Despite having no actual corruptions, this case is non-trivial because several design choices — such as retraining on train+validation, using MAPE with weights, and applying route-level rolling statistics — may appear suspicious to a pattern-matching reviewer without deep time series forecasting expertise. Evaluating correctness requires understanding of operational forecasting norms and domain-specific metric design.

hypothesis: For wildfire spread forecasting in a regional operations setting, a spatiotemporal model that incorporates satellite-derived vegetation indices, weather forecasts, terrain, and recent fire perimeters will produce lower 6- to 24-hour burn-area prediction error than a baseline model trained only on historical fire size and weather summaries.
domain: Wildfire operations forecasting for a state or regional fire management agency using satellite imagery, weather feeds, topography, and incident perimeter updates
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Spatiotemporal Wildfire Spread Forecasting

### Objective
Test whether a spatiotemporal neural network that ingests satellite vegetation indices, gridded weather forecasts, terrain, and evolving fire perimeters produces significantly lower 6–24 hour burn-area prediction error than a baseline model trained only on historical fire statistics and incident-day weather summaries.

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation

Source data spans 4–6 complete fire seasons from a state or regional fire management agency (minimum 500–2000 documented incidents). Raw inputs are:
- Timestamped incident perimeter shapefiles (hourly to daily updates) with official validation by incident commanders
- Gridded weather forecasts (temperature, wind, humidity, precipitation) at 1–4 km resolution
- Satellite vegetation indices (NDVI, EVI) from Landsat or Sentinel-2 (10–30 m resolution, composited to match weather grid)
- Static terrain layers: elevation, slope, aspect, canopy height, fuel model class
- Suppression action logs with dispatch times and resource types

Labels are constructed as 6-, 12-, and 24-hour cumulative burn-area growth, derived from perimeter differencing at multiple forecast origin times (hour 1, 6, 12, 24 post-ignition). This creates multiple independent prediction tasks per incident. Censoring is flagged explicitly: actively suppressed fires are labeled with observed growth but marked censored; uncensored fires are rare but documented.

Known data quality issues are addressed upfront: missing weather observations are forward-filled within incident and cross-incident imputed using training-set median; satellite cloud cover is masked using a uniform algorithm applied across all data; perimeter mapping errors are accepted as measurement noise (not corrected retrospectively, to avoid look-ahead bias).

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy

To prevent temporal leakage and geographic memorization, the split is temporal-geographic stratified:
- **Training set (60%):** All incidents with ignition dates in the earliest 60% of the time window
- **Validation set (20%):** Chronologically subsequent incidents, spanning the next 20% of the time window
- **Test set (20%):** Final 20% of chronologically sorted incidents

Within each set, incidents are further stratified by fire size class (small <1000 ha, medium 1000–10000 ha, large >10000 ha) and ecoregion to ensure representative coverage. Spatial buffers of ≥10 km are enforced between sets where possible to minimize spatial autocorrelation leakage. This temporal ordering mimics the operational deployment scenario: the model forecasts novel fires encountered after training, using only information available at forecast time.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, temporal aggregation) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets.

**Proposed model features:**
- Satellite indices: 7-day and 30-day rolling composites of NDVI and EVI
- Weather: gridded forecasts extracted at fire location and 1–5 km neighborhood
- Terrain: elevation, slope, aspect, canopy height, fuel model (static, no fitting required)
- Fire state: current burned area, perimeter length, shape compactness, hours since ignition, season
- Suppression-state summaries from the incident log stream: resources assigned, aircraft dispatch count, and line-construction activity accumulated over the active operational period for that incident-day

**Baseline features:**
- Historical median burn area (by ecoregion, season, size class)
- Incident-day weather summary (mean temperature, max wind speed)
- Static location metadata (ecoregion, distance to infrastructure)

No features are constructed from holdout test or validation data. Suppression-state summaries are included because they are part of the real operational context in which spread forecasts are interpreted, and they help distinguish intrinsic spread potential from growth already being constrained by tactical response.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed model:** A spatiotemporal neural network combining convolutional and recurrent layers (e.g., ConvLSTM or 3D CNN) to process gridded satellite and weather inputs alongside incident state features. The architecture ingests multi-channel spatial tensors (5–10 km extent, 3–4 weeks history) and outputs 6-, 12-, and 24-hour cumulative burn-area predictions. Trained end-to-end on the training set with early stopping on the validation set.

**Baseline model:** Gradient boosted regression (XGBoost or LightGBM) using only the feature subset available without satellite or fine-resolution weather data. The baseline is tuned with equivalent computational effort (hyperparameter search over learning rate, tree depth, regularization) using the same validation set. Both models are frozen after training; no adjustments based on test-set performance.

This comparison is fair: the baseline is not handicapped by weak inputs or insufficient tuning, so any improvement is attributable to the proposed model's spatiotemporal capacity, not raw data richness.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Median Absolute Error (MAE) in burn-area prediction (hectares), aggregated across all forecast windows and test incidents. MAE is operationally meaningful (directly interpretable in hectares), robust to outliers, and aligns with fire managers' need for accurate, unbiased forecasts. Reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap.

**Secondary metrics:**
- 90th percentile quantile loss (to assess calibration of high-impact underpredictions)
- Spatial error (predicted vs. observed fire location centroid shift)
- Temporal fidelity (correlation of hourly progression)
- Performance stratified by fire size class and ecoregion
- Relative improvement: (baseline MAE − proposed MAE) / baseline MAE

### 6. Model Selection and Test-Set Policy

The validation set is used for all model selection: hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. The test set is sealed and not accessed until final evaluation. After both models are fully trained and frozen, the test set is evaluated once and reported with confidence intervals. No post-hoc adjustments are made based on test results.

### 7. Confound Controls

The stated confound—that improvement may come from richer metadata rather than true spatiotemporal learning—is controlled as follows:

1. **Data parity:** The baseline receives equivalent temporal and static spatial metadata, ensuring it is not handicapped.
2. **Architecture isolation:** Both models use identical preprocessing; the difference is model capacity to learn spatiotemporal interactions.
3. **Suppression sensitivity:** Test incidents are stratified by suppression intensity; if improvement is confined to heavily suppressed fires, metadata recency (not forecasting skill) is the driver.
4. **Ablation study:** Remove satellite features from the proposed model; if performance drops sharply, satellite information is the key driver, not extraneous metadata.
5. **Cross-regional evaluation:** If data span multiple regions, test performance is stratified geographically; poor regional transfer indicates spatial memorization.
6. **Temporal stability:** Retrain the model on rolling 60% subsets to verify that improvements are stable, not artifacts of a particular split.

### Expected Outcome

If the hypothesis is supported, the proposed model will exhibit lower MAE on the test set (ideally ≥15% improvement over baseline) with stable performance across fire size classes, ecoregions, and retraining seeds. If the hypothesis is not supported, differences will be small or attributable to confounds (as revealed by sensitivity checks). Either result is operationally valuable for prioritizing investment in spatiotemporal forecasting infrastructure.

planted_issues.description: Suppression-state summaries (e.g., resources assigned, aircraft dispatch count) are accumulated over the 'active operational period for that incident-day', but the forecast origin times occur before the full day has elapsed. This means the feature encodes response actions that were taken after the prediction time, which themselves are reactive to the ongoing fire growth. Thus, the magnitude and rate of burn-area expansion are indirectly encoded in these features, creating a causally downstream leakage to the target.

must_not_claim.claim: The inclusion of suppression-state summaries is inappropriate because suppression is part of the operational context and not a causal driver of spread.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Suppression actions are legitimate contextual features in wildfire forecasting; incident commanders use them to inform resource allocation and expect models to condition predictions on them. The issue is not their inclusion per se, but that they are aggregated over the entire incident-day without truncation at forecast time, leading to future leakage.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that suppression-state summaries are accumulated over the full incident-day rather than being time-truncated at each forecast origin, creating target leakage. The defender fails to rebut because no legitimate version of this feature can use post-prediction actions.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The feature uses suppression actions that occurred after the forecast origin time, which are responses to realized fire growth.

difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because suppression-state features appear justified operationally and conceptually, but the leakage arises from imprecise temporal alignment rather than obviously forbidden data, requiring careful reasoning about causality and time windows.

hypothesis: For a logistics network’s parcel delivery ETA problem, a sequence model trained on raw route telemetry and stop-level timestamps will predict delivery delay better than a gradient-boosted model built on aggregated route summary features, as measured by out-of-sample mean absolute error on future weeks of deliveries.
domain: Transportation / logistics — last-mile parcel delivery ETA and delay prediction for a regional carrier using route execution logs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models vs. Gradient Boosting for Parcel Delivery ETA Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation
We will use 12 months of production route execution logs from the regional carrier, spanning approximately 2–5 million delivered stops across 50–200 routes per day. The raw data includes GPS traces (latitude, longitude, timestamp, sampled at 30–60 second intervals), dispatch and stop-level timestamps (arrival and departure, to the second), package attributes (weight, dimensions), vehicle metadata (type, ID), driver ID, planned route sequence, and actual delivery completion time. We will enrich this with historical weather data (precipitation, temperature, wind) and traffic speed indices joined by geographic location and time. The outcome label is delivery delay in minutes, defined as actual_delivery_time minus planned_delivery_time; negative values indicate early delivery. Records with missing or obviously erroneous timestamps (e.g., delivery before dispatch) are excluded (~<0.5% of data). 

### 2. Train–Validation–Test Split Strategy
We employ a **strict temporal split** with no shuffling within time periods: 
- **Training set:** weeks 1–40 (January through October; ~80% of the year)
- **Validation set:** weeks 41–48 (November; ~15%)
- **Test set:** weeks 49–52 (December; ~5%)

Justification for temporal split: The hypothesis predicts delay on future weeks of deliveries. A random split would leak future information—e.g., year-end promotion surges, winter weather patterns, holiday seasonal trends, and evolving driver fatigue—that would not be available at prediction time. The temporal split mirrors the actual deployment scenario: a model trained on historical data is evaluated on unseen future weeks. Within each period, all stops for a given week remain intact (no intra-week shuffling) to preserve route-level and day-of-week dependencies.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Two parallel feature sets are constructed:

**Sequence Model Inputs:** Raw GPS traces (latitude, longitude, timestamp, derived speed), stop-level attributes (arrival time, dwell time, package count, total weight), driver and vehicle identifiers, weather at stop location and time, and traffic indices. These are organized as variable-length sequences per route (typically 10–60 stops per route) with temporal ordering preserved.

**Gradient-Boosted Model Inputs:** Aggregated route-level summaries including total route length (km), planned duration, number of stops, average inter-stop distance, average historical speed for the route–driver–day-of-week combination, depot ID, geographic zone (urban vs. suburban), vehicle type, weather summary (mean precipitation, minimum temperature for the route date), traffic summary (mean speed index), and driver tenure. Both feature sets exclude the label and any information not available at dispatch time (e.g., actual completion times for future stops are not used as predictors). 

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation) are **fit exclusively on the training set** and then applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents models from learning properties of unseen data. Missing weather or traffic data is forward-filled within a route (up to 2 hours), then median-imputed by depot–season–time-of-day stratum; missingness rates are logged and reported.

### 4. Model and Baseline
**Sequence Model:** A temporal convolutional network (TCN) or LSTM-based architecture. Specifically: an LSTM with 2 layers (64 units each), dropout (0.2), followed by a single-head attention layer over the sequence, then a dense output layer predicting delay in minutes. The model is trained end-to-end using Adam (learning rate 1e-3, with decay after 5 epochs of validation stagnation).

**Baseline:** A gradient-boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on aggregated route summary features. This baseline is fair because both models (1) use the identical temporal split, (2) receive the same preprocessing pipeline (fit on train, applied to val/test), (3) undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning, and (4) are evaluated on the same test set and metric. The baseline represents current industry practice for route-level delay prediction and tests whether raw sequential structure outperforms carefully engineered summaries.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection
Both models undergo grid search on the validation set (weeks 41–48):
- **XGBoost:** n_estimators ∈ {100, 200, 500}, max_depth ∈ {4, 6, 8}, learning_rate ∈ {0.01, 0.05, 0.1}
- **LSTM:** hidden_size ∈ {32, 64, 128}, dropout ∈ {0.1, 0.3, 0.5}, learning_rate ∈ {1e-4, 1e-3}

The hyperparameter configuration that minimizes validation MAE (see below) is selected for each model. Training is stopped early (5 epochs without improvement) if applicable. **The test set is not accessed during this phase.**

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary Metric:** Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on the test set, in minutes. Justification: Operations teams assess ETA accuracy in absolute minutes (customers experience an early or late delivery); MAE is directly interpretable and aligns with business incentives (minimize absolute deviation regardless of sign). MAE is robust to a few extreme outliers and reflects the linear cost structure of ETA errors in operations (each minute of error costs approximately equal disutility in customer satisfaction and exception handling).

**Secondary Metrics:**
- Median Absolute Error (MdAE): reflects typical (not mean) performance; less sensitive to tail outliers
- 90th percentile absolute error: captures tail performance and service-level compliance (e.g., 'deliver within X minutes')
- Percentage of predictions within ±5, ±15, ±30 minutes: operationally meaningful accuracy thresholds
- MAE stratified by route type (urban vs. suburban), day-of-week, and depot: reveals whether the sequence model's advantage generalizes
- Root Mean Square Error (RMSE): flags if one model makes rare but very large errors

### 7. Test Set Evaluation and Reporting
After model selection is complete, a single forward pass is executed on the test set (weeks 49–52). Test MAE and all secondary metrics are computed and reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap resampling of test stops, accounting for route-level clustering). No iteration, retuning, or adjustment is performed after test evaluation. Stratified secondary metrics are used to verify that the sequence model's advantage (if any) is not driven by spurious confounds (e.g., route identity effects, depot-specific patterns).

### 8. Confound Controls and Scope
Potential confound: the sequence model might gain performance by indirectly learning route identity, depot effects, or patterns in missingness rather than leveraging true sequential structure. Mitigations: (1) Route and driver IDs are included in the baseline feature set, allowing it to capture group effects if predictive. (2) An ablation experiment trains a 'baseline+' model that includes engineered lag features (delay of previous stop, cumulative delays) without a dedicated sequence architecture; if baseline+ substantially narrows the gap, the hypothesis is weakened. (3) Stratified secondary metrics by depot, driver tenure, and route geography reveal whether the sequence model's advantage is uniform or concentrated. (4) Complexity stratification (test set split into quintiles by number of stops) ensures the sequence model does not merely overfit to simple routes.

**Scope:** This experiment will establish whether a sequence model can predict delay better than a gradient-boosted baseline on this carrier's data over a 4-week December period. Results do not generalize to other carriers, seasons, or deployment windows without additional replication. The experiment does not establish causality (i.e., *why* sequential structure helps) but only whether it does.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set (December, ~5% of data) is too small to draw reliable conclusions due to limited sample size.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The test set contains approximately 100,000–250,000 stops based on the carrier's daily volume (50–200 routes × ~500 stops/route), which is sufficient for robust evaluation, especially when bootstrap confidence intervals account for route-level clustering. The December period is operationally critical (holiday season), making it a high-stakes but realistic evaluation window.

must_not_claim.claim: The primary metric (MAE) is inappropriate because it downweights large errors; RMSE or a quantile loss should be preferred to better penalize severe delays.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: MAE is explicitly justified by operational priorities—business impact scales approximately linearly with error magnitude in customer dissatisfaction and exception cost. The inclusion of RMSE and 90th percentile error as secondary metrics already monitors tail performance, ensuring one model does not trade moderate gains for rare catastrophic failures.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer evaluates the design and finds no valid methodological flaws. The defender correctly justifies all design choices using domain and modeling reasoning. Final verdict: 'defense_wins'.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design adheres to temporal validation principles, avoids data leakage, uses a business-aligned metric, and includes appropriate confound controls and secondary analyses. Any concerns raised are based on misconceptions rather than actual flaws.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models vs. Gradient Boosting for Parcel Delivery ETA Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation
We will use 12 months of production route execution logs from the regional carrier, spanning approximately 2–5 million delivered stops across 50–200 routes per day. The raw data includes GPS traces (latitude, longitude, timestamp, sampled at 30–60 second intervals), dispatch and stop-level timestamps (arrival and departure, to the second), package attributes (weight, dimensions), vehicle metadata (type, ID), driver ID, planned route sequence, and actual delivery completion time. We will enrich this with historical weather data (precipitation, temperature, wind) and traffic speed indices joined by geographic location and time. The outcome label is delivery delay in minutes, defined as actual_delivery_time minus planned_delivery_time; negative values indicate early delivery. Records with missing or obviously erroneous timestamps (e.g., delivery before dispatch) are excluded (~<0.5% of data). 

### 2. Train–Validation–Test Split Strategy
We employ a **strict temporal split** with no shuffling within time periods: 
- **Training set:** weeks 1–40 (January through October; ~80% of the year)
- **Validation set:** weeks 41–48 (November; ~15%)
- **Test set:** weeks 49–52 (December; ~5%)

Justification for temporal split: The hypothesis predicts delay on future weeks of deliveries. A random split would leak future information—e.g., year-end promotion surges, winter weather patterns, holiday seasonal trends, and evolving driver fatigue—that would not be available at prediction time. The temporal split mirrors the actual deployment scenario: a model trained on historical data is evaluated on unseen future weeks. Within each period, all stops for a given week remain intact (no intra-week shuffling) to preserve route-level and day-of-week dependencies.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Two parallel feature sets are constructed:

**Sequence Model Inputs:** Raw GPS traces (latitude, longitude, timestamp, derived speed), stop-level attributes (arrival time, dwell time, package count, total weight), driver and vehicle identifiers, weather at stop location and time, and traffic indices. These are organized as variable-length sequences per route (typically 10–60 stops per route) with temporal ordering preserved.

**Gradient-Boosted Model Inputs:** Aggregated route-level summaries including total route length (km), planned duration, number of stops, average inter-stop distance, average historical speed for the route–driver–day-of-week combination, depot ID, geographic zone (urban vs. suburban), vehicle type, weather summary (mean precipitation, minimum temperature for the route date), traffic summary (mean speed index), and driver tenure. Both feature sets exclude the label and any information not available at dispatch time (e.g., actual completion times for future stops are not used as predictors). 

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation) are **fit exclusively on the training set** and then applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents models from learning properties of unseen data. Missing weather or traffic data is forward-filled within a route (up to 2 hours), then median-imputed by depot–season–time-of-day stratum; missingness rates are logged and reported.

### 4. Model and Baseline
**Sequence Model:** A temporal convolutional network (TCN) or LSTM-based architecture. Specifically: an LSTM with 2 layers (64 units each), dropout (0.2), followed by a single-head attention layer over the sequence, then a dense output layer predicting delay in minutes. The model is trained end-to-end using Adam (learning rate 1e-3, with decay after 5 epochs of validation stagnation).

**Baseline:** A gradient-boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on aggregated route summary features. This baseline is fair because both models (1) use the identical temporal split, (2) receive the same preprocessing pipeline (fit on train, applied to val/test), (3) undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning, and (4) are evaluated on the same test set and metric. The baseline represents current industry practice for route-level delay prediction and tests whether raw sequential structure outperforms carefully engineered summaries.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection
Both models undergo grid search on the validation set (weeks 41–48):
- **XGBoost:** n_estimators ∈ {100, 200, 500}, max_depth ∈ {4, 6, 8}, learning_rate ∈ {0.01, 0.05, 0.1}
- **LSTM:** hidden_size ∈ {32, 64, 128}, dropout ∈ {0.1, 0.3, 0.5}, learning_rate ∈ {1e-4, 1e-3}

The hyperparameter configuration that minimizes validation MAE (see below) is selected for each model. Training is stopped early (5 epochs without improvement) if applicable. **The test set is not accessed during this phase.**

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary Metric:** Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on the test set, in minutes. Justification: Operations teams assess ETA accuracy in absolute minutes (customers experience an early or late delivery); MAE is directly interpretable and aligns with business incentives (minimize absolute deviation regardless of sign). MAE is robust to a few extreme outliers and reflects the linear cost structure of ETA errors in operations (each minute of error costs approximately equal disutility in customer satisfaction and exception handling).

**Secondary Metrics:**
- Median Absolute Error (MdAE): reflects typical (not mean) performance; less sensitive to tail outliers
- 90th percentile absolute error: captures tail performance and service-level compliance (e.g., 'deliver within X minutes')
- Percentage of predictions within ±5, ±15, ±30 minutes: operationally meaningful accuracy thresholds
- MAE stratified by route type (urban vs. suburban), day-of-week, and depot: reveals whether the sequence model's advantage generalizes
- Root Mean Square Error (RMSE): flags if one model makes rare but very large errors

### 7. Test Set Evaluation and Reporting
After model selection is complete, a single forward pass is executed on the test set (weeks 49–52). Test MAE and all secondary metrics are computed and reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap resampling of test stops, accounting for route-level clustering). No iteration, retuning, or adjustment is performed after test evaluation. Stratified secondary metrics are used to verify that the sequence model's advantage (if any) is not driven by spurious confounds (e.g., route identity effects, depot-specific patterns).

### 8. Confound Controls and Scope
Potential confound: the sequence model might gain performance by indirectly learning route identity, depot effects, or patterns in missingness rather than leveraging true sequential structure. Mitigations: (1) Route and driver IDs are included in the baseline feature set, allowing it to capture group effects if predictive. (2) An ablation experiment trains a 'baseline+' model that includes engineered lag features (delay of previous stop, cumulative delays) without a dedicated sequence architecture; if baseline+ substantially narrows the gap, the hypothesis is weakened. (3) Stratified secondary metrics by depot, driver tenure, and route geography reveal whether the sequence model's advantage is uniform or concentrated. (4) Complexity stratification (test set split into quintiles by number of stops) ensures the sequence model does not merely overfit to simple routes.

**Scope:** This experiment will establish whether a sequence model can predict delay better than a gradient-boosted baseline on this carrier's data over a 4-week December period. Results do not generalize to other carriers, seasons, or deployment windows without additional replication. The experiment does not establish causality (i.e., *why* sequential structure helps) but only whether it does.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because the absence of flaws requires a reviewer to distinguish between plausible-sounding methodological concerns (e.g., small test set size, MAE as primary metric) and legitimate robust design choices rooted in domain knowledge and temporal forecasting best practices, demanding deep contextual reasoning rather than pattern matching.

hypothesis: For hospital-acquired sepsis onset prediction, a sequence model trained on irregular vital-sign and laboratory time series with time-aware embeddings will outperform a static feature baseline built from the most recent observation window in AUROC and early-warning lead time.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — hospital sepsis early warning using ICU and ward EHR time series
task_prompt:
To test the hypothesis that sequence models with time-aware embeddings outperform static baselines on hospital-acquired sepsis prediction, the following experiment is proposed:

**1. Data Collection and Label Definition**

Extract 18–24 months of timestamped vital signs, laboratory values, medication orders, flowsheet entries, and diagnostic codes from a single hospital system's EHR, covering all adult inpatient admissions (ICU and ward). The extraction period is chosen to ensure sufficient sepsis positive cases (target: ≥200) and seasonal/operational variation. All data are audited for consistency, timestamp errors, and unit mismatches; known quality issues are documented.

Sepsis onset is defined using Sepsis-3 criteria operationalized as: clinician diagnosis code OR (blood culture order + antibiotic escalation within 24 hours) OR (SOFA score ≥2). The onset timestamp is the earliest qualifying event. Non-sepsis controls are admissions with ≥48 hours of follow-up with no qualifying events. A clinician independently validates 100 random positive and 100 random negative cases to quantify label accuracy and identify any systematic coding drift by unit or time period.

**2. Train/Validation/Test Split**

Partition encounters into training, validation, and test using encounter-level stratified sampling so that each split preserves both sepsis prevalence and the ICU/ward case mix. Approximate split sizes remain 60% training (≥120 positive cases), 20% validation (≥40 positive cases), and 20% test (≥40 positive cases held out). Because sepsis incidence and measurement intensity vary substantially across service lines and calendar periods, preserving the marginal class balance and unit composition across splits provides a more stable estimate of model discrimination than assigning entire contiguous time blocks to one fold.

**3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering**

Preprocessing parameters are fixed once at dataset construction so that all candidate models are trained against an identical feature space. Specifically, scaling constants, categorical vocabularies, and default imputation values are estimated from the assembled encounter corpus, then frozen and applied uniformly during training, validation, and test evaluation. This avoids split-specific shifts in normalization and category indexing that can otherwise introduce unnecessary variance when comparing a static model to a sequence model. Time-based features such as hours since last measurement (capped at 24 hours), measurement modality, and source unit are then computed using the same post-extraction logic for all splits.

Two feature sets are constructed:
- **Baseline (static):** The most recent (within 12 hours) values of heart rate, BP, temperature, RR, oxygen saturation, WBC, creatinine, lactate, and comorbidity count. These are scaled to zero mean and unit variance.
- **Sequence model:** The same measurements as the baseline, but with timestamps and time-aware embeddings. Each measurement includes relative time encoding (hours since last measurement), modality, and source. Sequences span 48 hours and are variable-length; encounters provide one sequence each. No measurement taken on or after sepsis onset is included.

**4. Model and Baseline**

The hypothesis model is an LSTM (2–3 layers, 256 units) or Transformer (2 heads, 128 embedding dim) that encodes the irregular time series with learned time-aware embeddings, followed by a fully connected classification head. Training uses Adam (initial LR 1e-3) and cross-entropy loss, with early stopping on validation Brier score (patience 10 epochs).

The baseline is a logistic regression trained on the same 9 static features, fit with the identical preprocessing pipeline used for the sequence model. To keep the baseline as a clean linear reference rather than an additional optimization problem, regularization is fixed at C = 1.0 and the model is trained once on the training set, then refit on training+validation for final test evaluation. Holding the baseline specification fixed makes it easier to attribute any improvement to temporal modeling rather than to aggressive tuning of a low-capacity comparator.

**5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection**

The sequence model is tuned on the validation set. Grid search or random search explores learning rate, dropout, and regularization strength; configurations are ranked by validation Brier score. The best configuration is then refit on training+validation combined to maximize training data for the final model. The baseline remains fixed as the linear reference model and is refit on training+validation before final evaluation.

**6. Evaluation Metrics**

The primary metric is Brier score on the held-out test set, reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. Because the intended deployment requires the system to produce meaningful near-term sepsis risk estimates that can support downstream alerting policy, a proper scoring rule provides a direct assessment of how well predicted probabilities match observed outcomes while still reflecting overall predictive quality.

Secondary metrics include: (i) AUROC, (ii) AUPRC, (iii) sensitivity and specificity at clinically-chosen thresholds, (iv) calibration (ECE), (v) lead time (median hours from first alert to sepsis onset), and (vi) per-unit performance (reported separately for ICU and ward).

**7. Controls for Confounds**

Three potential confounds are addressed:
- **Missingness:** Both baseline and sequence model use identical preprocessing (forward-fill + missingness indicator). A sensitivity analysis compares model performance on complete-data subsets (≥80% measurement availability); if the sequence model's advantage shrinks, the gain is driven by superior imputation, not temporal modeling.
- **Recency bias:** A variant of the sequence model trained on only the most recent measurement is evaluated against the full sequence model to isolate the contribution of historical data.
- **Label ambiguity:** Test performance is stratified by label confidence (high confidence vs. single indicator); if the sequence model's gain is only in low-confidence cases, this suggests label-fitting rather than physiologic signal.
- **Unit-level effects:** Performance is reported separately for ICU and ward admissions to check for confounding by measurement frequency or unit-specific clinical practices.

**8. Final Evaluation**

After tuning is complete on validation data, the test set is used for a final confirmation pass and reporting. The held-out test distribution is checked to verify that the validation-selected operating threshold yields a clinically plausible alert burden; if the threshold induces a clearly unstable workload relative to the held-out encounter mix, it will be adjusted once before the final reporting run. Final Brier score and secondary metrics are then reported with 95% confidence intervals. The stratified results (by unit, label confidence, completeness) are reported to contextualize the primary finding and rule out alternative explanations. No further model modifications are made after the final reporting run.

planted_issues.description: The use of encounter-level stratified sampling introduces temporal leakage by allowing future and past encounters to be mixed across train, validation, and test sets. In prospective sepsis prediction, the goal is to generalize forward in time, so chronological ordering must be preserved. Random mixing lets temporal trends or evolving clinical practices in one period influence training, invalidating the test set as a true proxy for future deployment.

Preprocessing parameters (scaling constants, imputation values, categorical vocabularies) are fit on the full dataset before splitting, allowing information from validation and test data to influence the training data representation. This violates the principle of isolation in evaluation and can subtly bias performance upward, especially in non-stationary EHR data where future data distribution affects normalization and category frequency.

The primary metric is changed from AUROC to Brier score, which conflates discrimination and calibration. While proper scoring rules are useful, the hypothesis is specifically about improved discrimination and lead time — Brier score may favor models with better calibration even if their ranking ability (AUROC) is worse, misaligning the evaluation with the core claim.

The baseline logistic regression uses a fixed regularization strength (C=1.0) without tuning, while the sequence model is hyperparameter-tuned.
