### Objective
To test whether a temporal model trained on irregularly sampled vital signs, laboratory trajectories, and medication administrations outperforms a snapshot-based tabular baseline in predicting hospital-acquired bloodstream infection (BSI) within 48–72 hours of admission, with a focus on cases developing after 24 hours.

### 1. Data and Label Definition
Data are sourced from a large hospital system's de-identified EHR spanning 3–4 years, comprising ≥50,000 adult inpatient encounters. The dataset includes all timestamped vital signs, laboratory results, medication orders, and microbiology records. Known data quality issues (monitoring lapses, lab delays, variable documentation frequency) are retained as explicit missingness rather than imputed at preprocessing; temporal models are expected to handle irregular sampling natively.

The primary label is a positive blood culture result 48–5 days post-admission, adjudicated to exclude contaminants using standard microbiologic criteria. Onset time is defined as the culture draw timestamp. Cases with positive cultures within 48 hours of admission are excluded as likely community-acquired. A secondary adjudicated label (clinician-confirmed HAI from discharge summary) is used for robustness checks.

### 2. Temporal Stratified Split
Encounters are chronologically ordered by admission date and divided into three non-overlapping temporal periods: Training (60%, ≈18–24 months), Validation (20%, ≈6–9 months), and Test (20%, ≈6–9 months). Within each split, positive cases are stratified to maintain representative class ratios. This temporal split prevents leakage from future measurements and post-hoc laboratory patterns, ensuring the evaluation simulates prospective deployment. Patient-level exclusion ensures no individual appears across splits.

### 3. Feature Construction and Preprocessing
All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, missing-value indicators) are fit on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. For the temporal model, the prediction window spans from admission to infection onset (or 72 hours for negatives), preserving the full sequence of measurements with relative timestamps (hours since admission) and inter-measurement intervals. Snapshot baseline features are extracted at a fixed 24-hour post-admission window: max/min/mean vitals, most recent labs, counts of new antibiotics, and culture positivity flags. Both models receive identical information up to their decision point; the temporal model alone observes sequence and timing.

### 4. Models and Fair Baseline Comparison
The temporal model is a Transformer or LSTM encoder on the irregular time series, with learnable embeddings for timestamps and missing-value handling. Hyperparameters (layer depth, hidden dimensions, dropout, learning rate) are optimized on the validation set via grid search with early stopping on validation AUROC.

The baseline is logistic regression on snapshot tabular features, given equivalent tuning effort: L1/L2 regularization and class-weight balancing are optimized via grid search on the validation set. Both models use identical preprocessing pipelines (scaling, encoding, indicators). This ensures the comparison isolates temporal structure, not engineering or optimization rigor.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
Primary metric: AUROC, chosen because it is threshold-agnostic, appropriate for class imbalance, and clinically interpretable—it measures the model's ability to rank high-risk patients higher, enabling downstream triage decisions. Secondary metrics include AUPRC (sensitive to minority-class performance), calibration (Brier score), sensitivity at fixed specificity (e.g., 95%), and median alert lead time (hours between model alert and culture draw). All metrics are computed on the held-out test set with 95% confidence intervals.

### 6. Model Selection and Test-Set Integrity
Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping are performed exclusively on the validation set using validation AUROC. The test set is untouched until final evaluation, at which point all metrics are computed once and reported.

### 7. Confound Controls
To address the risk that temporal models exploit measurement intensity rather than physiologic signal: (1) Stratified analysis by measurement frequency tertile (vitals/day, labs/day); if gain concentrates in high-frequency settings, intensity is likely the driver. (2) Negative control: a temporal model trained on randomly shuffled timestamps should show no improvement; if it does, measurement intensity is the confound. (3) Care escalation adjustment: both models are re-evaluated with explicit escalation features (ICU transfer, new vasopressors); if temporal gain persists, signal beyond care intensity is captured. (4) Subgroup analysis: performance is separately reported for cases with infection onset >24 hours post-admission (the hypothesis target) and all cases combined.

### 8. Scope of Claims
This experiment establishes whether a temporal model discriminates better than a snapshot baseline *within its training distribution and evaluation window*. It does not establish causality, external validity to other hospital systems, or clinician adoption impact. Confound controls isolate the temporal structure effect from measurement intensity and care escalation patterns.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult due to subtle target leakage: the contaminated feature appears operationally plausible and might be used in real-time systems, but its inclusion invalidates the scientific claim. Evaluators must distinguish between operational availability and experimental integrity, requiring deep understanding of label construction and temporal causality.

hypothesis: In enterprise cybersecurity, a sequence model trained on email text plus sender-recipient graph features will detect phishing emails more accurately than a text-only bag-of-words baseline, as measured by PR-AUC on future-mail holdout data.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity: internal corporate email threat detection using production mail gateway logs and analyst-reviewed phishing labels
task_prompt:
# Experiment Design: Sequence + Graph Model vs. Text-Only Baseline for Phishing Detection

## 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses production email gateway logs spanning 18 months from a large enterprise or managed security provider. The raw dataset comprises millions of timestamped messages with metadata (sender, recipients, subject, body, headers, attachments) and directed sender-recipient relationships. Labels are obtained from security analyst investigations and incident response tickets, applied retrospectively to a subset of messages (estimated 5–15% coverage). 

We define the label explicitly: phishing = 1 if analyst-confirmed credential harvesting, business email compromise, malware delivery, or redirect attack; phishing = 0 for benign mail. Unlabeled messages are excluded from training and evaluation to avoid silent negatives. This results in a severe class imbalance (~5–10% phishing in the labeled subset), which motivates our metric choice below.

## 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The 18-month period is divided chronologically into three disjoint windows: months 1–12 (training), months 13–15 (validation), months 16–18 (test). No random shuffling is applied. 

**Rationale for temporal split:** Phishing emails exhibit strong temporal dependencies. Adversaries reuse and iterate on templates within weeks or days; a random split would mix near-identical phishing variants (e.g., two credential-harvesting campaigns from the same source, sent 3 weeks apart) across training and test. This creates artificial generalization by allowing the model to memorize template variations rather than learning robust features. The sequence model is especially prone to this via its capacity to model subtle variations in phrasing. A chronological split ensures that test phishing is temporally novel, properly evaluating generalization to future campaigns that the model has not seen.

## 3. Feature Engineering

**Text features:**
- Email body and subject are concatenated and tokenized into a fixed vocabulary established once from the labeled corpus and then frozen across temporal windows.
- Sequences are encoded as token IDs, padded or truncated to 512 tokens.
- Auxiliary TF-IDF features (unigrams and bigrams) are computed using the same fixed dictionary and provide dense input to the model.

**Graph features:**
- Sender in-degree (number of distinct outbound recipients in training period).
- Sender out-degree (number of messages sent from this sender in training period).
- Recipient in-degree (number of distinct senders in training period).
- Edge weight between sender and recipient (prior message count between pair).
- Sender clustering coefficient in the 1-hop neighborhood of the training graph.

All graph statistics are computed on the training-period graph structure. For validation/test messages, features are inherited from the training graph: known senders retain their training statistics; new external senders unseen in training are assigned minimum observed degree + 1 to reflect rarity without zero-inflation.

**Preprocessing fit point:** To keep representation drift from comparatively small holdout windows from overwhelming the architectural comparison, the tokenizer, vocabulary, TF-IDF dictionary, and imputation rules are established once from the labeled corpus before model training and then held fixed for every train/validation/test run. This keeps feature coordinates stable across time windows so that differences in performance can be attributed to the model classes rather than to shifting token coverage. Validation and test examples do not alter these transformers once they are frozen, and graph-derived features continue to use only pre-message historical interactions available at send time.

## 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed model:** A bidirectional LSTM or Transformer encoder processes the token sequence, with graph features concatenated after sequence pooling/attention. A 2-layer dense head (64 units + ReLU, then sigmoid) outputs a phishing probability. The model is trained with binary cross-entropy loss on the training set.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on TF-IDF text features alone, receiving no graph features and no sequence modeling. The baseline uses the same text preprocessing, the same train/val/test split, and equal hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search over regularization strength). This isolates the contribution of sequence + graph modeling, not superior tuning or preprocessing.

## 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Gridsearch is performed over the sequence model's hyperparameters (embedding dimension: {100, 200}, LSTM hidden size: {128, 256}, dropout: {0.3, 0.5}) and the baseline's regularization (C: {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}). Each configuration is trained on the training set and evaluated on the validation set. The configuration yielding the highest validation ROC-AUC is selected for each model. The test set remains untouched during tuning.

## 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: ROC-AUC on the test set.** ROC-AUC is chosen to measure how well each model orders phishing ahead of benign mail across heterogeneous campaign types and changing prevalence across time windows. Because deployment thresholds may vary by tenant, season, or alert budget, the primary comparison will emphasize ranking quality rather than any single operating point. Threshold selection for production will still be carried out on the validation window using an operational false-positive-rate budget.

**Secondary metrics on the test set:**
- Recall at 1% and 5% FPR: operationally relevant decision points.
- Precision at recall = 0.80: cost of high-coverage detection.
- Calibration (Expected Calibration Error): whether predicted probabilities align with true incidence, critical for threshold selection in production.

## 7. Confound Control: Template Exploitation

A known confound is that the sequence model may exploit duplicate or near-duplicate phishing templates in the training set, appearing to generalize well to the validation/test set while failing on truly novel attacks. To isolate this bias:

1. **Temporal split (primary control):** Chronological ordering prevents identical templates in test.
2. **Novelty stratification (diagnostic):** Compute approximate token-level edit distance between each test phishing and the nearest training-set phishing. Stratify test-set performance by novelty (e.g., >20% token distance = "novel"). Report separate ROC-AUC values for novel vs. template-similar test instances. A large performance gap reveals template-exploitation bias.
3. **Campaign grouping (secondary check):** Group training and test emails by campaign (similar subjects, repeated sender, time clustering). Exclude any campaign present in training from the test-set performance report. Recompute ROC-AUC on campaign-novel test phishing.

This design does not prevent template exploitation entirely—that is inherent to the task—but makes it visible and quantifies its impact on generalization.

## 8. Summary of Claims and Scope

This experiment will establish whether a sequence + graph model achieves higher ROC-AUC than text-only logistic regression on future (chronologically held-out) phishing detection. It will not establish perfect generalization to adversarially novel attacks or out-of-distribution senders. The temporal split ensures that the result reflects realistic performance on unseen campaigns, not memorization of training templates.

planted_issues.description: The text preprocessing components — including the vocabulary and TF-IDF dictionary — are fit on the full labeled corpus, which includes validation and test data. This leaks future lexical patterns into the model training phase, as phishing campaigns evolve over time. A model trained with future vocabulary knowledge may appear to perform better due to improved text representation, not due to superior modeling architecture. This invalidates the temporal integrity of the experiment.

The primary evaluation metric is ROC-AUC, which is inappropriate for this highly imbalanced phishing detection task. ROC-AUC gives misleadingly optimistic performance estimates by averaging over irrelevant high-FPR regions where false positives dominate. Operational objectives require high precision at low false positive rates, which PR-AUC captures. Using ROC-AUC as the headline metric risks selecting a model that performs poorly under real-world constraints.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is unbalanced (40%/30%/30%) and should be rebalanced or randomized to improve statistical power.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The split ratio is appropriate because it follows chronological order over real-world data accumulation, preserving temporal dynamics and campaign evolution. Balancing or randomizing would violate temporal integrity and risk data leakage, which is a greater methodological flaw.

must_not_claim.claim: The LSTM model uses pooling after sequence encoding, which may discard temporal structure important for phishing detection.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While sequence modeling retains structure during encoding, final pooling is standard in many architectures for classification. This design choice is acceptable and consistent with common practice; the corruption lies elsewhere, not in architectural specifics.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that preprocessing parameters were fit on future data, invalidating temporal boundaries, and demonstrates how this enables leakage of future lexical patterns into model training.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that vocabulary derived from validation/test data introduces knowledge of future phishing campaigns (e.g., new lures, domains, language) that the model should not have access to during training.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly challenges ROC-AUC as the primary metric, arguing that it misrepresents operational utility in an extremely imbalanced setting where false positives directly impact analyst workload.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic references the domain context — high class imbalance and low tolerance for alert overload — and explains that PR-AUC or threshold-specific precision/recall would better reflect real-world deployment needs.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies both flaws and argues that the reported result (higher ROC-AUC) may be artificially inflated by both the vocabulary leakage and the choice of metric, rendering the conclusion invalid.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: There is recognition that the two flaws compound: vocabulary leakage can inflate ROC-AUC artificially, and using ROC-AUC obscures the resulting precision degradation, leading to a misleadingly positive assessment.

# Experiment Design: Sequence + Graph Model vs. Text-Only Baseline for Phishing Detection

## 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses production email gateway logs spanning 18 months from a large enterprise or managed security provider. The raw dataset comprises millions of timestamped messages with metadata (sender, recipients, subject, body, headers, attachments) and directed sender-recipient relationships. Labels are obtained from security analyst investigations and incident response tickets, applied retrospectively to a subset of messages (estimated 5–15% coverage). 

We define the label explicitly: phishing = 1 if analyst-confirmed credential harvesting, business email compromise, malware delivery, or redirect attack; phishing = 0 for benign mail. Unlabeled messages are excluded from training and evaluation to avoid silent negatives. This results in a severe class imbalance (~5–10% phishing in the labeled subset), which motivates our metric choice below.

## 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The 18-month period is divided chronologically into three disjoint windows: months 1–12 (training), months 13–15 (validation), months 16–18 (test). No random shuffling is applied. 

**Rationale for temporal split:** Phishing emails exhibit strong temporal dependencies. Adversaries reuse and iterate on templates within weeks or days; a random split would mix near-identical phishing variants (e.g., two credential-harvesting campaigns from the same source, sent 3 weeks apart) across training and test. This creates artificial generalization by allowing the model to memorize template variations rather than learning robust features. The sequence model is especially prone to this via its capacity to model subtle variations in phrasing. A chronological split ensures that test phishing is temporally novel, properly evaluating generalization to future campaigns that the model has not seen.

## 3. Feature Engineering

**Text features:**
- Email body and subject are concatenated and tokenized into a fixed vocabulary fit on the training set only.
- Sequences are encoded as token IDs, padded or truncated to 512 tokens.
- Auxiliary TF-IDF features (unigrams and bigrams) are computed on the training corpus and provide dense input to the model.

**Graph features:**
- Sender in-degree (number of distinct outbound recipients in training period).
- Sender out-degree (number of messages sent from this sender in training period).
- Recipient in-degree (number of distinct senders in training period).
- Edge weight between sender and recipient (prior message count between pair).
- Sender clustering coefficient in the 1-hop neighborhood of the training graph.

All graph statistics are computed on the training-period graph structure. For validation/test messages, features are inherited from the training graph: known senders retain their training statistics; new external senders unseen in training are assigned minimum observed degree + 1 to reflect rarity without zero-inflation.

**Preprocessing fit point:** All vocabulary, tokenizers, TF-IDF matrices, and graph statistics are fit on the training set. Validation and test sets use only the fitted transformers; no information from future periods influences preprocessing.

## 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed model:** A bidirectional LSTM or Transformer encoder processes the token sequence, with graph features concatenated after sequence pooling/attention. A 2-layer dense head (64 units + ReLU, then sigmoid) outputs a phishing probability. The model is trained with binary cross-entropy loss on the training set.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on TF-IDF text features alone, receiving no graph features and no sequence modeling. The baseline uses the same text preprocessing (vocabulary fit on training), the same train/val/test split, and equal hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search over regularization strength). This isolates the contribution of sequence + graph modeling, not superior tuning or preprocessing.

## 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Gridsearch is performed over the sequence model's hyperparameters (embedding dimension: {100, 200}, LSTM hidden size: {128, 256}, dropout: {0.3, 0.5}) and the baseline's regularization (C: {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}). Each configuration is trained on the training set and evaluated on the validation set. The configuration yielding the highest validation PR-AUC is selected for each model. The test set remains untouched during tuning.

## 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: PR-AUC on the test set.** Precision-Recall AUC is chosen because it appropriately weights phishing detection (recall) and alert volume (precision) under severe class imbalance. Standard accuracy and ROC-AUC inflate performance in imbalanced domains. The operational objective—catch more phishing without overwhelming analysts with false positives—is directly reflected in PR-AUC.

**Secondary metrics on the test set:**
- Recall at 1% and 5% FPR: operationally relevant decision points.
- Precision at recall = 0.80: cost of high-coverage detection.
- Calibration (Expected Calibration Error): whether predicted probabilities align with true incidence, critical for threshold selection in production.

## 7. Confound Control: Template Exploitation

A known confound is that the sequence model may exploit duplicate or near-duplicate phishing templates in the training set, appearing to generalize well to the validation/test set while failing on truly novel attacks. To isolate this bias:

1. **Temporal split (primary control):** Chronological ordering prevents identical templates in test.
2. **Novelty stratification (diagnostic):** Compute approximate token-level edit distance between each test phishing and the nearest training-set phishing. Stratify test-set performance by novelty (e.g., >20% token distance = "novel"). Report separate PR-AUC values for novel vs. template-similar test instances. A large performance gap reveals template-exploitation bias.
3. **Campaign grouping (secondary check):** Group training and test emails by campaign (similar subjects, repeated sender, time clustering). Exclude any campaign present in training from the test-set performance report. Recompute PR-AUC on campaign-novel test phishing.

This design does not prevent template exploitation entirely—that is inherent to the task—but makes it visible and quantifies its impact on generalization.

## 8. Summary of Claims and Scope

This experiment will establish whether a sequence + graph model achieves higher PR-AUC than text-only logistic regression on future (chronologically held-out) phishing detection. It will not establish perfect generalization to adversarially novel attacks or out-of-distribution senders. The temporal split ensures that the result reflects realistic performance on unseen campaigns, not memorization of training templates.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because one flaw (preprocessing leakage) is subtle and couched in reasonable-sounding language about stability, while the other (metric mismatch) is context-dependent and may be overlooked without domain awareness of operational constraints in imbalanced threat detection.

task_prompt:
# Experiment Design: Transformer Model for Enterprise Email Phishing Detection

## 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 24 months of production email gateway logs from a single enterprise organization, encompassing all inbound and internal messages. Raw data includes message headers (From, To, Subject, Date, SPF/DKIM/DMARC results), body text, URLs, attachment metadata, and native gateway threat signals. Labels are obtained from the organization's SOC team: emails are marked as phishing (label = 1) if they were confirmed by an analyst as malicious, matched a known phishing campaign, or were validated through user reports and forensic investigation. Unconfirmed or low-confidence alerts are excluded to maintain label quality. This produces a highly imbalanced dataset, with positive cases typically representing 0.1–1% of all emails.

## 2. Split Strategy

To prevent information leakage and respect temporal ordering in a real-world deployment scenario, a temporal split is applied: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, and months 16–24 for the held-out test set. Critically, within the training set, a campaign-aware stratification is performed. Each confirmed phishing campaign is assigned a unique campaign ID by the SOC; these campaign IDs group related or near-duplicate malicious emails. Stratified random sampling ensures that messages from the same campaign are not split across folds—all messages in a campaign go entirely into either train or validation. This prevents the model from learning high-precision rules on campaign-specific artifacts (e.g., sender domain, repeated URLs) and then encountering the same campaign in the test set by accident. The temporal split further isolates train and test in time, reducing the influence of seasonal trends or evolving attacker tactics.

## 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, vocabulary, URL domain encoder, numerical scaler, and imputation rules) are fit exclusively on the training set and frozen before application to validation and test data. Text preprocessing is standardized across all splits: lowercasing, removal of quoted threads and signatures, replacement of URLs with a [URL] token (but preserving domain and TLD in a separate structured feature), and replacement of attachments with [ATTACHMENT]. The transformer model receives raw email body text (preprocessed) concatenated with structured graph and metadata features: sender-to-recipient network embeddings (computed via node2vec on a rolling 6-month training-period graph), sender in-degree and out-degree, and sender reputation (message volume and complaint rate from the training window). The baseline model receives TF-IDF vectors (5,000-token vocabulary from training data) computed from subject and body text, plus handcrafted features: sender domain (one-hot encoded), recipient count, SPF/DKIM/DMARC pass rates, presence of suspicious TLDs, and attachment count. Both models use identical text preprocessing to ensure fair comparison.

## 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a fine-tuned transformer (BERT or RoBERTa pretrained on general English) with a custom head: text embeddings are pooled and concatenated with graph embeddings, passed through a 2-layer MLP, and output via a sigmoid layer. The baseline is logistic regression trained on the handcrafted feature set. Logistic regression is chosen because it is interpretable, represents current practice in many security operations, and uses only shallow features—isolating the contribution of deep learning and graph features. Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning on the validation set using grid search or Bayesian optimization, both optimizing for PR-AUC. This ensures the comparison is fair and the baseline is not strawmanned.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC) on the test set. PR-AUC is chosen because phishing detection has extreme class imbalance and the operational goal is to balance detection (recall) against false positive burden (precision). Unlike ROC-AUC, PR-AUC does not reward correct rejection of the massive negative class and aligns with how SOC analysts prioritize alerts. Secondary metrics include precision at fixed recall levels (80% and 90%), ROC-AUC for transparency, and a calibration plot to verify that predicted probabilities are trustworthy for downstream ranking and thresholding.

## 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 13–15) using the primary metric (PR-AUC). Both the transformer and baseline models are tuned on this same validation data to ensure fairness. Once hyperparameters are fixed, no further adjustments are made. The test set (months 16–24) is touched exactly once, at the end, for final evaluation. Results are reported as point estimates with 95% confidence intervals.

## 7. Controls for Confounds

Several design choices mitigate confounding. Campaign-aware stratification prevents memorization of campaign-specific sender domains or URLs. Graph embeddings are fit only on training data, avoiding future information leakage. The baseline includes sender domain features, allowing post-hoc analysis: if the transformer outperforms the baseline primarily on emails from novel senders (not in training), this suggests the gain comes from learned text patterns, not sender artifacts. Manual inspection of high-disagreement cases between the two models provides qualitative evidence. The experiment establishes superior PR-AUC for the transformer model on this organization's email over the test window but does not prove generalization to other organizations or assertion that the transformer architecture alone is responsible—graph features and raw text processing are confounded with architectural choice. An ablation study would be required to isolate the transformer's contribution.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split (12 months train, 3 validate, 9 test) is unbalanced and may hurt model performance due to limited training data.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In enterprise email threat detection, attacks evolve over time and temporal correlation is strong. A temporal split is methodologically correct to simulate real deployment. The 12-month training window is sufficient for large enterprises with high email volume, and the design explicitly uses campaign-aware stratification to ensure training set diversity. Imbalance is intentional and domain-appropriate.

must_not_claim.claim: Using logistic regression as a baseline is too weak; a more sophisticated model like random forest or gradient boosting should be used to avoid trivially outperforming an underspecified baseline.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis compares a transformer-plus-graph approach to current practice in SOC environments, where interpretability and simplicity are prioritized. Logistic regression is representative of deployed baselines in security operations. Including more complex models would confound architectural improvements with implementation sophistication. The baseline includes handcrafted features and equivalent tuning, ensuring a fair comparison.

must_not_claim.claim: PR-AUC is not a standard enough metric; ROC-AUC should be primary for comparability with literature.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: PR-AUC is the appropriate primary metric in extreme class imbalance scenarios (0.1–1% positives) because it focuses on the precision-recall tradeoff under high negative skew. ROC-AUC can be misleading in such settings by rewarding correct classification of the vast majority negative class. PR-AUC aligns with operational goals: SOC analysts care about how many alerts are true positives at usable thresholds.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer approves the design after critical evaluation, recognizing that the methodology is sound and addresses all known confounds appropriately.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer correctly identifies that the temporal + campaign-aware split prevents leakage; that preprocessing is fit only on training data; that the baseline is fairly constructed; and that PR-AUC is the appropriate metric for imbalanced phishing detection.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises concerns but the defender successfully rebuts them with domain-appropriate justifications, leading to eventual approval.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Reviewer questions the temporal split length or baseline model choice, but defender explains the operational context, prevalence of temporal trends in attacker behavior, and representativeness of logistic regression in current SOC pipelines, leading to resolution in favor of the design.

difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the design is sound but contains elements that could be misinterpreted without deep domain knowledge—such as the temporal split duration, the use of logistic regression as a baseline, and the focus on PR-AUC—requiring the reviewer to distinguish between legitimate methodological concerns and valid design choices grounded in operational reality.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email threat detection, a transformer model fine-tuned on email text plus header metadata will detect analyst-reviewed phishing emails more accurately than a gradient-boosted model built on handcrafted lexical and sender-reputation features, especially on campaigns that use novel lures and spoofed domains.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity: internal corporate email threat detection using production mail gateway logs and analyst-reviewed phishing labels
task_prompt:
# Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Model for Email Phishing Detection

## 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 24 months of production mail gateway logs from a large enterprise or security vendor. Raw data comprises email subject lines, body text, sender and recipient headers, SMTP authentication results (SPF, DKIM, DMARC), URL and attachment metadata, and timestamps. Labels are derived from analyst-reviewed phishing incident data: emails are marked phishing (confirmed or high-confidence suspected) or benign (not flagged). To ensure label maturity, only emails with analyst review completed ≥7 days after receipt are included, allowing incident response workflows to reach confirmation. Emails with uncertain or conflicting labels are excluded. This results in a naturally imbalanced dataset (phishing is typically 2–5% of volume) reflecting real operational conditions.

## 2. Split Strategy

Data is partitioned using label-stratified sampling across the full 24-month corpus into training, validation, and test sets. The split preserves phishing prevalence in each partition so that model selection and final evaluation occur under comparable base-rate conditions. This is important because phishing volume can fluctuate substantially during campaign spikes or analyst-response surges; aligning class balance across splits makes precision-at-high-recall estimates more stable and reduces the chance that one split is disproportionately easy or difficult due to transient prevalence changes.

The resulting partitions are used as follows: 50% of examples for training, 25% for validation, and 25% for testing. Class distribution is verified after partitioning to confirm consistency across the three sets.

## 3. Feature and Input Design

Both models receive identical raw email text (subject + body, concatenated, capped at 2048 tokens) and headers (sender domain, authentication results, URL count, attachment count). The transformer fine-tunes on this raw text; the gradient-boosted model operates on engineered features derived from the same inputs. Engineered features include lexical cues (character and word n-grams, subject line entropy, urgency keywords), sender reputation signals (domain age, WHOIS freshness, public blacklist status, SPF/DKIM/DMARC results), and structural metadata (email length, URL/attachment counts, suspicious TLDs). By providing the same underlying signals to both models, the experiment isolates the hypothesis (transformer's semantic advantage) from differences in feature access.

All preprocessing (tokenization, vocabulary construction, domain reputation lookups, numeric scaling) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets, preventing data leakage and reflecting realistic deployment.

## 4. Model Training and Hyperparameter Selection

**Transformer:** A pretrained transformer encoder (e.g., DistilBERT) is fine-tuned on the training partition with a binary classification head. Training uses AdamW optimization with learning rate tuned over {1e-5, 2e-5, 5e-5}, batch size 32, dropout 0.2, and early stopping (patience 3 epochs, max 10 epochs). The model achieving the best precision at 95% recall on the validation set is selected.

**Baseline (Gradient-Boosted):** XGBoost or LightGBoost is trained on handcrafted features with identical hyperparameter search: tree depth 4–8, learning rate 0.01–0.1, subsample 0.7–0.9. Selection uses the same validation set and metric (precision at 95% recall), ensuring equivalent tuning effort.

This approach tests whether the transformer's richer representational capacity yields better generalization, not whether one model family is tuned more carefully than the other.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Precision at 95% recall. This reflects the operational objective: catch the vast majority of phishing (95% recall) while keeping false positives manageable for analyst triage. Precision quantifies the analyst workload (emails incorrectly flagged as phishing per email flagged). Metrics like accuracy or AUC-ROC would obscure the class imbalance; precision at fixed recall provides a fair, interpretable comparison.

**Secondary Metrics:** Recall at 90% precision (inverse perspective), F1-score, area under the precision-recall curve (AUC-PR), and false positive rate at multiple recall thresholds (90%, 95%, 99%) to understand performance across operating points.

## 6. Controls for Confounds

The experiment controls for three key confounds:

- **Feature leakage:** Both models access the same email text and headers. Sender reputation and domain features available to the gradient-boosted model are implicit in headers available to the transformer, preventing one model from having an information advantage.
- **Class-balance distortion across splits:** Stratified partitioning ensures that phishing prevalence remains consistent across training, validation, and test sets so that model selection and final comparison are not driven by a split-specific prevalence anomaly.
- **Campaign-specific overfiting:** Post-hoc analysis stratifies test performance by email properties (length, URL presence, sender domain age, campaign cohort). If the transformer's advantage is concentrated in a specific subgroup, this suggests the gain comes from memorizing campaign properties rather than semantic understanding.

## 7. Test Set and Final Evaluation

The test set is held out and evaluated exactly once after model selection is complete. Confidence intervals (95%) are estimated via bootstrap resampling. A secondary check compares performance across campaign cohorts and sender-domain-age strata within the held-out test partition to confirm that gains are not confined to a narrow subset of messages.

**Expected Outcome:** The experiment will determine whether the transformer achieves materially higher precision at 95% recall than the gradient-boosted baseline, and whether this gain holds across novel campaigns and email properties, supporting the hypothesis.

planted_issues.description: The use of label-stratified random sampling across the full 24-month period introduces temporal leakage by mixing emails from different time periods into training and test sets. This allows models to learn and exploit patterns from future campaigns during training, artificially inflating performance estimates. Since phishing tactics, sender infrastructure, and campaign signatures evolve over time, the test set should reflect unseen future conditions — which requires a chronological split.

must_not_claim.claim: The lack of stratification on class balance across splits is a flaw that undermines evaluation stability.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Class imbalance is preserved naturally through the chronological split. The sound design explicitly monitors for shifts in class prevalence post-hoc, ensuring that any meaningful drift is detected. Stratification is unnecessary and harmful in this context because it violates temporal independence, which is more critical than maintaining balanced splits.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that the stratified split introduces temporal leakage by mixing time-correlated phishing campaigns across train and test sets, violating the assumption of generalization to future unseen threats. The correct resolution is to revert to a chronological (temporal) split to ensure that models are evaluated on truly novel campaigns.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that attacker behavior evolves over time and that random splits with stratification allow models to memorize campaign-level artifacts that recur across the dataset, leading to inflated performance estimates that do not generalize to production deployment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: There is no valid defense against temporal leakage in this domain. Any argument that stratification improves evaluation reliability without addressing the consequent leakage from time-correlated examples fails to engage with the core operational requirement: detecting novel phishing campaigns.

# Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Model for Email Phishing Detection

## 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 24 months of production mail gateway logs from a large enterprise or security vendor. Raw data comprises email subject lines, body text, sender and recipient headers, SMTP authentication results (SPF, DKIM, DMARC), URL and attachment metadata, and timestamps. Labels are derived from analyst-reviewed phishing incident data: emails are marked phishing (confirmed or high-confidence suspected) or benign (not flagged). To ensure label maturity, only emails with analyst review completed ≥7 days after receipt are included, allowing incident response workflows to reach confirmation. Emails with uncertain or conflicting labels are excluded. This results in a naturally imbalanced dataset (phishing is typically 2–5% of volume) reflecting real operational conditions.

## 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Data is divided chronologically: months 1–12 for training, months 13–18 for validation, and months 19–24 for testing. A temporal (not random) split is critical because phishing campaigns and sender infrastructure evolve over time. Random splitting would allow a model to memorize sender domains, campaign templates, or authentication patterns that recur within the dataset, creating artificial leakage and inflating performance on truly novel campaigns. The chronological split ensures that both models generalize to unseen attacker tactics and infrastructure, directly addressing the operational goal: detecting novel phishing campaigns, not repeating patterns from the training era.

## 3. Feature and Input Design

Both models receive identical raw email text (subject + body, concatenated, capped at 2048 tokens) and headers (sender domain, authentication results, URL count, attachment count). The transformer fine-tunes on this raw text; the gradient-boosted model operates on engineered features derived from the same inputs. Engineered features include lexical cues (character and word n-grams, subject line entropy, urgency keywords), sender reputation signals (domain age, WHOIS freshness, public blacklist status, SPF/DKIM/DMARC results), and structural metadata (email length, URL/attachment counts, suspicious TLDs). By providing the same underlying signals to both models, the experiment isolates the hypothesis (transformer's semantic advantage) from differences in feature access.

All preprocessing (tokenization, vocabulary construction, domain reputation lookups, numeric scaling) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets, preventing data leakage and reflecting realistic deployment.

## 4. Model Training and Hyperparameter Selection

**Transformer:** A pretrained transformer encoder (e.g., DistilBERT) is fine-tuned on months 1–12 with a binary classification head. Training uses AdamW optimization with learning rate tuned over {1e-5, 2e-5, 5e-5}, batch size 32, dropout 0.2, and early stopping (patience 3 epochs, max 10 epochs). The model achieving the best precision at 95% recall on the validation set is selected.

**Baseline (Gradient-Boosted):** XGBoost or LightGBoost is trained on handcrafted features with identical hyperparameter search: tree depth 4–8, learning rate 0.01–0.1, subsample 0.7–0.9. Selection uses the same validation set and metric (precision at 95% recall), ensuring equivalent tuning effort.

This approach tests whether the transformer's richer representational capacity yields better generalization, not whether one model family is tuned more carefully than the other.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Precision at 95% recall. This reflects the operational objective: catch the vast majority of phishing (95% recall) while keeping false positives manageable for analyst triage. Precision quantifies the analyst workload (emails incorrectly flagged as phishing per email flagged). Metrics like accuracy or AUC-ROC would obscure the class imbalance; precision at fixed recall provides a fair, interpretive comparison.

**Secondary Metrics:** Recall at 90% precision (inverse perspective), F1-score, area under the precision-recall curve (AUC-PR), and false positive rate at multiple recall thresholds (90%, 95%, 99%) to understand performance across operating points.

## 6. Controls for Confounds

The experiment controls for three key confounds:

- **Feature leakage:** Both models access the same email text and headers. Sender reputation and domain features available to the gradient-boosted model are implicit in headers available to the transformer, preventing one model from having an information advantage.
- **Temporal leakage:** The chronological split ensures that recurring sender patterns or campaign signatures cannot be memorized during training and recognized in test data.
- **Campaign-specific overfitting:** Post-hoc analysis stratifies test performance by email properties (length, URL presence, sender domain age, campaign cohort). If the transformer's advantage is concentrated in a specific subgroup, this suggests the gain comes from memorizing campaign properties rather than semantic understanding.

## 7. Test Set and Final Evaluation

The test set (months 19–24) is held out and evaluated exactly once after model selection is complete. Confidence intervals (95%) are estimated via bootstrap resampling. A secondary check compares performance on the final 2 months of test data against earlier test months to confirm generalization to the most recent unseen period.

**Expected Outcome:** The experiment will determine whether the transformer achieves materially higher precision at 95% recall than the gradient-boosted baseline, and whether this gain holds across novel campaigns and email properties, supporting the hypothesis.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because stratified sampling appears methodologically sound for managing class imbalance, and the justification provided in the corrupted design sounds plausible. However, in time-evolving cybersecurity data, temporal independence is more important than class balance stability, making this a high-stakes methodological error that requires domain-specific reasoning to detect.

hypothesis: For an industrial logistics provider, a gradient-boosted model trained on raw shipment stop sequences and event timestamps will predict late delivery more accurately than a baseline using only aggregate route and carrier features, especially on newly opened lanes.
domain: Transportation / logistics — last-mile and linehaul shipment delay prediction for a parcel carrier using route planning and scan-event logs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Stop-Sequence Modeling for Late-Delivery Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 36 months of production shipment records from the parcel carrier's operational warehouse. Each record contains: origin and destination, planned and actual stop sequences with timestamps, vehicle/route assignment, scan events with event type and timestamp, weather conditions at transit points, exception codes, and service-level commitment deadline. The label is binary and unambiguous: a shipment is late if its delivery timestamp exceeds the SLA deadline for its service tier; otherwise, it is on-time. Labels are inherently available post-hoc from delivery records and do not require annotation.

Data quality will be validated by checking timestamp consistency (no non-monotonic events), scan log completeness (percentage of expected scans recorded), and missing-value patterns. Any systematic quality issues (e.g., regions with incomplete scanning) are documented and handled via stratified analysis in evaluation.

### 2. Partitioning and Stratification Strategy

The 36-month dataset is partitioned into train, validation, and test subsets using a lane-stratified allocation across the full corpus: 67% train, 17% validation, and 16% test. Stratification is applied jointly on lane (origin–destination pair), service tier, and late-delivery label so that each subset preserves the same operational mix and class balance. Because late rates vary materially across seasons and lane types, this design reduces variance in threshold selection and makes model comparisons less sensitive to the idiosyncrasies of a single calendar window.

This split strategy is especially important because the hypothesis targets performance on newly opened lanes, which can be sparse if partitioning is tied too tightly to a specific time segment. By preserving lane composition in each subset, the evaluation can compare performance across high-volume stable lanes and lower-volume emerging lanes without confounding representation effects with split composition.

For analysis, the held-out test partition is further broken into two cohorts: *test_stable* (shipments on lanes well represented in model development) and *test_new* (shipments on comparatively low-history or recently established lanes). This stratification supports direct examination of whether sequence-level modeling retains its advantage when lane-specific history is limited.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Cutoff Discipline

Two feature sets are constructed in parallel:

**Feature Set A (Gradient-Boosted Model):** Raw shipment stop sequences, encoded as fixed-length feature vectors. For each shipment, we extract: stop type (pickup, intermediate hub, delivery), dwell time at each stop, inter-stop distance and planned transit time, scan event type sequence (e.g., 'out for delivery', 'exception'), and weather at each stop region. Crucially, all features are computed from information available *at or before the dispatch decision point*. For example, scan events are windowed to the first 10% of planned transit time, ensuring no leakage from post-delivery scans. Stop sequences are encoded using learned embeddings (XGBoost's built-in categorical handling or pre-trained RNN embeddings) pooled or attention-weighted into a fixed vector.

**Feature Set B (Baseline):** Aggregate route and carrier features: total planned route distance, number of stops, carrier identifier, origin and destination hub pairs, service tier, day-of-week, and historical on-time rate for the carrier–lane pair computed from the training set only. No future information is used.

Preprocessing parameters—scaling, categorical encoding, imputation defaults, and rare-category pooling thresholds—are established once from the modeling corpus before model fitting and then frozen across train, validation, and test transformations. This keeps the feature space definition stable for low-frequency lanes, carrier codes, and route patterns that may otherwise appear inconsistently across subsets. Unseen categorical values after vocabulary construction are mapped to a reserved 'unknown' category. Time-derived features remain deterministic, and sequence aggregation windows are applied identically across all subsets.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The hypothesis model is a gradient-boosted decision tree (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on feature set A. Hyperparameters (tree depth, learning rate, L1/L2 regularization, subsampling rate) are tuned via grid or random search on the validation set, with early stopping applied using validation-set loss.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on feature set B (aggregate features only) as a deliberately lightweight operational reference. To keep the baseline interpretable and avoid tailoring a linear model too closely to a particular holdout composition, it uses a fixed regularization setting and standard class weighting rather than an extensive hyperparameter sweep. Both models apply class-weight balancing to handle imbalance (if late rate is ~10%, class weights are inverse-proportional). This comparison is intended to test whether the richer sequence features in set A justify additional model complexity relative to a simple deployable reference.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Thresholds

The primary metric is **recall at a fixed precision floor of 0.70**, computed on the test set. Rationale: operations prioritize detecting genuinely late shipments (high recall) to enable intervention (rerouting, expediting), but false alarms incur cost. A precision floor of 0.70 reflects the acceptable cost ratio. If deployment will allow threshold tuning, the primary metric is instead **AUPRC (Area Under the Precision–Recall Curve)**, which summarizes performance across all thresholds and is preferable to AUROC under class imbalance.

Secondary metrics include:
- Precision at fixed recall ≥ 0.85
- F1 score at the validation-derived optimal threshold
- Calibration: Brier score and expected calibration error (ECE) by decile, ensuring that predicted probabilities are trustworthy for intervention cost–benefit decisions
- Stratified recall, precision, and F1 by lane type (stable vs. new lanes) to reveal whether sequence-level modeling generalizes or merely memorizes training lanes

### 6. Model Selection and Validation Protocol

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation partition only. After validation selects the candidate configuration, a single confirmation pass on the held-out test partition is used to verify that the chosen threshold lands in the intended operating regime before locking the model for the final benchmark. This additional check is meant to reduce the chance that the selected intervention threshold is an artifact of validation composition rather than a stable operating point for the carrier's shipment mix.

Final test reporting is then produced once, with no iterative feature changes or repeated model redevelopment after that confirmation step.

### 7. Confound Control and Scope

**Confound 1 — Lane-specific delay history:** Both the boosted model and baseline have access to historical carrier–lane on-time rates (computed from training data). If the boosted model's advantage is merely memorizing these rates, the baseline would capture similar patterns. Stratified analysis on test_new (lanes with limited historical depth) isolates true sequence-modeling gains from lane-specific memorization.

**Confound 2 — Post-dispatch information leakage:** Feature engineering enforces a strict cutoff: all sequence features (scans, stops) are timestamped and windowed to occur before a specified point (e.g., first scan or first 10% of planned transit). This ensures no post-delivery information contaminates the feature set.

**Confound 3 — Seasonality and temporal drift:** Because late rates can vary by season, the evaluation reports stratified metrics by calendar month and lane type. This reveals whether performance degrades under differing seasonal conditions and whether any lift is concentrated on stable lanes versus lower-history lanes.

**Confound 4 — Data quality:** Data quality metrics (scan completeness, timestamp validity) are computed per split and reported. If quality differs significantly between train and test, results are interpreted conditionally.

### 8. Interpretation and Scope

This design will establish whether gradient-boosted stop-sequence features provide better late-delivery prediction than aggregate features on stable lanes (test_stable result vs. baseline). It will also reveal whether this advantage generalizes to lower-history or recently established lanes (test_new result). However, the design does not prove causation: if the boosted model outperforms the baseline, it demonstrates correlation and predictive utility, not that stop-sequence timing causally produces delays. Similarly, if performance is poor on test_new, the experiment identifies a generalization problem but does not automatically prescribe a solution (e.g., domain adaptation, retraining frequency).

planted_issues.description: The design uses a non-temporal, stratified split applied across all 36 months of data, which allows future shipments to appear in training while past shipments appear in test. This invalidates the time-series nature of the prediction task, leaking future information into training and undermining the realism of forecasting delays. For a logistics forecasting problem, evaluation must respect temporal order to reflect real deployment conditions.

Preprocessing parameters (scaling, imputation, categorical encodings) are fitted on the full dataset instead of only the training set, allowing information from validation and test data to influence the feature representation during training. This is especially damaging in low-frequency lanes and emerging carrier codes, which are part of the intended generalization test for new lanes.

The baseline model (logistic regression) is evaluated with a fixed regularization setting and no parameter tuning, while the hypothesis model undergoes full hyperparameter optimization. This creates an unfair comparison where performance gains may stem from tuning effort rather than the value of sequence features, invalidating the core hypothesis test.

The model uses a confirmation pass on the held-out test set to verify if the validation-selected threshold performs acceptably, which means the test set influences the final model configuration. This compromises the independence of test evaluation and risks overfitting, turning the test set into a de facto secondary validation set.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratification by label (late vs on-time) introduces bias or is inappropriate.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by label is a standard practice in rare-event prediction (low late-rate) to ensure sufficient minority-class examples in all splits and does not imply leakage when applied correctly. The flaw is not the stratification itself, but the temporal mixing it enables.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a fixed class-weighting scheme in the baseline suggests laziness or lack of rigor.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Fixed class weighting is a legitimate operational choice for lightweight baselines — the problem is not the simplification itself, but the asymmetry in tuning effort compared to the complex model.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of stratified metrics by lane type (stable vs new) is flawed because the split is not temporal.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratified analysis post-hoc is methodologically sound and useful; the issue lies in how the test set was formed, not how it is analyzed.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer identifies that the split methodology allows future data into training, which invalidates the temporal forecasting premise. Defender cannot legitimately justify using non-temporal splits for a real-world shipment delay prediction system where only past data is available.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer correctly points out that mixing time periods across splits violates causal order and leaks distributional information from the future.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer identifies that preprocessing is fitted on the full dataset, thus contaminating the train/test boundary. A legitimate defense is not possible because this constitutes clear data leakage, particularly impactful for rare/unknown categories central to the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer explains that preprocessing fit on test data (especially categorical encodings and imputation) leaks information and biases performance estimates, especially for emerging lanes.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer identifies that the baseline receives no hyperparameter tuning while the hypothesis model does, making the comparison invalid. The defender cannot argue that this reflects 'realistic simplicity' because the test is about predictive power of features, not deployment cost.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer demonstrates that unequal tuning effort confounds the comparison: improvement could be due to optimization, not richer features.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer flags that the 'confirmation pass' on the test set for threshold validation contaminates the final evaluation. The model's decision depends on test behavior, making the final test performance no longer independent.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer argues that any use of test set feedback during model selection — even post-validation — biases the final estimate and violates hold-out protocol.

## Experiment Design: Stop-Sequence Modeling for Late-Delivery Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 36 months of production shipment records from the parcel carrier's operational warehouse. Each record contains: origin and destination, planned and actual stop sequences with timestamps, vehicle/route assignment, scan events with event type and timestamp, weather conditions at transit points, exception codes, and service-level commitment deadline. The label is binary and unambiguous: a shipment is late if its delivery timestamp exceeds the SLA deadline for its service tier; otherwise, it is on-time. Labels are inherently available post-hoc from delivery records and do not require annotation.

Data quality will be validated by checking timestamp consistency (no non-monotonic events), scan log completeness (percentage of expected scans recorded), and missing-value patterns. Any systematic quality issues (e.g., regions with incomplete scanning) are documented and handled via stratified analysis in evaluation.

### 2. Temporal and Stratified Split Strategy

The 36-month dataset is split chronologically to prevent temporal leakage: months 1–24 form the training set, months 25–30 form the validation set, and months 31–36 form the test set. This respects the causal order: a model trained on early shipments predicts later shipments, mirroring real deployment.

Within each temporal window, data is further stratified by lane (origin–destination pair). This ensures that both high-volume, stable lanes and newly opened lanes are represented proportionally in each split. Stratification is critical because the hypothesis explicitly targets performance on newly opened lanes, where historical patterns are absent. By allocating new lanes proportionally to train, val, and test, we avoid confounding lane allocation with model capability.

The validation set (months 25–30) spans a different seasonal period than training, providing a first check for seasonal drift. The test set (months 31–36) is further split post-hoc into two cohorts for analysis: *test_stable* (shipments on lanes present in training) and *test_new* (shipments on lanes opened during months 25–36). This stratification directly tests whether sequence-level modeling generalizes to new lanes or merely interpolates from training-lane patterns.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Cutoff Discipline

Two feature sets are constructed in parallel:

**Feature Set A (Gradient-Boosted Model):** Raw shipment stop sequences, encoded as fixed-length feature vectors. For each shipment, we extract: stop type (pickup, intermediate hub, delivery), dwell time at each stop, inter-stop distance and planned transit time, scan event type sequence (e.g., 'out for delivery', 'exception'), and weather at each stop region. Crucially, all features are computed from information available *at or before the dispatch decision point*. For example, scan events are windowed to the first 10% of planned transit time, ensuring no leakage from post-delivery scans. Stop sequences are encoded using learned embeddings (XGBoost's built-in categorical handling or pre-trained RNN embeddings) pooled or attention-weighted into a fixed vector.

**Feature Set B (Baseline):** Aggregate route and carrier features: total planned route distance, number of stops, carrier identifier, origin and destination hub pairs, service tier, day-of-week, and historical on-time rate for the carrier–lane pair computed from the training set only. No future information is used.

All preprocessing—scaling, categorical encoding, imputation—is fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–24) and applied without refitting to validation and test. Unseen categorical values (e.g., a carrier appearing only in validation) are mapped to a reserved 'unknown' category fitted during training. This discipline prevents data leakage through the preprocessing step.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The hypothesis model is a gradient-boosted decision tree (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on feature set A. Hyperparameters (tree depth, learning rate, L1/L2 regularization, subsampling rate) are tuned via grid or random search on the validation set, with early stopping applied using validation-set loss.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on feature set B (aggregate features only). The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: regularization parameters (L1/L2) and class weights are tuned on the same validation set using the same primary metric. Both models apply class-weight balancing to handle imbalance (if late rate is ~10%, class weights are inverse-proportional). This ensures fair comparison: any performance gap is attributable to the richer sequence features in set A, not tuning effort or regularization.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Thresholds

The primary metric is **recall at a fixed precision floor of 0.70**, computed on the test set. Rationale: operations prioritize detecting genuinely late shipments (high recall) to enable intervention (rerouting, expediting), but false alarms incur cost. A precision floor of 0.70 reflects the acceptable cost ratio. If deployment will allow threshold tuning, the primary metric is instead **AUPRC (Area Under the Precision–Recall Curve)**, which summarizes performance across all thresholds and is preferable to AUROC under class imbalance.

Secondary metrics include:
- Precision at fixed recall ≥ 0.85
- F1 score at the validation-derived optimal threshold
- Calibration: Brier score and expected calibration error (ECE) by decile, ensuring that predicted probabilities are trustworthy for intervention cost–benefit decisions
- Stratified recall, precision, and F1 by lane type (stable vs. new lanes) to reveal whether sequence-level modeling generalizes or merely memorizes training lanes

### 6. Model Selection and Validation Protocol

Hyperparameter tuning and threshold optimization are performed on the validation set (months 25–30) only. The test set (months 31–36) is held untouched and is evaluated exactly once, after the model and threshold are finalized. This one-shot evaluation on test prevents overfitting to the test set and provides an unbiased estimate of production performance.

### 7. Confound Control and Scope

**Confound 1 — Lane-specific delay history:** Both the boosted model and baseline have access to historical carrier–lane on-time rates (computed from training data). If the boosted model's advantage is merely memorizing these rates, the baseline would capture similar patterns. Stratified analysis on test_new (new lanes with no training history) isolates true sequence-modeling gains from lane-specific memorization.

**Confound 2 — Post-dispatch information leakage:** Feature engineering enforces a strict cutoff: all sequence features (scans, stops) are timestamped and windowed to occur before a specified point (e.g., first scan or first 10% of planned transit). This ensures no post-delivery information contaminates the feature set.

**Confound 3 — Seasonality and temporal drift:** Validation (months 25–30) and test (months 31–36) span different seasons. Stratified metrics by calendar month reveal whether performance degrades under seasonal shift. Performance on test_stable vs. test_new also isolates whether lift degrades when facing new lanes, a key form of distribution shift.

**Confound 4 — Data quality:** Data quality metrics (scan completeness, timestamp validity) are computed per split and reported. If quality differs significantly between train and test, results are interpreted conditionally.

### 8. Interpretation and Scope

This design will establish whether gradient-boosted stop-sequence features provide better late-delivery prediction than aggregate features on stable lanes (test_stable result vs. baseline). It will also reveal whether this advantage generalizes to new lanes (test_new result). However, the design does not prove causation: if the boosted model outperforms the baseline, it demonstrates correlation and predictive utility, not that stop-sequence timing causally produces delays. Similarly, if performance is poor on test_new, the experiment identifies a distribution-shift problem but does not automatically prescribe a solution (e.g., domain adaptation, retraining frequency).
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because it contains multiple layered issues of varying detectability: one obvious (broken baseline), two subtle (preprocessing leakage, evaluation contamination), and one moderate (temporal leakage) that requires understanding the operational context. The corruptions interact (e.g., non-temporal splits make preprocessing leakage appear benign), increasing cognitive load for accurate evaluation.

hypothesis: In corporate email threat detection, a transformer model fine-tuned on message text plus sender-recipient graph features will detect analyst-reviewed phishing emails better than a metadata-only gradient-boosted baseline, measured by precision-recall performance on a temporally held-out test period.
