hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email threat detection, a transformer model fine-tuned on email body text plus header features will detect analyst-reviewed phishing emails with higher precision-recall AUC than a gradient-boosted model trained on handcrafted lexical and sender-reputation features, especially on campaigns that use novel lures and obfuscated language.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity: internal corporate email threat detection using production mail gateway logs and analyst-reviewed phishing labels
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Models for Enterprise Email Phishing Detection

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 24 months of production email records from the enterprise mail gateway, including raw email bodies, subject lines, sender/recipient metadata, authentication headers (SPF/DKIM/DMARC status), attachment indicators, and URL counts. Security analyst labels are derived from post-hoc triage and incident investigation, recorded at the time of analyst action. Unlabeled emails (those never reviewed by an analyst) are excluded to avoid systematic bias from analyst attention patterns. Emails that are reclassified after incident discovery use the final authoritative label, with the adjudication date recorded.

A known data quality issue is label noise and delayed adjudication: some suspicious emails are never reviewed, and some initially benign emails are reclassified as phishing after incident investigation. The design mitigates this by (a) excluding never-reviewed emails, and (b) performing a secondary validation on a manually re-reviewed subset of test-set samples to quantify label quality effects.

### 2. Split Strategy

The dataset is split chronologically rather than randomly. Training set spans months 1–14 (58%), validation set months 15–18 (17%), and test set months 19–24 (25%). This temporal split is critical because phishing tactics and lures evolve over time. A random split would allow a model trained on recent campaigns to unfairly leverage future campaign signatures when evaluated on older tactics, and would mask real-world degradation on novel campaigns. The temporal split reflects the operational deployment scenario: the model must generalize to future unseen phishing variants. This design prevents information leakage from future campaigns into the training window.

### 3. Preprocessing and Features

All preprocessing transformers (tokenizers, scalers, imputation strategies) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without retraining to validation and test sets. This prevents information leakage and reflects production deployment where the tokenizer is frozen after training.

Two feature sets are constructed for the two model arms:
- **Transformer arm:** Raw email body text (up to 512 tokens), subject line, sender domain, and recipient domain. These are tokenized using a pretrained BERT tokenizer and passed through a BERT-base sequence classification model.
- **Baseline arm:** Handcrafted lexical features (term frequency for known phishing indicators, URL count, attachment count, suspicious TLD presence), sender reputation features (domain age, SPF/DKIM/DMARC pass status, historical send volume, presence on phishing-complaint lists), and metadata (body/subject length, recipient count).

Both models receive a 'days_since_training_cutoff' feature to allow temporal trend modeling.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The transformer model is BERT-base with a sequence classification head, fine-tuned end-to-end on the 14-month training set. The baseline is a LightGBM gradient-boosted ensemble trained on the same training window with the handcrafted features. Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning: Bayesian optimization over at least 50 trials, with model selection criterion set to maximize precision-recall AUC on the validation set. This ensures a fair comparison between methods.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **Precision-Recall AUC**, chosen because the enterprise prioritizes the tradeoff between catching phishing (high recall) and avoiding analyst alert fatigue (high precision). Unlike ROC-AUC, PR-AUC is sensitive to class imbalance and emphasizes performance in the minority phishing class. Secondary metrics include precision at 90% recall (P@R90), recall at 95% precision (R@P95), and F1-score, to provide multiple operating-point estimates.

### 6. Validation Approach

Hyperparameter tuning and model selection are performed on the validation set (months 15–18) using precision-recall AUC as the selection criterion. The test set is never touched during tuning. After model selection is complete, both the final transformer and baseline are evaluated once on the test set. No threshold adjustment or feature engineering is performed based on test results.

### 7. Confound Controls

To rule out the hypothesis that the transformer is exploiting message length or campaign-specific artifacts rather than genuine semantic understanding:
- Disaggregate precision-recall curves by email body text length. Performance gains should hold across length strata; otherwise, length is a confound.
- Stratify test-set evaluation by campaign (1-week sender/subject clusters). Report per-campaign PR-AUC. Sharp degradation on novel campaigns would indicate the transformer is overfitting to campaign signatures.
- Inspect baseline feature importance to confirm lexical and sender-reputation features (not campaign identity) drive baseline performance.
- Perform secondary manual re-review of 100–500 test-set samples to estimate label noise effects on both models.

This design establishes whether the transformer achieves higher precision-recall AUC on the test set due to better semantic phishing detection, while controlling for temporal leakage, length bias, and campaign overfitting.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split (months 1–14, 15–18, 19–24) introduces bias due to uneven proportions across train/validation/test sets.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Unequal split ratios are appropriate and necessary in temporal settings where the goal is to evaluate generalization to future time periods. The proportions reflect realistic data availability and deployment constraints. The priority is chronological integrity, not balance, to prevent future information leakage and simulate real-world drift.

must_not_claim.claim: Using 'days_since_training_cutoff' as a feature introduces contamination because it depends on the training set boundary.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This feature is a valid temporal proxy that helps models adjust for concept drift (e.g., changing baseline threat rates) and is consistent with operational deployment where the model knows its training cutoff date. It does not leak label information or enable direct access to future data, and its use is explicitly non-adaptive (frozen at inference).

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges that while some design choices may appear unusual (e.g., unequal temporal split, use of time-since-cutoff), they are methodologically sound given the domain constraints. The defender successfully rebuts any criticism by citing the operational deployment context, temporal evolution of phishing campaigns, and need to avoid future leakage.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer correctly identifies that no methodological flaws exist and accepts the design as valid for testing the hypothesis.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises concerns about specific elements like preprocessing fit scope or confound controls but concedes they are properly addressed in the design upon deeper analysis. The final verdict affirms that the experiment would yield a valid comparison between transformer and gradient-boosted models.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic withdraws challenges after the defender provides domain-specific justifications for temporal splits, feature engineering choices, and robustness checks.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Models for Enterprise Email Phishing Detection

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 24 months of production email records from the enterprise mail gateway, including raw email bodies, subject lines, sender/recipient metadata, authentication headers (SPF/DKIM/DMARC status), attachment indicators, and URL counts. Security analyst labels are derived from post-hoc triage and incident investigation, recorded at the time of analyst action. Unlabeled emails (those never reviewed by an analyst) are excluded to avoid systematic bias from analyst attention patterns. Emails that are reclassified after incident discovery use the final authoritative label, with the adjudication date recorded.

A known data quality issue is label noise and delayed adjudication: some suspicious emails are never reviewed, and some initially benign emails are reclassified as phishing after incident investigation. The design mitigates this by (a) excluding never-reviewed emails, and (b) performing a secondary validation on a manually re-reviewed subset of test-set samples to quantify label quality effects.

### 2. Split Strategy

The dataset is split chronologically rather than randomly. Training set spans months 1–14 (58%), validation set months 15–18 (17%), and test set months 19–24 (25%). This temporal split is critical because phishing tactics and lures evolve over time. A random split would allow a model trained on recent campaigns to unfairly leverage future campaign signatures when evaluated on older tactics, and would mask real-world degradation on novel campaigns. The temporal split reflects the operational deployment scenario: the model must generalize to future unseen phishing variants. This design prevents information leakage from future campaigns into the training window.

### 3. Preprocessing and Features

All preprocessing transformers (tokenizers, scalers, imputation strategies) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without retraining to validation and test sets. This prevents information leakage and reflects production deployment where the tokenizer is frozen after training.

Two feature sets are constructed for the two model arms:
- **Transformer arm:** Raw email body text (up to 512 tokens), subject line, sender domain, and recipient domain. These are tokenized using a pretrained BERT tokenizer and passed through a BERT-base sequence classification model.
- **Baseline arm:** Handcrafted lexical features (term frequency for known phishing indicators, URL count, attachment count, suspicious TLD presence), sender reputation features (domain age, SPF/DKIM/DMARC pass status, historical send volume, presence on phishing-complaint lists), and metadata (body/subject length, recipient count).

Both models receive a 'days_since_training_cutoff' feature to allow temporal trend modeling.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The transformer model is BERT-base with a sequence classification head, fine-tuned end-to-end on the 14-month training set. The baseline is a LightGBM gradient-boosted ensemble trained on the same training window with the handcrafted features. Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning: Bayesian optimization over at least 50 trials, with model selection criterion set to maximize precision-recall AUC on the validation set. This ensures a fair comparison between methods.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **Precision-Recall AUC**, chosen because the enterprise prioritizes the tradeoff between catching phishing (high recall) and avoiding analyst alert fatigue (high precision). Unlike ROC-AUC, PR-AUC is sensitive to class imbalance and emphasizes performance in the minority phishing class. Secondary metrics include precision at 90% recall (P@R90), recall at 95% precision (R@P95), and F1-score, to provide multiple operating-point estimates.

### 6. Validation Approach

Hyperparameter tuning and model selection are performed on the validation set (months 15–18) using precision-recall AUC as the selection criterion. The test set is never touched during tuning. After model selection is complete, both the final transformer and baseline are evaluated once on the test set. No threshold adjustment or feature engineering is performed based on test results.

### 7. Confound Controls

To rule out the hypothesis that the transformer is exploiting message length or campaign-specific artifacts rather than genuine semantic understanding:
- Disaggregate precision-recall curves by email body text length. Performance gains should hold across length strata; otherwise, length is a confound.
- Stratify test-set evaluation by campaign (1-week sender/subject clusters). Report per-campaign PR-AUC. Sharp degradation on novel campaigns would indicate the transformer is overfitting to campaign signatures.
- Inspect baseline feature importance to confirm lexical and sender-reputation features (not campaign identity) drive baseline performance.
- Perform secondary manual re-review of 100–500 test-set samples to estimate label noise effects on both models.

This design establishes whether the transformer achieves higher precision-recall AUC on the test set due to better semantic phishing detection, while controlling for temporal leakage, length bias, and campaign overfitting.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately challenging because several correct design choices (temporal split, time-since-cutoff feature, exclusion of unlabeled data) may appear suspicious to reviewers who default to IID assumptions or balanced design norms. Success requires domain-aware reasoning about operational deployment and temporal nonstationarity in cybersecurity.

hypothesis: For enterprise software support tickets, a transformer-based multi-label classifier that uses the full ticket body plus agent conversation history and product metadata will outperform a bag-of-words baseline on rare issue-tag assignment, especially under temporal holdout evaluation.
domain: Enterprise customer support analytics for a B2B SaaS platform with ticketing, live-chat, and product metadata
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Multi-Label Classifier for Support Ticket Tagging

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis
The hypothesis states that a transformer-based classifier using full ticket body, conversation history, and metadata will outperform a bag-of-words baseline on rare issue-tag assignment under held-out evaluation. We will test this by comparing macro-averaged F1 scores on a held-out test set, with particular attention to tail-class performance.

### 2. Data Collection and Preparation
We will use 36 months of production support tickets from the B2B SaaS platform. Each ticket includes the body text, the full agent-customer conversation history with timestamps, account tier, product area, and multi-label tags assigned by agents. Tickets are filtered to include only those with complete conversation history and at least one assigned tag. We will canonicalize the tag taxonomy to remove synonyms and flatten hierarchical relationships to leaf-level labels, resulting in approximately 80–150 distinct labels.

No crowdsourced relabeling is performed. We use agent-assigned tags as ground truth, accepting the noise this introduces because it reflects real operational conditions. Post-resolution metadata (resolution codes, time-to-resolution, outcomes) are explicitly excluded from the feature set to prevent label leakage.

### 3. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy
We partition tickets into training, validation, and test sets using a label-stratified split across the full 36-month corpus, preserving the marginal frequency of both common and tail tags in each partition:
- **Training**: approximately 24 months' worth of ticket volume
- **Validation**: approximately 6 months' worth of ticket volume
- **Test**: approximately 6 months' worth of ticket volume

This split design is important for the multi-label setting because many rare tags occur sporadically rather than uniformly over time. Preserving their prevalence across all three partitions makes validation macro F1 more stable, avoids label-dropout in the held-out sets, and supports a like-for-like comparison between the transformer and bag-of-words baseline on the same tail-label mix.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
The input to the model is a concatenated sequence: [ticket body] + [agent conversation history], truncated to 1024 tokens and tokenized using a pretrained tokenizer (e.g., DistilBERT). This length cap prevents an artificial advantage from extremely long tickets while preserving the key benefit of the transformer: handling longer context than a typical bag-of-words model.

Metadata features are encoded as token-level embeddings: account tier (ordinal 0–3), product area (one-hot, ~12 categories), and ticket age in days (normalized). These metadata features are included in both the model and baseline to ensure fair comparison and to control for the confound that gains might simply come from better use of metadata.

All preprocessing—tokenizer vocabulary fitting, metadata normalization, and sequence length decisions—is fit on the training set only and applied consistently to validation and test sets.

### 5. Model Architecture and Baseline
**Proposed Model**: A pretrained transformer (DistilBERT or RoBERTa) with a multi-label classification head: dense layer (256 units, ReLU) → dropout (0.2) → sigmoid output (one neuron per label). The model is fine-tuned end-to-end using binary cross-entropy loss with per-label class weights to upweight rare tags. Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set: learning rate (1e-5 to 5e-5), batch size 32, warmup 500 steps, up to 10 epochs with early stopping.

**Baseline**: Bag-of-words logistic regression with L2 regularization. Input is a TF-IDF vector computed on the concatenation of ticket body and agent history (max features 10,000, min_df 5, max_df 0.8), augmented with the same metadata features as the model. The baseline is tuned with equivalent effort: regularization strength C and class weight balancing are optimized via grid search on the validation set. Both model and baseline use binary cross-entropy loss with class weights, ensuring the comparison is fair on the loss function and optimization setup.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary Metric**: Macro-averaged F1 score on the test set. This metric gives equal weight to rare and frequent tags, directly aligning with the operational goal of correctly identifying uncommon high-severity issues. The decision threshold for each label is fixed at 0.5 (default sigmoid); no threshold tuning occurs post-training.

**Secondary Metrics**:
- Macro-averaged recall (per-label recall, unweighted) to assess whether rare tags are actually detected.
- Per-label F1 for the 10 rarest tags on the training set, computed on the test set, to isolate tail-class performance.
- Hamming loss as a sanity check on overall correctness.
- Expected calibration error (ECE) on the test set to verify that sigmoid probabilities are reliable for downstream routing logic.

### 7. Model Selection and Validation Protocol
During training, we monitor validation macro F1 and select the model checkpoint with the highest validation macro F1. Early stopping is applied if validation loss does not improve for 3 epochs. No hyperparameter tuning or model selection occurs on the test set; the test set is evaluated exactly once at the end.

### 8. Confound and Bias Controls
To isolate semantic understanding from other sources of advantage:

1. **Metadata Control**: Both model and baseline receive identical metadata features; any difference in performance cannot be attributed to better metadata use.

2. **Text Length**: Sequence length is capped at 1024 tokens to prevent artificial advantages from extremely long tickets, while preserving the transformer's ability to handle longer context than bag-of-words.

3. **Leakage Prevention**: Post-resolution metadata are explicitly excluded from features.

4. **Stratification Check**: Macro F1 is computed separately by account tier and product area on the test set. Uniform performance across these groups indicates the model is not simply memorizing tier or product associations.

5. **Frequency Breakdown**: Performance is reported by label frequency quartile (rarest 25%, next 25%, etc.) on the test set. This reveals whether transformer gains are real on rare tags or concentrated on frequent tags where both models already perform well.

### 9. Expected Outcomes and Limitations
If the transformer achieves higher macro F1 than the baseline on the test set, particularly on rare tags, the hypothesis is supported: the model's ability to capture long-range dependencies and leverage pretrained semantic knowledge provides a real advantage for this task. If performance is similar, the hypothesis is not supported.

This design does not establish causality or explain *why* one model outperforms another; it only measures whether the transformer's operational performance is superior. Class imbalance and tag noise remain, and the results apply specifically to this platform's support workflow and tag taxonomy.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a label-stratified random split across all 36 months of data instead of a chronological split. This allows tickets from future time periods to appear in training while also having their labels represented in validation and test, enabling the model to learn temporal patterns, product updates, or policy changes not yet present in earlier data. Since the hypothesis emphasizes performance under temporal holdout evaluation—meant to simulate real-world deployment on future tickets—this split invalidates the evaluation by permitting information from the future to leak into the training process.

must_not_claim.claim: The validation and test sets are too small—only 6 months of data each—to provide stable macro-F1 estimates, especially for rare tags.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Six months of data per set is sufficient given the high volume of ongoing support tickets in a B2B SaaS environment. The original design explicitly accounts for rare tag stability through chronological split structure and frequency reporting, not artificial balancing. In real deployment, models must generalize to naturally occurring test distributions, not artificially enriched ones.

must_not_claim.claim: Using agent-assigned labels introduces too much noise and invalidates the ground truth.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The operational requirement is to predict the agent-assigned tagging behavior under real conditions, not a hypothetical perfect label. Accepting this noise aligns with the business goal of improving routing and response time based on current human practices. Relabeling would reflect an alternative objective, not the stated hypothesis.

acceptable_resolutions.description: A correct critique identifies that the label-stratified random split enables temporal leakage, undermining the 'temporal holdout evaluation' central to the hypothesis. The final verdict must be that this design flaw invalidates the claim of future generalization, and the experiment must be redone with a chronological split.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer points out that mixing time periods in train/validation/test allows the model to learn from future language, product features, or support policies that were not yet introduced during earlier periods, violating the deployment assumption.

### 7. Model Selection and Validation Protocol

### 8. Confound and Bias Controls
To isolate semantic understanding from other sources of advantage:

1. **Metadata Control**: Both model and baseline receive identical metadata features; any difference in performance cannot be attributed to better metadata use.

2. **Text Length**: Sequence length is capped at 1024 tokens to prevent artificial advantages from extremely long tickets in later time periods, while preserving the transformer's ability to handle longer context than bag-of-words.

3. **Leakage Prevention**: Post-resolution metadata are explicitly excluded from features.

4. **Stratification Check**: Macro F1 is computed separately by account tier and product area on the test set. Uniform performance across these groups indicates the model is not simply memorizing tier or product associations.

This design does not establish causality or explain *why* one model outperforms another; it only measures whether the transformer's operational performance is superior. Class imbalance and tag noise remain, and the results apply specifically to this platform's support workflow and tag taxonomy.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because label-stratified sampling appears methodologically sound for multi-label stability and is commonly used in static benchmarks. Correct evaluation requires understanding that the hypothesis specifically demands temporal holdout, making future leakage a critical violation even if the metric appears stable.

hypothesis: For a large food-delivery platform, a sequence model trained on raw order timestamps, basket composition, delivery ETA, and restaurant-level context will predict next-day courier no-show risk more accurately than a baseline using only aggregated shift-level summary features, with the largest gains during weather-disrupted and weekend peaks.
domain: Transportation / logistics — courier workforce reliability prediction for a food-delivery marketplace using dispatch, shift, and delivery operational logs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models for Courier No-Show Prediction

### 1. Data and Label Strategy

This experiment uses 18 months of production courier dispatch logs from the food-delivery platform, spanning all operational regions and shift types. The data includes courier shift assignments, accepted order metadata (distance, restaurant, ETA, basket composition), delivery outcomes, restaurant-level context (cuisine, prep time), and external signals (weather by zone and hour, day-of-week, demand aggregates, local events).

The no-show label is defined deterministically: an assignment is a no-show if it was accepted AND the scheduled pickup window elapsed AND no delivery was completed AND the courier did not cancel before the window closed. Assignments cancelled by the platform or customer are excluded. This definition is applied uniformly across regions and shift types.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To respect the temporal structure of the problem and prevent leakage of future information into training, we use a strict temporal split:
- **Training:** Months 1–12 (12 months, ~70% of data)
- **Validation:** Months 13–15 (3 months, ~15% of data)
- **Test:** Months 16–18 (3 months, ~15% of data)

Within each temporal window, assignments are stratified by region and shift type (peak vs. off-peak, weekend vs. weekday) to ensure representative coverage across operational conditions. This temporal split is essential because courier no-show is a real-time prediction problem; the model must generalize to future operational states (future weather, demand, courier availability) not seen during training. A random split would leak information from the future into the training set, artificially inflating reported performance.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Sequence model input:** For each courier-shift, we construct a sequence of the last N=10 accepted assignments in chronological order. For each assignment in the sequence, we extract: time-since-previous-assignment, delivery distance, promised ETA, restaurant prep-time estimate, basket size category, cuisine category, zone ID, hour-of-day (cyclic encoding), and day-of-week. We append static shift context: cumulative assignments completed, total shift duration, shift type, zone-level demand decile, zone-level weather (temperature, precipitation, wind speed), and local event flags.

**Baseline features:** Aggregated summary statistics computed over the shift-to-date (mean, std, max, min of delivery distances, ETAs, prep times; shift duration; zone ID; weather at shift start; shift type; day-of-week; cuisine mix). The baseline feature set is matched in recency (all shift-to-date) but lacks sequence order and inter-assignment timing.

**Preprocessing:** All normalization (z-score scaling of continuous features), categorical encoding (one-hot or ordinal), and imputation (forward-fill weather/demand within zone, then overall mean) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–12) and applied identically to validation and test sets. No parameters derived from validation or test data are used during development.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed model:** A 2-layer LSTM/GRU encoder (64 units per layer, dropout=0.3) processes the sequence of 10 assignments; the final hidden state is concatenated with static shift context; a 2-layer MLP (128→64 units, ReLU, dropout=0.2) processes the concatenated vector; sigmoid output for binary classification.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on aggregated shift-level summary features. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search (regularization strength, class weight balancing) is performed on the validation set using the same search budget as the sequence model. Both models use class-weight balancing to handle the rare-event nature of no-shows (~5–10% incidence).

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, layer size, dropout, batch size, sequence length) is performed on the validation set (months 13–15) using PR-AUC as the selection metric. Early stopping can be applied within a hold-out portion of validation data, but the final selection metric is PR-AUC on the full validation set. The test set is held out and accessed exactly once at the end for final evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: PR-AUC.** Justification: No-show events are rare, and the operations team can only act on a limited fraction of high-risk assignments in any dispatch window. The most relevant question is therefore how concentrated true no-shows are among the model's top-ranked predictions, rather than how well the model separates all positives and negatives across the full score range. PR-AUC directly captures that concentration in the actionable portion of the ranking.

**Secondary metrics:** AUROC (to measure overall ranking stability), sensitivity at 90% specificity (to cap false alarms), and expected calibration error (to validate probability estimates). Performance is also stratified by region, shift type, and weather condition to test whether gains concentrate in weather-disrupted and weekend peaks, as hypothesized.

### 7. Confound Controls

- **Geography:** Evaluation is stratified by region; uniform improvements across zones support true sequence learning, not zone-specific artifacts.
- **Seasonality/drift:** The temporal split naturally controls for seasonal effects; test-set performance on months 16–18 measures robustness to future conditions.
- **Feature parity:** The baseline receives all static and aggregated features available to the sequence model, isolating the effect of sequence representation.
- **Class imbalance:** Both models use class-weight balancing.
- **Label consistency:** The deterministic no-show rule is applied uniformly across all data.

### 8. Claims and Scope

This design will establish whether a sequence model with access to assignment-level timing, distance, and ETA information outperforms an aggregated baseline on PR-AUC for next-day no-show prediction in held-out future data. It will not establish whether the sequence model captures true temporal dependencies (as opposed to simply benefiting from richer features) without additional ablation studies; that is left to future work.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses PR-AUC as the primary metric and selection criterion, but this choice misaligns with the operational goal of flexible thresholding under shifting class distributions over time. Because PR-AUC is highly sensitive to the prevalence of positive cases, performance comparisons across temporally separated validation and test periods (e.g., months 13–15 vs. 16–18) can reflect shifts in no-show rates rather than true model ranking ability. This undermines the validity of conclusions about generalization to future conditions.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is too long and doesn't represent real-world deployment cycles, making it unrealistic.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The 12-month training window is justified by the need to capture seasonal patterns and long-term operational trends; the 6-month future horizon (validation + test) allows robust assessment of generalization to unseen future states. Temporal splits in logistics forecasting often use extended windows to reflect real drift patterns.

must_not_claim.claim: Using class-weight balancing unfairly advantages the models and distorts probability calibration.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Class weighting is appropriate and common for rare-event prediction tasks like no-shows (~5–10% prevalence). It prevents the model from degenerating to a trivial 'all-zero' predictor while preserving probabilistic outputs when combined with proper calibration checks.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that PR-AUC is an inappropriate primary metric due to its sensitivity to class distribution shifts over time, and correctly argues that AUROC better supports cross-temporal comparison and threshold-agnostic model selection. The defender fails to rebut this, as no operational or statistical justification can overcome the metric's instability under distribution shift in this context.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that PR-AUC values can vary significantly between validation and test sets due to changes in base no-show rate, even if ranking quality is stable, thus confounding performance interpretation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic acknowledges that PR-AUC is valuable for understanding high-precision regimes but correctly insists that it should be a secondary, not primary, metric when the evaluation design involves temporal generalization to potentially shifting prevalence. The verdict requires downgrading PR-AUC to secondary status and restoring AUROC as the primary metric for model selection and comparison.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that the operational need for consistent cross-time model evaluation requires a prevalence-invariant metric, and that PR-AUC's usefulness for top-k ranking does not outweigh its instability in this longitudinal setup.

We append static shift context: cumulative assignments completed, total shift duration, shift type, zone-level demand decile, zone-level weather (temperature, precipitation, wind speed), and local event flags.

**Baseline features:** Aggregated summary statistics computed over the shift-to-date (mean, std, max, min of delivery distances, ETAs, prep times; shift duration; zone ID; weather at shift start; shift type; day-of-week; cuisine mix). The baseline feature set is matched in recency (all shift-to-date) but lacks sequence order and inter-assignment timing.

**Preprocessing:** All normalization (z-score scaling of continuous features), categorical encoding (one-hot or ordinal), and imputation (forward-fill weather/demand within zone, then overall mean) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–12) and applied identically to validation and test sets. No parameters derived from validation or test data are used during development.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed model:** A 2-layer LSTM/GRU encoder (64 units per layer, dropout=0.3) processes the sequence of 10 assignments; the final hidden state is concatenated with static shift context; a 2-layer MLP (128→64 units, ReLU, dropout=0.2) processes the concatenated vector; sigmoid output for binary classification.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on aggregated shift-level summary features. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search (regularization strength, class weight balancing) is performed on the validation set using the same search budget as the sequence model. Both models use class-weight balancing to handle the rare-event nature of no-shows (~5–10% incidence).

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, layer size, dropout, batch size, sequence length) is performed on the validation set (months 13–15) using AUROC as the selection metric. Early stopping can be applied within a hold-out portion of validation data, but the final selection metric is AUROC on the full validation set. The test set is held out and accessed exactly once at the end for final evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: AUROC.** Justification: No-show events are rare; accuracy is not informative. The operations team's goal is to rank courier-shifts by risk for targeted intervention, not to produce hard classifications. AUROC measures separation across all thresholds and is robust to class imbalance. Threshold selection is deferred post-hoc based on intervention capacity.

**Secondary metrics:** PR-AUC (for minority-class focus), sensitivity at 90% specificity (to cap false alarms), and expected calibration error (to validate probability estimates). Performance is also stratified by region, shift type, and weather condition to test whether gains concentrate in weather-disrupted and weekend peaks, as hypothesized.

### 7. Confound Controls

- **Geography:** Evaluation is stratified by region; uniform improvements across zones support true sequence learning, not zone-specific artifacts.
- **Seasonality/drift:** The temporal split naturally controls for seasonal effects; test-set performance on months 16–18 measures robustness to future conditions.
- **Feature parity:** The baseline receives all static and aggregated features available to the sequence model, isolating the effect of sequence representation.
- **Class imbalance:** Both models use class-weight balancing.
- **Label consistency:** The deterministic no-show rule is applied uniformly across all data.

### 8. Claims and Scope

This design will establish whether a sequence model with access to assignment-level timing, distance, and ETA information outperforms an aggregated baseline on AUROC for next-day no-show prediction in held-out future data. It will not establish whether the sequence model captures true temporal dependencies (as opposed to simply benefiting from richer features) without additional ablation studies; that is left to future work.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because PR-AUC is often recommended for rare events, but the critical issue—its instability under temporal distribution shift—is context-dependent and requires understanding of both metric properties and the longitudinal evaluation design. This demands nuanced reasoning beyond pattern matching.

hypothesis: For enterprise IT support ticket triage, a transformer model fine-tuned on ticket text plus thread context and knowledge-base article retrieval scores will predict the correct issue category better than a baseline using bag-of-words text features and metadata alone, especially on rare categories.
domain: Enterprise IT support / internal knowledge management for a large software company with service desk tickets, chat/email threads, and linked knowledge-base articles
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Ticket Triage vs. Bag-of-Words Baseline

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation

This experiment uses 24 months of historical support tickets from the company's service desk system. We extract all tickets with complete labels assigned within 90 days of creation (to filter ambiguous or reworked tickets), resulting in approximately 200k–400k records. Each ticket includes the initial description, full email/chat thread, linked knowledge-base article IDs with retrieval scores, device and application metadata, and the agent-assigned category label. We document the category distribution and identify any highly imbalanced or rare classes (e.g., categories comprising <1% of tickets).

### 2. Temporal Stratified Split Strategy

To prevent information leakage and test realistic generalization, we employ a **temporal stratified split**:

- **Training set**: Months 1–14 (70% of data)
- **Validation set**: Months 15–18 (15% of data)  
- **Test set**: Months 19–24 (15% of data)

Within each time window, we stratify by category to preserve the class distribution in each split. This split reflects the deployment scenario: the model is trained on past tickets and must generalize to future tickets. It captures temporal drift (changes in category prevalence, language, or product releases) and ensures rare categories are represented in each split. Records are ordered by creation timestamp; no random shuffling occurs across time boundaries.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

We compare two feature groups:

**Feature Group A (Transformer input):**
- Concatenated ticket description + full thread text, tokenized via BPE to a 512-token limit (truncating from the end, as early context is most informative).
- Knowledge-base article retrieval scores: top-3 linked article IDs are encoded as learnable embeddings; retrieval confidence scores are normalized to [0, 1].

**Feature Group B (Baseline input):**
- Bag-of-words: TF-IDF representation of ticket text (unigrams + bigrams), vocabulary limited to the 5,000 most frequent terms from the training set.
- Metadata: device OS, application version, environment (one-hot encoded).
- Knowledge-base presence: binary indicator of whether ≥1 article was linked.

All preprocessing transformers (tokenizer vocabulary, TF-IDF statistics, numerical scaling) are **fit exclusively on the training set** and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage. No workflow-dependent features (agent ID, assignment team, resolution time) are included, as these would not be available at triage time.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer Model:** We fine-tune a pretrained DistilBERT encoder end-to-end on Feature Group A with a classification head for multi-class prediction. Training runs for 3 epochs with early stopping based on validation macro F1. Hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, warmup steps) are tuned via grid search.

**Baseline Model:** Logistic regression trained on Feature Group B, with L2 regularization and class-weight balancing. The baseline is tuned with equivalent hyperparameter search effort (grid search over regularization strength and class weights) using the same validation metric. This ensures a fair comparison: both models receive equal tuning investment and are evaluated under identical conditions.

The hypothesis is tested by comparing the transformer (on Feature Group A) to the baseline (on Feature Group B). If the transformer outperforms the baseline on the test set, we attribute the gain to richer context and pre-trained representations, not simply to different input features or tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Strategy

**Primary Metric: Macro-averaged F1 Score**

Macro F1 treats each category equally regardless of class frequency, directly measuring performance on rare but operationally critical categories. This aligns with the stakeholder's objective: reducing misrouting on all category types, especially those underrepresented in the dataset.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- Weighted F1 (F1 weighted by class support) — ensures overall quality on high-volume categories is not sacrificed.
- Per-category F1, precision, and recall — to diagnose performance disparities and identify problem categories.
- Micro-accuracy — standard benchmark.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

During development, we perform all hyperparameter tuning and model selection on the **validation set (months 15–18)** using macro F1. The best checkpoint is selected based on validation performance.

The **test set (months 19–24)** is held-out and evaluated exactly once after tuning is complete. No further optimization, threshold adjustment, or post-hoc analysis occurs on test data. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap resampling to account for variance, especially in rare classes.

### 7. Confound Controls

- **Metadata leakage:** Features are restricted to pre-triage signals (description, thread, KB scores, device metadata); workflow fields are excluded.
- **KB article causality:** KB retrieval scores (external signals) are used instead of agent-selected article links, avoiding reverse causality.
- **Temporal drift:** Stratified splits and temporal ordering ensure validation and test sets reflect future conditions; per-category performance is tracked over time windows.
- **Context availability:** The design assumes thread and KB scores are available at triage time; this assumption is documented.

### 8. Interpretation and Scope

If the transformer achieves higher macro F1 on the test set, the hypothesis is supported: pre-trained representations and richer context improve rare-category prediction. If performance is equivalent, the hypothesis is rejected; the added complexity of a transformer does not justify its use. Per-category analysis will reveal whether gains are uniform or concentrated in specific categories, informing deployment decisions.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split creates insufficient training data and risks overfitting due to a long training window (months 1–14) compared to shorter validation and test sets.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The 70-15-15 split is intentional and realistic: it reflects the deployment scenario where large volumes of historical data are used to train a model that must generalize to future tickets. The longer training window accommodates the need for sufficient data to train a transformer, while preserving chronological order prevents leakage. Stratification ensures rare categories are preserved across splits, making this design methodologically sound.

must_not_claim.claim: Using macro F1 as the primary metric is inappropriate because it gives equal weight to very rare categories that may not be operationally significant.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The stakeholder explicitly prioritizes reducing misrouting on rare but critical categories (e.g., security or licensing issues), even if they are infrequent. Macro F1 directly aligns with this operational goal by penalizing poor performance on underrepresented classes, whereas micro-accuracy or weighted F1 would let those be ignored. This design correctly centers stakeholder needs.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model (logistic regression on bag-of-words) is too weak to make the comparison meaningful, making any superior performance of the transformer trivial.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline is intentionally chosen to reflect a plausible, real-world triage system currently in use at the company. Logistic regression with TF-IDF and metadata is interpretable, fast, and commonly deployed. It is also given equivalent tuning effort and evaluation conditions, ensuring a fair and operationally relevant comparison. The hypothesis is not whether transformers are better than all possible baselines, but whether they improve performance over the current likely practice.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewers assess the design and determine there are no valid flaws. The temporal split, preprocessing isolation, confound controls, metric choice, and baseline are all appropriate for the hypothesis and domain. The defender can successfully justify all design decisions based on stakeholder needs and methodological rigor.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design uses a temporal stratified split that prevents leakage, evaluates generalization to future tickets, selects a stakeholder-aligned metric (macro F1), employs a fair and realistic baseline with equal tuning, and controls for key confounds such as metadata leakage and reverse causality from KB article selection.

acceptable_resolutions.description: A reviewer raises concerns about design choices, but those concerns are unfounded upon deeper analysis that considers the operational context. The defender successfully rebuts the critique by citing stakeholder objectives, data constraints, or standard ML best practices.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Critiques are based on generic heuristics (e.g., 'always use 80-10-10 splits' or 'macro F1 is dangerous') without considering the specific hypothesis about rare categories or the longitudinal nature of the data. The defender correctly grounds their response in domain-specific reasoning.

hypothesis: For enterprise IT support, a semantic search model that fine-tunes a dual-encoder transformer on historical ticket-to-article click and resolution pairs will retrieve the correct knowledge-base article more accurately than a TF-IDF/BM25 baseline, especially for short or paraphrased incident descriptions.

task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Semantic Search vs. BM25 for Enterprise IT Support

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 18–24 months of production support ticket data from the enterprise's ticketing system. The raw data consists of:
- Ticket descriptions and timestamped agent notes
- Timestamped article opens and explicit citations recorded during ticket triage
- Ticket resolution status and time-to-resolution
- A versioned knowledge base of troubleshooting articles (with publication and retirement dates)

Ground truth labels are constructed as follows: a ticket–article pair is a **positive example** if the agent opened or explicitly cited that article during triage **and** the ticket resolved within 24 hours (indicating the article likely aided resolution). Pairs where articles were opened but not formally cited are treated as **weak positives** (lower loss weight) to account for indirect usage patterns. Pairs are **negative** if the ticket resolved without referencing the article. Articles that are deprecated or archived after their test-window creation are excluded from test-set evaluation to avoid penalizing retrieval of obsolete content.

This labeling acknowledges the noisy reality of support workflows while preserving a measurable ground truth grounded in agent behavior.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Because the knowledge base and query vocabulary evolve with product releases and renamed features, a **temporal split is essential**. Data are split by ticket creation date:
- **Training set:** Months 1–12
- **Validation set:** Months 13–15  
- **Test set:** Months 16–18

This split prevents future information (e.g., articles published after training) from leaking into training and ensures the test set represents genuinely unseen temporal conditions. Within each window, articles are stratified by category (OS, network, application, etc.) to ensure both retrieval models face similar article distributions and to enable post-hoc analysis of category-specific gains.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing is fit exclusively on the training set:
- Tokenization and vocabulary are fixed using a standard BERT tokenizer (e.g., WordPiece) trained only on training-set tickets and articles.
- Text is truncated to 512 tokens; stopword removal and case normalization follow standard practices.
- BM25 IDF and term-frequency statistics are computed on training articles and queries only.
- No validation or test statistics inform any transformation.

Features are minimal and identical for both models: the semantic model receives concatenated ticket description + agent notes (query side) and article title + body (document side). BM25 receives the same text inputs. No metadata features (article age, ticket priority, requester role) are included, ensuring the comparison isolates text retrieval capability.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Semantic model:** A dual-encoder transformer architecture using a pre-trained BERT-family encoder (e.g., BERT-base-uncased). Query text (tickets) and document text (articles) are encoded separately to fixed-dimensional embeddings (~768D). The model is fine-tuned on the training set using **contrastive loss** (InfoNCE or supervised contrastive) to maximize similarity of (ticket, cited_article) pairs and minimize similarity to other articles sampled in-batch. Early stopping uses validation-set recall@5.

**Baseline:** BM25 with Okapi parameters (k1, b) tuned on the same validation set using the same primary metric (recall@5). BM25 receives identical text and ranks the full article corpus. This ensures fair comparison: both models are optimized on the same data with equivalent tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** **Recall@5** on the test set—the fraction of test tickets for which the correct article appears in the top 5 results. This metric directly reflects operational value: agents scan the top few results, so improving top-k recall reduces search time and improves first-response quality.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Recall@1, Recall@10, and Mean Reciprocal Rank to measure ranking sensitivity
- Category-stratified recall@5 to detect whether gains are uniform or concentrated
- **Temporal drift:** recall@5 separately for test months 16, 17, 18 to detect degradation
- **Article age bias check:** recall@5 for articles published during training vs. articles published/updated after training, isolating whether improvements come from semantic understanding or metadata leakage
- Sensitivity analysis: performance with weak positives excluded entirely, to measure robustness to label noise

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning—learning rate, batch size, fine-tuning epochs, contrastive margin, BM25 parameters—is performed on the validation set (months 13–15) using recall@5 as the selection criterion. The model checkpoint with the highest validation recall@5 is selected for final testing. The validation set is then **never touched again**; it is held strictly for model selection, not for final claims.

### 7. Test Set and Confound Controls

The test set (months 16–18) is used exactly once, after all tuning is complete. At test time, both models rank articles as they exist in each test month, accounting for article retirements and updates. No threshold adjustments or additional tuning occur on test data.

Confounds are controlled as follows:
1. **Temporal drift:** The temporal split ensures both models face the same product evolution and article versioning, isolating semantic gains from metadata confounds.
2. **Query length bias:** Both models receive identical ticket text; differences cannot be attributed to length-handling alone.
3. **Metadata leakage:** The article age bias check detects whether gains concentrate on recently-published articles (a sign of metadata exploitation).
4. **Label noise:** Weak positives are down-weighted in training; sensitivity analysis measures robustness.
5. **Template/duplicate artifacts:** Queries from the same ticket cluster are evaluated separately to confirm gains hold for novel tickets.
6. **Tuning fairness:** BM25 is tuned as carefully as the semantic model, preventing artificial disadvantage to the baseline.

The experiment thus isolates whether fine-tuned semantic search genuinely improves retrieval for unseen, future tickets, or whether apparent gains reflect metadata leakage, query-length bias, or label noise.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of weak positives (lower-weighted labels for article opens without citations) introduces unacceptable noise into the training signal and biases model evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The inclusion of weak positives with reduced loss weight is a legitimate design choice that acknowledges real-world agent behavior—articles may be used implicitly even if not formally cited. Down-weighting prevents overfitting to noisy signals while still capturing useful behavior patterns. The design explicitly includes sensitivity analysis to test robustness to this choice, making it methodologically sound.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set spans only 3 months (16–18), which is too short to draw reliable conclusions about generalization to future data.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The temporal split (12 months training, 3 validation, 3 test) is appropriate given the 18–24 month data window and the need to simulate future deployment. A 3-month test window is operationally realistic and sufficient to evaluate recall trends and temporal drift. The duration is justified by the domain’s evolution pace and aligns with standard practices in time-series ML evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: BM25 parameters are not tuned thoroughly enough; relying on validation-set recall@5 alone leads to a suboptimal baseline and inflates the perceived gains of the semantic model.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline is tuned on the same validation data and primary metric (recall@5) as the semantic model, with hyperparameters (k1, b) selected to optimize performance. This represents a strong, fair baseline. There is no indication of inadequate tuning effort—both models receive equivalent optimization, ensuring a valid comparison.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer concludes that the experiment design is methodologically sound and does not contain critical flaws. The defender successfully rebuts any claims about label noise, temporal leakage, or baseline weakness by citing the design's explicit controls and alignment with domain needs.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic raises concerns about label quality, temporal splits, or baseline strength, but the defender demonstrates that appropriate safeguards (weak positive weighting, stratified temporal split, equal tuning effort) are in place and contextually justified.

hypothesis: In enterprise cybersecurity email threat detection, a transformer model fine-tuned on full email thread text and header metadata will outperform a bag-of-words baseline on phishing classification, especially for low-volume spear-phishing messages, when evaluated on a strictly time-based holdout set.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity: internal corporate email threat detection using production mail gateway logs and analyst-reviewed phishing labels
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Bag-of-Words for Enterprise Phishing Detection

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18 months of raw email records from an enterprise mail gateway, spanning multiple threat campaigns and seasonal patterns. Data includes email bodies, subjects, headers (sender/recipient), attachment indicators, URL counts, and delivery metadata. Labels are sourced from analyst-reviewed triage logs, where each email is marked as phishing or benign during security operations. The label timestamp is recorded; we use this to enforce strict temporal ordering during splitting.

Known data quality issues are handled as follows: analyst review may be incomplete (some phishing slips through unlabeled) or delayed (labels arrive days after delivery). We accept this noise as realistic but document the labeling lag distribution and exclude any emails labeled more than 7 days after delivery to reduce label noise from very delayed reviews. Missing values in structured features (e.g., sender reputation) are imputed using mean/mode from the training set only, ensuring no leakage.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

A strict temporal split is used: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, and months 16–18 for testing. **Why temporal?** Phishing campaigns evolve; senders, domains, and attack payloads are ephemeral. A random split would allow the model to overfit to campaign-specific patterns (e.g., a spear-phishing sender active in month 8) that never recur, creating false confidence in generalization. Temporal splits respect the real deployment scenario: the model is trained on historical threats and must generalize to future threats it has never seen.

This split prevents information leakage: no future campaign data influences training, and the test set represents a genuine out-of-time validation environment.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two classes of features are constructed:

**Text features:** Email body and subject line are concatenated and tokenized via the transformer's native tokenizer (subword units). Historical thread context (prior messages in the thread) is included, capped at 512 tokens total to manage computational cost. All text preprocessing (vocabulary building, tokenization rules) is fit on the training set and frozen before validation and test.

**Structured metadata:** Sender domain, recipient domain, attachment presence (binary), URL count, message length in tokens, time-of-day, and day-of-week are encoded numerically. Sender reputation (if available from gateway reputation databases) is encoded as categorical and fit on training data only. No future information—such as bounce-back status or user-reported-as-phishing flags that arrive after deployment—is included.

Both transformer and baseline receive identical features, ensuring fair comparison.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed model:** A BERT-like transformer (e.g., BERT-base) is fine-tuned end-to-end. The model processes concatenated email text, outputs a [CLS] embedding, concatenates this with learned embeddings of metadata (domain identities) and numerical scalings of counts and time features, then passes the combined representation through a small MLP (1–2 hidden layers) to a sigmoid output. This architecture leverages the transformer's ability to capture long-range dependencies and contextual word interactions.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on TF-IDF features (unigrams + bigrams, vocabulary capped at 5,000 terms fit on training data only) plus the same structured metadata. The baseline sacrifices contextual understanding but uses identical information, making it a fair test of whether context (the transformer's advantage) improves phishing detection.

Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort via grid search on the validation set.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Precision at 95% recall, measured on the test set. Operationally, the security team must catch a high fraction of phishing (95% recall is the requirement); the question is whether the transformer achieves this with fewer false positives. The threshold on the model's phishing probability is chosen on the validation set to achieve 95% recall, then held constant on the test set.

**Secondary metrics:** Recall at 99% precision (stricter precision); false negative rate; area under the precision-recall curve; and per-campaign precision and recall to detect if gains are uneven or confounded by campaign memorization.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

The validation set (months 13–15) is used to tune hyperparameters for both models, selecting the best via primary metric. After selection, both models are retrained on combined train + validation data (months 1–15) to maximize available information before testing. The test set (months 16–18) is held isolated and evaluated only once, preventing any leakage or overfitting.

### 7. Confound Control

To ensure gains are not artifacts of sender memorization or thread-level shortcuts:

- **Campaign stratification:** Precision and recall are computed separately per threat campaign (identified by sender clustering). Sharp divergence suggests domain memorization.
- **Sender-disjoint subset:** Performance is re-computed on emails from senders never in training. If the transformer's gains vanish, it exploited sender reputation.
- **Metadata ablation:** A transformer variant trained on text-only (no metadata) is compared to the full model. Large differential drops suggest over-reliance on sender signals.
- **Temporal drift:** Precision and recall are plotted per week within the test set to detect campaign shifts or model brittleness.

These controls establish not just whether the transformer wins, but *why* and *under what conditions*, limiting claims to true language-understanding benefits rather than superficial memorization.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split reduces the size of the training set and may hurt model performance due to less data.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits are mandatory in non-stationary domains like cybersecurity; reducing training data is a necessary trade-off for valid generalization. The design prioritizes real-world deployment validity over apparent training performance, which is correct for this hypothesis.

must_not_claim.claim: Using TF-IDF with 5,000 terms is too limiting and may hurt the baseline unfairly.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The vocabulary cap is standard practice and prevents overfitting to rare n-grams. The baseline is constructed to use the same metadata and receive the same preprocessing constraints as the transformer, ensuring a fair comparison of modeling capability, not feature engineering.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set (months 16–18) is too short to provide reliable evaluation of model performance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The 3-month test set captures multiple evolving threat campaigns and seasonal patterns, and performance is assessed with confidence intervals and trend analysis. In real enterprise settings, test periods are often short due to threat evolution, making this evaluation realistic and sufficient.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic attempts to identify flaws in the design, but the defender correctly rebuts all claims by showing that choices are justified by operational realism, temporal validity, and fair comparison. The final verdict is that the experiment design is sound and appropriate for the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: A real methodological flaw were present and demonstrable (e.g., leakage, unfair baseline, invalid metric)
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic raises concerns based on incorrect assumptions about domain practices, such as objecting to temporal splits or metadata usage, and the defender shows these are not flaws but necessary features of the design.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic proposes alternative designs (e.g., different metric, longer test duration, additional ablations), but concedes that the current design is valid and sufficient to test the hypothesis. The final verdict affirms that no fatal flaw exists and the baseline is fairly constructed.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The alternative design would materially improve evaluation rigor in a way that affects conclusion reliability
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The existing controls and metrics already address the core confounds, and any proposed changes are enhancements, not corrections

## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Bag-of-Words for Enterprise Phishing Detection

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18 months of raw email records from an enterprise mail gateway, spanning multiple threat campaigns and seasonal patterns. Data includes email bodies, subjects, headers (sender/recipient), attachment indicators, URL counts, and delivery metadata. Labels are sourced from analyst-reviewed triage logs, where each email is marked as phishing or benign during security operations. The label timestamp is recorded; we use this to enforce strict temporal ordering during splitting.

Known data quality issues are handled as follows: analyst review may be incomplete (some phishing slips through unlabeled) or delayed (labels arrive days after delivery). We accept this noise as realistic but document the labeling lag distribution and exclude any emails labeled more than 7 days after delivery to reduce label noise from very delayed reviews. Missing values in structured features (e.g., sender reputation) are imputed using mean/mode from the training set only, ensuring no leakage.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

A strict temporal split is used: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, and months 16–18 for testing. **Why temporal?** Phishing campaigns evolve; senders, domains, and attack payloads are ephemeral. A random split would allow the model to overfit to campaign-specific patterns (e.g., a spear-phishing sender active in month 8) that never recur, creating false confidence in generalization. Temporal splits respect the real deployment scenario: the model is trained on historical threats and must generalize to future threats it has never seen.

This split prevents information leakage: no future campaign data influences training, and the test set represents a genuine out-of-time validation environment.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two classes of features are constructed:

**Text features:** Email body and subject line are concatenated and tokenized via the transformer's native tokenizer (subword units). Historical thread context (prior messages in the thread) is included, capped at 512 tokens total to manage computational cost. All text preprocessing (vocabulary building, tokenization rules) is fit on the training set and frozen before validation and test.

**Structured metadata:** Sender domain, recipient domain, attachment presence (binary), URL count, message length in tokens, time-of-day, and day-of-week are encoded numerically. Sender reputation (if available from gateway reputation databases) is encoded as categorical and fit on training data only. No future information—such as bounce-back status or user-reported-as-phishing flags that arrive after deployment—is included.

Both transformer and baseline receive identical features, ensuring fair comparison.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed model:** A BERT-like transformer (e.g., BERT-base) is fine-tuned end-to-end. The model processes concatenated email text, outputs a [CLS] embedding, concatenates this with learned embeddings of metadata (domain identities) and numerical scalings of counts and time features, then passes the combined representation through a small MLP (1–2 hidden layers) to a sigmoid output. This architecture leverages the transformer's ability to capture long-range dependencies and contextual word interactions.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on TF-IDF features (unigrams + bigrams, vocabulary capped at 5,000 terms fit on training data only) plus the same structured metadata. The baseline sacrifices contextual understanding but uses identical information, making it a fair test of whether context (the transformer's advantage) improves phishing detection.

Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort via grid search on the validation set.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Precision at 95% recall, measured on the test set. Operationally, the security team must catch a high fraction of phishing (95% recall is the requirement); the question is whether the transformer achieves this with fewer false positives. The threshold on the model's phishing probability is chosen on the validation set to achieve 95% recall, then held constant on the test set.

**Secondary metrics:** Recall at 99% precision (stricter precision); false negative rate; area under the precision-recall curve; and per-campaign precision and recall to detect if gains are uneven or confounded by campaign memorization.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

The validation set (months 13–15) is used to tune hyperparameters for both models, selecting the best via primary metric. After selection, both models are retrained on combined train + validation data (months 1–15) to maximize available information before testing. The test set (months 16–18) is held isolated and evaluated only once, preventing any leakage or overfitting.

### 7. Confound Control

To ensure gains are not artifacts of sender memorization or thread-level shortcuts:

- **Campaign stratification:** Precision and recall are computed separately per threat campaign (identified by sender clustering). Sharp divergence suggests domain memorization.
- **Sender-disjoint subset:** Performance is re-computed on emails from senders never in training. If the transformer's gains vanish, it exploited sender reputation.
- **Metadata ablation:** A transformer variant trained on text-only (no metadata) is compared to the full model. Large differential drops suggest over-reliance on sender signals.
- **Temporal drift:** Precision and recall are plotted per week within the test set to detect campaign shifts or model brittleness.

These controls establish not just whether the transformer wins, but *why* and *under what conditions*, limiting claims to true language-understanding benefits rather than superficial memorization.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because it requires understanding of temporal non-stationarity in cybersecurity, appropriate metric alignment with operational needs, and correct interpretation of fair baseline design. A reviewer might misinterpret domain-justified choices (e.g., temporal splits, metadata usage) as flaws without recognizing the deployment context.

hypothesis: For a telecom customer retention problem, a survival-analysis model with time-varying usage and billing features will predict 60-day churn risk more accurately and earlier than a standard binary classifier trained on a single aggregated snapshot, as measured by time-dependent AUC and calibration on a future holdout period.
domain: Telecommunications customer retention using monthly billing records, call/data usage logs, plan changes, and customer service interactions from a large mobile carrier
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Survival Analysis vs. Binary Classification for Telecom Churn Prediction

### 1. Data and Cohort Definition

We will use 48 months (Jan 2020–Dec 2023) of monthly billing, usage, and service records from the mobile carrier. The dataset includes ~5M unique customers with complete snapshots of usage volumes (voice, data, SMS), billing features (plan cost, overage charges, payment status), service changes (plan updates, device changes, renewals), and customer service interactions.

Churn is defined as voluntary account closure or port-out; involuntary closures are excluded. For each customer, the churn outcome is recorded as a date (days from entry) or marked as censored if the account was active at the observation window end.

### 2. Split Strategy

We will partition customers into train, validation, and test sets at the customer level using stratified sampling on churn status and entry month so that each split preserves both overall event incidence and seasonal enrollment mix. The target allocation is 70% train, 15% validation, and 15% test.

All monthly observations for a given customer will remain within the same partition, so the longitudinal structure required for survival analysis is preserved. This setup keeps each split comparable with respect to acquisition season and churn prevalence, which is important in telecom data where campaign timing and regional promotions can create sharp month-to-month shifts. Stratification by entry month will also reduce the chance that one split is disproportionately influenced by a narrow acquisition window.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers are fit exclusively on the train partition using the first 12 months of each customer's history. The resulting transformers are applied without refitting to validation and test data, ensuring a consistent transformation pipeline.

Features are constructed in two groups:

1. **Static features** (per customer): tenure, geographic region, device type, initial plan tier.

2. **Time-varying features** (monthly): 3-month rolling average of usage (voice minutes, data MB, SMS count), billing trend (month-over-month plan cost change), payment timeliness (days late on most recent bill), support contact frequency (prior 3 months), binary plan change indicator, and contract remaining (months).

Usage and billing features are normalized using robust scaling (fit on train only) to handle outliers from promotion periods. Categorical features are one-hot encoded using the vocabulary observed in the train partition; unseen categories in validation/test are mapped to an 'other' bin.

### 4. Model Architecture and Fair Baseline

**Survival model (primary):** Cox proportional hazards regression with time-varying coefficients. The model is fit on the full observation sequence for each customer (one row per customer-month), allowing it to observe temporal patterns in usage and billing trends. If the proportional hazards assumption is violated (detected via Schoenfeld residuals), we use a Weibull accelerated failure time (AFT) model or stratified Cox regression as appropriate.

**Binary classifier (baseline):** Logistic regression or gradient boosted trees (XGBoost), trained on a single 12-month aggregated snapshot per customer, predicting binary churn within 60 days post-observation. The feature set is identical to the survival model (static + rolled time-varying features) but collapsed to one row per customer.

**Hyperparameter tuning:** Both models receive equivalent tuning effort. Grid search with 5-fold cross-validation is applied within the validation partition, using the primary metric (C-index for both model families, computed from predicted risk scores and observed event ordering) to select hyperparameters. Once tuned, both models are retrained on train + validation combined and evaluated on test only.

This design ensures the baseline is fair: both models see 12 months of identical information, receive identical feature engineering, and are tuned with equal computational budget. Observed differences are attributable to the representation (sequence vs. snapshot) and model type (survival vs. binary), not data or tuning disparity.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Business Alignment

**Primary metric:** Concordance index (C-index) over the full follow-up period. This metric measures whether the model correctly rank-orders customers by relative churn risk across their observed trajectories, which is useful in this setting because customers enter with heterogeneous contract lengths and follow-up durations. A higher C-index indicates better discrimination between customers who churn earlier and those who remain active longer.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Calibration in 10 deciles (observed vs. predicted probability at 60 days, expected calibration error). Essential for the business, which needs reliable risk scores to drive proportional retention offers.
- Time-dependent sensitivity and specificity at the 60th percentile risk threshold.
- Time-dependent AUC at 60 days, reported as a horizon-specific discrimination check.
- Brier score (integrated over time).
- Binary AUC at 60 days (to ensure both models are comparable on a shared metric).

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Model selection occurs on the validation partition using C-index as the selection criterion. The test partition is held out from all tuning and development; it is used exactly once at the end to report all metrics, with 95% confidence intervals computed via bootstrap.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

1. **Information parity:** Both models receive identical 12-month feature histories and are censored at 60 days; differences are not due to unequal data access.
2. **Customer-level partitioning:** Train, validation, and test splits are defined at the customer level so that no longitudinal record is shared across partitions.
