hypothesis: A transformer model trained on longitudinal ICU event sequences plus medication and lab time-series will predict 48-hour unplanned ICU transfer better than a tabular baseline using only the latest vitals and demographics, because it can exploit short-term temporal trends and treatment responses.
domain: Hospital inpatient monitoring in a multi-hospital adult ICU using de-identified electronic health record streams
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Tabular Baseline for 48-Hour ICU Deterioration Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 36 months of de-identified ICU EHR data from 3–5 hospitals in a single health system. The data include timestamped vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, temperature—collected at variable intervals, typically 1–4 hourly), laboratory results (creatinine, lactate, WBC, platelets, and others), medication administrations with timestamps and doses, nursing flowsheet events, and admission demographics. We include all adult ICU admissions (≥18 years) with ≥24 hours of continuous observation before the prediction point, and exclude direct post-operative admissions, patients on comfort-focused care at admission, and admissions missing core vital signs for >50% of the window.

The outcome—unplanned ICU transfer within 48 hours—is defined as transfer to higher level of care (e.g., ECMO), emergency intubation or escalation not planned at admission, or in-hospital death. Transfers are extracted from bed movement logs and physician orders; deaths from vital status records. A physician reviewer assesses a stratified random sample of 200 borderline cases to validate label quality.

### 2. Temporal and Stratified Split Strategy

To prevent information leakage and reflect the deployment scenario (a model trained on historical data), data are split by calendar order: the first 60% of admissions (chronologically, ~21 months) form the training set; the next 20% (~6 months) form the validation set; the final 20% (~9 months) are held-out test data. Within each temporal window, admissions are stratified by hospital site and admission source (emergency vs. planned) to ensure each site and admission pathway is represented proportionally. This approach ensures that the test set covers all hospitals and future time periods unseen during training, directly testing generalization to new hospitals and new time.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer model:** Events are represented as time-stamped sequences covering a fixed 24-hour lookback window before the prediction point. Each event includes a type (vital, lab, medication, flowsheet entry), a timestamp, and a value. Timestamps are encoded using sinusoidal positional embeddings adjusted for irregular spacing. Values are binned into discrete tokens to reduce dimensionality and improve generalization. Missingness is explicitly encoded as a feature (e.g., whether a vital was absent in the last 2 hours), which can capture clinically meaningful patterns of monitoring intensity or data availability.

**Baseline model:** The same 24-hour lookback window is used. Features include the most recent vital and lab value within 2 hours of the prediction point, demographic variables (age, sex, admission source, comorbidities, primary diagnosis), and summary statistics over the 24-hour window (min, max, mean, SD for all vital and lab variables). Missingness indicators are included to control for the possibility that the transformer's benefit arises from exploiting missingness patterns rather than true sequence structure.

All preprocessing—imputation, standardization, and categorical encoding—is fit exclusively on the training set using multivariate imputation (missForest or KNN), z-score standardization, and one-hot encoding. Validation and test sets are transformed using the training set's fitted parameters, with novel categories mapped to 'unknown'.

### 4. Model Training and Baseline Selection

The **transformer model** is a multi-head self-attention encoder (6–12 layers, 8 heads, embedding dimension 256–512). Events are tokenized and passed through the encoder; the final representation is pooled ([CLS] token or mean pooling) and passed through a small MLP (1–2 layers, 128 units) to produce the binary classification logit.

The **primary baseline** is logistic regression on the same tabular feature set, receiving equivalent hyperparameter tuning (regularization strength, class weighting, threshold selection). A **secondary baseline** (XGBoost or LightGBM on tabular features) controls for the possibility that any gain from the transformer comes from model complexity rather than sequence modeling. If the transformer substantially outperforms both, this strengthens the claim about temporal sequence structure.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Both models undergo hyperparameter tuning on the validation set using grid search or Bayesian optimization. Within the training set, stratified 5-fold cross-validation (stratified by outcome and site) is used to estimate performance and tune regularization. The final model is trained on the entire training set using the best hyperparameters. The model with the highest PR-AUC on the validation set is selected for test evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics and Justification

The **primary metric is precision-recall AUC (PR-AUC)** on the test set. Operationally, clinicians choose an alert threshold to balance missed deteriorations (false negatives, which risk patient harm) against false alarms (false positives, which drive alert fatigue and unnecessary escalation). PR-AUC measures discrimination across all feasible thresholds and is insensitive to class imbalance. If unplanned transfers occur in 5–10% of admissions, standard AUC is less informative than PR-AUC, because a high-AUC model may have poor precision at clinically useful thresholds.

**Secondary metrics** include sensitivity and specificity at the F1-optimized threshold, positive and negative predictive values (PPV/NPV), Brier score, and expected calibration error (ECE) to assess probability calibration. Results are stratified by hospital site and by admission source / primary diagnosis to assess generalization and detect whether the model's benefit is concentrated in specific populations.

### 7. Confound and Leakage Controls

**Temporal leakage prevention:** The temporal split ensures no future information enters training. The observation window and prediction point are fixed and applied identically across splits.

**Measurement recency and missingness:** Both the transformer and baseline use the same 24-hour window and include explicit missingness indicators. If the transformer outperforms despite this alignment, the gain reflects sequence structure, not information advantage.

**Cross-hospital generalization:** The test set is stratified by site; results are reported overall and by site. Performance drops at specific sites trigger recommendations for site-specific calibration.

**Label quality:** A physician review of 200 borderline cases estimates label noise; if substantial, sensitivity analyses use probabilistic labels or exclude high-uncertainty cases.

**Class imbalance:** Class-weighted losses and PR-AUC mitigate imbalance effects.

Final test results include 95% bootstrap confidence intervals to quantify sampling uncertainty. The experiment directly tests whether the transformer's ability to model temporal sequences and treatment responses outperforms a carefully engineered tabular baseline using the same observation window and prediction task.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set covers only future time periods and not all hospital sites, risking poor generalization.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design explicitly stratifies by hospital site and admission source within each temporal split, ensuring all sites are proportionally represented in train, validation, and test sets. This supports evaluation of cross-hospital generalization while preserving temporal integrity.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model is unfairly disadvantaged because it lacks sequence information.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The tabular baseline includes summary statistics (min, max, mean, SD) over the 24-hour window and the most recent values, making it a strong, temporally informed competitor. The inclusion of a secondary tree-based baseline (XGBoost/LightGBM) further ensures the comparison isolates model architecture (sequence vs. tabular) rather than feature capacity alone.

must_not_claim.claim: Using PR-AUC as the primary metric is inappropriate because AUC is the standard for model comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Given the low prevalence (5–10%) of unplanned ICU transfers, PR-AUC is more informative than ROC-AUC for evaluating performance at clinically relevant thresholds. High ROC-AUC can coexist with poor precision, whereas PR-AUC directly captures the tradeoff between false alarms and missed cases that matters in clinical operations.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer acknowledges the experiment is sound: temporal split prevents leakage, baselines are well-matched, preprocessing is correctly isolated to training data, and PR-AUC is justified for the operational context. No valid critique is levied.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer correctly identifies strength of temporal validation, properly engineered baselines, and alignment of evaluation metric with clinical utility.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer raises concerns about feature engineering or baseline strength, but the defender successfully rebuts by showing shared observation window, missingness controls, and secondary complexity-matched baseline.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Critic's concern hinges on a legitimate aspect (e.g., recency or missingness) but defender demonstrates it was controlled via design: same lookback window, explicit missingness features in baseline, and complexity control via XGBoost/LightGBM.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Tabular Baseline for 48-Hour ICU Deterioration Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 36 months of de-identified ICU EHR data from 3–5 hospitals in a single health system. The data include timestamped vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, temperature—collected at variable intervals, typically 1–4 hourly), laboratory results (creatinine, lactate, WBC, platelets, and others), medication administrations with timestamps and doses, nursing flowsheet events, and admission demographics. We include all adult ICU admissions (≥18 years) with ≥24 hours of continuous observation before the prediction point, and exclude direct post-operative admissions, patients on comfort-focused care at admission, and admissions missing core vital signs for >50% of the window.

The outcome—unplanned ICU transfer within 48 hours—is defined as transfer to higher level of care (e.g., ECMO), emergency intubation or escalation not planned at admission, or in-hospital death. Transfers are extracted from bed movement logs and physician orders; deaths from vital status records. A physician reviewer assesses a stratified random sample of 200 borderline cases to validate label quality.

### 2. Temporal and Stratified Split Strategy

To prevent information leakage and reflect the deployment scenario (a model trained on historical data), data are split by calendar order: the first 60% of admissions (chronologically, ~21 months) form the training set; the next 20% (~6 months) form the validation set; the final 20% (~9 months) are held-out test data. Within each temporal window, admissions are stratified by hospital site and admission source (emergency vs. planned) to ensure each site and admission pathway is represented proportionally. This approach ensures that the test set covers all hospitals and future time periods unseen during training, directly testing generalization to new hospitals and new time.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer model:** Events are represented as time-stamped sequences covering a fixed 24-hour lookback window before the prediction point. Each event includes a type (vital, lab, medication, flowsheet entry), a timestamp, and a value. Timestamps are encoded using sinusoidal positional embeddings adjusted for irregular spacing. Values are binned into discrete tokens to reduce dimensionality and improve generalization. Missingness is explicitly encoded as a feature (e.g., whether a vital was absent in the last 2 hours), which can capture clinically meaningful patterns of monitoring intensity or data availability.

**Baseline model:** The same 24-hour lookback window is used. Features include the most recent vital and lab value within 2 hours of the prediction point, demographic variables (age, sex, admission source, comorbidities, primary diagnosis), and summary statistics over the 24-hour window (min, max, mean, SD for all vital and lab variables). Missingness indicators are included to control for the possibility that the transformer's benefit arises from exploiting missingness patterns rather than true sequence structure.

All preprocessing—imputation, standardization, and categorical encoding—is fit exclusively on the training set using multivariate imputation (missForest or KNN), z-score standardization, and one-hot encoding. Validation and test sets are transformed using the training set's fitted parameters, with novel categories mapped to 'unknown'.

### 4. Model Training and Baseline Selection

The **transformer model** is a multi-head self-attention encoder (6–12 layers, 8 heads, embedding dimension 256–512). Events are tokenized and passed through the encoder; the final representation is pooled ([CLS] token or mean pooling) and passed through a small MLP (1–2 layers, 128 units) to produce the binary classification logit.

The **primary baseline** is logistic regression on the same tabular feature set, receiving equivalent hyperparameter tuning (regularization strength, class weighting, threshold selection). A **secondary baseline** (XGBoost or LightGBM on tabular features) controls for the possibility that any gain from the transformer comes from model complexity rather than sequence modeling. If the transformer substantially outperforms both, this strengthens the claim about temporal sequence structure.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Both models undergo hyperparameter tuning on the validation set using grid search or Bayesian optimization. Within the training set, stratified 5-fold cross-validation (stratified by outcome and site) is used to estimate performance and tune regularization. The final model is trained on the entire training set using the best hyperparameters. The model with the highest PR-AUC on the validation set is selected for test evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics and Justification

The **primary metric is precision-recall AUC (PR-AUC)** on the test set. Operationally, clinicians choose an alert threshold to balance missed deteriorations (false negatives, which risk patient harm) against false alarms (false positives, which drive alert fatigue and unnecessary escalation). PR-AUC measures discrimination across all feasible thresholds and is insensitive to class imbalance. If unplanned transfers occur in 5–10% of admissions, standard AUC is less informative than PR-AUC, because a high-AUC model may have poor precision at clinically useful thresholds.

**Secondary metrics** include sensitivity and specificity at the F1-optimized threshold, positive and negative predictive values (PPV/NPV), Brier score, and expected calibration error (ECE) to assess probability calibration. Results are stratified by hospital site and by admission source / primary diagnosis to assess generalization and detect whether the model's benefit is concentrated in specific populations.

### 7. Confound and Leakage Controls

**Temporal leakage prevention:** The temporal split ensures no future information enters training. The observation window and prediction point are fixed and applied identically across splits.

**Measurement recency and missingness:** Both the transformer and baseline use the same 24-hour window and include explicit missingness indicators. If the transformer outperforms despite this alignment, the gain reflects sequence structure, not information advantage.

**Cross-hospital generalization:** The test set is stratified by site; results are reported overall and by site. Performance drops at specific sites trigger recommendations for site-specific calibration.

**Label quality:** A physician review of 200 borderline cases estimates label noise; if substantial, sensitivity analyses use probabilistic labels or exclude high-uncertainty cases.

**Class imbalance:** Class-weighted losses and PR-AUC mitigate imbalance effects.

Final test results include 95% bootstrap confidence intervals to quantify sampling uncertainty. The experiment directly tests whether the transformer's ability to model temporal sequences and treatment responses outperforms a carefully engineered tabular baseline using the same observation window and prediction task.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because the design is fully sound, requiring the reviewer to recognize legitimate strengths — such as temporally valid splitting, confound-matched baselines, and clinically appropriate metrics — without over-criticizing well-justified choices that might resemble common pitfalls in less rigorous designs.

hypothesis: For a large online news aggregation app, a ranking model that incorporates user-session context and publisher freshness features will yield higher next-click NDCG@10 than a content-only semantic retrieval baseline that ranks articles solely by query-article text similarity.
domain: Media/content personalization in a large-scale news aggregation app with logged search queries and article interactions
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Session-Context and Freshness Features for News Ranking

### 1. Data Strategy and Labeling

This experiment uses 6 months of production logs from the news aggregation app. Each log entry contains a user query, the articles shown (impressions), interaction signals (clicks, dwell time), article metadata (publish timestamp, publisher, topic tags), and pre-computed query-article text embedding similarity.

We filter to queries with 2–100 impressions per query to focus on ranking scenarios with non-trivial signal. Queries with only 1 result (no ranking decision) and extremely high-volume queries (>100 impressions per query, often noise or system artifacts) are excluded.

The relevance label is defined as **click + dwell ≥30 seconds**, or **click + final article in session**. This hybrid definition reduces noise from accidental clicks and position bias while preserving genuine engagement signals. Non-clicks are labeled irrelevant. This labeling scheme trades some recall (we miss reads that lasted <30s) for precision and robustness to positional effects.

### 2. Data Partitioning and Distribution Control

News traffic is highly heterogeneous: relevance rates vary by query volume, publisher mix, and event intensity. To make the offline comparison sensitive to modeling differences rather than to a particular burst of the news cycle, the data will be partitioned by query instances using stratified sampling across the 180-day corpus.

**Split:** 67% training, 17% validation, and 16% test, stratified by relevance prevalence, query-size bucket, and broad publisher/topic composition.

**Justification:** This split preserves comparable class balance and query structure across partitions while ensuring that both common and long-tail query types are represented in each stage of model development. In practice, localized spikes around major events can otherwise dominate a narrow holdout window and make differences between models look larger or smaller than they really are. Keeping the partitions distributionally aligned provides a lower-variance estimate of whether the added session-context and freshness signals improve ranking quality over the content-only baseline.

### 3. Features and Preprocessing

**Session-context features:**
- Session length: count of prior clicks in the current user session.
- Time since last click: seconds elapsed since the user's previous click in the same session.
- Session topic diversity: entropy of topic tags of articles clicked earlier in the session, capturing user focus vs. exploration.
- User historical CTR: empirical click-through rate for this user in the available interaction window, winsorized at 5th and 95th percentiles to reduce outlier effects.

**Publisher freshness features:**
- Article age: minutes elapsed since the article was published at ranking time.
- Publisher historical freshness: median age of articles this publisher's articles had when they were clicked.
- Publisher click volume: total clicks received by this publisher (popularity proxy, also indicates signal strength).

**Query representation:**
- Query-article text embedding similarity: a scalar feature derived from pre-computed embeddings (external, not trained here).

All numerical features are z-score normalized (zero mean, unit variance) using a single set of statistics established for the experiment corpus so that feature scaling is identical across training, validation, and test. Missing-value rules are frozen once, and categorical features (publisher ID, topic) are one-hot encoded using the full observed vocabulary to avoid split-specific sparsity patterns. This keeps the feature space stable during hyperparameter search and final evaluation, which is particularly important for sparse publisher/topic indicators and for timestamp-derived features whose ranges can shift sharply around major events.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed model:** LightGBM with ranking objective (rank:ndcg). The model is trained on query-level groups (each query is a group containing its impressed articles, labeled with binary relevance). Hyperparameter tuning uses random search over 300 trials on the validation set, varying learning rate, number of leaves, min data in leaf, max depth, and regularization strength. The best hyperparameters are selected by maximizing validation AUROC at the impression level, with query-averaged AUROC used as a tie-breaker when configurations are close.

**Baseline:** Content-only semantic retrieval. Articles are ranked purely by descending query-article text embedding similarity. This baseline is deterministic, requires no training, and represents the 'content-only' comparison stated in the hypothesis. It is evaluated on the exact same test set and under the same label definition as the proposed model, ensuring a fair comparison. While the baseline requires no tuning, we measure its validation-set AUROC to confirm it is a reasonable starting point.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: impression-level AUROC**, with query-level AUROC also aggregated across the test set as a robustness view.

*Justification:* AUROC measures how well the model separates relevant from non-relevant impressions across heterogeneous query sizes and candidate sets, which is useful here because the proposed features are intended to improve the overall relevance signal rather than only a single display position. It also supports segmented analysis by publisher, topic, and query-size regime so we can determine where the contextual and freshness features are adding discrimination power. Top-k ranking quality will still be examined in secondary metrics.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **NDCG@10:** Quality of the top-ranked results under a practical display depth.
- **NDCG@5:** Stricter focus on the very top results.
- **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR):** Where is the first relevant result ranked; interpretable measure of utility.
- **Recall@10:** Fraction of all relevant items that appear in top-10 (order-insensitive, but useful sanity check).
- **Click-through rate on test set:** Observed user behavior under the ranking. (Note: CTR is inherently biased by position, but provides a ground-truth signal of user behavior to compare against offline relevance metrics.)
- **Freshness@10:** Median article age of articles in top-10 across test queries. Ensures the model does not sacrifice timeliness for ranking gain.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Handling

The validation set is used to select the best hyperparameters. A held-out portion of the validation partition is used for early stopping during training to avoid overfitting the hyperparameter search process.

After the leading configuration is identified on validation data, it will be run once on the test partition as a stability check. If the validation winner underperforms in a way that suggests the validation slice was unrepresentative, the next-best validation configuration will be promoted as the final choice to reduce dependence on a single holdout segment. That selected configuration will then be retrained with the same preprocessing pipeline and used for the reported test-set metrics. No additional feature changes or threshold adjustments will be made after this step.

### 7. Controlling Confounds

**Position bias and exposure bias:** Click labels are inherently biased by where an article was ranked. We mitigate this by (a) using the click + dwell definition, which is less sensitive to position than clicks alone, and (b) computing propensity weights (inverse probability of exposure for each position) and re-weighting relevance labels at analysis time. Both the baseline and proposed model are evaluated under identical position-bias conditions, ensuring fair comparison.

**Recency/popularity leakage:** A model could gain performance simply by preferring recent articles or popular publishers, independent of the session-context or freshness signals we propose. We address this by explicitly reporting **Freshness@10** as a secondary metric and by performing post-hoc ablation: training a variant of the proposed model without freshness features and comparing NDCG@10. If the gain persists, session-context features are the driver; if the gain disappears, we have over-optimized for recency.

**Distributional heterogeneity:** Because news volume can spike sharply around major events, stratified partitioning is used to keep query-size and relevance prevalence comparable across train, validation, and test. We additionally compute AUROC and NDCG@10 separately across query-size buckets and publisher/topic segments to verify that gains are not confined to one traffic slice.

### 8. Interpretation and Scope

This experiment establishes whether a learning-to-rank model using session-context and freshness features achieves better offline relevance discrimination, and stronger top-k ranking metrics as a secondary check, than a content-only baseline under the chosen held-out evaluation protocol with engagement-based labels. It does *not* establish causal impact on user satisfaction, long-term retention, or downstream business metrics—those would require online A/B testing. It does establish that the proposed features improve offline ranking quality as measured on held-out query impressions, under the assumption that the engagement-based labels reflect true user intent.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified random split over the full time period instead of a temporal split. This introduces future information (articles, topics, user behavior) into training relative to evaluation time windows, violating the chronological structure of news data. As a result, the model may exploit trends or patterns from the future, leading to over-optimistic and non-representative performance estimates on test data that should simulate unseen conditions.

Feature preprocessing (normalization, encoding) is fit on the full dataset before splitting, allowing information from validation and test periods to leak into training. In a domain with strong concept drift, this means the model benefits from statistical knowledge of future feature distributions (e.g., publisher popularity, topic coverage), biasing results upward and undermining the validity of generalization claims.

The primary evaluation metric is AUROC instead of NDCG@10. AUROC evaluates global discrimination ability across all impression pairs but does not reflect ranking quality at the top of the list, which is the actual user-facing impact. Since the hypothesis is about improving top-k relevance (specifically NDCG@10), the primary metric fails to align with the stakeholder's objective and may reward models that reorder irrelevant items at the bottom without improving user-visible positions.

The test set is used as a 'stability check' to influence model selection—if the validation winner performs poorly on test, a backup model is selected instead. This means the test data indirectly determines the final model, violating the principle that test sets must remain unseen until final evaluation. The result is an optimistic bias in reported performance estimates.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline does not need to be evaluated on the same test set or under the same label definition as the proposed model, since it is rule-based and deterministic.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Fair comparison requires identical evaluation conditions. The baseline must be tested on the same data and with the same labels to ensure that any observed differences are due to modeling choices and not data or labeling differences.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a stratified split by query type is acceptable because it reduces variance and balances representation of rare query types.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While stratification helps in stable domains, in this high-concept-drift context, it breaks temporal fidelity. News relevance and topic prevalence shift rapidly, so preserving temporal order is more important than balancing label distributions. Chronological order reflects real deployment, where future data is unseen.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of propensity weighting for position bias is unnecessary because the label definition already uses dwell time to filter accidental clicks.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Dwell time helps but does not fully eliminate position bias—top-ranked items still receive more attention regardless of relevance. Propensity weighting is a standard and necessary correction to debias offline metrics in ranking problems.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic successfully identifies that the data split method violates temporal order, allowing future information into training. The resolution concludes that this invalidates generalization claims because the model sees future articles and trends during training. The verdict must be that the experiment cannot support the hypothesis under production-like conditions.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that news ranking systems face strong concept drift and must generalize to future content; random stratified splits fail to simulate this realistic deployment requirement.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that preprocessing is fit on the full corpus, leading to data leakage of future statistics into the training pipeline. The resolution confirms that normalization and categorical encoding based on future data distort the model's feature geometry and inflate performance beyond what would be possible in production.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that transformer fitting (e.g., mean/std for z-score, categorical vocab) on any data beyond the training period introduces information from the future, which cannot be available in real deployment.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic argues that AUROC is not the appropriate primary metric for a ranking task focused on top-k quality. The resolution accepts that while AUROC measures discrimination, it does not assess ranking order within queries, which is the core objective. NDCG@10 should be primary as it directly evaluates the quality of results users see.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic links the metric to the stakeholder’s goal: showing relevant results in visible positions. They show examples where AUROC can be high even if the model fails to place relevant items in the top 10.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic points out that the model is selected based on test set 'stability', meaning the test data influences which model is finally reported. The resolution agrees that this contaminates the test set, making it no longer an unbiased estimate of generalization. Retraining a different configuration based on test performance invalidates the independence of evaluation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic notes that any use of test results to choose model variants—even as a fallback—transforms the test set from an evaluation tool into a selection tool, which biases performance upward.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Session-Context and Freshness Features for News Ranking

### 1. Data Strategy and Labeling

This experiment uses 6 months of production logs from the news aggregation app. Each log entry contains a user query, the articles shown (impressions), interaction signals (clicks, dwell time), article metadata (publish timestamp, publisher, topic tags), and pre-computed query-article text embedding similarity.

We filter to queries with 2–100 impressions per query to focus on ranking scenarios with non-trivial signal. Queries with only 1 result (no ranking decision) and extremely high-volume queries (>100 impressions per query, often noise or system artifacts) are excluded.

The relevance label is defined as **click + dwell ≥30 seconds**, or **click + final article in session**. This hybrid definition reduces noise from accidental clicks and position bias while preserving genuine engagement signals. Non-clicks are labeled irrelevant. This labeling scheme trades some recall (we miss reads that lasted <30s) for precision and robustness to positional effects.

### 2. Temporal Split and Concept Drift

News rankings exhibit strong concept drift: trending topics emerge, publisher behavior changes, and seasonal patterns shift. A temporal split is essential and superior to random splits for this domain.

**Split:** Days 1–120 (training, 120 days), days 121–150 (validation, 30 days), days 151–180 (test, 30 days).

**Justification:** This split respects the chronological structure of the data. The validation set is held out during training to tune hyperparameters; the test set is completely untouched until final evaluation. Both validation and test contain unseen articles, publishers, and temporal patterns relative to the training window, simulating real deployment where the model must rank articles that did not exist when it was trained. This structure prevents information leakage from future events and ensures the evaluation reflects generalization to new news cycles.

### 3. Features and Preprocessing

**Session-context features:**
- Session length: count of prior clicks in the current user session.
- Time since last click: seconds elapsed since the user's previous click in the same session.
- Session topic diversity: entropy of topic tags of articles clicked earlier in the session, capturing user focus vs. exploration.
- User historical CTR: empirical click-through rate for this user in the training window, winsorized at 5th and 95th percentiles to reduce outlier effects.

**Publisher freshness features:**
- Article age: minutes elapsed since the article was published at ranking time.
- Publisher historical freshness: median age of articles this publisher's articles had when they were clicked (computed on training data).
- Publisher click volume: total clicks received by this publisher in the training window (popularity proxy, also indicates signal strength).

**Query representation:**
- Query-article text embedding similarity: a scalar feature derived from pre-computed embeddings (external, not trained here).

All numerical features are z-score normalized (zero mean, unit variance) using statistics computed only on the training split. Categorical features (publisher ID, topic) are one-hot encoded. **Critical:** All preprocessing transformers are fit exclusively on days 1–120 and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage and ensures validation/test metrics reflect truly unseen data.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed model:** LightGBM with ranking objective (rank:ndcg). The model is trained on query-level groups (each query is a group containing its impressed articles, labeled with binary relevance). Hyperparameter tuning uses random search over 300 trials on the validation set, varying learning rate, number of leaves, min data in leaf, max depth, and regularization strength. The best hyperparameters are selected by maximizing NDCG@10 on validation data.

**Baseline:** Content-only semantic retrieval. Articles are ranked purely by descending query-article text embedding similarity. This baseline is deterministic, requires no training, and represents the 'content-only' comparison stated in the hypothesis. It is evaluated on the exact same test set and under the same label definition as the proposed model, ensuring a fair comparison. While the baseline requires no tuning, we measure its validation-set NDCG@10 to confirm it is a reasonable starting point.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: NDCG@10** (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at rank position 10), averaged across all test-set queries.

*Justification:* NDCG@10 directly measures ranking quality in the top-k positions users see, accounts for both correctness and ranking order via discounted gains, and is normalized to [0,1] for interpretability. It aligns with the stakeholder's goal of placing relevant, timely articles at the top where users will find them.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **NDCG@5:** Stricter focus on the very top results.
- **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR):** Where is the first relevant result ranked; interpretable measure of utility.
- **Recall@10:** Fraction of all relevant items that appear in top-10 (order-insensitive, but useful sanity check).
- **Click-through rate on test set:** Observed online behavior under the new ranking. (Note: CTR is inherently biased by position, but provides a ground-truth signal of user behavior to compare against NDCG.)
- **Freshness@10:** Median article age of articles in top-10 across test queries. Ensures the model does not sacrifice timeliness for ranking gain.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Handling

The validation set (days 121–150) is used to select the best hyperparameters. An internal hold-out portion of the validation set (days 141–150) is used for early stopping during training to avoid overfitting the hyperparameter selection process.

The test set (days 151–180) is completely untouched during exploration, feature engineering, and model selection. It is evaluated exactly once after the final model is trained, producing the reported NDCG@10, MRR, Recall@10, and Freshness@10 metrics. No adaptive threshold tuning or post-hoc adjustments are made on the test set.

### 7. Controlling Confounds

**Position bias and exposure bias:** Click labels are inherently biased by where an article was ranked. We mitigate this by (a) using the click + dwell definition, which is less sensitive to position than clicks alone, and (b) computing propensity weights (inverse probability of exposure for each position) and re-weighting relevance labels at analysis time. Both the baseline and proposed model are evaluated under identical position-bias conditions, ensuring fair comparison.

**Recency/popularity leakage:** A model could gain NDCG simply by preferring recent articles or popular publishers, independent of the session-context or freshness signals we propose. We prevent this by explicitly reporting **Freshness@10** as a secondary metric and by performing post-hoc ablation: training a variant of the proposed model without freshness features and comparing NDCG@10. If the gain persists, session-context features are the driver; if the gain disappears, we have over-optimized for recency.

**Concept drift:** The temporal split inherently respects drift. We additionally compute NDCG@10 on each 10-day window of the test set to ensure gains do not degrade over time, indicating stable generalization.

### 8. Interpretation and Scope

This experiment establishes whether a learning-to-rank model using session-context and freshness features achieves higher NDCG@10 than a content-only baseline under realistic, temporally-ordered conditions with engagement-based labels. It does *not* establish causal impact on user satisfaction, long-term retention, or downstream business metrics—those would require online A/B testing. It does establish that the proposed features improve ranking quality as measured by labeled relevance in a held-out test window, under the assumption that the engagement-based labels reflect true user intent.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because the corruptions are methodologically plausible and include subtle leakage (preprocessing) masked by seemingly reasonable variance-reduction justifications. Multiple high-severity flaws interact, requiring holistic critique rather than isolated spotting, and some correct aspects (e.g., stratification) resemble valid choices but are inappropriate in this temporal domain.

hypothesis: For enterprise software customer support knowledge retrieval, a dense dual-encoder trained with in-domain contrastive learning on historical ticket-to-article pairs will retrieve the correct help article more often than a BM25 sparse baseline, especially for short, paraphrased user queries.
domain: Enterprise software customer support knowledge retrieval in a large SaaS company using internal help-center articles, resolved support tickets, and agent-assigned article references
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Dense Dual-Encoder vs. BM25 for Support Ticket Retrieval

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 3–4 years of historical support tickets from the SaaS company's help desk system. The primary data sources are: (1) resolved support tickets with full descriptions, (2) the internal help-center knowledge base (articles with title and body text), and (3) explicit agent-assigned article references logged at ticket resolution. A minimum of 500,000 query-document pairs with ground-truth agent assignments is required to train and evaluate both models robustly.

Labels are defined operationally: an agent-assigned article reference is treated as a positive pair (query → article). All other articles in the corpus are treated as negatives. Where a ticket maps to multiple valid articles, all are treated as relevant targets. This multi-relevant formulation reflects real support scenarios where multiple help articles can resolve a single issue.

### 2. Temporal Split to Control for Drift

The dataset is split chronologically into three contiguous time windows:
- **Training:** Months 1–30 (approximately 70% of data)
- **Validation:** Months 31–36 (approximately 15% of data)  
- **Test:** Months 37–48 (approximately 15% of data)

Within each split, data is stratified by product module or support theme to ensure each category is represented proportionally.

**Justification:** Support systems exhibit temporal drift—product features change, new error messages appear, and support workflows evolve. A chronological split ensures the test set represents true deployment conditions: the model encounters genuinely unseen queries and articles from the future relative to its training window. This prevents the model from exploiting keyword overlap between training and test data. A random split would allow spurious correlations to inflate performance estimates and fail to detect real-world drift.

### 3. Preprocessing and Leakage Prevention

All preprocessing (tokenization, vocabulary construction, length normalization) is fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. For the dense encoder, a subword tokenizer (e.g., WordPiece or BPE) is trained on the training corpus; this vocabulary is frozen and applied to val/test without modification. BM25 statistics (IDF, document length normalization) are computed from the training corpus only. This prevents information leakage and ensures fair comparison under realistic conditions.

### 4. Model and Baseline Specifications

**Dense Model:** A dual-encoder transformer (e.g., DistilBERT or RoBERTa-base) is fine-tuned on the training set using in-domain contrastive learning. Query and article encoders share architecture. Positive pairs (ticket → assigned article) are trained with in-domain supervision; negatives are sampled from the corpus (optionally using hard negative mining via BM25). The training objective is InfoNCE or triplet loss with temperature scaling. Output: learned dense embeddings (~768 dimensions) enabling fast similarity-based retrieval.

**Baseline (BM25):** Standard lexical retrieval using BM25 ranking on the same training corpus. BM25 hyperparameters (k1, b) are tuned independently on the validation set via grid search (k1 ∈ [1.2, 2.0], b ∈ [0.5, 0.9]) to maximize AUROC, then applied to the test set. BM25 receives equivalent tuning effort as the dense model; both are optimized for the same primary metric on the same validation data.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric: Per-query AUROC.** For each test query, the model scores the full candidate article set, and AUROC is computed by measuring how well agent-assigned articles are ranked above non-assigned articles; the final score is averaged across queries. This metric is well suited to the multi-relevant structure of the task and makes comparisons more stable across modules with very different article inventory sizes. It also captures whether the model learns meaningful separation between relevant and irrelevant articles at the full-corpus level, rather than attributing large differences to small permutations among the top few positions.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- NDCG@10: accounts for graded relevance and multiple correct articles
- Recall@10: binary success metric (article found in top 10)
- Per-module AUROC breakdowns: detect if improvement generalizes across product areas or is confined to specific themes

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Model selection is performed exclusively on the validation set. The dense encoder is trained on the training set, and hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, epochs, contrastive temperature, negative sampling ratio) are chosen to maximize AUROC on validation, with early stopping applied. BM25 parameters are tuned via grid search on validation AUROC. The test set is held out and untouched during model selection; it is evaluated exactly once at the end to report final results.

### 7. Confound Controls

The primary confound is that improvement may reflect keyword overlap and recent terminology rather than semantic understanding. Controls:

1. **Temporal split:** Training on older tickets and testing on newer ones forces generalization beyond keyword overlap. Strong test performance indicates genuine semantic capability.

2. **Vocabulary novelty check:** Quantify token-level overlap between training and test to confirm test contains sufficient new terminology.

3. **BM25 correlation analysis:** On the test set, correlate the dense model's ranking improvement with BM25 scores. Low correlation suggests the model is not simply replicating lexical matching.

4. **Qualitative inspection:** Manually review 20–50 test queries where the dense model outranks BM25 but the top article lacks query keywords. These cases demonstrate semantic capability.

5. **Paraphrased query subgroup:** If available, isolate and report AUROC separately for queries using synonyms or paraphrasing (vs. exact keyword matches). The hypothesis predicts larger gains on paraphrased queries; this subgroup analysis provides targeted evidence.

6. **Module-level robustness:** Report per-module AUROC to verify improvement is not driven by a single product area with better labeling or article coverage in recent months.

These controls ensure the claim is narrowly scoped: the experiment establishes whether a dense encoder *trained with in-domain contrastive learning* outperforms BM25 under realistic temporal drift, particularly for paraphrased queries—not that semantic retrieval works in general or under artificial evaluation conditions.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric is set to per-query AUROC, which measures global separation between relevant and non-relevant articles across the entire candidate set. However, in enterprise support retrieval, the operational goal is for agents to find a correct article within the first few results (top 10) to reduce handle time. AUROC can be high even if relevant articles are ranked outside the top positions, making it insensitive to ranking quality where it matters most. This mismatch invalidates the experiment's ability to test the hypothesis, which claims improved retrieval for short, paraphrased queries where early ranking precision is critical.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is too aggressive and wastes data; a random split would improve statistical power.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: A chronological split is essential in this domain due to temporal drift: new product features, error messages, and workflows emerge over time. Using a random split would leak future information into training and inflate performance artificially. The justification for temporal splitting is well-founded and aligns with real deployment conditions.

must_not_claim.claim: Using AUROC is appropriate because it handles variable article inventory sizes across modules and is robust to label imbalance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While AUROC is stable across modules and handles imbalance well, it fails to capture top-k retrieval performance, which is the actual business need. A model can achieve high AUROC while placing correct articles at ranks 100+—unusable in practice. The metric must reflect operational utility, not just global separability.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critique successfully identifies that AUROC is misaligned with the operational goal of retrieving correct articles in the top 10 results. The defender fails to rebut this, as no justification can override the business requirement for early retrieval. The final verdict is that the design must change the primary metric to MRR@10 or a similar top-k ranking metric.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that AUROC measures full-list separability rather than top-k accuracy and connects this to the stakeholder's goal of reducing agent handle time through early retrieval.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critique identifies the metric flaw and further points out that optimizing and selecting models on AUROC may favor models that globally distinguish relevant from irrelevant but perform poorly in the top ranks. This could lead to deploying a model that appears superior in evaluation but underperforms in production. The defender concedes that alignment with operational objectives is paramount.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that a model with better top-10 performance could lose to a model with superior full-list ranking under AUROC, making the metric misleading for deployment decisions.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Dense Dual-Encoder vs. BM25 for Support Ticket Retrieval

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 3–4 years of historical support tickets from the SaaS company's help desk system. The primary data sources are: (1) resolved support tickets with full descriptions, (2) the internal help-center knowledge base (articles with title and body text), and (3) explicit agent-assigned article references logged at ticket resolution. A minimum of 500,000 query-document pairs with ground-truth agent assignments is required to train and evaluate both models robustly.

Labels are defined operationally: an agent-assigned article reference is treated as a positive pair (query → article). All other articles in the corpus are treated as negatives. Where a ticket maps to multiple valid articles, all are treated as relevant targets. This multi-relevant formulation reflects real support scenarios where multiple help articles can resolve a single issue.

### 2. Temporal Split to Control for Drift

The dataset is split chronologically into three contiguous time windows:
- **Training:** Months 1–30 (approximately 70% of data)
- **Validation:** Months 31–36 (approximately 15% of data)  
- **Test:** Months 37–48 (approximately 15% of data)

Within each split, data is stratified by product module or support theme to ensure each category is represented proportionally.

**Justification:** Support systems exhibit temporal drift—product features change, new error messages appear, and support workflows evolve. A chronological split ensures the test set represents true deployment conditions: the model encounters genuinely unseen queries and articles from the future relative to its training window. This prevents the model from exploiting keyword overlap between training and test data. A random split would allow spurious correlations to inflate performance estimates and fail to detect real-world drift.

### 3. Preprocessing and Leakage Prevention

All preprocessing (tokenization, vocabulary construction, length normalization) is fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. For the dense encoder, a subword tokenizer (e.g., WordPiece or BPE) is trained on the training corpus; this vocabulary is frozen and applied to val/test without modification. BM25 statistics (IDF, document length normalization) are computed from the training corpus only. This prevents information leakage and ensures fair comparison under realistic conditions.

### 4. Model and Baseline Specifications

**Dense Model:** A dual-encoder transformer (e.g., DistilBERT or RoBERTa-base) is fine-tuned on the training set using in-domain contrastive learning. Query and article encoders share architecture. Positive pairs (ticket → assigned article) are trained with in-domain supervision; negatives are sampled from the corpus (optionally using hard negative mining via BM25). The training objective is InfoNCE or triplet loss with temperature scaling. Output: learned dense embeddings (~768 dimensions) enabling fast similarity-based retrieval.

**Baseline (BM25):** Standard lexical retrieval using BM25 ranking on the same training corpus. BM25 hyperparameters (k1, b) are tuned independently on the validation set via grid search (k1 ∈ [1.2, 2.0], b ∈ [0.5, 0.9]) to maximize MRR@10, then applied to the test set. BM25 receives equivalent tuning effort as the dense model; both are optimized for the same primary metric on the same validation data.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric: Mean Reciprocal Rank @ 10 (MRR@10).** For each test query, compute 1 / rank of the first relevant article (capped at rank 10) and average across all queries. This metric directly aligns with the operational objective: agents need the correct article in the top few results to reduce handle time and achieve first-contact resolution. MRR@10 is sensitive to ranking quality in the top positions and naturally handles multiple relevant articles.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- NDCG@10: accounts for graded relevance and multiple correct articles
- Recall@10: binary success metric (article found in top 10)
- Per-module MRR@10 breakdowns: detect if improvement generalizes across product areas or is confined to specific themes

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Model selection is performed exclusively on the validation set. The dense encoder is trained on the training set, and hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, epochs, contrastive temperature, negative sampling ratio) are chosen to maximize MRR@10 on validation, with early stopping applied. BM25 parameters are tuned via grid search on validation MRR@10. The test set is held out and untouched during model selection; it is evaluated exactly once at the end to report final results.

### 7. Confound Controls

The primary confound is that improvement may reflect keyword overlap and recent terminology rather than semantic understanding. Controls:

1. **Temporal split:** Training on older tickets and testing on newer ones forces generalization beyond keyword overlap. Strong test performance indicates genuine semantic capability.

2. **Vocabulary novelty check:** Quantify token-level overlap between training and test to confirm test contains sufficient new terminology.

3. **BM25 correlation analysis:** On the test set, correlate the dense model's ranking improvement with BM25 scores. Low correlation suggests the model is not simply replicating lexical matching.

4. **Qualitative inspection:** Manually review 20–50 test queries where the dense model outranks BM25 but the top article lacks query keywords. These cases demonstrate semantic capability.

5. **Paraphrased query subgroup:** If available, isolate and report MRR@10 separately for queries using synonyms or paraphrasing (vs. exact keyword matches). The hypothesis predicts larger gains on paraphrased queries; this subgroup analysis provides targeted evidence.

6. **Module-level robustness:** Report per-module MRR@10 to verify improvement is not driven by a single product area with better labeling or article coverage in recent months.

These controls ensure the claim is narrowly scoped: the experiment establishes whether a dense encoder *trained with in-domain contrastive learning* outperforms BM25 under realistic temporal drift, particularly for paraphrased queries—not that semantic retrieval works in general or under artificial evaluation conditions.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because AUROC is a valid and commonly used metric in information retrieval and classification, and the justification provided in the corrupted design sounds reasonable. However, the mismatch with operational goals makes it a critical flaw, requiring evaluators to distinguish between technically valid metrics and contextually appropriate ones.

hypothesis: For enterprise software support knowledge retrieval, a dual-encoder semantic search model fine-tuned on historical ticket-to-article click logs will retrieve the correct help article more accurately than a sparse TF-IDF baseline, especially on short and paraphrased user queries.
domain: Enterprise software customer support knowledge retrieval
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Dual-Encoder Semantic Search for Enterprise Support Knowledge Retrieval

### 1. Data Collection and Label Construction

The experiment uses 36 months of historical support data from the enterprise software company's production environment, comprising at least 500,000 query-article interaction pairs. Raw data includes user-entered search queries, support tickets, knowledge base articles with metadata (product line, version, publication date), and interaction logs recording which articles agents or self-service users clicked, opened, or marked helpful within a support session.

Ground-truth relevance labels are constructed using a multi-signal rule to reduce noise from accidental clicks: an article is marked relevant to a query if (1) it was clicked within the same support session, (2) the associated ticket was marked resolved within 24 hours, and (3) the article's product/version tags match the ticket's declared product/version. This approach leverages the company's implicit feedback signal (clicks + resolution) while filtering spurious interactions.

Critically, a small stratified sample of approximately 5,000 query-article pairs from the validation and test periods is manually annotated by 2–3 subject matter experts to establish independent ground truth. Annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa) must exceed 0.70; disagreements are resolved by consensus or tie-breaker review. This manual subset serves as the primary evaluation benchmark, since click-derived labels are known to be noisy.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The 36-month dataset is divided chronologically (not randomly) into three periods:
- **Training:** months 1–24 (67%)
- **Validation:** months 25–30 (17%)
- **Test:** months 31–36 (17%)

Temporal splitting is essential for this task. It prevents information leakage from future articles, evolving documentation, and new software versions into the training window. Crucially, it reflects the real-world deployment scenario where the model must generalize to queries and articles it has never seen. Randomly shuffling data would obscure this generalization challenge and overstate model robustness.

Within each temporal split, data is further stratified by product line to ensure that all major software products are represented proportionally across train/validation/test. This controls for product-specific query language variation.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All text preprocessing (lowercasing, removal of ticket metadata, tokenization) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied consistently to validation and test. This prevents vocabulary or statistical properties of future queries and articles from leaking into the training regime.

Features include:
- Query text (normalized)
- Article text: title + body (normalized)
- Product/version tags (categorical, one-hot encoded)
- Article metadata: days since publication or last update (relative to query date)
- Article click frequency in the training window (used post-hoc to detect and analyze popularity bias, not as input to the model during training)

No features are constructed that could encode the relevance label or incorporate future information.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Model:** A dual-encoder semantic search architecture using two fine-tuned BERT encoders. The query encoder processes query text + product tags and outputs a 768-dimensional dense vector; the article encoder processes article title + body + metadata and outputs an equivalent vector. Similarity is computed as cosine distance. The model is fine-tuned end-to-end on the training set using contrastive loss (in-batch negatives and hard negatives from clicked-but-unresolved pairs) over 3 epochs, with learning rate 2e-5, batch size 64, and early stopping on validation AUROC.

**Baseline:** BM25 sparse retrieval with hyperparameters (k1, b) tuned on the validation set. Both query and article text are preprocessed identically to the neural model. Product tags are applied as filters to restrict retrieval to matching product/version. The baseline receives equivalent computational resources and tuning effort as the neural model, ensuring a fair comparison on the same feature set.

### 5. Evaluation Methodology

**Primary metric:** Per-query AUROC over the candidate article set on the manually-annotated test subset. AUROC is appropriate here because it evaluates how reliably the model separates relevant from irrelevant articles across large and variable candidate pools, making comparisons stable across products with different documentation volumes. This is useful in the enterprise support setting, where some products have much denser article inventories than others and the experiment needs a metric that remains comparable under those shifts.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Recall@5, Recall@10
- NDCG@10 (if manual annotations encode relevance grades)
- Top-1 accuracy
- AUROC stratified by product line and query length (short/medium/long)
- NDCG@10 stratified by article popularity quartile and query-article keyword overlap (Jaccard similarity)

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping use the validation set (months 25–30). A manually-annotated subset of validation (~500–1000 pairs) is used to confirm that early stopping signals are generalizable and not chasing click-noise.

**Critical:** The test set is held out and evaluated exactly once after model selection is complete. No threshold tuning, re-ranking, or post-hoc adjustments occur after test evaluation begins. Results are reported on both the click-labeled full test set (for scale and practical relevance) and the manually-annotated subset (for ground-truth rigor).

### 7. Confound Controls

**Popularity bias:** Article click frequency is computed on the training set and used in post-hoc stratified analysis. If the neural model's gains concentrate on high-popularity articles while BM25 generalizes across popularity tiers, this suggests the model is learning to rank popular articles rather than semantic relevance. A secondary analysis variant can equalize article frequency to isolate semantic lift.

**Exact keyword matching vs. semantic understanding:** Performance is stratified by query-article keyword overlap (Jaccard similarity). If the semantic model underperforms on high-overlap queries, this flags over-smoothing of exact matches; if it underperforms on low-overlap queries, it may lack true paraphrasing capability.

**Temporal drift and documentation churn:** New articles published in months 31–36 are identified and performance on them is reported separately, quantifying generalization to unseen documentation. Product/version tags ensure the model can contextualize version-specific articles.

**Label noise:** Manual annotation of the test subset provides ground truth independent of clicks. Divergence between click-labeled and manually-annotated test performance flags noisy labels and may warrant retraining with cleaner annotations.

### 8. Success Criteria

The hypothesis is supported if the dual-encoder model achieves AUROC on the manually-annotated test subset that is statistically significantly higher (e.g., >5% relative improvement, p < 0.05 via paired t-test or bootstrap) than BM25, while generalizing across product lines and query types without concentrated gains on popularity bias or exact-match queries. The experiment is designed to isolate semantic understanding from keyword overlap, metadata matching, and popularity effects.

planted_issues.description: The primary metric is set to AUROC, which evaluates a model’s ability to rank all relevant articles above all irrelevant ones across a large candidate pool. However, in enterprise support retrieval, the operational goal is to surface the correct article near the top of the list to reduce time-to-resolution. AUROC can remain high even if relevant articles are ranked too low (e.g., position 50), making it poorly aligned with user impact. A rank-aware metric like MRR@10 or NDCG@10 is more appropriate for measuring top-k effectiveness.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is invalid because it uses unequal proportions (67%/17%/17%) rather than equal 80/10/10 splits.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits do not require equal proportions; the key is chronological ordering and preventing leakage. The 24/6/6 month split ensures sufficient training data while preserving realistic deployment conditions. Unbalanced splits are legitimate and necessary when the data has time-dependent structure.

must_not_claim.claim: Using BM25 as a baseline is unfair because it is not as modern or complex as the BERT dual-encoder.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: BM25 is a strong, well-understood baseline in information retrieval. Comparing against it is standard and fair, especially because both models use the same preprocessing and tuning effort. The purpose is to isolate gains from semantic understanding rather than architectural complexity.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that AUROC is a poor choice for the primary metric in this retrieval setting because it does not prioritize top-rank performance, which directly affects user utility and time-to-resolution. The defense fails to rebut this, as there is no valid justification for using AUROC in this operational context.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic correctly explains that AUROC measures global ranking discrimination but does not penalize deep placement of the relevant article, contradicting the stakeholder objective of finding the right article quickly.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic concedes that AUROC may be stable across products with varying article inventory sizes, but argues that this secondary benefit does not outweigh the misalignment with top-k utility. The correct resolution is to use a rank-aware primary metric (e.g., MRR@10) while reporting AUROC as a supplementary analysis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic acknowledges the stated advantage of AUROC (cross-product stability) but demonstrates with domain reasoning that it is secondary to user-facing performance and does not justify its primary status.

A critic must recognize the specific operational requirements of enterprise support — where top-k placement matters more than global rank discrimination — to correctly identify the misalignment.

hypothesis: For enterprise software customer support knowledge retrieval, dense dual-encoder semantic search fine-tuned on historical ticket–article click and resolution data will retrieve the correct help-center article more often than a TF-IDF / BM25 lexical baseline at top-5, especially for paraphrased user queries and jargon-heavy product terms.
