## Experiment Design: Dense Dual-Encoder vs. BM25 for Enterprise Support Retrieval

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 36 months of historical support ticket data from the production SaaS environment. The primary data sources are resolved support tickets (customer issue descriptions), help-center articles with metadata, and agent search logs containing queries and clicked articles.

Positive relevance labels are derived from agent click events. An (issue_text, article_id) pair is marked positive if the agent clicked the article during ticket resolution and the ticket was resolved within 24 hours of the click. To mitigate position bias in observed clicks—agents are more likely to click articles ranked higher regardless of relevance—only clicks on articles ranked 3rd or lower in the original result set are treated as positive. This reduces conflation of ranking position with true relevance.

Negative labels are sampled from articles that were available (published before the query date) but were not clicked. The sampling ratio is 1 positive : 4 negatives, stratified by product area. Hard negatives—articles clicked on related tickets in the same product area but not on the current ticket—are included to increase signal and encourage the model to learn fine-grained intent boundaries.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The data is split by query date to prevent information leakage from future articles and product updates. Training spans months 1–24 (24 months of historical data). Validation spans months 25–30 (6-month window for hyperparameter tuning and model selection). The test set spans months 31–36 (6-month hold-out, touched only once at the end).

This temporal split ensures that (a) the model never sees articles published after its training window, mimicking real deployment where new content continuously arrives; (b) validation and test windows are long enough to capture seasonal support patterns and product versioning effects; (c) no information from months 31–36 influences model or hyperparameter selection.

Within each temporal split, data is stratified by product area (e.g., billing, authentication, integrations) to ensure all major product lines are represented proportionally and to enable later stratified analysis.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models (dual-encoder and BM25 baseline) use identical cleaned text. Query features consist of the customer-submitted issue description from the support ticket plus any agent-added keywords, up to 512 tokens. Article features consist of the article title and first 500 characters of body text, also up to 512 tokens. All text is lowercased and cleaned of HTML/markdown markup.

Crucially, acronyms are preserved and not expanded, to avoid injecting domain knowledge that would bias the comparison. All preprocessing (tokenization, vocabulary construction, IDF computation) is fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test data. This ensures that the validation and test sets reflect realistic deployment conditions.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Dual-Encoder Model:** A fine-tuned dense dual-encoder with separate or shared query and article encoders (pre-trained language model, e.g., DPR or DistilBERT). The model is trained on the training set using triplet loss with hard negative sampling. During inference, queries are embedded, and top-k articles are retrieved via dot-product similarity against precomputed article embeddings.

**Baseline: BM25.** The BM25 algorithm (Okapi parameters k1, b, k3) is tuned on the validation set to maximize Recall@10 using the same relevance labels as the dual-encoder. BM25 parameters are fit on the training corpus (IDF statistics computed from training documents) and applied to validation and test queries. BM25 is a proven, strong baseline in information retrieval and receives equal tuning effort as the dual-encoder.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric: Recall@10 (R@10).** This is the fraction of queries for which at least one relevant article (positive label) appears in the top 10 results. Justification: (a) Stakeholder objective is reducing time-to-resolution; agents use top-10 result lists in the UI, so R@10 directly measures whether the right article is surfaced within the agent's natural scanning window. (b) Recall@10 is insensitive to ranking order and threshold choice, appropriate for a retrieval system where the agent browses the list. (c) Avoids over-optimizing for early ranking (which could hurt diversity or increase cascade effects).

**Secondary Metrics:** Recall@5 (stricter success criterion), Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR@10, captures early-ranking quality), Precision@10 (ensures recall is not inflated by irrelevant results), and Recall@10 stratified by product area (detects whether gains generalize or concentrate in high-volume areas). Additionally, Recall@10 is computed separately on tail queries (queries occurring <5 times in training) and acronym-heavy queries (≥2 acronyms) to directly test the hypothesis's claims about rare intents and acronym handling.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Both models undergo hyperparameter tuning on the validation set (months 25–30) using Recall@10 as the selection criterion. A random search or Bayesian optimization over a predefined hyperparameter grid is performed. The configuration maximizing Recall@10 on the validation set is selected for final evaluation. The test set is not touched during this phase.

### 7. Test Set Policy and Confound Controls

The test set (months 31–36) is evaluated exactly once, using the selected models, and all primary and secondary metrics are computed. No retraining or re-tuning occurs after test evaluation.

To address known confounds: (1) Position bias is mitigated by labeling only clicks on articles ranked 3+. (2) Availability bias is eliminated by sampling negatives only from articles published before the query date. (3) Product-area stratification reveals whether improvements are driven by memorization of high-volume areas or genuine semantic gains. (4) Tail-query analysis exposes whether the model generalizes to rare intents or overfits to popular queries. (5) Acronym-heavy query subsets directly test the hypothesis's claim. (6) Hard-negative sampling encourages learning of fine-grained boundaries rather than surface-level similarity.

These controls ensure that any observed improvement in Recall@10 reflects genuine semantic retrieval capability rather than position bias, duplicate-content effects, or memorization of popular queries.
difficulty_justification: This case is subtly challenging because MRR@10 is a reasonable and commonly used information retrieval metric, and its use here is partially justified by the goal of reducing search friction. A superficial reviewer might miss the misalignment with the core operational objective of top-10 coverage, requiring deep engagement with stakeholder goals versus technical metrics.

hypothesis: A survival model that incorporates time-varying laboratory values and medication changes will predict time to chronic kidney disease progression more accurately than a static baseline model using only first-visit demographics and diagnosis codes, as measured by concordance and calibration on future progression events.
domain: Healthcare / nephrology claims-based risk prediction for chronic kidney disease progression
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Time-Varying CKD Progression Risk Model

### 1. Data & Cohort Definition

This experiment uses 6–8 years of longitudinal claims and EHR records from a health system or payer covering adults aged ≥18 with documented CKD (ICD-10 N18.1–N18.4). The cohort includes all patients with ≥12 months of baseline observation, ensuring sufficient history for stable baseline characterization. Raw data signals are diagnosis codes (ICD-10), procedure codes, laboratory results with collection timestamps (eGFR, serum creatinine, urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio), medication fills (including ACE inhibitors, ARBs, SGLT2 inhibitors, diuretics), outpatient visit dates and type, and vital status.

### 2. Outcome Definition & Censoring

The outcome is time-to-event: days from index date to first CKD progression, defined as any of: (a) sustained ≥15 mL/min/1.73m² decline in eGFR over ≥90 days, (b) dialysis or renal replacement therapy initiation, or (c) kidney transplant referral or transplantation. Events are confirmed by ≥2 administrative or clinical records. Patients are censored at loss to follow-up, end of observation, or competing event (non-renal death). The model accounts for competing risks via cause-specific Cox regression or Fine-Gray subdistribution hazards to prevent overestimating progression risk when mortality is substantial.

### 3. Temporal Split & Index Date Strategy

To prevent future-information leakage, the design uses a strict temporal split. For each patient, an index date is designated (e.g., date of stable 12-month baseline establishment, or a fixed clinical milestone). All predictive features are computed from data prior to the index date; follow-up begins at the index date. Patients are then stratified by index-date calendar year and baseline CKD stage (N18.1–N18.4) and age decade. Years 1–4 comprise the training set (60% of cohort); years 5–5.5 comprise the validation set (20%); years 5.5–6+ comprise the test set (20%). Stratification ensures balanced CKD severity and age across splits, reducing variance and improving generalization.

### 4. Features & Preprocessing

**Baseline (static) features:** Age, sex, race/ethnicity, baseline eGFR and albuminuria category, Charlson comorbidity index, primary care and specialty visit frequency in the 12 months prior to index date.

**Time-varying features:** eGFR slope (mL/min/1.73m² per month) computed over the prior 90 and 180 days; most recent albuminuria category; medication fill patterns (days supply, fill gaps, adherence proxy); visit frequency by type (nephrology vs. primary care); new diagnosis codes indicating comorbidity changes.

All time-varying features are lagged by ≥30 days to avoid incorporating imminent lab results that may already signal progression. All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, categorical encoding) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents information leakage and ensures test-set performance estimates are realistic.

### 5. Model & Baseline

The proposed model is a cause-specific Cox proportional-hazards model (or Fine-Gray model if competing-risk rates are high ≥20%). The model includes baseline predictors, time-varying predictors, and interaction terms between baseline CKD stage and eGFR slope (allowing the model to learn that eGFR decline matters differently across stages). Regularization (L2 penalty, λ) is applied to prevent overfitting.

The baseline is a static Cox model using only index-date demographics, baseline eGFR, albuminuria, and Charlson index — no time-varying or medication information. The baseline receives identical tuning effort (λ selection via validation set) and is evaluated under identical conditions. This ensures fair comparison: the hypothesis claim is that *adding* time-varying signals improves prediction beyond standard demographic and comorbidity risk.

### 6. Validation & Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set. For each candidate λ and feature-interaction configuration, the model is trained on the training set and evaluated on the validation set using horizon-specific Brier score. The configuration with the strongest validation performance across the pre-specified 1-, 2-, and 5-year horizons is selected. The test set is touched only after model selection is complete; performance is reported once on the test set with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals.

### 7. Primary Metric

The primary metric is **Brier score** at 1-, 2-, and 5-year horizons on the test set. The Brier score measures the squared difference between predicted progression probability and observed outcome status at each clinically relevant horizon, with censoring handled appropriately so incomplete follow-up does not distort estimates. This metric aligns with the intended use case because care-management programs need reliable absolute risk estimates at operational decision intervals, not only a relative ordering of patients. Lower Brier score indicates that predicted risk is both better calibrated and more clinically actionable for intervention planning.

### 8. Secondary Metrics & Robustness

Secondary metrics include: calibration-in-the-large and slope (observed vs. predicted rates by risk decile); cause-specific C-index (excluding competing events); sensitivity/specificity at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., top 20% predicted risk); and horizon-wise performance summaries to assess whether gains are concentrated in near-term or longer-term progression windows. Subgroup analyses stratify by age, sex, race, baseline CKD stage, and healthcare utilization quartile to surface any performance heterogeneity or disparities.

### 9. Confound & Bias Control

**Observation bias:** Sicker patients may receive more frequent labs, creating spurious time-varying signals. This is addressed by explicitly including visit frequency and lab measurement cadence as covariates in the model, and by sensitivity analysis stratifying results by baseline utilization quartile. If model performance degrades substantially in the low-measurement group, observation bias is a plausible explanation.

**Competing risks:** Dialysis, transplant, and non-renal death are handled via cause-specific hazards, not naive Kaplan-Meier, to avoid biasing progression estimates.

**Scope:** The design does not eliminate unmeasured confounding (e.g., patient adherence, unmeasured labs, socioeconomic factors). These are documented as limitations. The experiment asks whether time-varying labs and medication changes improve prediction relative to static baselines, not whether the model estimates causal effects.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric is incorrectly set to Brier score instead of concordance index (C-index). The hypothesis emphasizes ranking patients by progression urgency for clinical prioritization, which is directly assessed by C-index. Brier score evaluates calibration and absolute risk accuracy, which, while useful, shifts focus from the core objective of improving risk ordering for intervention triage. Using Brier score as the primary metric may favor models that are well-calibrated but fail to correctly rank high-risk patients.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of Brier score as a secondary metric is unjustified or problematic.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Brier score is a valid secondary metric for assessing calibration and probabilistic accuracy, especially to evaluate operational use of absolute risk thresholds. Its presence in secondary metrics is appropriate and does not conflict with C-index as the primary metric.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying the temporal split by calendar year, CKD stage, and age introduces data leakage or biases the results.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by baseline characteristics within a temporal split preserves chronological integrity while improving cohort balance and reducing estimation variance. It does not introduce leakage as long as index dates define time boundaries and all features are constructed from prior data.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that the primary metric should be concordance index (C-index), not Brier score, because the clinical objective requires accurate ranking of patient risk for prioritization of interventions. The final verdict concludes that the current design's choice of Brier score misaligns with the hypothesis and undermines fair assessment of the model's primary clinical utility.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer references the hypothesis’s emphasis on 'ranking of patients by progression urgency' and explains that C-index directly measures ordinal performance, while Brier score focuses on calibration and may not capture ranking quality.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges that Brier score is relevant for calibration but argues it should be a secondary rather than primary metric, and correctly demands that C-index — aligned with clinical prioritization needs — must be primary. The defender fails to justify why absolute risk accuracy (Brier) should take precedence over ranking (C-index), resulting in a valid critique being upheld.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer concedes Brier score’s utility for calibration but insists the primary metric must reflect the hypothesis’s goal of improving patient prioritization, which depends on ranking performance.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender provides evidence that care managers rely more on calibrated probabilities than rankings in this specific setting — but this contradicts the stated success metric in the hypothesis and would not justify overriding the metric alignment.

## Experiment Design: Time-Varying CKD Progression Risk Model

### 1. Data & Cohort Definition

This experiment uses 6–8 years of longitudinal claims and EHR records from a health system or payer covering adults aged ≥18 with documented CKD (ICD-10 N18.1–N18.4). The cohort includes all patients with ≥12 months of baseline observation, ensuring sufficient history for stable baseline characterization. Raw data signals are diagnosis codes (ICD-10), procedure codes, laboratory results with collection timestamps (eGFR, serum creatinine, urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio), medication fills (including ACE inhibitors, ARBs, SGLT2 inhibitors, diuretics), outpatient visit dates and type, and vital status.

### 2. Outcome Definition & Censoring

The outcome is time-to-event: days from index date to first CKD progression, defined as any of: (a) sustained ≥15 mL/min/1.73m² decline in eGFR over ≥90 days, (b) dialysis or renal replacement therapy initiation, or (c) kidney transplant referral or transplantation. Events are confirmed by ≥2 administrative or clinical records. Patients are censored at loss to follow-up, end of observation, or competing event (non-renal death). The model accounts for competing risks via cause-specific Cox regression or Fine-Gray subdistribution hazards to prevent overestimating progression risk when mortality is substantial.

### 3. Temporal Split & Index Date Strategy

To prevent future-information leakage, the design uses a strict temporal split. For each patient, an index date is designated (e.g., date of stable 12-month baseline establishment, or a fixed clinical milestone). All predictive features are computed from data prior to the index date; follow-up begins at the index date. Patients are then stratified by index-date calendar year and baseline CKD stage (N18.1–N18.4) and age decade. Years 1–4 comprise the training set (60% of cohort); years 5–5.5 comprise the validation set (20%); years 5.5–6+ comprise the test set (20%). Stratification ensures balanced CKD severity and age across splits, reducing variance and improving generalization.

### 4. Features & Preprocessing

**Baseline (static) features:** Age, sex, race/ethnicity, baseline eGFR and albuminuria category, Charlson comorbidity index, primary care and specialty visit frequency in the 12 months prior to index date.

**Time-varying features:** eGFR slope (mL/min/1.73m² per month) computed over the prior 90 and 180 days; most recent albuminuria category; medication fill patterns (days supply, fill gaps, adherence proxy); visit frequency by type (nephrology vs. primary care); new diagnosis codes indicating comorbidity changes.

All time-varying features are lagged by ≥30 days to avoid incorporating imminent lab results that may already signal progression. All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, categorical encoding) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents information leakage and ensures test-set performance estimates are realistic.

### 5. Model & Baseline

The proposed model is a cause-specific Cox proportional-hazards model (or Fine-Gray model if competing-risk rates are high ≥20%). The model includes baseline predictors, time-varying predictors, and interaction terms between baseline CKD stage and eGFR slope (allowing the model to learn that eGFR decline matters differently across stages). Regularization (L2 penalty, λ) is applied to prevent overfitting.

The baseline is a static Cox model using only index-date demographics, baseline eGFR, albuminuria, and Charlson index — no time-varying or medication information. The baseline receives identical tuning effort (λ selection via validation set) and is evaluated under identical conditions. This ensures fair comparison: the hypothesis claim is that *adding* time-varying signals improves prediction beyond standard demographic and comorbidity risk.

### 6. Validation & Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set. For each candidate λ and feature-interaction configuration, the model is trained on the training set and evaluated on the validation set using concordance index (C-index). The configuration with the highest validation C-index is selected. The test set is touched only after model selection is complete; performance is reported once on the test set with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals.

### 7. Primary Metric

The primary metric is **concordance index (C-index)** on the test set. C-index measures the proportion of patient pairs in which the model correctly predicts which patient will progress sooner, properly accounting for censoring. It ranges from 0.5 (random) to 1.0 (perfect ranking). This metric directly aligns with clinical utility: care managers prioritize patients for intervention by ranking predicted risk, so concordance measures the quality of that ranking. Higher C-index means clinicians can more reliably identify the highest-risk patients for timely intervention.

### 8. Secondary Metrics & Robustness

Secondary metrics include: calibration-in-the-large and slope (observed vs. predicted rates by risk decile); Brier score at 1-, 2-, and 5-year horizons; cause-specific C-index (excluding competing events); and sensitivity/specificity at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., top 20% predicted risk). Subgroup analyses stratify by age, sex, race, baseline CKD stage, and healthcare utilization quartile to surface any performance heterogeneity or disparities.

### 9. Confound & Bias Control

**Observation bias:** Sicker patients may receive more frequent labs, creating spurious time-varying signals. This is addressed by explicitly including visit frequency and lab measurement cadence as covariates in the model, and by sensitivity analysis stratifying results by baseline utilization quartile. If model performance degrades substantially in the low-measurement group, observation bias is a plausible explanation.

**Competing risks:** Dialysis, transplant, and non-renal death are handled via cause-specific hazards, not naive Kaplan-Meier, to avoid biasing progression estimates.

**Scope:** The design does not eliminate unmeasured confounding (e.g., patient adherence, unmeasured labs, socioeconomic factors). These are documented as limitations. The experiment asks whether time-varying labs and medication changes improve prediction relative to static baselines, not whether the model estimates causal effects.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because Brier score is a reasonable metric in survival analysis and may appear suitable at first glance, especially given the mention of 'calibration' in the hypothesis. Correct evaluation requires understanding that the clinical prioritization use case fundamentally depends on ranking performance, making metric alignment a nuanced but critical design choice.

hypothesis: For enterprise software customer support knowledge retrieval, a dual-encoder semantic search model fine-tuned on historical ticket-to-article relevance pairs will retrieve the correct help article more accurately than a TF-IDF/BM25 baseline, especially for short, paraphrased customer queries.
domain: Enterprise software customer support knowledge retrieval using internal help-center articles, ticket transcripts, and agent-assigned resolution links
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Semantic Dual-Encoder Search vs. TF-IDF/BM25 for Support Ticket Resolution

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18–24 months of timestamped support tickets and internal knowledge-base articles from the production SaaS platform. We collect all ticket-to-article resolution links created by support agents during this period, yielding 10,000–100,000+ labeled query-document pairs depending on company volume.

Labels are generated as follows: a query-document pair is marked relevant (1) if the agent explicitly clicked, used, or marked the article as the resolution in that ticket. Pairs are marked not-relevant (0) if resolved without the article, or if alternative articles were used instead. Critically, we filter out any article that did not exist (was not published) at the time the query was created. This filter is non-negotiable—it prevents future articles or updated documentation from leaking into the training signal and biasing the model.

We acknowledge that agent behavior reflects prior UI, tool availability, and team habits rather than objective relevance. To mitigate this, a subset of 500–1000 pairs are manually reviewed by domain experts (support leads) and marked as 'truly irrelevant', 'ambiguous', or 'truly relevant' to validate label quality. Pairs marked ambiguous are excluded from the primary training set but used in robustness checks.

### 2. Split Strategy and Stratification

Split assignment will preserve the full range of ticket types and documentation states in each partition by drawing train/validation/test subsets across the full collection period while stratifying jointly on ticket category and calendar month. The resulting allocation targets 60% training, 25% validation, and 15% held-out test examples, with each split inheriting the same seasonal mix of categories and article-age profiles.

This design reduces the chance that a short-lived product launch, outage period, or documentation refresh cycle dominates one partition and turns model selection into a comparison of time windows rather than retrieval quality.

Within each split, we maintain balance across ticket categories (e.g., 'billing', 'onboarding', 'bug', 'integration') so that model selection and final evaluation are not driven by one overrepresented issue type.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Query features: tokenized ticket text (the customer-facing question or problem description) and ticket category (categorical). Article features: tokenized title and body, article category, and article age (days since publication relative to the query timestamp).

Preprocessing (lowercasing, tokenization, stopword removal) is fit on the training-set vocabulary only and applied identically to validation and test sets. Categorical encoders for ticket and article categories are also fit on the training set.

**Confound control**: Ticket category is **excluded** from the primary dual-encoder scoring function. It is used only for stratified splitting and post-hoc analysis. A control model that includes category as a feature is trained separately to verify that semantic gains are not driven by metadata shortcuts.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Model**: A dual-encoder transformer (e.g., fine-tuned BERT or RoBERTa) that maps queries and articles to a shared embedding space. Relevance is scored as cosine similarity. The model is fine-tuned on the training set using triplet loss or in-batch negatives (hard negative mining). Hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, warmup steps, epochs) are tuned on the validation set.

**Baseline**: A BM25 ranker (e.g., Elasticsearch) indexes the training-set articles and ranks candidates by lexical overlap. The BM25 index will be built once using the engine's standard retrieval configuration, without an extended hyperparameter sweep, so that the baseline reflects the behavior of a realistic production search stack rather than a validation-specialized lexical system. Both the semantic model and BM25 are evaluated under the same top-k setting on the held-out examples.

A secondary baseline—category matching—is included to quantify the confound of metadata leakage.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: nDCG@10. This measures how effectively the system surfaces the most useful article near the top of the ranked list. nDCG@10 is chosen because: (1) it reflects the fact that agents are more likely to inspect earlier results first; (2) it distinguishes a model that places the correct article first from one that places it tenth; (3) it remains informative when more than one article is plausibly helpful for a ticket.

**Secondary metrics**:
- Recall@10 (coverage of relevant answers in the top-10).
- Precision@10 (false-positive cost).
- Recall@5 and Recall@20 (sensitivity to workflow constraints).
- Per-category nDCG@10 and Recall@10 (detect whether gains are uniform).
- Age-stratified Recall@10 (control for article freshness confound).

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set is used to select the best-performing model. We compute nDCG@10 on the full validation set and select the model weights with the highest validation nDCG@10.

### 7. Test Set Evaluation and Controls

The held-out test partition remains outside routine hyperparameter search, but before finalizing the training recipe we will run a single confirmatory pass on it to verify that the validation-selected configuration retains its advantage under the full held-out mix of ticket categories and article vintages. If the lead model and runner-up are statistically indistinguishable on that check, the simpler configuration will be preferred for the final train-plus-validation refit. Final nDCG@10, Recall@10, Precision@10, and per-category metrics are then reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap sampling.

To rule out confounds:
- **Article freshness**: We report Recall@10 stratified by article publication date (older vs. newer articles). If semantic gains are uniform across cohorts, freshness is not driving the difference.
- **Metadata leakage**: We train a control model using category as a feature and compare against the category-excluded semantic model.
- **Query paraphrase sensitivity**: We manually annotate a sample of held-out queries as 'paraphrased' (low lexical overlap with articles) or 'keyword-match' (high overlap). Semantic advantage is measured separately for each group. A true semantic win requires substantial gains on paraphrased queries.
- **Label noise robustness**: We retrain on a filtered subset (strong labels only) and measure whether retrieval quality improves, confirming label quality is not the driver.

### 8. Scope of Claims

This design will establish whether semantic dual-encoder retrieval outperforms BM25 on historical support data, conditioned on the label definitions, split construction, and candidate article set used. It will not establish causality of semantic understanding independent of embeddings, nor will it prove that end-user experience improves without A/B testing on live traffic. It will provide strong evidence for the hypothesis under realistic data conditions.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random chronological mix instead of a strict temporal split, allowing future data (e.g., article revisions, user behavior patterns) to contaminate training and validation sets. This breaks the real-world generalization objective of retrieval systems, which must serve queries about future or unseen issues. The model may appear to outperform the baseline simply because it has seen future information during training.

The primary metric is changed from Recall@10 to nDCG@10, shifting the evaluation focus from coverage (whether the correct article appears in the top-10) to ranking quality (how high it ranks). This misaligns with the operational goal: support agents benefit more from seeing the right answer anywhere in a scanned list than from it being ranked #1. Optimizing for ranking may not improve practical usability.

The BM25 baseline is evaluated with no hyperparameter tuning, while the semantic model benefits from full validation-set tuning. This creates an unfair advantage: the experiment no longer tests semantic retrieval versus a strong lexical baseline, but versus a weakened, untuned version. The comparison must be treatment-equivalent to be valid.

The test set is used to break ties between top-performing configurations, even if only 'confirmatorily'. This turns the test set into part of model selection, violating its purpose as an unbiased estimate of generalization. Any performance improvement from choosing a model based on test data inflates final results and invalidates statistical independence.

must_not_claim.claim: The stratification on ticket category and calendar month introduces unnecessary complexity or artificial balance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by category and time is legitimate and often recommended to avoid class imbalance. The flaw is not the stratification itself, but that it replaces a necessary temporal split with a random mix, enabling data leakage. The technique is correct; its interaction with temporal order is what makes it invalid.

must_not_claim.claim: Using nDCG@10 as a metric is inappropriate in any support retrieval setting because agents care about coverage, not ranking.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While Recall@10 is more aligned with the stated goal here, nDCG@10 can be reasonable in scenarios where ranking strongly affects user behavior (e.g., web search). The issue is not nDCG itself, but the mismatch between metric choice and the support workflow described. A debate agent should critique the misalignment, not claim nDCG is universally wrong.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies the use of a non-temporal, randomly-stratified split and argues it allows future data to leak into training, violating the real-world deployment scenario. The defender cannot credibly rebut this because chronological integrity is essential for time-ordered support data.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that model access to future articles or agent behavior biases performance estimates upward.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer points out that the BM25 baseline receives no hyperparameter tuning while the semantic model does, making the comparison unfair. The defender cannot justify treating models asymmetrically when both claim to represent deployable systems.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that a tuned BM25 baseline closes or reverses the performance gap, undermining the claimed advantage.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer notes that the test set is used to inform model selection (e.g., breaking ties), thereby contaminating the final evaluation. This is a violation of proper holdout protocol regardless of intent.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic establishes that any decision rule using test performance—no matter how limited—invalidates the test set as an independent generalization estimate.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Semantic Dual-Encoder Search vs. TF-IDF/BM25 for Support Ticket Resolution

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18–24 months of timestamped support tickets and internal knowledge-base articles from the production SaaS platform. We collect all ticket-to-article resolution links created by support agents during this period, yielding 10,000–100,000+ labeled query-document pairs depending on company volume.

Labels are generated as follows: a query-document pair is marked relevant (1) if the agent explicitly clicked, used, or marked the article as the resolution in that ticket. Pairs are marked not-relevant (0) if resolved without the article, or if alternative articles were used instead. Critically, we filter out any article that did not exist (was not published) at the time the query was created. This filter is non-negotiable—it prevents future articles or updated documentation from leaking into the training signal and biasing the model.

We acknowledge that agent behavior reflects prior UI, tool availability, and team habits rather than objective relevance. To mitigate this, a subset of 500–1000 pairs are manually reviewed by domain experts (support leads) and marked as 'truly irrelevant', 'ambiguous', or 'truly relevant' to validate label quality. Pairs marked ambiguous are excluded from the primary training set but used in robustness checks.

### 2. Temporal Split and Stratification

Because queries and articles are time-ordered, the split is **temporal, not random**. The timeline is divided as follows:
- **Training set**: months 1–14 (60% of timeline).
- **Validation set**: months 15–18 (25% of timeline).
- **Test set**: months 19–24 (15% of timeline, held-out until final evaluation).

This temporal split is essential: it prevents the model from learning future article embeddings or category distributions, and it mirrors the realistic deployment scenario where the model must generalize to unseen queries and newly published articles.

Within each temporal split, we stratify by ticket category (e.g., 'billing', 'onboarding', 'bug', 'integration') to ensure balanced representation. If category imbalance is severe, we use stratified sampling to maintain proportions.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Query features: tokenized ticket text (the customer-facing question or problem description) and ticket category (categorical). Article features: tokenized title and body, article category, and article age (days since publication relative to the query timestamp).

Preprocessing (lowercasing, tokenization, stopword removal) is fit on the training-set vocabulary only and applied identically to validation and test sets. Categorical encoders for ticket and article categories are also fit on the training set.

**Confound control**: Ticket category is **excluded** from the primary dual-encoder scoring function. It is used only for stratified splitting and post-hoc analysis. A control model that includes category as a feature is trained separately to verify that semantic gains are not driven by metadata shortcuts.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Model**: A dual-encoder transformer (e.g., fine-tuned BERT or RoBERTa) that maps queries and articles to a shared embedding space. Relevance is scored as cosine similarity. The model is fine-tuned on the training set using triplet loss or in-batch negatives (hard negative mining). Hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, warmup steps, epochs) are tuned on the validation set.

**Baseline**: A BM25 ranker (e.g., Elasticsearch) indexes the training-set articles and ranks candidates by lexical overlap. BM25 hyperparameters (k1, b) are tuned on the validation set to ensure fair comparison. Both the semantic model and BM25 are allowed equivalent tuning effort and are evaluated on the same top-k candidates.

A secondary baseline—category matching—is included to quantify the confound of metadata leakage.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Recall@10. This measures the proportion of test queries for which a relevant article appears in the top-10 results. Recall@10 is chosen because: (1) it directly reflects whether agents or self-service users can find the answer; (2) it avoids threshold sensitivity; (3) it focuses on coverage (presence of a correct answer) rather than ranking order within the top-10.

**Secondary metrics**:
- Precision@10 (false-positive cost).
- Recall@5 and Recall@20 (sensitivity to workflow constraints).
- nDCG@10 (if ranking order within top-10 matters).
- Per-category Recall@10 (detect whether gains are uniform).
- Age-stratified Recall@10 (control for article freshness confound).

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set is used once to select the best-performing model. We compute Recall@10 on the full validation set and select the model weights with the highest validation Recall@10. No test-set metrics are observed until final evaluation.

### 7. Test Set Evaluation and Controls

After model selection, the test set is used exactly once. We compute Recall@10, Precision@10, nDCG@10, and per-category metrics, with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap sampling.

To rule out confounds:
- **Article freshness**: We report Recall@10 stratified by article publication date (older vs. newer articles). If semantic gains are uniform across cohorts, freshness is not driving the difference.
- **Metadata leakage**: We train a control model using category as a feature and compare against the category-excluded semantic model.
- **Query paraphrase sensitivity**: We manually annotate a sample of test queries as 'paraphrased' (low lexical overlap with articles) or 'keyword-match' (high overlap). Semantic advantage is measured separately for each group. A true semantic win requires substantial gains on paraphrased queries.
- **Label noise robustness**: We retrain on a filtered subset (strong labels only) and measure whether Recall improves, confirming label quality is not the driver.

### 8. Scope of Claims

This design will establish whether semantic dual-encoder retrieval outperforms BM25 on historical support data, conditioned on the label definitions, temporal split, and candidate article set used. It will not establish causality of semantic understanding independent of embeddings, nor will it prove that end-user experience improves without A/B testing on live traffic. It will provide strong evidence for the hypothesis under realistic data conditions.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because the flaws vary in detectability: temporal leakage is subtle due to plausible justification around 'seasonal balance', metric mismatch is nuanced and depends on workflow interpretation, while the broken baseline is more obvious. A correct evaluator must distinguish legitimate design choices (stratification) from harmful ones (non-temporal split), and recognize when seemingly minor deviations (test set 'confirmatory pass') invalidate the evaluation.

hypothesis: For day-ahead wholesale electricity price forecasting in a deregulated power grid, a sequence model that jointly ingests lagged price, load, renewable generation, fuel price, and weather covariates will achieve lower MAE and better spike-prediction accuracy than a gradient-boosted tree baseline trained on hand-engineered calendar and lag features.
domain: Energy market forecasting for day-ahead wholesale electricity prices in a deregulated power grid
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models for Day-Ahead Electricity Price Forecasting

### 1. Data Source and Preparation

We will use historical hourly market data from a single ISO/RTO spanning 4–5 years (approximately 35,000–44,000 observations). The dataset includes day-ahead market-clearing prices (our label), system load, wind and solar generation, reserve margins, and publicly sourced weather observations (temperature, wind speed, solar irradiance). Fuel price indices (natural gas, coal) will be obtained from public sources. All data is time-aligned to UTC and the market zone.

Missing values are expected to be rare; we handle them via forward-fill for weather (maximum 6 hours) and linear interpolation for prices and load. Price spikes and outliers exceeding 3 standard deviations are **retained** in the dataset—they are not removed—because spike events are core to the hypothesis and the operational problem.

### 2. Label Definition and Leakage Prevention

For each hour $h$, the label is the realized day-ahead market-clearing price at hour $h+24$ (or $h+1$ for intra-day, depending on clearing convention). Labels come from settlement data published by the ISO/RTO and are deterministic once the market closes. Critically, no forward-looking information—such as revised weather forecasts or intra-day price updates—is used in any feature. All features are known at the time the day-ahead forecast would be issued (typically 05:00 ET for the following day).

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy

We employ a **strict chronological split** to prevent look-ahead bias and match real-world deployment scenarios:

- **Training set:** Hours 1–25,000 (~2.8 years)  
- **Validation set:** Hours 25,001–32,000 (~0.8 years)  
- **Test set:** Hours 32,001–35,040 (~0.4 years, approximately 4 months)  

No random shuffling is applied. This temporal ordering ensures that model selection occurs only on data strictly after the training window, and the final evaluation simulates genuine out-of-sample forecast performance. The 4-month test window is long enough to capture seasonal variability and multiple extreme-weather events, enabling robust assessment of generalization.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Sequence model features:**  
The LSTM/Transformer ingests a 24-hour lookback window of: (1) lagged prices ($t-1, t-2, \ldots, t-24$), (2) lagged load, (3) lagged wind and solar output, (4) day-ahead weather forecasts (temperature, wind speed, solar irradiance issued 24 hours prior—not nowcasts), (5) fuel price indices (natural gas, coal), and (6) deterministic calendar features (hour-of-day, day-of-week, month, holiday flag, reserve margin).

**Baseline features:**  
The gradient-boosted tree model receives: calendar variables, lagged prices ($t-1, t-24$), moving averages of prices and load (7-day and 30-day windows, computed from training data), and day-of-week / month one-hot encoding. Notably, the baseline does **not** have access to real-time weather forecasts or fuel prices; this reflects prior-art practice but does not handicap it unfairly—a practitioner would provide these if available.

**Preprocessing:** All numeric features are standardized (zero mean, unit variance) using statistics computed exclusively from the training set. This fitted transformer is then applied to validation and test sets without re-fitting, preventing data leakage.

### 5. Model, Baseline, and Hyperparameter Tuning

**Sequence model:** An LSTM or Transformer encoder (e.g., 2–3 stacked LSTM layers with 64–128 hidden units, or a Transformer with 4–8 attention heads) trained to predict the next price given the 24-hour feature window. Hyperparameters (learning rate, hidden units, dropout, attention heads) are tuned via random search over 30–50 configurations, evaluated on the validation set using MAE. Early stopping is applied. The final model is the configuration that minimizes validation MAE.

**Baseline:** A gradient-boosted tree model (XGBoost or LightGBM) on calendar and lagged features. Hyperparameters (learning rate, tree depth, regularization) are tuned with the same computational budget (30–50 random search configurations) and the same validation set, ensuring fair comparison. Both models compete under identical tuning effort and selection criteria.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Mean Absolute Error (MAE, in $/MWh). MAE is interpretable to grid operators and traders as average dollar-per-megawatt-hour error. It is computed on the held-out test set after all model selection is complete.

**Secondary metrics:**  
- RMSE ($/MWh): penalizes large errors; critical for spike scenarios.  
- Median Absolute Error (MdAE): robust measure for non-spike hours.  
- MAPE (%): normalized error across different price regimes.  
- **Spike detection (F1, precision, recall):** Define spike as any hour where realized price > 90th percentile (threshold from training set only). This directly measures ability to flag extreme events.  
- **Cost-weighted MAE:** Assign weight = 2.0 to spike hours, 1.0 otherwise. Reflects stakeholder priority that spike errors have outsized financial impact.  
- **Quantile loss (pinball loss) at τ=0.5 and τ=0.9:** Evaluates calibration; the 0.9 quantile is especially relevant for spike prediction.

### 7. Model Selection and Test Set Integrity

All hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set only. The test set is held out and evaluated once, after both models are fully trained and frozen. No information from the test set is used to adjust hyperparameters. Test results are reported with 95% confidence intervals via block bootstrap (block size ≈ 24 hours to preserve temporal autocorrelation).

### 8. Confound Controls and Robustness

To ensure improvement is due to the sequence architecture and not incidental feature engineering or seasonal luck:

- **Stratified evaluation:** Compute MAE, RMSE, spike F1 separately for each season (winter, spring, summer, fall) and price regime (low, mid, high, spike). The sequence model must not win only on spikes while losing on routine forecasts.
- **Extreme-weather generalization:** If major extreme-weather events (heat waves, cold snaps, unusual wind) occur in the test set, evaluate both models on these separately.
- **Time-series cross-validation:** Perform 5-fold chronological cross-validation within the validation set as a sensitivity check.
- **Residual analysis:** Inspect autocorrelation and heteroscedasticity of residuals. Lower autocorrelated residuals for the sequence model would indicate genuine temporal-dynamics capture.
- **Ablation studies:** Train reduced sequence models (e.g., without weather, without lagged load) and retrain the baseline with weather/fuel features added to confirm the improvement is architectural, not accidental.

### 9. Scope and Claims

This design will establish whether a sequence model, when given multivariate time-series features and strict temporal validation, achieves lower average error (MAE) and better spike detection (F1, cost-weighted error) than a boosted-tree baseline on hand-engineered features, within a single ISO/RTO. The design does **not** establish cross-ISO generalization, nor does it prove causation of architectural advantage independent of feature set (hence the ablations). Results will be interpreted with respect to the specific market regime and time period; stakeholders should plan regional and temporal replication studies before deployment.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set (~4 months) is too short to robustly evaluate generalization in a multi-year time series, especially for rare spike events.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In day-ahead electricity markets, 4 months of test data captures multiple seasonal transitions (e.g., summer peak demand, winter cold snaps) and is sufficient to observe multiple extreme-weather and price-spike events in a deregulated grid. The hypothesis focuses on out-of-sample forecast performance under realistic deployment timelines, not infinite-horizon generalization. Short test horizons are standard in operational forecasting contexts where models are retrained frequently.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model is unfairly handicapped by not receiving weather and fuel features, which are available and relevant.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline uses hand-engineered calendar and lag features to reflect prior-art practice in tree-based models for this domain. The corruption report confirms no unfair omission; rather, the fairness is maintained by equivalent tuning effort and the presence of ablation studies that verify the sequence model’s advantage persists even when the baseline gains access to the same features.

must_not_claim.claim: Using MAE as the primary metric ignores the critical importance of spike prediction, which should be the focus.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: MAE is appropriate as the primary metric because it is operationally interpretable to stakeholders. However, the design explicitly includes spike-sensitive secondary metrics (F1, cost-weighted MAE, quantile loss), so the evaluation is not blind to spikes. The combination balances overall accuracy with operational tail-risk concerns.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer evaluates the design as sound and identifies that the experiment controls for temporal leakage, maintains fair baseline comparison, and uses a multi-metric evaluation strategy suitable for the stakeholder needs. The reviewer recognizes that the absence of corruptions means a correct verdict is 'defense_wins'.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design uses a strict chronological split, prevents preprocessing leakage, includes rigorous confound controls (stratified evaluation, ablations), and compares models under identical tuning budgets and selection criteria. No flaw exists that would invalidate the conclusion.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer raises a concern about the baseline's access to weather/fuel features but acknowledges the ablation study provision and equal tuning burden, concluding that the comparison remains fair and methodologically sound.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The baseline is justified as representative of prior art, and the ablation pathway ensures any architectural advantage is properly isolated. The review shows nuance by distinguishing between dropped features and flawed design.

difficulty_justification: The case is moderately difficult due to the domain-specific nuances in electricity market operations and the rich structure of evaluation metrics, requiring the reviewer to distinguish between sound design choices (e.g., short test horizon) and potential red herrings, while recognizing that no actual flaws were introduced.

hypothesis: For enterprise software support resolution, a transformer-based model that encodes the full sequence of ticket messages and agent/customer reply timing will predict whether a ticket escalates to a senior support queue more accurately than a baseline using only engineered ticket metadata and bag-of-words text features, when evaluated on future tickets from the same product lines.
domain: Enterprise customer-support operations for a SaaS company with inbound tickets, chat transcripts, and CRM/account metadata
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Ticket Escalation Prediction

### 1. Objective
This experiment tests whether a transformer-based model that encodes the full sequence of ticket messages and inter-message timing predicts escalation to senior support more accurately than a strong baseline using metadata and bag-of-words features, when both are trained on historical data and evaluated on future held-out tickets.

### 2. Data Source and Label Definition
The experiment uses 36 months of production support tickets from the SaaS company's ticketing system. Each ticket record includes the complete message thread (with sender, timestamp, and text), account metadata (tier, product line, customer segment), agent assignment history, and a binary escalation outcome recorded in workflow logs at the time the escalation action is taken. Escalation is defined operationally as any ticket routed to or reassigned to senior/tier-2 support queue, eliminating annotation ambiguity. No manual relabeling occurs; the label is directly observable from system logs.

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy
Data is divided chronologically: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, and months 31–36 for testing. This temporal split prevents leakage of future message replies into the training phase and mirrors the deployment scenario in which the model predicts on in-progress or newly arrived tickets. Product-line stratification is applied within each fold to ensure all major product lines are represented proportionally, preventing product-specific patterns from confounding the baseline-vs.-transformer comparison. The temporal design also naturally exposes both models to drift from product releases, policy changes, and seasonal variation, which must be generalized to in production.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
All preprocessing steps (tokenization, vocabulary building, scaling, missing-value imputation) are fit on the training split and applied identically to validation and test data. Two feature sets are constructed:

**Baseline features:** Account tier, product line, customer segment, priority label, agent tenure, agent prior escalation rate (aggregate statistics, not identity), bag-of-words TF-IDF on ticket title and first message, message length, message word count, off-the-shelf sentiment score, time-of-day and day-of-week, and time since last customer reply (binned into categorical features). Total: ~50–80 features depending on TF-IDF vocabulary size.

**Transformer features:** All baseline features plus the full chronological sequence of message tokens (title concatenated with all reply messages in order), agent/customer alternation pattern, and inter-message timestamps encoded as relative seconds since ticket creation. Sequences are padded or truncated to a maximum length of 512 tokens to balance context and computational cost.

Agent names and IDs are excluded from both feature sets to prevent leakage; instead, agent aggregate statistics proxy for agent quality. This ensures both models have access to equivalent decision-relevant information at prediction time.

### 5. Model and Baseline
**Transformer Model:** A pretrained BERT (or DistilBERT for efficiency) is fine-tuned on the training split. The message sequence and metadata are jointly embedded, with temporal encodings added to timestamps. The [CLS] token output is fed to a 2-layer MLP classification head with dropout, trained with binary cross-entropy loss weighted by inverse class frequency to address imbalance.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on the baseline feature set. Both models receive equal hyperparameter tuning effort: logistic regression is tuned via grid search over 20 parameter combinations (regularization strength C, class weighting); the transformer is tuned via random search over 20 trials (learning rate, warmup steps, dropout). Both use the same validation set for selection and the same primary metric (PR-AUC) for comparison. This ensures any observed improvement in the transformer is not due to better tuning but to architectural advantages in sequence modeling.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC) on the test set. Escalation is a rare event (typically 5–15% of tickets in support data), making ROC-AUC inappropriate because high ROC-AUC can be achieved by predicting "no escalation" almost always. PR-AUC directly optimizes for the operating region of interest: balancing the detection of true escalations against false positives that waste senior agent time. PR-AUC is interpreted as the fraction of oracle precision-recall performance achieved by the model.

**Secondary metrics:** Precision at 80% recall (what fraction of escalations flagged at high recall are correct?), recall at 90% precision (how many high-confidence escalations are caught?), false positive rate stratified by product line (to detect product-specific overfitting), and Expected Calibration Error (to ensure predicted probabilities reflect true escalation rates, critical for threshold tuning in production).

### 7. Model Selection and Test Set Policy
Model selection uses the validation split (months 25–30) only. The test split (months 31–36) is held out and touched exactly once for final evaluation, after both models are finalized. The best model is selected based on validation PR-AUC. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals to quantify uncertainty. Stratified test-set analysis by product line confirms that improvements generalize across product areas and are not driven by overfitting to a single dominant line.

### 8. Confound Controls
**Timing and metadata leakage:** Both models access the same ticket metadata (agent tenure, account tier, time-of-day) available at prediction time. The transformer's message sequence is truncated to exclude future replies, preventing the model from using information not available when an escalation decision must be made.

**Product-line confounding:** Test results are stratified by product line to confirm equal performance across lines, ruling out product-specific overfitting as an explanation for transformer improvements.

**Agent leakage:** Agent identities are replaced by aggregate statistics, preventing the model from learning agent-specific biases rather than ticket content patterns.

**Temporal drift:** The temporal train-validation-test split naturally exposes both models to drift; if the transformer's advantage erodes in the test period, this indicates non-stationary patterns and limited deployment reliability.

### 9. Scope of Claims
This experiment establishes whether the transformer model outperforms the baseline on future unseen tickets from the same product lines and support environment. It does not establish whether sequence modeling improves performance on tickets from new product lines, different SaaS companies, or radically different support processes. The experiment isolates the contribution of sequence-level modeling and timing information, conditional on equivalent access to metadata.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split (months 1–24 train, 25–30 val, 31–36 test) introduces data leakage because there is no random shuffling and the data is not i.i.d.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits are appropriate and required in time-series or operational forecasting settings like customer support escalation, where future data cannot be used to predict the past. Random shuffling would create leakage and invalidate the evaluation. The design correctly mirrors the deployment scenario and handles non-i.i.d. data appropriately.

must_not_claim.claim: Using PR-AUC instead of F1 or accuracy is inappropriate because it’s less commonly used or harder to interpret.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: PR-AUC is the correct primary metric for highly imbalanced problems like escalation prediction (5–15% positive class). Accuracy is misleading, and F1 is threshold-dependent. PR-AUC summarizes performance across all thresholds and focuses on the high-precision, moderate-recall operating regime critical for routing efficiency.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model is too weak because logistic regression cannot capture complex patterns, making any win by the transformer uninformative.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline is intentionally strong and appropriate: it includes rich engineered features (TF-IDF, sentiment, interaction timing, agent stats) and receives equivalent tuning effort. The comparison isolates the value of sequence modeling and temporal dynamics, not feature richness. A more complex baseline (e.g., XGBoost) would conflate architectural differences and obscure the hypothesis.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The defender successfully rebuts all criticism by showing that the experiment design correctly isolates the impact of sequence modeling through a temporally sound split, fair feature access, appropriate metric choice, and controlled confounds. The reviewer fails to identify any actual flaws.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic raises concerns that are already addressed by the design (e.g., temporal leakage, baseline strength, metric choice), and the defender correctly explains why these are not flaws given the domain and hypothesis.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises a concern about a potential confound (e.g., agent leakage or product drift), and the defender confirms that it is already controlled for in Section 8, with test stratification and aggregate statistics preventing such biases.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender provides specific evidence from the design (e.g., 'agent identities are replaced with aggregate statistics') that the concern is mitigated, showing robustness to plausible alternative explanations.
